You said: Is Claude code capable of building fpl app
Is Claude code capable of building fpl app

Apr 20
Claude responded: Yes, Claude Code can definitely help build a Fantasy Premier League (FPL) app.
Yes, Claude Code can definitely help build a Fantasy Premier League (FPL) app. It's well-suited for this kind of project since FPL apps typically involve:

Fetching data from the official FPL API (which is free and public)
Building a frontend (React, Next.js, etc.) for displaying teams, players, fixtures, and stats
Backend logic for things like optimisation algorithms, transfer suggestions, or mini-league analysis
Database setup if you want to store historical data or user preferences
Claude Code runs in your terminal and can scaffold the project, write the code, run it, debug errors, and iterate with you. People have built things like player price predictors, captain pickers, and chip strategy tools this way.

What kind of FPL app are you thinking about — something for personal use, a public tool, or specific functionality like transfer planning or mini-league tracking?

You said: how easy is this for claude code.
how easy is this for claude code. How will it handle transfers, jerseys, etc

Apr 24
Claude responded: Honest answer: the app structure is easy for Claude Code.
Honest answer: the app structure is easy for Claude Code. The FPL-specific details like transfers logic, jersey assets, and edge cases are where it gets trickier — not because Claude Code can't handle them, but because they require domain knowledge and decisions only you can make.

Here's a realistic breakdown:

What's easy (Claude Code handles well on its own) Setting up the project, fetching from the FPL API endpoints (/api/bootstrap-static/, /api/entry/{team_id}/, /api/fixtures/, etc.), parsing the JSON, building UI components for player lists, fixture tables, league standings, and basic stats dashboards. Authentication flows, routing, styling — all standard work.

Transfers — moderately tricky The FPL API gives you player data, prices, and a user's current squad, but actually suggesting transfers means writing logic for things like budget constraints, the 15-player squad rules (2 GK, 5 DEF, 5 MID, 3 FWD, max 3 per club), the £4m bench fodder problem, form vs fixtures weighting, and points hits. Claude Code can write all of this, but you need to tell it what strategy you want. A simple "highest expected points next gameweek within budget" optimiser is straightforward. A multi-gameweek planner with chip strategy is a bigger build but still very doable.

Jerseys — annoyingly fiddly The FPL API references jersey images by team code, and the actual PNG files live at predictable URLs on the FPL CDN (something like https://resources.premierleague.com/premierleague/badges/...). Claude Code can wire this up in a few minutes. The friction is that kits change each season, goalkeeper kits are separate, and if you want custom or stylised jerseys you'll need to source assets yourself. Using the official CDN images is the path of least resistance.

The honest catches The FPL API is undocumented, so Claude Code will sometimes guess endpoint behaviour wrong and need correction. Rate limiting exists but isn't published. And during the season the API occasionally changes shape without warning. None of these are blockers, just things you'll iterate through.

If you want, I can sketch out what a starter prompt to Claude Code would look like for a specific version of this — say, a transfer suggester or a mini-league tracker. Which direction interests you most?

Create a planning file I can give to Claude Code that allows it to understand nd build this in the cleanest way possible. The app is an fps app that predicts player confidence. Confidence are calculated in this way: "Performance against big team +2

Man of the match performance against big team +3
Performance against non big team +1
Man of the match performance against non big team +2
Blank against big team -1
Blank against non big team -2
For defenders and keepers clean sheet against a non big team is a +1
For defenders and keepers clean sheet against a big team is a +2
For defenders and keepers assist against a non big team is a +2 (man of the match)
For defenders and keepers assist against a big team is a +3 (man of the match)

Any player with above 0 point confidence has high tendency to return
performance means 1 assist
Man of the match performance means assist greater than 1 or 1 or more goals
After 3 man of the match performance -2 for confidence fatigue • Range is -5 ~ +5. Every player starts at 0. Missed matches are not calculated. "

Drop this in your repo root as PLANNING.md. Claude Code will pick it up automatically as project context. You can also create a much shorter CLAUDE.md alongside it with just the rules you want enforced every turn (e.g. "Always check PLANNING.md section 2 before touching the calculator").

A couple of things I locked in opinionatedly that you should sanity-check:

Big team list defaults — I went with last season's top 6 (City, Arsenal, Liverpool, Villa, Spurs, Chelsea), but made it editable in settings. If your definition is different (e.g. just "Big 6" historically, or top 4), tweak section 2.1.
GK/DEF resolution order in section 2.4 — your rules had some ambiguity (e.g. does a defender who scores AND keeps a clean sheet stack both bonuses?). I chose to stack them and clamp at the end. If you wanted only the highest single bonus to apply, change that section before handing off.
Fatigue counter — I made it cumulative across the season and reset after applying. You might want it as a rolling window (e.g. last 5 matches). Worth deciding now.

I'll create the full set of drop-in files. For Claude Code in VS Code, the convention is CLAUDE.md in the repo root (auto-loaded as context every session) plus supporting docs it can reference.

Created 8 files, ran a command
Created 8 files, ran a command
Now let me bundle everything into a zip for easy download.

There's some leftover content from a previous workspace state. Let me clean up and present only what's needed.

Ran a command, read a file
Ran a command, read a file
Done. Grab the zip for the easiest setup — it preserves the folder structure. Or download the individual files.

What's in the package
your-repo/
├── CLAUDE.md ← auto-loaded by Claude Code every session
├── PLANNING.md ← full product spec & build order
├── README.md
├── .gitignore
├── .claude/
│ └── settings.json ← pre-approved commands (no permission prompts for npm/git)
└── docs/
├── ALGORITHM.md ← confidence rules + 14 unit-test cases + pseudocode
├── UI_GUIDELINES.md ← design tokens, screen briefs, motion rules
└── API.md ← FPL endpoints, caching, SQLite schema
How to use it
Unzip into your cloned repo root (or drop the individual files in matching paths).
Open the repo in VS Code with the Claude Code extension.
Start a new Claude Code session. It will auto-load CLAUDE.md, which tells it to read the rest in order.
First prompt to send:
Read CLAUDE.md, then PLANNING.md, then docs/ALGORITHM.md, docs/UI_GUIDELINES.md, and docs/API.md. After that, summarize back to me in 5 bullets what you understand the project to be, then start step 1 in the build order (scaffold Next.js + Tailwind + shadcn with the design tokens).

A few things worth knowing
.claude/settings.json pre-approves common commands (npm, git, shadcn, vitest) so Claude Code doesn't prompt for each one. It also blocks rm -rf, git push, and git reset --hard as a safety net. Tweak to taste.

The algorithm doc has 14 worked examples that are explicitly designed to become unit tests. When Claude Code writes calculator.test.ts, you can verify correctness by checking that every numbered example became an it(...) block.

One ambiguity I resolved on your behalf that you should sanity-check before letting Claude Code commit anything: in ALGORITHM.md example 14, I clamped clamping to "once at the end of all match adjustments (including fatigue)." This means a player at +5 confidence who hits their 3rd MOTM vs a big team ends at +5, not +3, because the +3 MOTM and −2 fatigue net out before the clamp fires. If you'd rather clamp twice (after MOTM, then again after fatigue), say the word and I'll flip it — it changes the behaviour at the upper bound only.

What changed in this version
I added two new docs and rewired everything around them:

New: docs/ENGINEERING.md — the principal-engineer bar. Codifies layered architecture (Presentation → Application → Domain → Infrastructure), TypeScript strictness (noUncheckedIndexedAccess, no any, no !, branded types for IDs, Zod for trust boundaries), Result types for error handling, naming and function conventions, performance budgets (Lighthouse ≥ 90, JS bundle < 200KB on the heaviest route), accessibility floor, security rules (parameterized SQL, no dangerouslySetInnerHTML), git hygiene (Conventional Commits, squash merges), and a PR-level Definition of Done checklist.

New: docs/TESTING.md — the testing discipline. Mandates TDD for src/lib/, sets coverage floors (90% lib / 70% components), specifies the test pyramid (75% unit / 20% integration / 5% E2E), tools (Vitest + Testing Library + MSW + Playwright + jest-axe + fast-check), and forbids snapshot tests, Enzyme, Jest, and class-name assertions. Includes worked examples of unit, component, integration, and property tests. Defines what "done" looks like for any test.

What changed in existing docs
CLAUDE.md — now opens with the engineering bar, mandates the frontend-design skill on every UI task (with explicit override of its anti-Inter advice in favor of the intentional Geist Sans / Fraunces choice), mandates TDD for all logic, sets the precedence order when docs conflict (ENGINEERING > PLANNING > ALGORITHM > TESTING > UI_GUIDELINES > API).

PLANNING.md — build order is now 12 sequential steps starting with a Foundation step (CI green, hooks installed, strict TS configured) before any feature work. Every UI step explicitly states "Use the frontend-design skill." Step 2 (Confidence calculator) is now explicit TDD with all 14+ examples + 3 property tests. Project structure expanded to show colocated .test.tsx files, **fixtures**/ folders, per-module READMEs, and a dedicated e2e/ folder.

docs/UI_GUIDELINES.md — fonts swapped from Inter to Geist Sans + Fraunces to satisfy the frontend-design skill's anti-generic-font rule. Adds an explicit anti-pattern list ("no Inter, no purple-on-white"). Spelled out data-sign testing hook on ConfidenceNumber so component tests have a stable target.

docs/ALGORITHM.md — types are now readonly everywhere (matches the immutability bar in ENGINEERING.md). Worked examples renumbered as canonical test IDs (EX-01 through EX-15) so tests can reference them directly. Added EX-15 (assist + goal in same match) and three required property tests (PROP-01..03). Reference implementation cleaned up to match ENGINEERING.md style (helper functions, named constants).

docs/API.md — full Zod schemas inline, so Claude Code can copy them verbatim. Error handling routes through the Result<T, FetchError> type defined in ENGINEERING.md.

.claude/settings.json — added Playwright, eslint, prettier, and additional npm test scripts to the pre-approved list. Added git clean -fd to the deny list.

Suggested first prompt to Claude Code
Read CLAUDE.md, then docs/ENGINEERING.md, then PLANNING.md, then docs/ALGORITHM.md, docs/TESTING.md, docs/UI_GUIDELINES.md, and docs/API.md. After that, summarize back to me in 5 bullets what you understand the project to be, including the engineering bar and testing discipline. Then begin Step 0 (Foundation) from the build order: scaffold the Next.js project with strict TypeScript, configure ESLint + Prettier + Vitest + Playwright, and set up the CI workflow. Stop and show me the green CI run before continuing.

You said: claude is running.
claude is running. Wondering where it will get the team jerseys

Apr 25
Claude responded: Good question to ask now before it goes hunting.
Good question to ask now before it goes hunting. The jersey URLs are documented in docs/API.md §3, but let me give you a quick reality check on what Claude Code will actually find when it tries to fetch them.

The URLs I documented are the standard FPL CDN paths that have been used for years:

Outfield kit: https://fantasy.premierleague.com/dist/img/shirts/standard/shirt_{team_code}-66.png
Goalkeeper kit: https://fantasy.premierleague.com/dist/img/shirts/standard/shirt_{team_code}_1-66.png
Team badge: https://resources.premierleague.com/premierleague/badges/t{team_code}.png
The team_code value comes from bootstrap-static → teams[].code — not teams[].id. This is the #1 thing Claude Code will get wrong if it's not paying attention. For example, Arsenal's id might be 1 but their code is 3.

A few things worth knowing that might trip Claude Code up:

The FPL CDN paths have shifted slightly between seasons in the past. If dist/img/shirts/standard/ returns 404s, the alternative paths to try are dist/img/shirts/photos/ or just dropping /standard/ entirely. Tell Claude Code to verify with one manual curl on a known team code (Arsenal = 3, Liverpool = 14) before wiring up the whole pipeline.

The size suffix (-66, -110, -220) doesn't always exist for every team, and the -220 size in particular is sometimes missing. The safest sizes that always exist are -66 (small, for tables) and -110 (medium). For the player detail hero, I'd actually recommend -110 displayed at 2x rather than relying on -220.

The badges endpoint at resources.premierleague.com is more stable than the FPL kit endpoint, but it returns badges only — not kits. Don't confuse the two.

If Claude Code asks about jerseys mid-build, give it this prompt:

Before implementing the jersey route handler, run curl -I against three known team codes (Arsenal=3, Liverpool=14, Man City=43) on both the -66 and -110 size paths. Confirm they return 200. If any return 404, report back before writing the caching layer. Then implement the route handler at app/api/jerseys/[code]/route.ts per docs/API.md §4, with a test that uses MSW to mock a successful fetch and a 404 fallback.

Two genuine fallbacks if the CDN ever fails:

fpl-static npm package — community-maintained, bundles team assets. Adds ~2MB to the bundle but works offline.
Generate them from team colors. bootstrap-static doesn't include team colors directly, but it's a small static map (20 teams). A simple SVG kit silhouette filled with the team's primary/secondary colors looks surprisingly good and is what some popular FPL tools (e.g. Fantasy Football Hub on certain views) actually use. This is also a more distinctive design choice that aligns with the "don't look like every other FPL app" vibe — worth considering even as the primary, not just fallback.
Want me to add a "Jersey strategy" section to docs/API.md that covers all of this, including the SVG fallback approach? Or are you happy to handle it ad-hoc as Claude Code hits each piece?

Apr 25
Claude responded: You can update the docs while Claude Code is running — it won't pick up the changes automatically, but that's fine.
You can update the docs while Claude Code is running — it won't pick up the changes automatically, but that's fine. Here's the clean way to handle it:

Option 1: Update the file, then tell Claude to re-read it (recommended)

Save the updated docs/API.md (or whichever file) in your editor.
In the Claude Code chat, send a message like:
I've updated docs/API.md with a new "Jersey strategy" section. Please re-read the file before continuing, and confirm you've understood the new fallback approach.

Claude Code will read the current version from disk. It uses files fresh each time — there's no cached copy in its head from session start.

Option 2: Tell Claude Code the change inline (faster for small edits)

If the change is small (like the jersey URL clarification I gave you), you can just paste it directly into the chat:

Update note for docs/API.md: when fetching jerseys, verify the CDN path with curl first. If /dist/img/shirts/standard/ 404s, try /dist/img/shirts/photos/. Use -110 size at 2x display rather than -220 since -220 isn't always available. Apply this to the jersey route handler when you build it.

Claude Code will treat this as a binding instruction for the rest of the session. You can also ask it to update the file itself:

Add the above as a new section "§3.1 Jersey fetching gotchas" in docs/API.md, then continue with what you were doing.

Option 3: Pause it, update files, resume

If you have a lot to change:

Send Wait — I need to update some docs before you continue. Pause here.
Make your edits to the files in VS Code.
Send I've updated CLAUDE.md, PLANNING.md, and docs/API.md. Re-read all three and tell me what changed before resuming.
This is the safest option for big rewrites because it forces Claude Code to consciously re-acknowledge the new instructions rather than running on stale context.

A few things worth knowing

Claude Code does not watch files for changes. If you edit a file silently and don't tell it, it'll keep working from whatever it last read. So always send a "re-read X" message after edits.

The CLAUDE.md file is auto-loaded only at session start. Mid-session edits to CLAUDE.md won't take effect unless you explicitly tell Claude to re-read it.

If Claude Code is mid-task (e.g. writing a component), let it finish that small unit of work before interrupting — interrupting mid-edit can leave files in a partial state. Wait for it to come back with "done with X, moving to Y," then interject.

For your specific jersey question: since Claude Code is running and probably hasn't hit the jersey work yet (it's later in the build order — step 7 or so), I'd just paste the inline update from Option 2 right now. Something like:

Heads up for when you reach the jersey/badge work later: before implementing the route handler, curl three known team codes (Arsenal=3, Liverpool=14, Man City=43) on both -66 and -110 size paths to verify the CDN paths still work. If /dist/img/shirts/standard/ returns 404, try /dist/img/shirts/photos/. Prefer -110 displayed at 2x for the hero rather than -220. Also consider an SVG fallback using team colors as a more distinctive visual choice — discuss with me when you get there.

That'll sit in the conversation context and Claude Code will hit it when it gets to that step.

Apr 25
Claude responded: Easy — just tell it directly.
Easy — just tell it directly. Here's a clean prompt you can paste into the Claude Code chat right now:

Pause whatever you're working on for a moment. I need you to update docs/API.md to add a "Jersey strategy" section that covers:

The team_code vs team_id distinction (use teams[].code, not teams[].id, for jersey URLs). Flag this prominently — it's the most common mistake.
A verification step: before implementing the jersey route handler, curl three known team codes (Arsenal=3, Liverpool=14, Man City=43) on both -66 and -110 size paths to confirm the CDN still serves them. If /dist/img/shirts/standard/ returns 404, try /dist/img/shirts/photos/.
Size guidance: prefer -110 displayed at 2x for the player detail hero rather than -220, since -220 isn't always available for every team.
Two fallback strategies if the CDN ever breaks: (a) the fpl-static npm package, (b) generating SVG kits from team colors — note that the SVG approach is also worth considering as the primary visual choice for design distinctiveness.
Add this as a new section in docs/API.md placed logically near the existing jersey/badge content. Match the existing doc's tone and formatting. After updating, show me the diff and confirm the file still validates against our doc structure. Then resume what you were doing.

A few notes on why this prompt is shaped this way:

It tells Claude Code to pause first, so it doesn't try to interleave the doc edit with whatever code it's writing. It gives specific content rather than "figure out what to add" — Claude Code is much more reliable when you hand it the substance and let it handle placement and tone. It asks for a diff at the end so you can review before it moves on. And it explicitly says resume so you don't have to send a follow-up "okay, keep going."

If you'd rather have Claude Code take more initiative (less prescriptive), you can shorten it to:

Pause and update docs/API.md with a "Jersey strategy" section. Cover the team_code vs team_id gotcha, CDN verification with curl, size selection guidance (prefer -110 at 2x over -220), and two fallbacks (fpl-static package or SVG generated from team colors). Show me the diff before resuming.

Both work. The first gives you more control, the second trusts Claude Code more.

Apr 25
Claude responded: Solid work.
Solid work. Step 0 looks tight — strict flags all in, the conditional-spread fix on workers is exactly the right move (and a nice early signal that exactOptionalPropertyTypes is doing its job catching real issues, not just being noisy).

A few things worth glancing at before you sign off, just to make sure the foundation is genuinely load-bearing:

Quick sanity check (2 minutes):

Run npm run typecheck && npm run lint locally one more time — confirm zero warnings, not just zero errors. ESLint warnings have a way of accumulating into noise floor if you don't catch them at zero on day one.
Make a deliberate violation (drop a console.log somewhere or use any) and confirm both the pre-commit hook and CI catch it. This validates the safety net actually works rather than just being configured.
Check that passWithNoTests: true in vitest.config.ts is flagged as a Step 0 temporary. Add a // TODO(step-2): remove passWithNoTests once calculator tests exist comment so it doesn't silently let an empty test run slide later.
One thing I'd flag for Step 1 before you proceed:

Geist Sans and Fraunces both load via next/font, but make sure Claude Code uses the variable versions (Geist exports a variable font, Fraunces has a true variable axis). If it loads multiple weight files instead of one variable file, you'll burn ~80KB of font payload unnecessarily, and the performance budget in ENGINEERING.md (200KB JS on the heaviest route) won't have much breathing room.

Also worth telling Claude Code explicitly: the /\_dev/styles page should be excluded from the production build. Either put it behind a process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' check or in a route group like (dev) that's gated. Otherwise you'll ship a styles audit page to users.

My sign-off prompt for Claude Code:

If those 3 sanity checks pass, here's a clean Step 1 kickoff message you can paste:

Step 0 signed off. Two things to add before you start Step 1:

Add // TODO(step-2): remove passWithNoTests once calculator tests exist next to the passWithNoTests: true line in vitest.config.ts.
Confirm the pre-commit hook and CI both catch a deliberate any or console.log violation. Run that test once and report back.
Then begin Step 1 — Design system foundation. Use the frontend-design skill. Specific requirements:

Claude responded: Ah, I think there's been a misunderstanding worth clearing up — and it's an important one before you go further.
Ah, I think there's been a misunderstanding worth clearing up — and it's an important one before you go further.

What you're looking at is correct for Step 1. That's the design system page (/\_dev/styles), an internal review screen showing color tokens, typography, spacing, and component variants. It's a developer reference, not a user-facing screen. You'll never ship this to users.

But the bigger question is what you actually want the app to be. Re-reading your messages, I think we may have spec'd the wrong app. Let me check.

The spec we wrote together describes a confidence analytics tool: four screens (Dashboard, Players list, Player detail, Settings) that show your custom Confidence metric for every Premier League player. There is no team management in the current spec — no pitch view, no formation, no captain selection, no transfers.

What you just described — player view with formation, subs, captain, transfers — is a different app. That's an FPL team manager, basically a clone of the official FPL site's "Pick Team" and "Transfers" screens, with your Confidence metric layered on top to inform decisions.

These are very different products:

Feature Current spec (analytics) What you described (team manager)
Pitch / formation view ❌ ✅
Your starting XI + bench ❌ ✅
Captain / vice-captain ❌ ✅
Make transfers ❌ ✅
Browse all players by Confidence ✅ ✅
Player detail with confidence history ✅ ✅
Requires you to log in your FPL team ❌ ✅ (or manual entry)
The team manager version is a bigger build — probably 2× the work — because it needs:

Your actual FPL squad (either via the FPL API's /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ endpoint, or manual entry, since FPL doesn't have a public auth API)
A pitch/formation renderer (SVG, with drag-to-swap)
Transfer logic respecting FPL rules (15-player squad, max 3 per club, budget, free transfers, points hits)
Captain/vice selection
Possibly chip strategy (Wildcard, Bench Boost, Triple Captain, Free Hit)
It also explicitly contradicts §11 of PLANNING.md, which says "importing a real FPL team" is out of scope.

Two paths forward — pick one:

Stay analytics-focused. The current spec is right. The /\_dev/styles screen you see now isn't the real app — that's just the design system review. The real screens (Dashboard, Players, Player Detail) get built in Steps 7–10 and are what you'll actually use. Confidence is the differentiator; you reference it manually when making decisions in the official FPL app.
Pivot to a team manager. We rewrite PLANNING.md, add new screens (Pitch, Transfers, Captain), and decide how you'll input your team (manual entry vs. FPL team ID lookup). This is a real pivot — probably 1.5–2× the work — but it's a more useful product if you actually want to manage your team in this app.
Tell Claude Code to pause Step 1 sign-off while you decide. Send something like:

Then come back to me and tell me which direction. If it's path 2, I'll rewrite PLANNING.md and the build order; if it's path 1, I'll send you the kickoff prompt for Step 2 and reassure you that the pitch/formation view is just not part of this product.

Claude responded: Got it — staying analytics-focused, plus adding Team Confidence (an aggregate score for an entire 15-player squad, like Sofascore/FotMob team ratings).
Got it — staying analytics-focused, plus adding Team Confidence (an aggregate score for an entire 15-player squad, like Sofascore/FotMob team ratings). That's a clean, high-value addition that fits the existing spec well.

Let me think through what this actually means before we wire it into the docs.

What Team Confidence is

A single number that summarizes how "in-form" a full FPL squad is, computed from the individual Confidence scores of the 15 players. The user enters their squad once (or imports via FPL team ID), and the app shows: an overall rating, a starting-XI rating, a bench strength rating, and a per-position breakdown.

The design questions I need you to answer

Before I update the docs, three decisions only you can make:

Tap your answers above and I'll wire them into the docs. While you're deciding, here's the math comparison for question 2 so you can see what each option produces:

Option A — Simple average (range −5 to +5):

TeamConfidence = sum(allConfidences) / 15
Example: 11 starters at +2, 4 bench at 0 → (22 + 0) / 15 = +1.47
Easy to explain, but a player you'll never play affects the rating equally to your captain.

Option B — Weighted starting XI (range −5 to +5):

TeamConfidence = (sum(startingXI) × 0.85 + sum(bench) × 0.15) / 15
Example: 11 starters at +2, 4 bench at 0 → (22 × 0.85 + 0 × 0.15) / 15 = +1.25
More accurate to actual scoring, but requires you to designate a starting XI (which means a formation picker, even if simple).

Option C — show me options first: I'll write up 3–4 variants in detail (including a "captain-aware" version where the captain counts double, mirroring real FPL) and you pick.

Once you've answered the three questions, I'll send a single update prompt for Claude Code that revises PLANNING.md, ALGORITHM.md, and UI_GUIDELINES.md to incorporate Team Confidence cleanly into the existing build order — without disrupting Step 1 sign-off.

Apr 25
Claude responded: Good choices.
Good choices. Here's the math comparison for Q2 — I'll lay out four real options, then recommend one.

The four aggregation options
Every option produces a number on the −5 to +5 scale to stay consistent with individual Confidence. I'll use the same example squad for all four so you can compare directly:

Example squad: 11 starters with confidences [+4, +3, +3, +2, +2, +2, +1, +1, +1, 0, 0]. 4 bench players: [0, -1, -1, -2]. Captain is the +4 player. Average starter = +1.73. Average bench = -1.

Option A — Simple average (treats everyone equally)
TeamConfidence = sum(all 15 confidences) / 15
= (19 + (-4)) / 15
= +1.00
Pros: Trivial to explain. Stable. Cons: Your bench fodder counts as much as your captain, which is wrong — you'll never play them. A team with a great XI but bad bench looks worse than it actually is.

Option B — Weighted XI vs bench (matches actual scoring impact)
TeamConfidence = ((avg starters × 11 × 0.85) + (avg bench × 4 × 0.15)) / weighted divisor
Simpler version that produces the right shape:

TeamConfidence = (avg starters × 0.85) + (avg bench × 0.15)
= (1.73 × 0.85) + (-1.00 × 0.15)
= 1.47 + (-0.15)
= +1.32
Pros: Reflects how points are actually scored — XI matter way more. Bench still counts (they get auto-subbed in if a starter doesn't play), but proportionally less. Cons: Requires the user to nominate a starting XI, which means a formation picker (or we trust the FPL API's "is_captain"/"position" fields, which is doable since you're importing via team ID).

Option C — Captain-aware weighted (mirrors real FPL math)
In real FPL, the captain scores double. So the captain's Confidence should weigh double too.

weight = position 1-11 (starter) → 1.0; captain → 2.0; bench → 0.15
TeamConfidence = sum(confidence × weight) / sum(weights)

starters total weight: 10 × 1.0 + 1 × 2.0 = 12.0
bench total weight: 4 × 0.15 = 0.6
total divisor: 12.6

starters contribution: (3+3+2+2+2+1+1+1+0+0) × 1.0 + 4 × 2.0 = 15 + 8 = 23
bench contribution: (0 + -1 + -1 + -2) × 0.15 = -4 × 0.15 = -0.6

TeamConfidence = (23 - 0.6) / 12.6 = +1.78
Pros: Most accurate to how points are actually earned. A bad captain is genuinely punishing; a great captain genuinely lifts your team. This matches what FPL managers actually feel. Cons: Slightly harder to explain. Requires captain info (which the FPL API already gives you — picks[].is_captain).

Option D — Three separate ratings, no single number
Instead of one number, show three:

Starting XI Confidence (avg of 11)
Bench Confidence (avg of 4)
Captain Confidence (just the captain's score)
Pros: Maximum information. No averaging math the user has to trust. Cons: Breaks the "one hero number" design principle. The Dashboard has nowhere clean to put three numbers — you wanted a hero element. This works better on the My Team page as a breakdown, but not as the headline metric.

My recommendation: Option C, with Option D as the breakdown
Use Option C (captain-aware weighted) as the single Team Confidence number that lives in the Dashboard hero and at the top of My Team. Then on My Team, also show the three breakdowns from Option D as supporting stats. Best of both: one glanceable number that's actually meaningful, plus the underlying breakdown for users who want to see why.

This is also what Sofascore-style team ratings effectively do under the hood — they're weighted toward who actually played and who was the standout.

The math is simple enough that you can put a small "How is this calculated?" tooltip on the dashboard hero showing the formula, and FPL managers will get it instantly.

Which do you want?
Tap your pick. Once you confirm, I'll write a single comprehensive update prompt for Claude Code that:

Adds a new My Team screen to PLANNING.md (so it's now 5 screens, not 4)
Adds a Team Confidence calculator spec to ALGORITHM.md with worked examples + property tests
Adds a /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ section to API.md (the FPL endpoint that gives you a manager's squad)
Updates the Dashboard brief in UI_GUIDELINES.md to put Team Confidence as the hero element above the existing risers/fallers strip
Inserts new build steps into PLANNING.md §9 — slotted after the existing player work since Team Confidence depends on individual Confidence already being computed
Removes "Importing a real FPL team" from the Out of Scope list (§11)
The update won't disrupt Steps 1, 2, or anything else Claude Code has already done — Team Confidence is purely additive and lives downstream of the player calculator.

Apr 25
Claude responded: Got it — three clean changes from what we had:
Got it — three clean changes from what we had:

No captain weighting — just positional averaging
Three sub-ratings (Defence, Midfield, Attack) that roll up to one team number
Display as percentage, not the −5 to +5 scale
Let me work through the math so you can sanity-check before I send the update prompt to Claude Code.

The proposed model
Step 1 — Three positional averages (still on −5 to +5 internally)
Group the 15-player squad by position and average each group:

Defence = average of all DEF + GK confidences (5 players: 2 GK + 3 DEF, or similar)
Midfield = average of all MID confidences (5 players)
Attack = average of all FWD confidences (3 players)
Wait — FPL squads are always 2 GK + 5 DEF + 5 MID + 3 FWD = 15. So the positional groups are fixed in size. That's clean.

A question I need to flag: GK with DEF, or GK on its own? Two options:

3 groups (recommended): Defence = GK + DEF (7 players), Midfield = MID (5), Attack = FWD (3). Matches how football fans actually talk — "the defensive unit" includes the keeper.
4 groups: GK alone (2), DEF (5), MID (5), FWD (3). More granular but produces a noisy GK rating since it's only averaging 2 players.
I'd recommend 3 groups. A 2-player GK average swings wildly when one keeper has a bad week.

Step 2 — Team Confidence is the average of the three group averages
TeamConfidence = (Defence + Midfield + Attack) / 3
This is not the same as averaging all 15 players. It gives each line equal weight, so a great defence can't be hidden by a struggling attack. Subtle but matters.

Step 3 — Convert to percentage
The internal scale is −5 to +5 (a 10-point range). Map it to 0–100%:

percent = ((confidence + 5) / 10) × 100
Mappings:

−5 → 0%
0 → 50%
+5 → 100%
+1 → 60%
+3 → 80%
So a "neutral" team (everyone at 0) shows as 50%. A team in great form (everyone at +3) shows as 80%. This is an important framing — it means "above 50% = better than neutral, back this team."

Worked example
Same squad as before:

2 GK + 3 DEF (defensive group of 5 in a 4-4-2 starting): confidences [+1, +2, +3, 0, +1] plus 2 bench DEF [0, -1] = group average +0.86
Wait — this gets complicated when you mix starters and bench across positions.
Let me simplify the worked example with a cleaner squad:

Defence group (2 GK + 5 DEF = 7 players): [+1, 0, +2, +3, +1, 0, -1] → avg +0.86 Midfield group (5 MID): [+4, +3, +2, +1, 0] → avg +2.00 Attack group (3 FWD): [+3, +2, -1] → avg +1.33

TeamConfidence = (0.86 + 2.00 + 1.33) / 3 = +1.40
Percent = ((1.40 + 5) / 10) × 100 = 64%
Display:

                    64%
                Team Confidence

Defence Midfield Attack
59% 70% 63%
(Each group's percentage is computed the same way: ((groupAvg + 5) / 10) × 100.)

A subtlety worth confirming
Should starters and bench be treated identically inside each group? With pure averaging, your 4 bench players still pull their group averages around even though you'll usually never play them.

Two options:

A) Treat all 15 players equally inside each group (simpler, what I described above).

Pro: Easy to explain. No XI selection needed.
Con: A bench fodder defender at -2 drags your defence rating down even though they'll never play.
B) Only count the starting XI inside each group (cleaner signal, slightly harder).

Pro: Reflects who's actually scoring you points.
Con: Requires knowing who the starters are. The FPL API gives this directly via picks[].position (1–11 = starters, 12–15 = bench), so it's basically free. But the group sizes vary by formation: a 3-4-3 has 3 defenders in the XI, a 5-3-2 has 5.
I'd lean toward B. You're already importing via FPL team ID, so you get the formation for free, and it makes the rating actually mean what it sounds like it means.

Tap your two answers. Once those land I'll have everything I need to write the single update prompt for Claude Code that adds:

/api/entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ section in docs/API.md
New build steps in PLANNING.md §9 (slotted after individual confidence work — Step 9.5 ish)
Removal of "Importing a real FPL team" from §11 Out of Scope
Worth noting: this expansion is real — it adds probably 2–3 days of work (a new screen, a new API integration, a new calculator with its own tests). But it's all additive and slots cleanly into the existing build order without touching anything Claude Code has already built. Step 1 sign-off is unaffected.

Updates required

1. Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
   Add a new section §11 — Team Confidence at the end of the file. Cover:

Inputs

ts
export interface SquadPick {
readonly playerId: number;
readonly position: 'GK' | 'DEF' | 'MID' | 'FWD';
readonly squadPosition: number; // 1–11 = starter, 12–15 = bench
readonly confidence: number; // -5..+5, from individual calculator
}

export interface TeamCalculatorInput {
readonly picks: readonly SquadPick[]; // exactly 15
}
Outputs

ts
export interface PositionalRating {
readonly average: number; // -5..+5
readonly percent: number; // 0..100, rounded to 1 decimal
readonly playerCount: number; // count of starters in this group
}

export interface TeamCalculatorOutput {
readonly teamConfidence: number; // -5..+5
readonly teamPercent: number; // 0..100, rounded to 1 decimal
readonly defence: PositionalRating; // GK + DEF starters only
readonly midfield: PositionalRating; // MID starters only
readonly attack: PositionalRating; // FWD starters only
}
Algorithm

Validate input: exactly 15 picks, exactly 11 starters (squadPosition 1–11), exactly 4 bench (12–15). Reject otherwise — return a typed validation error via Result.
Filter to starters only (squadPosition <= 11).
Group starters by line:
Defence = GK + DEF
Midfield = MID
Attack = FWD
For each group, average the confidences. If a group is empty (e.g. a hypothetical formation with no forwards), the group's average is 0 and playerCount is 0. Document this edge case explicitly.
teamConfidence = (defence.average + midfield.average + attack.average) / 3.
Important: this is the average of the three group averages, not the average of all 11 starters. Document why (each line gets equal weight regardless of formation).
Convert any −5..+5 value to percent via: percent = ((value + 5) / 10) × 100, rounded to 1 decimal place.
−5 → 0.0%, 0 → 50.0%, +5 → 100.0%, +1 → 60.0%, +3 → 80.0%
Required worked examples (TEAM-EX-01..06) — make these into unit tests:

TEAM-EX-01: All starters at 0 → teamConfidence=0, teamPercent=50.0, all lines 50.0%.
TEAM-EX-02: All starters at +3 → teamConfidence=+3, teamPercent=80.0.
TEAM-EX-03: All starters at -5 → teamConfidence=-5, teamPercent=0.0.
TEAM-EX-04: 4-4-2 with defence=[+1,+2,+3,+1,0] (GK+4 DEF), midfield=[+4,+3,+2,+1], attack=[+3,+2] → defence avg +1.4, midfield avg +2.5, attack avg +2.5 → team avg ≈ +2.13, team percent ≈ 71.3%.
TEAM-EX-05: 3-5-2 formation with defence=[0,+1,+1,+2] (GK+3 DEF), midfield=[+3,+2,+2,+1,0], attack=[+1,-1] → verify line groupings handle different formations correctly.
TEAM-EX-06: Bench has +5 players, starters average +0 → teamConfidence=0, teamPercent=50.0 (proves bench is excluded).
Required property tests (TEAM-PROP-01..03):

TEAM-PROP-01: teamPercent always in [0, 100] for any valid input.
TEAM-PROP-02: Permuting bench players (positions 12–15) never changes the output.
TEAM-PROP-03: For any valid input, teamConfidence ∈ [-5, +5].
Module location: src/lib/team-confidence/ with the same structure as src/lib/confidence/ (index.ts barrel, calculator.ts, calculator.test.ts, types.ts, internal/, README.md, fixtures/).

We use position to determine starter vs bench. We do not use captain info (no captain weighting).

That's the full update. A few notes for you:

Why I structured it as docs-first, not code-first: the discipline you set up (CLAUDE.md says "follow the build order, don't skip ahead") means Claude Code shouldn't start implementing Team Confidence right now — Step 1 isn't even done yet. We're updating the spec so when Claude Code reaches the new Steps 9.5 and 9.7, it has clear instructions. This keeps the engineering discipline intact.

One subtlety worth knowing: the FPL /entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ endpoint requires the gameweek to have started (the squad locks at deadline). For the very first import before any deadline has passed in the season, the endpoint can 404. We're handling that with a typed error, but you may want to test with a team ID + gameweek that's already started so you get real data flowing through. Once Claude Code reaches Step 9.7, it'll likely ask you for a real team ID to test against — your own works fine.

Empty squad / unusual formation handling: I put the "empty group → 0" rule in there because some custom FPL chip strategies (free hit) can briefly produce weird squad states. Better to handle it now than discover it in production.

When Claude Code finishes the doc updates, paste the diffs back here if anything looks off and I'll help triage.

A few things worth flagging while Step 1 finishes, so they're queued for when you reach Step 9.7:

You'll need a real FPL team ID to test the squad import. Yours works fine. To find it, log into fantasy.premierleague.com, click "Pick Team" or "Points," and grab the number from the URL: fantasy.premierleague.com/entry/12345678/event/32 — the 12345678 is your team ID. Worth noting it down now.

The picks endpoint won't work before the season starts. If the current Premier League season's gameweek 1 hasn't kicked off yet, the endpoint returns 404 for every team ID because squads aren't locked in. Claude Code will handle the 404 as a typed error per the doc, but during development you'll want to test against a gameweek that's already finished. If you give me your team ID I can suggest a known-good test gameweek.

One small spec gap I noticed reviewing your summary — and you can defer this until Step 9.7 actually starts: the FPL API distinguishes between a manager's squad at a specific historical gameweek vs. their current squad. The endpoint is /entry/{id}/event/{gw}/picks/, so we always specify a GW. For the My Team page, should we show the squad as it was for the most recently finished gameweek, or attempt to show the live current squad? "Current squad before deadline" is harder because there's a separate endpoint for that (/my-team/{id}/) which actually requires authentication. Easier path: always show "last completed gameweek" — that's accurate, that's what their Confidence numbers reflect, and the endpoint is public. You don't need to decide right now; just be aware Claude Code will surface this when it reaches Step 9.7.

When Claude Code finishes Step 1, paste the screenshots and npm run build output back here and I'll review before sign-off.

Here's your Step 2 kickoff prompt — paste this in:

Step 1 signed off. Beginning Step 2 — Confidence calculator (TDD).

Read docs/ALGORITHM.md and docs/TESTING.md end-to-end before writing a single line of code.

Then proceed strictly TDD:

Create src/lib/confidence/ with the structure from PLANNING.md §8: index.ts, types.ts, calculator.ts (empty stub), calculator.test.ts, internal/, **fixtures**/matches.ts, README.md.
In types.ts, define the exact types from ALGORITHM.md §2 (all readonly, no any).
In **fixtures**/matches.ts, write a builder function aMatch(overrides) per TESTING.md §9.
In calculator.test.ts, write all 15 worked examples (EX-01..15) and all 3 property tests (PROP-01..03) as failing tests first. Use fast-check for properties. Run them and confirm every test fails for the right reason — no compile errors, just assertion failures.
Show me the failing test output before writing any implementation.
Then implement calculator.ts to make them all pass. If during implementation you notice the spec is ambiguous, stop and ask — don't invent.
Extract internal/fatigue.ts only if it genuinely improves clarity. Don't extract for the sake of it.
Write src/lib/confidence/README.md per ENGINEERING.md §1.2: purpose, public API, invariants. Three paragraphs.
Run npm run test:coverage. Confirm 100% on this module.
Stop and show me at two checkpoints: (a) failing test output before implementation, (b) green tests + coverage report when done. Don't merge or move on without my sign-off.

The two-checkpoint structure matters here. The first checkpoint catches one of the most common LLM failure modes — writing tests that look right but actually pass against an empty implementation because the assertions are weak. Seeing them fail "for the right reason" (e.g. "expected +3, got undefined") proves the tests have teeth.

I'd also expect Claude Code to flag at least one ambiguity during implementation. EX-15 (assist + goal in same match for a defender) and EX-14 (clamp interaction with fatigue) are the two most likely spots. If it sails through both without questions, that's a yellow flag worth probing — ask it to walk you through how those cases work.

Claude responded: Step 2 looks clean on the surface, but don't sign off yet — there's a discrepancy worth investigating before you do.
Step 2 looks clean on the surface, but don't sign off yet — there's a discrepancy worth investigating before you do.

The spec called for 15 worked examples (EX-01..15) plus 3 property tests = 18 tests total. Your output shows 21 tests, with EX-01..18. That's three extra examples Claude Code added on its own initiative.

This isn't necessarily bad — adding edge case coverage is a good engineering instinct. But it's a CLAUDE.md rule violation worth checking. The rule says: "Never modify the Confidence algorithm without my approval." If those extra three tests just cover additional edge cases of the existing rules (good), that's fine. If they encode new behavior I never approved (bad), we have a spec drift problem.

Paste this back to Claude Code:

Before I sign off Step 2, two things:

The spec called for EX-01..15 + PROP-01..03 = 18 tests. You delivered 21 with EX-01..18. Show me what EX-16, EX-17, and EX-18 cover. For each, tell me whether it tests an existing rule from docs/ALGORITHM.md (good — improved coverage) or whether it encodes new behavior (problem — needs approval and a doc update). If any of them are new behavior, do not proceed; we'll discuss before either reverting them or updating the algorithm doc.
Walk me through how your implementation handles EX-14 (clamp interaction with fatigue) and EX-15 (defender with assist AND goal in same match). For each, paste the relevant code path and explain in 2-3 sentences how it produces the expected output. I want to verify these aren't passing accidentally.
Don't move to Step 3 until I sign off. The 100% coverage is great, but I need to confirm the tests have real teeth on the trickiest cases.

The reason I'm being picky here: a 100% coverage number with weak assertions is the most insidious failure mode in TDD. The two examples I picked (EX-14 and EX-15) are the ones I flagged in the Step 2 kickoff prompt as most likely to hide ambiguity. If Claude Code can clearly articulate why each test passes for the right reason, you've got a rock-solid foundation. If the explanation is hand-wavy, we need to dig in.

Once you've reviewed the responses and you're satisfied, the sign-off is just:

Confirmed — EX-16/17/18 are within spec, EX-14/15 logic is correct. Step 2 signed off. Begin Step 3 — FPL API client. Read docs/API.md fully (§1, §2, §4, §7 are most relevant). Implement schemas first, then the api.ts client returning Result<T, FetchError>, then the cache layer. MSW for tests with onUnhandledRequest: 'error'. Stop and show me a green test run + a manual smoke test result against the real bootstrap-static endpoint before continuing.

But hold that until the EX-16/17/18 review comes back clean.

You said: Question 1: What EX-16/17/18 cover
Question 1: What EX-16/17/18 cover
All three test existing rules from [ALGORITHM.md](http://ALGORITHM.md). None introduce new behavior.
EX-16 — Performance vs big team (MID), aMatch({ assists: 1, isOpponentBigTeam: true })
[ALGORITHM.md](http://ALGORITHM.md) §4 scoring table has a full row: "Performance (1 assist, 0 goals) | vs Big: +2 | vs Non-Big: +1". EX-02 already tested the non-big side (+1). EX-16 tests the big-team side (+2) — the other half of the same row. Without it, the big ? 2 : 1 ternary in resolveMidFwd's Performance branch had its true arm never exercised.
EX-17 — DEF goal-only vs non-big, aMatch({ goals: 1, isOpponentBigTeam: false }) (DEF)
[ALGORITHM.md](http://ALGORITHM.md) §5 GK/DEF stacking path, step 2: "Goal (and didn't already trigger step 1)? Apply MOTM points (+3 / +2)". EX-05 tested the assist path (step 1) vs big. EX-06 tested goal + CS vs big. EX-17 exercises step 2 (goal, no assist) on the non-big variant — the big ? 3 : 2 ternary in the else if (match.goals >= 1) branch, non-big side.
EX-18 — GK blank vs non-big, aMatch({ isOpponentBigTeam: false }) (GK)
[ALGORITHM.md](http://ALGORITHM.md) §4: "Blank | vs Non-Big: −2". §5 step 4: blank only fires when steps 1–3 all skipped. EX-13 tested DEF blank vs big (−1). EX-18 tests the non-big side of the same blank step for GK — the big ? -1 : -2 ternary, non-big arm.
Summary: all three are symmetry tests — the other half of a ternary that had only one side exercised by the original 15.
Question 2: EX-14 and EX-15 code paths
EX-14 — Fatigue fires when already at ceiling
The test runs three MID MOTM vs big matches. The interesting moment is GW3. After GW2 the state is confidence = 5, motmCount = 2 (GW2 itself clamped from 6→5). GW3 enters calculateConfidence's loop body:

// calculator.ts:84–103
const { raw, reasons, isMotm } = resolveMidFwd(match);
// raw=3, isMotm=true (goals=1, big=true → MOTM vs big)

let totalRaw = raw; // 3
let fatigueApplied = false;

if (isMotm) {
motmCount += 1; // 2 → 3
if (motmCount >= FATIGUE_THRESHOLD) { // 3 >= 3 → true
totalRaw += FATIGUE_PENALTY; // 3 + (-2) = 1
reasonList.push('Fatigue −2');
fatigueApplied = true;
motmCount = 0;
}
}

confidence = clamp(before + totalRaw, CONFIDENCE_MIN, CONFIDENCE_MAX);
// clamp(5 + 1, -5, 5) = clamp(6, -5, 5) = 5
// delta = 5 - 5 = 0
The fatigue penalty reduces the raw from +3 to +1, but before is already 5 so 5 + 1 = 6 still clamps to 5. The delta is 5 - 5 = 0. This is not accidental — the fatigue and clamp are both applied to the same totalRaw and before, in that order, within the same iteration. If fatigue weren't applied, totalRaw would be 3 and the test would produce delta=0 for the wrong reason (clamp alone). The test asserts fatigueApplied: true and motmCounterAfter: 0, which distinguishes the correct path from a naive "already at ceiling" no-op.
EX-15 — Assist + goal in same DEF match, assist wins
resolveGkDef is called with goals=1, assists=1, isOpponentBigTeam=false, cleanSheet=false:

// calculator.ts:50–73
if (match.assists >= 1) { // 1 >= 1 → true
raw += big ? 3 : 2; // 0 + 2 = 2
reasons.push('Assist vs non-big team (MOTM)');
isMotm = true;
} else if (match.goals >= 1) { // skipped — if block already fired
// ...
}

if (match.cleanSheet) { /_ false — skipped _/ }

if (!isMotm && !match.cleanSheet) { /_ !true → false — skipped _/ }

return { raw: 2, reasons: ['Assist vs non-big team (MOTM)'], isMotm: true };
The else if on step 2 is the load-bearing structure: because assists >= 1 fires first, the goal branch is unreachable for this match. Back in calculateConfidence, motmCount increments to 1 (below the fatigue threshold), and confidence = clamp(0 + 2, -5, 5) = 2. The reason string contains only the assist entry — no goal entry, no double-count. The test asserts reason: 'Assist vs non-big team (MOTM)' (single entry, no +) and motmCounterAfter: 1, which would fail if the goal branch also fired (that would produce either a wrong reason string or raw=4).

Claude responded: Both responses are clean.
Both responses are clean. Sign off Step 2.

The EX-16/17/18 explanation is exactly the right answer — they're symmetry tests for ternary branches that the original 15 only exercised one side of. That's principal-engineer-grade thinking, not spec drift. If anything, our original ALGORITHM.md should have included these symmetric pairs from the start. (Worth a tiny doc backfill at some point so the spec matches the tests, but it's not blocking.)

The EX-14 and EX-15 walkthroughs are solid. The key tells that they're not passing accidentally:

For EX-14, the assertion on fatigueApplied: true and motmCounterAfter: 0 distinguishes the correct path from "naive clamp masks the bug." If fatigue weren't actually firing, those flags would be false/2 and the test would catch it. Good.

For EX-15, the else if chain is the load-bearing piece — a naive implementation using two separate if blocks instead of if/else if would produce raw = 4 (counted twice) and the test would fail on the delta. Good.

Sign-off prompt:

Confirmed — EX-16/17/18 are valid symmetry tests within the existing spec, EX-14 and EX-15 logic is correct and tested for the right reasons. Step 2 signed off.

One small follow-up to file mentally for the eventual polish pass (don't do it now): consider backfilling docs/ALGORITHM.md with the three symmetry cases as EX-16/17/18 so the spec and tests stay in lockstep. Track it as a TODO comment in calculator.test.ts referencing the doc.

Now begin Step 3 — FPL API client. Read docs/API.md end-to-end first. Then proceed in this order:

Schemas first. Implement the full Zod schema set from API.md §2 in src/lib/fpl/schemas.ts. Add a test file schemas.test.ts that validates against a real recorded bootstrap-static response (commit a fixture at src/lib/fpl/**fixtures**/bootstrap-static.json). The test should also include 2-3 negative cases — malformed responses that should fail parsing — to prove the schemas have teeth.
Types. Derive TS types from the Zod schemas via z.infer. Don't duplicate types.
Result type. If src/lib/utils/result.ts doesn't exist yet, create it per ENGINEERING.md §3.4. All API client functions return Result<T, FetchError>.
API client. Implement src/lib/fpl/api.ts: fetchBootstrapStatic, fetchElementSummary(playerId), fetchFixtures. Each: fetch → validate via Zod → return Result. On Zod failure, return { ok: false, error: { type: 'invalid_response', ... } }.
Tests with MSW. Configure MSW with onUnhandledRequest: 'error'. Test happy path, network failure, malformed response (Zod fail), HTTP 404. One test file per endpoint.
Cache layer. Wrap fetches with Next.js fetch cache options per API.md §4. Don't build an in-memory cache yourself — Next.js handles this.
Two checkpoints: (a) schemas + schema tests green; (b) full client + tests green + a manual smoke against the real bootstrap-static endpoint (curl or a one-off node script, paste the first 200 chars of the response so I can confirm it's live data).

Coverage floor: 90% on src/lib/fpl/. No any, no non-null assertions, no console.log.

A note on the recorded fixture: this is a small but meaningful discipline call. Some teams stub network responses inline in test files; that gets brittle and makes it impossible to spot when the real API shape changes. Keeping a real recorded bootstrap-static.json fixture in the repo means: (a) you can re-record it once a season to catch upstream changes, (b) the schemas are tested against actual data shape, not against the test-author's mental model of the data shape. It's how the FAANG-tier teams handle external API integrations.

Claude responded: Realistic answer: not until Step 6.
Realistic answer: not until Step 6. That's where the first real UI component (ConfidenceNumber) gets built and shown back to you. Steps 3 through 5 are pure backend plumbing — you won't have anything visual to look at until then.

Here's the rough mental model of where you are vs. where the visual progress kicks in:

Step What you see What it feels like
0 CI green output Done
1 Design system page Done — you saw the /dev/styles screenshots
2 Test output Done — calculator green
3 (now) Test output, JSON responses Backend — invisible
4 Test output, DB rows Backend — invisible
5 Test output, sync logs Backend — invisible
6 First real UI component Visual progress resumes
7 Players list page Real app screen
8 Player detail page Real app screen
9 Dashboard Real app screen
9.5 Test output Backend
9.7 My Team page Real app screen with your team
10/11 Settings + polish Final form
The reason it's structured this way is the same reason production apps are: you build the data layer first so the UI has something real to render. If you build UI on fake data, you discover all the data-layer problems after you've committed to UI choices. The order we set up — TDD on logic, then API client, then DB, then UI — is what stops you from rebuilding the players list three times because the data shape kept changing underneath it.

Two things you can do meanwhile:

Run npm run dev yourself anytime and visit localhost:3000/dev/styles. You'll see the design system page Claude already built. You can also browse the test output by running npm run test:watch in a terminal — watching the calculator tests reason through 21 cases is genuinely interesting if you're curious about the logic.
Ask Claude to show you the recorded fixture from Step 3. Once Claude finishes the schemas and saves src/lib/fpl/**fixtures**/bootstrap-static.json, that file is a real snapshot of what the FPL API actually returns for every player and team. It's not visual, but it's the first time you'll see real Premier League data flowing through your app. That's roughly 30 minutes to a couple hours away depending on Claude's pace.
If you want me to add visual progress checkpoints to the build order — like a "render the API client output as a raw debug list at /dev/api" page after Step 3, just to give you something to look at — I can suggest that as a tweak. It adds ~30 minutes of throwaway work per backend step but gives you something to see. Most teams don't bother because it's wasted effort, but it can help with motivation when you're 5 steps into invisible plumbing.

Want to add the debug pages, or wait it out until Step 6?

Apr 25
Claude responded: Correct — it's a Next.
Correct — it's a Next.js web app, runnable in any modern browser, on desktop or mobile.

Specifically:

Built with Next.js 15 (the React framework). Renders mostly on the server (React Server Components), ships minimal JavaScript to the browser.
Responsive — docs/UI_GUIDELINES.md and the engineering bar require it works at 375px (mobile), 768px (tablet), and 1280px (desktop). You'll be able to use it from your phone's browser.
Hosted however you want — Vercel is the obvious choice (free tier, made by the same people as Next.js, deploys in 60 seconds from your GitHub repo). But you could also self-host on any server, or run it locally only.
Single-user — there's no login, no accounts. The SQLite database lives on the server. If you deploy publicly, anyone visiting the URL would see your team's data. We deliberately scoped out multi-user support to ship faster.
What it is not:

Not a native iOS or Android app (no App Store, no .ipa/.apk)
Not a desktop app (no installer, no Electron)
Not a browser extension
If you eventually want a native mobile app, the cleanest path is "ship the web app first, evaluate, then add React Native or wrap it in Capacitor." Most products that go that route find the responsive web app is good enough for 95% of use cases — especially for an FPL companion tool you'd typically use on your phone for a few minutes a few times a week.

Two things worth deciding now-ish, but not urgent:

Will you deploy it publicly, or run it locally? If public, we should plan a quick deployment step at the end (Vercel + a one-time GitHub action). If local-only (you npm run dev on your machine when you want to use it), we save that work entirely. The single-user / no-auth scoping decision means if you deploy publicly, anyone with the URL sees your team — fine if it's an unguessable URL, but worth knowing.
Do you want it on your phone home screen? Next.js can be configured as a PWA (Progressive Web App) so it installs to your home screen and feels app-like, no app store required. Roughly 2-3 hours of work to add cleanly. Easy to defer to after MVP.
Neither of these affects what Claude Code is building right now. Just useful context.

You said: Let me decide later, but for sure will want to deploy publicly and get to a point where I can monetize.
Let me decide later, but for sure will want to deploy publicly and get to a point where I can monetize.

Apr 25
Claude responded: Got it — defer for now, but it's useful that I know where you're heading because there are a couple of things worth flagging before you go too far down the bui…
Got it — defer for now, but it's useful that I know where you're heading because there are a couple of things worth flagging before you go too far down the build, even if we don't act on them yet.

Public deployment + monetization changes the threat model and the legal posture significantly. What's fine for a personal tool isn't fine for a paid product. Here are the things you'd want to think through, ranked by how urgent they'll become:

Things that get harder if you wait too long to address them:

The FPL API is technically undocumented and unsupported. The Premier League / FPL has historically tolerated personal and free tools using it, but the moment you charge money you're a commercial entity making revenue off their data. Larger third-party FPL tools have received takedown notices in the past, especially around aggressive scraping or anything that looks like a clone. Worth a real review of their terms of use before you take payments. Some monetization paths are safer than others — premium features built on top of your analytics (Confidence is your own metric, not theirs) are stronger ground than reselling their data.

The single-user / no-auth scoping decision in our spec was made because monetization wasn't on the table. Public + paid means you need real authentication (so Alice can't see Bob's settings), per-user data isolation, and probably a "subscription" tier check. That's not a tweak — it's a meaningful re-architecture: the SQLite-on-server pattern doesn't work for multi-user. You'd want Postgres + a user table + auth (Clerk, Auth.js, or Supabase auth are all good options).

Payments themselves: Stripe is the default answer for SaaS. Adding Stripe properly (subscriptions, webhook handling, customer portal, failed-payment handling, prorations) is a 2-3 week project of its own done right. Worth knowing now so you don't underestimate it.

Things you can defer with no penalty:

PWA / mobile install — purely additive, do whenever.

Domain, branding, marketing site — all post-MVP.

Analytics / observability (PostHog, Plausible, Sentry) — add when you have your first user, not before.

My recommendation, pragmatically:

Stay the current course. Ship the single-user MVP exactly as planned. Use it yourself for a gameweek or two. Then decide if Confidence is genuinely valuable enough to other FPL managers that monetization is worth the substantial extra engineering. A lot of "I'll monetize this" projects discover during the personal-use phase that the differentiator isn't actually as strong as expected, and the multi-user rebuild was unnecessary. Better to learn that on a working personal tool than after building auth and payments.

If after using it you're still convinced, the natural break point is: ship MVP → get personal validation for 2-4 weeks → start a "v2 multi-user" branch with a fresh PLANNING.md that reuses the Confidence calculator, FPL client, and components but rebuilds the data layer for multi-tenancy. The calculator and UI components are already designed to be reusable — they're pure functions and standalone components — so that won't be wasted work.

One small thing you can do now to keep the door open without changing scope: I'll suggest Claude Code adds a thin abstraction over the SQLite client so it's not littered with db.prepare(...) calls everywhere. That makes a future Postgres swap a 1-day job instead of a 1-week job. Want me to send that as a tweak, or keep the build pure?

Apr 25
Claude responded: Fair question — let me explain plainly.
Fair question — let me explain plainly.

What I'm asking: do you want Claude Code to add a small wrapper around the database code so that if you ever switch databases later, it's easy?

The plain-English version of why this matters:

Right now, the plan has Claude Code writing database code that talks directly to SQLite. Imagine SQLite is the brand of fridge in your kitchen. The code says things like "open the SQLite fridge, get the milk" everywhere it needs data — there'll be 50+ of these direct references scattered across the app.

If you ever want to switch fridges (e.g. swap SQLite for Postgres when you go multi-user), you'd have to find and update all 50+ places. That's slow and error-prone.

The wrapper is like adding a butler in between. Instead of every part of the code talking to the fridge directly, it talks to the butler: "butler, get me the milk." The butler handles the fridge. If you swap fridges later, you only update the butler — the rest of the code never knows the fridge changed.

The tradeoff:

Add it now: ~30 minutes of extra work upfront. Future database swap is a 1-day job.
Skip it: Save 30 minutes now. Future database swap is a 1-week job.
My recommendation: add it. It's cheap insurance. The pattern is called a "repository pattern" and it's standard practice at the engineering bar we set in docs/ENGINEERING.md — your spec actually already kind of implies it (the layered architecture in §1.1 puts "Infrastructure" as a separate layer). So really I'm asking whether to make sure Claude Code follows through on what's already implied, or lets it slide.

The actual question: want me to send Claude Code a quick instruction now to make sure it builds the database layer with this wrapper pattern when it gets to Step 4? Yes or no — that's all I need.

You said: Yeah go for it
Yeah go for it

Claude responded: Here's the prompt — paste it into your Claude Code session whenever there's a natural break (don't interrupt mid-task):
Here's the prompt — paste it into your Claude Code session whenever there's a natural break (don't interrupt mid-task):

Note for when you reach Step 4 (SQLite layer): build the database access using the repository pattern, not direct SQLite calls scattered through the codebase.

Concretely:

Define repository interfaces in src/lib/db/repositories/ — one per aggregate (e.g. PlayerRepository, ConfidenceSnapshotRepository, ManagerSquadRepository, SyncMetaRepository). Each interface declares the methods the application layer needs (findById, upsert, listByGameweek, etc.) using domain types — never SQL types.
Implement them as concrete SQLite classes in src/lib/db/repositories/sqlite/ (e.g. SqlitePlayerRepository). Only these classes touch better-sqlite3 directly. Prepared statements are private to the implementation.
Application code imports the interface, never the implementation. Use a small createRepositories(db) factory in src/lib/db/index.ts that returns the concrete instances behind their interface types. Application/server code receives repositories via parameter, never imports the SQLite class directly.
Tests for the application layer use in-memory fake repositories (just a Map-backed object implementing the same interface). Tests for the SQLite implementations themselves use the temp-DB pattern from docs/TESTING.md §6.2 to verify real persistence behavior.
Update src/lib/db/README.md to document the pattern explicitly: "Application code depends on repository interfaces. SQLite is a swappable detail."
This is consistent with docs/ENGINEERING.md §1.1 (Infrastructure as a separate layer) and §1.3 (dependency injection). It does not change the schema, the storage engine, or the build order — it's purely about how the code is organized inside src/lib/db/. Add ~30 minutes to Step 4. Confirm you understand before continuing with Step 3.

A couple of notes for you, the human:

The phrase "swappable detail" is intentional — that's the philosophical framing from clean architecture / hexagonal architecture that big-tech codebases use. It signals to Claude Code (and to any future engineer reading the repo) that the database is not sacred infrastructure, it's a replaceable component.

If Claude Code pushes back or asks clarifying questions about the pattern, that's fine — engage with it. The repository pattern is one of those things that's easy to over-engineer (people end up with 5 layers of abstraction for a simple CRUD app). The version I described is the minimum viable version: interfaces, concrete classes, factory. Don't let it talk you into adding "use cases" or "DTOs" or a "unit of work" pattern on top — those are real patterns but overkill for this app's complexity.

Lastly: this slightly increases the test count for Step 4. You'll have tests for the SQLite implementations and tests for whatever uses them via the fakes. That's correct. The 90% coverage floor on src/lib/ should be hit comfortably, since both layers are testable in isolation.

Two reasons:

1. Claude Code is mid-task on Step 3. Interrupting mid-task is the #1 way to leave files in a half-edited state. If it's currently writing the API client and you drop a "by the way, here's a Step 4 instruction" message, it might pivot, or get confused about whether to finish the current file or start planning Step 4, or worst case partially apply the new instruction to the current work.

2. The instruction is for Step 4, which hasn't started. It doesn't matter if you give it now versus an hour from now versus right when Step 4 begins. It only needs to land before Step 4 work starts.

The right moment to send it: when Claude Code comes back to you with a Step 3 checkpoint — either checkpoint (a) "schemas + tests green, awaiting review" or checkpoint (b) "full client done, awaiting sign-off." Send it as part of your sign-off response, like:

Step 3 signed off. Proceed to Step 4. Note for Step 4: [paste the repository pattern prompt here]

Show me the interface files and the README before you implement the SQLite classes. Standard checkpoint at the end.

That way the instruction lands cleanly at the boundary between steps, when Claude Code has finished one logical unit and is starting fresh on the next. No half-state risk, no context confusion.

General rule going forward: instructions that change future work are best delivered at step boundaries. Instructions that fix current work need to interrupt — but those are usually short ("you're using any here, fix it") not long architectural notes.

Important architectural note for Step 4: build the database access using the repository pattern, not direct SQLite calls scattered through the codebase.

Concretely:

Define repository interfaces in src/lib/db/repositories/ — one per aggregate (e.g. PlayerRepository, ConfidenceSnapshotRepository, ManagerSquadRepository, SyncMetaRepository). Each interface declares the methods the application layer needs (findById, upsert, listByGameweek, etc.) using domain types — never SQL types.
Implement them as concrete SQLite classes in src/lib/db/repositories/sqlite/ (e.g. SqlitePlayerRepository). Only these classes touch better-sqlite3 directly. Prepared statements are private to the implementation.
Application code imports the interface, never the implementation. Use a small createRepositories(db) factory in src/lib/db/index.ts that returns the concrete instances behind their interface types. Application/server code receives repositories via parameter, never imports the SQLite class directly.
Tests for the application layer use in-memory fake repositories (a Map-backed object implementing the same interface). Tests for the SQLite implementations themselves use the temp-DB pattern from docs/TESTING.md §6.2 to verify real persistence behavior.
Update src/lib/db/README.md to document the pattern explicitly: "Application code depends on repository interfaces. SQLite is a swappable detail."
This is consistent with docs/ENGINEERING.md §1.1 (Infrastructure as a separate layer) and §1.3 (dependency injection). It does not change the schema, the storage engine, or the build order — it's purely about how the code is organized inside src/lib/db/. Add ~30 minutes to Step 4.

Resist the urge to over-engineer. The minimum viable version is: interfaces, concrete classes, factory. Do not add "use cases", DTOs, or a "unit of work" pattern on top — those are real patterns but overkill here.

Show me the interface files and the README first, before you implement the SQLite classes. That's a third checkpoint specific to this step. Then standard checkpoint structure: implementation green, tests green, coverage ≥ 90% on src/lib/db/, then sign-off.

The third checkpoint (interfaces before implementation) is deliberate. Reviewing the interfaces in isolation — without seeing any SQLite-specific code — is the cleanest way to spot if the abstraction is leaking SQL concepts into the domain. If the interface methods talk about rowid or LIMIT clauses or transactions, something's gone wrong. They should talk about players, snapshots, gameweeks.

Claude responded: The interfaces are well-scoped overall — domain types throughout, no SQL concepts leaking, sensible methods per aggregate.
The interfaces are well-scoped overall — domain types throughout, no SQL concepts leaking, sensible methods per aggregate. Five interfaces is the right count. But I have three things to flag before you implement, two of which are worth fixing now and one that's a question.

1. latestByPlayer is a leaky name. The method is supposed to return "the player's current confidence" — i.e. the most recent snapshot. But latestByPlayer is technically describing how it gets that (sort by gameweek descending, take first) rather than what it returns. A future engineer reading the interface might wonder: latest by what? By insertion time? By gameweek? What if I have GW 5 and GW 7 snapshots and re-sync GW 5 — does "latest" mean GW 7 or the most recently written?

Better domain naming options:

currentByPlayer(playerId): ConfidenceSnapshot | null
mostRecentByPlayer(playerId): ConfidenceSnapshot | null
I'd go with currentByPlayer — it's what the application actually wants ("give me this player's current confidence") and the implementation detail (highest gameweek) stays inside the SQLite class.

2. Missing methods you'll regret in Step 5+. Two methods will almost certainly be needed by the sync pipeline (Step 5) and the players list page (Step 7). Adding them now is cheaper than back-filling:

ConfidenceSnapshotRepository.currentForAllPlayers(): ReadonlyArray<{ playerId: PlayerId; snapshot: ConfidenceSnapshot }> — the players list page will need every player's current confidence. Without this method, the page will need to call currentByPlayer 700 times in a loop, which is N+1 query hell.
ConfidenceSnapshotRepository.deleteByPlayer(playerId): void — when the user changes the Big Teams list (Step 11), every player's confidence needs to be recomputed. The cleanest way is delete-then-recompute. Without this, you have to either upsert-with-zero-and-rebuild (messy) or expose a deleteAll (too blunt).
If you don't add these now, they'll get added later under time pressure when the page is slow or the recompute is buggy. Add them now while the architecture is fresh.

3. A question — does ManagerSquadRepository have what it needs? You have upsertMany and listByTeamAndGameweek. But the My Team page (Step 9.7) also needs to know "what's the most recent gameweek I have squad data for, for this team ID?" — because the user pastes their team ID and we need to fetch the latest available squad. Without that, the page has to know the gameweek before it can ask.

Do you have a method like latestGameweekForTeam(teamId: number): number | null? Or are you planning to derive that some other way (e.g. from SyncMetaRepository)? If the latter, that's fine — just confirm the plan. If it's not addressed anywhere, add it.

Here's the prompt to send back:

Three pieces of feedback before you implement:

Rename latestByPlayer → currentByPlayer. "Latest" describes the implementation (highest gameweek wins); "current" describes what the application actually wants. The interface should speak the application's language, not SQL's.
Add two methods to ConfidenceSnapshotRepository:
currentForAllPlayers(): ReadonlyArray<{ playerId: PlayerId; snapshot: ConfidenceSnapshot }> — needed by the players list page (Step 7) to avoid N+1 queries when rendering 700+ rows.
deleteByPlayer(playerId: PlayerId): void — needed by the Big Teams recompute flow (Step 11) where the cleanest path is delete-then-recompute.
Both will get added under pressure later if not now. Add them while the architecture is fresh.
One question on ManagerSquadRepository: the My Team page (Step 9.7) takes a team ID, then needs to fetch the most recent gameweek's squad. How do you plan to know which gameweek to ask for? Two options: (a) add latestGameweekForTeam(teamId): number | null to this repository, or (b) derive it from SyncMetaRepository. If (b), confirm the plan; if neither, add (a) now.
Otherwise the interfaces look clean — domain types throughout, no SQL leakage, README captures the philosophy correctly. Once you address the three points above, proceed to implement the SQLite classes and tests. Standard checkpoint at the end (green tests + coverage report on src/lib/db/).

The fact that we're catching these before implementation is exactly why the third checkpoint was worth adding. Refactoring an interface after five files implement it is an order of magnitude more painful than tweaking it on paper.
