Apr 26
Claude responded: This is a fantastic diagnostic.
This is a fantastic diagnostic. Bug fully traced, scope quantified, fix surface identified, false positives ruled out. Let me sign off on the analysis and then write the fix prompt.

The thing I want to acknowledge first: you caught this. The math didn't add up on a single match in a single screenshot, and you trusted the discrepancy enough to flag it. That's now three real bugs you've caught by trusting your eyes against the data — Salah, the seed-vs-sync collision, and now the double-gameweek aggregation. Tests didn't catch any of them. Lighthouse didn't. axe didn't. Domain expertise + skepticism caught all three. Worth filing mentally as a lesson: human review against real data is irreplaceable, no matter how green the test suite is.

On the diagnostic itself:

The fact that all 58 inconsistencies cluster cleanly into two gameweeks (GW26, GW33) is the smoking gun that confirms this is a systematic bug, not 58 random data points. Double gameweeks are well-known to FPL veterans (it's when a team plays twice in one gameweek due to fixture rescheduling), and the algorithm was simply never designed with them in mind. That's a spec gap, not a code defect — calculateConfidence is correctly producing one entry per match, and the database schema is correctly storing one row per (player, gameweek). They just disagree about the unit of analysis, and the upsert pipeline is the boundary where they collide.

On the proposed fix — I agree, but with one refinement.

The diagnostic suggests collapsing entries by aggregating: delta = sum of deltas, confidence_after = last entry's value, reason = last entry's value, etc. That works mathematically, but it loses information: a user looking at GW33 for Haaland will never know he played two matches that gameweek and which one drove the delta.

Two ways to handle this:

Option A — Aggregate, lose match-level granularity. What the diagnostic proposed. Sum the deltas, take the last confidence_after. Simple, schema unchanged. Match history strip shows one card per GW with the net delta and a combined reason like "MOTM vs big team + MOTM vs non-big team."

Option B — Aggregate for storage, but enrich the reason field. Same as A, but make the reason explicit when there's a DGW: "DGW: MOTM vs Arsenal (+3) + MOTM vs Bournemouth (+2) = +5". The sum is still stored as delta, but the user-facing string communicates "this was a double gameweek" so the +5 doesn't look like a bug to the user.

Option C — Schema change to store one row per match. PK becomes (player_id, gameweek, match_index). Truer to reality, but it's a real schema migration and breaks every query that assumes one-row-per-GW. The match history strip would need to render two cards for DGWs, which is its own UX problem.

My recommendation: Option B. It fixes the bug, preserves user-visible accuracy (the +5 net change in DGWs is a real and meaningful event), doesn't require schema changes, and uses the existing reason field as the carrier for "this was a double gameweek."

Option C is right longer-term but overkill for the current product. We'd file it as a Step 12 enhancement if double-gameweek visualization becomes important.

Send this to Claude Code:

Excellent diagnostic. The double-gameweek aggregation issue is a clean bug with a clean fix. 58 affected rows across two GWs (26 and 33), with confidence_after values correct and only delta wrong. Let's fix it.

Apply Option B — aggregate per-gameweek with enriched reasons. Specifically:

In syncConfidence.ts, after calculateConfidence returns the history, add a collapse step before upserting. Group consecutive entries by gameweek. For each group:
gameweek = the shared GW
confidence_after = the last entry's confidence_after (this is the cumulative ground truth)
delta = sum of all entries' deltas for that GW (mathematically equivalent to last.confidence_after − previous_gw.confidence_after but explicit summation is clearer)
reason = if 1 entry, use it as-is. If 2+ entries (DGW), build a string like "DGW: <reason1> (<delta1>) + <reason2> (<delta2>)". Sign each delta explicitly.
fatigue_applied = OR of all entries' values (fatigue can fire in any sub-match)
motm_counter = the last entry's value (final state after the GW)
Add a unit test in syncConfidence.test.ts covering a DGW scenario:
Player has 2 matches with round=33, one MOTM vs big and one MOTM vs non-big.
Confirm exactly one snapshot is upserted for GW33.
Confirm delta = sum of both individual deltas.
Confirm confidence_after = last match's cumulative value.
Confirm reason contains both individual reasons.
Add a regression test to lock in the specific Haaland GW33 case from the diagnostic. Use a fixture: 2 matches, MOTM vs big (+3) then MOTM vs non-big (+2), starting confidence -2. Expect: 1 snapshot, delta=+5, confidence_after=+3, reason mentions both opponents.
Re-run sync against the live API to repopulate the DB with corrected snapshots. Confirm the previously-inconsistent 58 rows are now consistent: prev_confidence_after + delta == confidence_after for all rows in the DB.
No schema change needed. No migration. Just the collapse step in the sync orchestrator.
Match history card UX: the MatchHistoryCard component in src/app/players/[id]/\_components/MatchHistoryCard.tsx displays the reason text. With the new DGW reason format, a card may show a longer multi-line reason. Glance at how it renders for Haaland GW33 and Timber GW26 — if the longer reason text breaks the card layout, fix the card to handle longer strings (probably wrap, or truncate with a tooltip on hover).
Two checkpoints:

(a) Show me the collapse logic + the two new tests passing. Don't run the full sync yet.

(b) After re-syncing, paste:

The DB consistency check: sqlite3 data/fpl.db "SELECT player_id, gameweek FROM confidence_snapshots cs1 WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM confidence_snapshots cs2 WHERE cs2.player_id = cs1.player_id AND cs2.gameweek = cs1.gameweek - 1) AND (SELECT cs2.confidence_after FROM confidence_snapshots cs2 WHERE cs2.player_id = cs1.player_id AND cs2.gameweek = cs1.gameweek - 1) + cs1.delta != cs1.confidence_after;" — should return zero rows.
Haaland's GW33 snapshot — should now show delta=+5, confidence_after=+3, reason="DGW: MOTM vs big team (+3) + MOTM vs non-big team (+2)" or similar.
Timber's GW26 snapshot — should now show delta=-2, confidence_after=+3, reason="DGW: Blank vs non-big team (-1) + Blank vs non-big team (-1)" or similar.
Screenshot of Haaland's player detail page showing the GW33 card with the new combined reason rendering correctly.
Don't proceed to Step 9.7 until (b) is signed off and the data is fully consistent.

A note for you, the human:

When you see the fixed Haaland page, GW33 will show as a single card with +5 and the reason "DGW: MOTM vs big team (+3) + MOTM vs non-big team (+2)" or similar. That's actually a more informative card than before — a user looking at it now learns that Haaland played two matches in one gameweek, scored in both, and against which opponents. That's useful FPL signal. The bug fix accidentally improved the product.

Also worth noting: the algorithm transparency we discussed earlier just got a tiny bit more exposed by this fix. The DGW reason explicitly shows +3 and +2 separately, which makes the per-event scoring more visible than the simple MOTM label did. If you eventually pick Option A (hide deltas) or B (percentage display) at Step 12, the DGW reason will need its own treatment — probably "DGW: 2 strong performances" or similar. Just file mentally.

Roughly 80% complete. Three steps remaining, plus the in-flight bug fix.

What's left and what each adds:

Currently in progress — DGW aggregation fix. Should land in 30-60 minutes. After this, the data layer is genuinely solid for the first time. Three architectural fixes deep (separate DBs, staleness filter, DGW aggregation) all came from your real-world auditing, not the spec.

Step 9.7 — My Team page (~half a day). This is the screen you've been waiting for since the project started. You paste your team ID 231177, the app fetches your actual squad from the FPL API, computes your Team Confidence percentage, and shows you a per-line breakdown (Defence / Midfield / Attack). This is the "moment of truth" screen — it makes the whole product personally useful instead of just analytically useful.

Step 11 — Settings (~2-3 hours). Big Teams editor (the toggle list of which teams count as "big"), theme persistence, cache controls. Functionally important but not visually exciting. Triggers a full confidence recompute when Big Teams changes.

Step 12 — Polish & a11y pass (~half a day). You said: how does the algorithm handle injuries
how does the algorithm handle injuries

Apr 26
Claude responded: The algorithm handles injuries by ignoring them — and that's actually correct, but worth understanding why.
The algorithm handles injuries by ignoring them — and that's actually correct, but worth understanding why.

The relevant rule from docs/ALGORITHM.md §1:

Confidence updates per match the player appeared in. Missed matches are skipped — no decay, no zero-fill.

And from mapMatchEvents in the sync pipeline:

Filter to minutesPlayed > 0 before passing to the calculator.

So the practical behavior is:

While injured (not playing):

Player accumulates zero snapshots during the injury period
Their last computed confidence value persists unchanged
Their match history strip shows a gap in gameweeks
When they return:

The first match back is processed normally — same scoring rules apply
No "rust penalty," no "freshness bonus," no decay-on-return
Their MOTM counter is preserved from before the injury (so if they had 2 MOTMs before getting hurt, the next MOTM still triggers fatigue)
Why this is the right default:

Confidence is meant to reflect what we know about a player's form based on their performance. If they haven't played, we have no new data. The honest answer is "their last known form value still applies." A player at +3 going into injury is still a +3 when we last saw them play — that's not stale, that's just the most recent signal we have.

Why it's also a real problem:

This creates the exact issue you saw with Salah-at-Leeds before we identified it as a different bug. If a player gets a long-term injury at GW5, then misses GW6 through GW20, our app shows them at their GW5 confidence value the whole time — making them look as fresh and reliable as a player who's been playing every week. From a captain-decision standpoint, a +3 confidence from "last played 15 weeks ago" is functionally meaningless, but it ranks identically to a +3 from "scored last weekend."

This is partly why we added the staleness filter at Step 9. Recall the rule:

A player's most recent snapshot gameweek is more than 3 gameweeks behind the current GW → exclude from dashboard leaderboard, risers, fallers.

So a player who's been injured for 4+ weeks gets quietly dropped from prominent surfaces. They still appear on the full Players list (/players) because that's a complete reference, but they don't pollute the "who's hot right now" views. That filter was originally about transferred-out players, but it incidentally handles long-term injuries too.

Edge cases worth knowing:

Cameo appearances (sub for 5 minutes). minutesPlayed > 0 includes any non-zero value, so a 5-minute cameo against Brighton counts as a "match the player appeared in." The rule applies normally — almost certainly a blank since they had no goals/assists in 5 minutes, so they get the blank penalty. This is probably wrong for the metric's intent. A 5-minute substitute appearance shouldn't be punished as a blank — they barely had a chance to do anything.
Injured but on the bench (didn't play at all). Filtered out correctly — minutes = 0, no snapshot.
Suspended. Same as injured — they don't play, no snapshot, no decay.
Rotated/rested (didn't play despite being healthy). Same — no snapshot, last value persists. The user can't tell from looking at the app whether the gap means injured, suspended, or rotated.
What the algorithm doesn't do that you might assume it does:

Doesn't read the FPL API's injury status (status field has values like 'a' for available, 'i' for injured, 's' for suspended)
Doesn't decay confidence over time
Doesn't communicate availability in the UI (no "injured" indicator on player cards)
Doesn't flag returning players as "back from injury — confidence is stale"
Worth filing as Step 12 considerations (don't act now):

Minimum minutes threshold. Change minutesPlayed > 0 to minutesPlayed >= 30 or >= 60 so cameos don't get punished as blanks. The FPL standard for "appearance points" is 60+ minutes. Worth thinking about whether that's the right threshold for confidence too.
Show availability status on player cards. Use the FPL API's status field. A player marked 'i' (injured) gets a red dot or "OUT" badge on their card. Doesn't affect confidence calculation, just communicates "this confidence value is from before they got hurt."
Stale-confidence visual indicator. A player whose last snapshot is more than 3 gameweeks old (already filtered from the dashboard) should be visually marked when they appear elsewhere — e.g. their confidence number renders in a more muted color, with a small "GW5" tag indicating when it was last computed. Communicates "trust this less."
These are all real product gaps, but they're all UX/communication issues, not algorithm bugs. The algorithm itself handles missed matches correctly per spec.

Want me to file these formally as Step 12 polish items so they don't get lost? Or hold them until you've used the app for a few captain decisions and form your own opinion about which ones actually matter?

Apr 26
Claude responded: Don't add it during the current build.
Don't add it during the current build. Add it as v2.

Here's the reasoning:

Why not now:

You're 80% done with the current product. The remaining 20% (Step 9.7, Step 11, Step 12) is precisely the work that closes the loop on what you've built. Adding two new metrics — each with their own algorithm, sync requirements, UI surface, and edge cases — would essentially restart the build at Step 2.

Each new probability metric needs:

Its own src/lib/probability-goal/ and src/lib/probability-assist/ modules with TDD
Updates to the sync pipeline to compute and persist them
New columns in confidence_snapshots (or new tables if they're independent)
UI surface in three places: Players list (new column or replacement for trend), Player Detail (new component alongside the hero), Dashboard (new sort options at minimum)
Tests, fixtures, smoke tests, and the same auditing-against-real-data discipline that's caught three bugs already
Realistically, two new metrics fully integrated would take as much work as Steps 2 through 8 combined. Maybe more. Adding them now would push the "shippable product" milestone from "next week" to "next month or two."

What you'd lose by adding now:

Momentum. Right now you're close to a usable product you can deploy and start collecting your own usage data on. If you delay, the product never ships, never gets used, and you never find out which metric (Confidence vs. P(Goal) vs. P(Assist)) actually drives your captain decisions. That insight is worth more than having all three metrics on day one.

What you'd gain by waiting:

Two big things.

First, using Confidence for a few weeks teaches you what's missing. You might discover Confidence alone is enough for your decisions and the probability metrics aren't urgent. You might discover the opposite — that Confidence is too noisy and probability metrics would replace it. You might discover a hybrid is the right answer. Right now, you'd be building all three on faith. After two weeks of usage, you'd be building based on evidence.

Second, the architecture from this build informs how you'd add v2 metrics. The repository pattern, the staleness filter, the DGW aggregation — these are all patterns the new metrics will need. By the time you build v2, you'll know which abstractions work and which need refactoring. Adding a third metric to a system that's been used for a while is much faster than adding it to a system that's still being built.

The right shape for "v2":

v1 (current build, finishing now):

- Confidence as the sole metric
- Ship it
- Use it for 2-4 weeks personally

v1.5 (small):

- Whatever polish/tweaks emerge from real usage
- Possibly the algorithm transparency decision (Option A/B/C)
- Possibly the injury status indicators we discussed

v2 (multi-metric):

- Add P(Goal) and P(Assist)
- Build them as siblings to Confidence, not replacements
- UI evolves: dashboard becomes a multi-metric leaderboard,
  player detail page adds new hero treatments
- Likely 4-6 weeks of work
  One concrete recommendation about the MD file you already have:

Save it. Drop it in the repo at docs/v2/probability-algorithms.md. It'll be ready when v2 starts. Don't link to it from CLAUDE.md or the existing docs — that would create accidental scope drift if Claude Code reads it during current work. Just a sibling file that sits dormant until you're ready.

When you're ready for v2, the kickoff prompt to me will be something like: "Read the existing CLAUDE.md, ENGINEERING.md, ALGORITHM.md, then read docs/v2/probability-algorithms.md. Help me plan how to add these as siblings to Confidence." We'll do a fresh PLANNING session for v2 then, not bolt it onto the current one.

Tactical answer:

Send me the MD file whenever you'd like and I can review it now — separate from the current build — and tell you if there's anything in the algorithm specs that should influence how we finish the v1 architecture (e.g. if P(Goal) needs the same MatchEvent shape as Confidence, we'd want to make sure the current shape is general enough). Reviewing the spec is cheap. Implementing it is the expensive part, and that should wait.

Want to send it for review-only?

You said: for review here is it

fpl_probability_algorithm.md
md

for review here is it

Apr 26
Claude responded: Reviewing this carefully because we're at a moment where assumptions about future scope can quietly shape the current build.
Reviewing this carefully because we're at a moment where assumptions about future scope can quietly shape the current build. Let me tell you what I see — what's strong, what's underspecified, and what (if anything) should influence the current architecture before we finish v1.

Overall read: this is a more sophisticated metric than Confidence.

Confidence is a simple deterministic counter — match happens, points adjust, clamp, store. The probability metrics are statistical models. They aggregate over windows of historical performance and produce continuous values (0-100%) that reflect rate of expected output. That's a real step up in complexity, and it changes what "right" means: Confidence is right if the math matches the spec; probability metrics are right if they predict the future better than naive baselines.

That distinction matters for how you build, test, and trust them. More on that below.

What's strong in the spec:

The conceptual model is sound. Both metrics share a common shape — recent appearances → events per appearance → calibrated probability — and they differ only in which event they're predicting. That symmetry means you can build them as a single module (src/lib/probability/) with two configurations rather than two separate modules. Clean architecture.

Position-aware calibration is the right move. Forwards have different goal-scoring base rates than midfielders, who differ from defenders. Treating all positions the same would make the metric meaningless — a defender with a 3% goal probability and a forward with a 3% goal probability are very different signals (the defender is unusually attacking; the forward is in a slump). Position adjustment is critical and you've correctly identified it.

The rolling window approach (last N matches with appearances) is the right primitive. It mirrors how the Confidence calculator already filters to minutesPlayed > 0 — same data shape, same exclusion rule.

What's underspecified or worth pinning down before v2 starts:

A few questions that the spec leaves open. None of these need to be answered right now, but they're worth flagging so you know to think about them:

1. The window size and recency weighting. "Last N matches" — what's N? 5? 10? Whole season? Different choices produce very different metrics. A 5-match window is reactive (Haaland's recent 3-goal week dominates), a 20-match window is stable (his season average barely moves on any single week). There's also a question of whether matches are weighted equally or whether recent matches count more. This is the single most important parameter in the model and the spec should pin it down before implementation.

2. The "calibration" step. The spec says raw event rates get calibrated to probabilities, but doesn't define how. Direct rate (goals_in_last_N / N) is the simplest, but it's noisy on small samples — a forward who scored once in 3 matches isn't actually a 33% goal-probability player. Bayesian shrinkage toward a prior is more robust but adds complexity. Logistic regression with features (opponent strength, home/away, fixture difficulty) is even more sophisticated. Each level of sophistication is more accurate but harder to validate. Decide your preferred level before implementation — and the answer is probably "start with the simplest, ship it, see how wrong it feels, then complicate."

3. Opponent adjustment. Should P(Goal) vs Burnley be higher than P(Goal) vs Liverpool? Empirically yes. But the spec doesn't say whether opponent strength is a factor. If it is, you need a team-strength model (FPL's strength_attack_home / strength_defence_away fields are usable). If it isn't, the metric is "general scoring rate" not "expected scoring this week." Both are valid metrics, but they answer different questions.

4. Fixture-specific vs. season-general. Related to above. Are these probabilities for "this player generally" or "this player in their next match specifically"? Captain decisions need the latter. General-form metrics need the former. This is a critical product decision.

5. Validation strategy. Confidence is internally validated — the algorithm spec defines correctness. Probability metrics need external validation: did the predicted probabilities actually match observed outcomes over a backtested period? If you predict 30% goal probability for 100 player-matches and 50 of them actually score, your model is wrong even if the code is correct. You need a backtest harness. This is a real piece of infrastructure that doesn't exist in the current build.

What this tells me about the current build:

Two things, neither of which is a blocker:

The current MatchEvent shape is general enough. Looking at the type definition in ALGORITHM.md, MatchEvent already carries goals, assists, minutesPlayed, isOpponentBigTeam, cleanSheet. P(Goal) and P(Assist) need exactly these fields plus possibly opponentTeamId (which is also there). So no architectural changes needed in v1 to keep v2 cheap. The data layer is general-purpose.

The repository pattern pays off here. When v2 lands, you'll add new repositories like ProbabilitySnapshotRepository alongside the existing ConfidenceSnapshotRepository. The application layer will compose them however the UI needs. No core refactor required. The Step 4 architectural call to invest in repositories — which felt like overkill at the time — is now genuinely paying dividends. If we'd written direct SQLite calls everywhere, adding two new metrics would be a major surgery.

One thing to flag for the current build that might be worth a small accommodation:

The probability metrics will need a current_gameweek value to know "what's the next match for this player" if you go with fixture-specific probabilities. We already have that in sync_meta. No change needed.

My recommendation:

Save the spec at docs/v2/probability-algorithms.md. Don't link from any current docs.
Don't change the v1 architecture for it. The data shape is already general; the repository pattern already accommodates new metrics.
Sharpen the spec before v2 starts. Specifically answer the five questions above (window size, calibration method, opponent adjustment, fixture-specific vs general, validation strategy). Each one is a 30-minute thinking exercise. Better done now while the algorithm is fresh in your head than during implementation.
Plan for a backtest harness in v2. This is a non-trivial piece of work — pull historical FPL data from previous seasons (the history_past field exists in the API), simulate the model's predictions retroactively, and measure calibration. Without this, you can't tell if the model is working. Budget a real chunk of v2 time for it.
One philosophical note:

Probability metrics introduce a different kind of trust problem than Confidence. With Confidence, the user trusts the rules ("I see why Salah is at +3"). With P(Goal), the user has to trust the model ("the app says 25% but I have no way to verify that"). When the user disagrees with the model, there's no algorithmic appeal. That's a real product challenge — you may want to show the inputs (recent goals, opponent strength, home/away) alongside the probability so users can sanity-check the prediction.

Save the spec. Finish v1. Use it for a few weeks. Sharpen the model questions while you're using v1 personally. Then build v2 with evidence about what you actually need.

Want to send me anything else from the v2 thinking, or is this enough for the current "don't lose the spec, don't let it bleed into v1" purpose?

Required reading
docs/UI_GUIDELINES.md §7 (My Team brief)
docs/ALGORITHM.md §11 (Team Confidence — already implemented in src/lib/team-confidence/ from Step 9.5)
docs/API.md §8 (Manager squad endpoint, EntryPicksSchema)

Mandatory: use the frontend-design skill
Per CLAUDE.md, required for every UI task.

Architecture
src/app/my-team/
├── page.tsx # Server Component (mostly)
└── \_components/
├── ConnectTeamForm.tsx # 'use client' — empty state with team ID input
├── MyTeamHero.tsx # 'use client' — Team Confidence percentage + animation
├── PositionalBreakdown.tsx # three cards: Defence / Midfield / Attack
├── StartingXIList.tsx # 11 starters with C/V badges
├── BenchSection.tsx # 4 bench players, lower opacity
├── TeamSyncFooter.tsx # last sync time + change team ID link
└── \*.test.tsx
Required behaviors
Team ID stored in localStorage under key fpl-team-id. Persisted client-side only — there's no server-side user model. The page reads it on mount.
Empty state — no team connected:
ConnectTeamForm renders centered card with "Connect your FPL team" heading.
Input field: numeric team ID. Helper text: "Find your team ID in the URL of your FPL team page: fantasy.premierleague.com/entry/12345678/..."
Primary "Load my team" button. On submit, validates the team ID is numeric, then triggers a fetch via the existing fetchEntryPicks from src/lib/fpl/api.ts, then persists to localStorage on success, then navigates to the loaded state.
Loading state during fetch: button shows spinner-free skeleton text "Loading…" and is disabled.
Error states: invalid team ID (404 from FPL), gameweek not yet started, network error. Each gets a designed inline error message under the input. No toasts.
Loaded state — team is connected:
PlayerHeader-style top section: manager name (44px Fraunces, the second use of the serif in the product) + team name + current overall rank. Pull from the FPL /api/entry/{team_id}/ endpoint (you'll need to add a fetch function for this; it's not in api.ts yet — add it with Zod schema, follow existing patterns).
Hero: MyTeamHero renders the Team Confidence percentage (96px, count-up animation from 50%). Color: green if ≥50%, red if <50%, neutral if exactly 50%. Subtitle: "Team Confidence" in 12px caption.
PositionalBreakdown immediately under hero: three equal cards side-by-side (stack on mobile). Each shows the line's percent (32px treatment), label ("Defence" / "Midfield" / "Attack"), and player count. Color-coded by sign relative to 50%.
StartingXIList: 11 rows sorted by squad_position (1-11). Each row shows player avatar + name + opponent badge for next fixture + individual confidence (sm size). Captain has subtle "C" badge, vice has "V" — visual indicator only. Helper text below the section: "Captain shown for context only — doesn't affect Team Confidence."
BenchSection: 4 rows in same format, set apart visually (lower opacity background, "Bench" label). Helper text: "Bench is excluded from Team Confidence — only starters count."
TeamSyncFooter: "Last synced N minutes ago" + "Change team ID" link (clears localStorage, returns to empty state).
Gameweek selection. Use the most recent gameweek the user's squad data is available for. Per docs/API.md §8, the FPL picks endpoint requires a specific GW. Two options:
Option A (simpler): call /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{currentGW-1}/picks/ since the current-week squad isn't locked until deadline. Use the most recently completed GW.
Option B (better UX): try currentGW, fall back to currentGW-1 on 404.
Pick A for v1. Document the choice in the file with a comment.
Linking from Dashboard. The dashboard's existing TeamConfidenceHero stub (or the ConnectTeamCta empty state) should now wire to actual data: if localStorage has a team ID, render the hero with real data; otherwise render the connect CTA pointing to /my-team.
Validation and persistence flow:
User pastes team ID → form validates numeric → calls fetchEntryPicks(teamId, gameweek) → on success, calls manager_squads repository to upsert the squad → persists team ID to localStorage → navigates to loaded view.
Computes Team Confidence on the server using calculateTeamConfidence from Step 9.5, joining squad picks against current confidence_snapshots.
Caches in the manager_squads table per the schema in API.md §6.
Tests
Required (colocated .test.tsx):

ConnectTeamForm validates numeric input, shows error states, calls submit handler with parsed team ID.
MyTeamHero renders the correct percentage with color, handles 50% as neutral.
PositionalBreakdown renders three cards, handles empty positions (e.g. 0 forwards in a hypothetical formation).
StartingXIList renders 11 rows, captain/vice badges visible.
BenchSection renders 4 rows with reduced opacity treatment.
axe clean on both empty and loaded states.

Enter team ID 231177 → click Load → land on populated view.
Confirm Team Confidence percentage is rendered.
Confirm 11 starters + 4 bench = 15 players visible.
Click "Change team ID" → returns to empty state, localStorage cleared.

That mirrors how an FPL manager actually thinks through their squad. The hierarchy of attention is right.

Lots of products would either silently weight the captain (making the metric harder to predict) or hide the captain entirely (losing useful context). Defence 45.0% (still below baseline, but only 6 players including Darlow at -4 dragging it down hard)

Apr 26
Claude responded: You're not just changing the floor — you're correctly diagnosing a real flaw in the metric.
You're not just changing the floor — you're correctly diagnosing a real flaw in the metric. Let me think through this carefully because it's a genuinely important call.

The real problem isn't the asymmetry. It's the recovery cost.

A player at -5 needs 5 positive deltas to reach 0. Even with MOTM streaks (+2 each non-big), that's 3 MOTM matches just to get back to neutral — and the 3rd one triggers a -2 fatigue penalty, so it's actually 4 MOTM matches to reach 0 from the floor. Four good matches just to be "average."

That's not how form works in real football. A striker who scored against Brighton last week, after a 5-match drought, isn't still "averaging negative" in the manager's mind — one match changes how you feel about them. Our metric doesn't reflect that intuition. The floor is too sticky.

Your proposed -4 to +5 range fixes this asymmetrically, and that asymmetry is correct.

Real form has a natural ceiling (you can only score so many goals, defenders can only keep so many clean sheets) but no real floor that's symmetric to it — bad players don't keep getting "more bad," they just stay bad-ish. The mental model is "how much have you proven yourself" with a hard ceiling but a softer floor. -4 to +5 captures that.

Let me show you what the numbers actually do under the new range:

Under current (-5 to +5):

Player at floor (-5) needs 4 MOTM to reach 0 = approximately 6-8 weeks of strong form
Pickford with Everton's 32% CS rate → permanently floored
Recovery from negative is harder than fall from positive (asymmetric in the wrong direction)
Under proposed (-4 to +5):

Player at floor (-4) needs 3 MOTM to reach 0 = approximately 4-6 weeks
Pickford breaks even at ~50% CS rate; below that he sits at -2 to -4 instead of pinned at -5
Same MOTM rules, same fatigue rules, just less basement to dig out of
One additional thing I'd consider, but only if you agree:

The percentage display in My Team currently maps -5 to +5 onto 0% to 100%. Under the new range, we'd map -4 to +5 onto 0% to 100%. Math: ((value + 4) / 9) × 100. Mappings:

-4 → 0%
0 → 44.4% (not 50%)
+5 → 100%
The "neutral" point is now 44.4%, not 50%. That's slightly weird. Two options:

A) Keep the asymmetric range internally, recalibrate the percentage display:

percent = ((value + 4) / 9) × 100
Honest math. But "neutral = 44.4%" is harder for users to internalize.

B) Keep the percentage display centered at 50% by treating 0 as the midpoint regardless:

if value >= 0: percent = 50 + (value / 5) _ 50
if value < 0: percent = 50 + (value / 4) _ 50
Mappings: -4 → 0%, 0 → 50%, +5 → 100%. The math is asymmetric (positive uses /5, negative uses /4) but the user-facing semantics are clean: 50% means neutral, above is good, below is bad.

I'd recommend B. The asymmetric internal scale should be invisible to users; what they see should be a clean 0-100% with 50% as the meaningful midpoint.

Things this change would NOT affect:

The calculator's per-match scoring rules stay identical
Fatigue still fires after 3 MOTMs
All existing tests still pass with their assertions about deltas and reasons
The visual ConfidenceNumber sizes/colors stay the same
No data migration is needed — existing -5 snapshots just become impossible to produce going forward, and the next sync will re-clamp them
Things this change WOULD affect:

The clamp constants in calculator.ts (one constant change)
The slider in ConfidenceSlider (axis range −4 to +5 instead of −5 to +5)
The percentage formula in team-confidence (if you pick option B)
Tests that explicitly assert clamping at -5 (need updating to -4)
The dev/styles page which displays sample values (-5 example needs to become -4)
Migration question: the existing 10,110 snapshots in your DB include rows with confidence_after = -5. Two choices:

Option X — Re-sync from scratch. Run the sync, recompute everything under the new clamp. Cleanest. Takes ~2-3 minutes.

Option Y — Mathematically convert existing rows. Replace any -5 with -4 in place. Faster but inconsistent — the historical chart for a player who was at -5 in GW7 would now show -4 there, which never actually happened under the rules at the time.

Go with X. We have the sync pipeline; let's use it. The data layer just got proven correct after the DGW fix; let's regenerate against the same clean pipeline.

My read: this is a real improvement and we should make it.

The current floor is producing data that doesn't match how managers actually think about form. Your instinct is right. The fix is small, surgical, doesn't risk regressions in the rules themselves, and produces a more usable metric.

The right time to do it is now, before Step 11. We're in algorithm territory, and Step 11 is where the user gets to edit Big Teams (which triggers a recompute anyway). It's better to land the new clamp + first recompute together than to ship Step 11, recompute, then change the clamp, then recompute again.

Tap your answer. If you want a different range entirely, tell me what you have in mind and the math implications and I'll work through it with you. Worth pushing on this until it feels right — every other piece of the metric depends on this calibration.

A: Apply the change — -4 to +5 with option B percentage

Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. Here's the change prompt — paste it into Claude Code:

Algorithm calibration change before Step 11. Reduce the lower clamp from -5 to -4; the upper clamp stays at +5. The reasoning: a player at the current floor needs 4 MOTM matches to reach neutral, which doesn't match real-world form recovery. -4 to +5 keeps the metric meaningful at the floor without making recovery feel impossibly slow.

The percentage display in Team Confidence stays centered at 50% via piecewise mapping (option B from our discussion).

Follow this sequence — docs first, tests second, code third, recompute fourth. Do not reorder.

Step A — Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
§1 Core principles, item 3: change "Confidence is hard-clamped to [-5, +5]" to "Confidence is hard-clamped to [-4, +5]. The asymmetric range reflects how form recovery works in practice — a player at the floor should be able to climb back to neutral within a realistic stretch of good performances, while the upper bound preserves the deliberate scarcity of +5."
§7 Clamping: update the range references throughout from [-5, +5] to [-4, +5].
§8 Worked examples — review and update:
EX-09 (Clamp at lower bound): currently expects confidenceAfter=-5. Update to confidenceAfter=-4. Recompute the worked-out math accordingly: player at -3, blank vs non-big (-2) → would be -5 → clamp to -4 → delta=-1.
PROP-01 (output always in [-5, +5]): update to [-4, +5].

§11 Team Confidence — percentage formula: update from the symmetric ((value + 5) / 10) _ 100 to the piecewise mapping:
ts
function confidenceToPercent(value: number): number {
if (value >= 0) return 50 + (value / 5) _ 50;
return 50 + (value / 4) \* 50;
}
Mappings: -4 → 0%, -2 → 25%, 0 → 50%, +2.5 → 75%, +5 → 100%. Document this and explain that 50% is the neutral baseline regardless of the asymmetric internal scale.

sql
SELECT COUNT(_) FROM confidence_snapshots WHERE confidence_after < -4;
Expected: 0.
Verify the count of players who were at -5 has redistributed:
sql
SELECT confidence_after, COUNT(_) FROM confidence_snapshots
WHERE gameweek = (SELECT current_gameweek FROM sync_meta)
GROUP BY confidence_after ORDER BY confidence_after;

Re-screenshot the user's My Team page (team ID 231177). The 32.8% number will change because Palmer and Calvert-Lewin (currently at -5) will now be at -4, lifting Midfield and Attack ratings. Show me the new percentage and per-line breakdown.

When the recompute finishes and you see your new My Team percentage, expect it to move up meaningfully. You said: What would you have suggested before?
What would you have suggested before? wanted to know before I proceed so I can compare

Apr 26
Claude responded: Honestly?
Honestly? I would have suggested keeping -5 to +5 and not changed the floor at all.

Here's what I would have said if you hadn't pushed me, and why I think your instinct was sharper than mine:

My default position before you raised this:

When we hit Pickford-at-floor in Step 5 and Salah-at-Leeds confusion later, my framing was: "the metric is meant to be hard to climb, that's a feature." I'd have argued that:

A bounded scale needs some fraction of players at the floor by definition (it's how bounded ranges work — the worst N% always clamp)
Pickford at -5 is correctly telling you "do not pick him" — which is useful FPL signal even if the value is sticky
Asymmetry in the algorithm complicates the mental model
Recovery from -5 IS hard — but so is recovery from a true bad run in real life
Symmetric ranges are easier to explain and reason about
I'd have pushed back on changing it and probably won that conversation if you'd been less certain.

What you saw that I missed:

The recovery-cost asymmetry. I was thinking about "is the floor mathematically correct" — and it is. You were thinking about "does the metric reflect how managers actually think about form recovery" — and it doesn't. Those are different questions, and yours is the right product question.

Specifically: the asymmetry between "how easy it is to fall" and "how hard it is to climb back" is what makes a metric feel fair or unfair to users. Under the old rules, a player who scored once and blanked five times sat at the same -5 as a player who blanked all six times. The user's intuition is "the first guy is showing some life, the second isn't" — but the metric flattened them together because the floor swallowed the difference.

Your -4 to +5 fix doesn't eliminate the floor — it just gives the metric room to differentiate just above the floor. A "had one bright moment" player can sit at -2 or -3 instead of being lumped with hopeless cases at -5. That's real signal that the symmetric clamp was destroying.

Where my analysis would have steered you wrong:

I would have argued for "soft floor at -3" earlier in the build (I think I did, actually, around the Pickford diagnostic), then accepted -5 to +5 when you said "keep it as is." I anchored too quickly on "respect the user's call" and didn't push back when you came around to "actually, the floor is the issue."

The lesson for me: when a user says "keep it as-is" but later says "I think this is wrong," the second instinct is usually closer to ground truth, because by then they've used the product. I should weight "I've seen this in action and it bothers me" more heavily than "I think it's fine in principle."

Why -4 to +5 specifically (which I now think is right):

The asymmetry isn't arbitrary. The ceiling at +5 represents "exceptional sustained form" which has natural saturation in football — there's a real ceiling on how often a player can score, and the +5 + fatigue mechanic correctly punishes the unrealistic case of someone scoring every match.

The floor at -5 represents... what? "Maximally bad form"? But form doesn't have a maximally-bad version in the same way. A player who's blanked five matches is bad. A player who's blanked ten matches isn't double-bad — they're just bad. The floor should compress sooner because the underlying signal naturally compresses.

So -4 to +5 isn't a hack — it's actually more faithful to the underlying domain. The ceiling preserves scarcity (stays hard to reach), the floor stops differentiating sooner (because nature stops differentiating sooner). I should have seen this earlier.

What I'd have suggested as alternatives, none of which are as good as your fix:

"Keep -5 to +5 but add a streak-break bonus" — a player at negative confidence who returns gets a small one-time bonus on their first MOTM. Too clever, more rules to explain.
"Keep -5 to +5 but show the metric as percentile-within-position" — your -5 might be the 5th percentile of forwards rather than the absolute floor. Useful but a different metric entirely, more complexity to implement.
"Soft floor at -3" — same idea as -4 but more aggressive. Probably too aggressive — would make recovery too easy and erode the "this player is genuinely bad" signal.
Your -4 is the right tradeoff: meaningfully better recovery dynamics without losing the "stay away from this guy" floor signal entirely.

Bottom line: before this conversation, I'd have defended the symmetric range. After working through the math you just made me work through, I think -4 to +5 is genuinely better and I'd defend the new version against the old one. You changed my mind, which means it was a good change.

Proceed with the prompt. The change will produce data that matches your intuition better, and that's the right test for any metric calibration.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Stop.
Stop. Important to think this through before you send the change to Claude Code, because what you're describing reverses an architectural decision we just made deliberately.

Let me make sure I understand what you're saying:

Current behavior (after the DGW fix two checkpoints ago): When a player has two matches in GW33 (e.g. vs Arsenal and vs Bournemouth), the match history strip shows one card for GW33, with delta=+5 and reason "DGW: MOTM vs big team (+3) + MOTM vs non-big team (+2)".

What you want: The match history strip shows two cards — one labeled GW33 (vs Arsenal) and another labeled GW33 (vs Bournemouth) — each with their individual delta (+3 and +2) so you can see the per-match contribution.

Is that right? If yes, this is a reasonable product request, but it's the opposite of what we just locked in, so I want to make sure we're aligning on the right tradeoff.

Here's the architectural tension:

We have two layers, and they want to disagree:

The data layer (DB schema): primary key is (player_id, gameweek) — one row per gameweek per player. This is fixed by the schema. If we store two rows for GW33 we break the primary key.
The UI layer (match history strip): the user wants to see one card per match, not one card per gameweek.
Three ways to resolve this — I'll lay them out and you pick:

Option 1: UI splits the card visually using the DGW reason string.

Keep the database schema and storage exactly as-is. In the match history component, parse the reason field — when it starts with "DGW:", render two visually adjacent cards both labeled "GW33", each showing one of the two component deltas. They share a single underlying snapshot row but render as two cards.

Pros: No schema change. No migration. The data layer stays clean. Implementation is purely in the component.
Cons: The UI is reverse-engineering structured data from a string. Brittle if the reason format ever changes. Lossy — you can extract deltas and team-types from the reason string but not opponent names.
Option 2: Store one row per match (schema change).

Change the primary key from (player_id, gameweek) to (player_id, gameweek, match_index). Each DGW writes two rows. The strip iterates rows directly. Aggregation for things like "current confidence" still uses the highest gameweek's last row.

Pros: The data is structurally correct. The UI just renders rows. Future features that need per-match data (opponent, score, individual contributions) are unblocked.
Cons: Schema migration. Every query that assumes one-row-per-GW (current confidence, leaderboard, deltas) needs to be reviewed. The Step 4 architectural assumption baked into multiple repositories needs updating.
Option 3: Separate match-level table alongside the per-GW snapshot.

Add a new table match_events with columns (player_id, gameweek, match_index, opponent_team_id, delta, reason, ...). The existing confidence_snapshots stays (one row per GW for fast leaderboard queries). The UI's match history strip queries the new table to get per-match cards.

Pros: Both data shapes available. Match cards get full opponent + score context, not just deltas. Snapshot queries stay fast.
Cons: Most expensive. Two tables, two repositories, two upsert paths. The sync pipeline writes both.
My recommendation: Option 1, but be honest about its limit.

Option 1 is the right pragmatic choice if the only ask is "show two cards for visual completeness." The DGW reason string already contains the deltas, and parsing them is trivial — it's just a structured string format we control.

But if you're asking because you want richer DGW info (which opponent for each card, the result of each match, separate event icons), Option 1 won't get you there. It can show "+3" and "+2" but not "vs Arsenal" and "vs Bournemouth" separately, because the opponent names aren't stored.

And here's the thing — looking at Haaland's GW33 in your earlier screenshots, the card already shows "DGW" as the label. The delta is +5. Splitting it into two cards would show "+3" and "+2". That's it. You'd lose the combined +5 number that tells you "Haaland's net contribution this gameweek was +5 confidence" — which is actually useful FPL signal for double gameweeks.

So the real question is: what are you trying to do with double gameweeks?

If it's just visual ("I want to see the rhythm of matches, not gameweeks"), Option 1.

If it's analytical ("I want to know which opponents he scored against, what his per-match form looks like"), Option 3.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Hold up — let me make sure I understand the request before agreeing or disagreeing, because there are a couple of distinct things this could mean.
Hold up — let me make sure I understand the request before agreeing or disagreeing, because there are a couple of distinct things this could mean.

What "defensive contribution" means in FPL:

Starting in the 2025/26 season, the official FPL added a new scoring category called Defensive Contributions (DefCon) — players earn FPL points for accumulating defensive actions (tackles + clearances + blocks + interceptions + recoveries, with thresholds of 10 for defenders and 12 for midfielders/forwards). This is a real new feature in FPL itself, and the data is exposed in the API as defensive_contribution on each match's history entry.

So when you say "defensive contribution point should give same point as assist," I think you mean: add a new event type to our Confidence algorithm, where if a player hits the FPL DefCon threshold in a match, they get the same Confidence delta as if they got an assist (Performance: +2 vs big / +1 vs non-big).

Is that right? If yes, let me think through it carefully because it's a bigger change than it looks.

My honest reaction:

This is a meaningful improvement to the metric. Here's why:

The current Confidence algorithm rewards goals and assists (attacking actions) plus clean sheets (team-level defensive outcomes). It doesn't directly reward individual defensive work. A defender who made 12 tackles and 5 clearances in a 0-3 loss — genuinely a great individual performance — gets nothing from our algorithm except a Blank penalty. That's wrong. The new FPL DefCon stat is the league's official acknowledgment that this kind of work deserves recognition, and our metric should reflect it.

It also addresses something we noticed earlier: the algorithm punishes defenders harshly when their team gives up goals. A center-back who personally was excellent in a defeat still gets a Blank under our rules. Adding DefCon as a positive event softens that — even in a losing effort, a player who hit the threshold gets a small lift.

But there are real questions to answer before changing the algorithm:

Is "Performance equivalent" (+1/+2) the right weight? I'd argue maybe slightly less. An assist directly creates a goal — high-value attacking outcome. A DefCon threshold is "you did your job a lot" — high-effort defensive outcome. They're both useful but assists are more directly correlated with FPL points than DefCons. Worth considering +1 across the board (regardless of opponent type) as a "consistency bonus" rather than treating it as Performance-equivalent.
Does it count as MOTM for fatigue purposes? If a defender hits DefCon threshold 3 weeks in a row, do they trigger fatigue? I'd say no — fatigue is about regression-to-the-mean for exceptional performance (goals/assists). DefCon is more about consistency, not exceptional output. Treating it like Performance (counts for points but not toward MOTM counter) is the cleaner choice.
Does it stack with clean sheets for GK/DEF? A defender with a clean sheet AND DefCon threshold in the same match — does that get +2 (CS) + +1 (DefCon) = +3? Or do we cap at one positive event per match? I'd stack them — they represent different things (team outcome vs individual effort).
Does it stack with goals/assists? A midfielder who hits DefCon threshold AND scores — under current rules they get MOTM (+2/+3). Adding DefCon would make it MOTM + DefCon. Probably overkill. Cleaner: if MOTM fires, DefCon doesn't add (the goal already proves they're contributing).
Position-specific thresholds. FPL's DefCon thresholds are different for defenders (10) vs midfielders/forwards (12). Our algorithm already differentiates by position, so this fits naturally — but it means the "10+ tackles" rule isn't just one thing, it's two thresholds.
Backwards data. The 2024/25 season's data may not have DefCon stats (it was added in 2025/26). That's fine because we're synced on the current season, but worth noting.
Proposed scoring rule (my suggestion, you can disagree):

Add a new event type called DefensiveContribution:

Triggers when defensive_contribution === 1 in the FPL match history (FPL has already done the threshold calculation, we just read the boolean)
Scoring: +1 regardless of opponent (no big/non-big distinction)
Stacks with: Clean Sheet (yes), Performance (yes, but only if they didn't get assist+goals), Blank (no — DefCon prevents the Blank penalty entirely)
Does NOT count toward fatigue MOTM counter
Does NOT trigger when MOTM fires (MOTM already represents elite output)
Resolution order for any position becomes:

MOTM (1+ goals OR 2+ assists) → MOTM points, done
Performance (1 assist, 0 goals) → Performance points, then check DefCon as add-on
GK/DEF Assist (treated as MOTM) → assist points, then check DefCon as add-on
Clean sheet (GK/DEF only) → CS points, then check DefCon as add-on
DefCon-only (no goals, no assists, no CS, but hit threshold) → +1
True blank (no positive events at all) → blank penalty
This is a bigger change than the range tweak.

The range change (-5 → -4) is a one-line constant. This DefCon change is:

A new event type in the algorithm
New unit tests covering the resolution order interactions
A new field in the MatchEvent type
The mapMatchEvents mapper needs to read defensive_contribution from the FPL API
The Zod schema for HistoryItem needs the new field
The reason string for cards needs new variants ("DefCon vs big team", "Performance + DefCon", "Clean sheet + DefCon")
Realistically: half a day of work, done right. Worth doing, but not a side dish to the range change.

My recommendation on sequencing:

Let the current Step 9.7 sign-off finish (Claude is still working on something).
Ship the range change next as a clean, isolated edit.
After the range change, ship the DefCon change as its own focused work.
Then move to Step 11.
Don't bundle. Each change deserves its own sign-off and recompute so we can isolate "did this change produce sensible new numbers" from "did this other change produce sensible new numbers." If we change three things at once and the metric feels weird, we won't know which one caused it.

Tap your answer. The DefCon idea is genuinely good — it fixes a real metric blind spot — but it's substantial enough that it deserves its own focused implementation rather than being slipped in alongside the range change. Whichever option you pick, we'll need to confirm the exact scoring rule (the +1 vs +1/+2 question) before any code is written.

Before we get to step 3, we need to nail down the DefCon scoring rule. Let me put my proposed rule on the table so you can react, then you can confirm or modify before I draft the actual implementation prompt.

Proposed DefCon scoring rule:

DefCon (FPL DefCon threshold met) → +1, regardless of opponent
Three reasons for +1 flat (instead of +1/+2 like Performance):

DefCon is consistency, not exceptional output. Hitting the threshold means "you did your job thoroughly" not "you produced an exceptional moment." A flat reward reflects the steady-state nature of it.
It's not opponent-dependent in the same way attacking output is. Tackling 12 times against Burnley vs. tackling 12 times against City — both are equally hard work, even if the latter is more impressive. Goals and assists are different — a goal vs. City is genuinely harder to come by than vs. Burnley, so the big-team bonus makes sense there. DefCon thresholds are absolute counts, less correlated with opponent quality.
A flat +1 keeps the magnitude modest. This is a new event source. Players will earn it more often than goals or assists. If we made it +1/+2, it would dominate the metric for defensive midfielders. +1 flat keeps it as a meaningful but not dominant signal.
Stacking rules:

Player's match Result
Just DefCon (no other events) +1
DefCon + clean sheet (GK/DEF) +1 (DefCon) + +1/+2 (CS)
DefCon + Performance (1 assist) +1 (DefCon) + +1/+2 (Performance)
DefCon + Goal (any position) MOTM points only — DefCon absorbed by MOTM
DefCon + 0 G/A + no CS +1 (replaces what would have been a Blank)
No DefCon + 0 G/A + no CS Blank penalty (-1 GK/DEF, -1/-2 MID/FWD)
Fatigue:

DefCon does NOT increment the MOTM counter
DefCon is not subject to fatigue penalty
Fatigue is reserved for "exceptional output" (goals or 2+ assists). DefCon is consistency, separate axis.
Reason string examples:

"DefCon vs non-big team" (DefCon only)
"Clean sheet vs big team + DefCon" (defender against City, kept clean sheet, hit threshold)
"Performance vs non-big team + DefCon" (midfielder with 1 assist + threshold)
One specific edge case to confirm:

A player who would have blanked (0 goals, 0 assists, no CS) BUT hit DefCon threshold — under my rule, they get +1 instead of the blank penalty. This is a meaningful change. A defender who lost 3-0 but worked hard defensively no longer gets dinged.

Question for you: is that the intended behavior, or do you want DefCon to be additive on top of a blank ("yeah you worked hard but the team still lost so you still get -1, +1 = 0 net")? My instinct is "DefCon prevents the blank entirely" — a player who hit the threshold genuinely contributed, and punishing them for the team's failure is unfair. But you might disagree.

Tap your answers when you're ready. While you decide, two questions you should be thinking about that will shape the implementation:

Confirmation question on the API: I assumed the FPL API exposes defensive_contribution as a field on each match's history entry, with a value indicating whether the threshold was hit. I'm 90% confident this is true based on the 2025/26 scoring changes, but I haven't verified it directly. When Claude Code starts the implementation, the first thing they'll do is verify the actual field name and shape against the live API. If my assumption is wrong, the rule still works — we just need to read the right field.

One product question to think about now even though we're not asking it formally: Once DefCon is in, the algorithm has a stronger bias toward defensive midfielders (the Casemiro / Rodri / Caicedo archetype). They'll get +1 most weeks just for showing up and tackling. Is that the signal you want? It might be — those players are reliable FPL picks who often go undervalued. But it's worth noting because the leaderboard composition will shift toward this archetype after the change. Worth watching when the recompute lands.

Tap your answers and we'll proceed.

Claude responded: Range change signed off.
Range change signed off. Numbers tell a clean story.

Quick read of what landed:

Your team moved from 32.8% → 38.9%. That's a 6-point lift purely from giving the floor more room. Palmer and Calvert-Lewin both went from -5 to -4, which looks small on individual cards but compounds across a team's worth of negative players. Midfield jumped from 13.3% → 20.0%, Defence 45.0% → 51.7% (now above neutral), Attack 40.0% → 45.0%. Each line moved in the right direction without any of them swinging dramatically — that's calibration working.

The distribution table is the more interesting signal. 115 players at -4 is the largest single bucket — they were the ones the floor was compressing. Now the cluster has somewhere to live. Above the floor, the spread looks healthy: 14 at -3, 24 at -2, 40 at -1, 5 each at +1/+2/+3, 5 at +5. The previous symmetric clamp was producing a bimodal "lots at floor / lots at +5" distribution; this is more naturally varied.

The 3-player traces are doing real diagnostic work too. Abraham's GW28 clamp is preserved (raw -6 → -4, not -5), confirming the new clamp fires correctly at the boundary. His GW33 recovery from -4 to -2 with a +2 delta is exactly the kind of "one good match shows real progress" signal we wanted — under the old rules he'd have been at -5 needing a longer climb out. Martinelli oscillating around the floor (at -4, lifts to -3, falls back to -4) shows the metric still differentiates "at floor" from "just above floor" — the floor isn't sticky in the bad way anymore.

One observation worth filing for later:

The 5/5/5 distribution at +1/+2/+3 is conspicuously flat. With 524 players, you'd expect a smoother spread above zero — say 25 / 18 / 12 / 8 / 5 as you go higher. Five players at each of three positive buckets is suspiciously regular. Could be coincidence in your particular sample, could be that the algorithm produces clusters because of how MOTM points (+2/+3) interact with the +5 ceiling and fatigue resets. Not a bug, probably, but worth keeping an eye on once DefCon lands and we re-look at the distribution.

Decision 1 — Team Confidence display: PERCENTAGE. Locked in.

This is on the My Team page and the Dashboard hero. Your team currently shows as 38.9% (was 32.8%). The positional breakdown shows Defence 51.7%, Midfield 20.0%, Attack 45.0%. All percentages. This is already shipped and working — you saw it in the screenshots from the range-change recompute.

Decision 2 — Individual player Confidence display: STILL DEFERRED.

This is on the Players list, the Player Detail hero, the leaderboard, the match history cards. Currently displayed as integer values from -4 to +5 (Gabriel +5, Palmer -4, etc.). You said earlier "Wait — let me see Step 9 dashboard first, then decide" between three options:

Option A: hide deltas, keep integer score
Option B: percentage display + hide deltas (full IP protection)
Option C: keep transparent
You picked "wait until I see the full app" and we filed it for Step 12 polish.

Where we are right now on this decision:

You've now seen everything — Dashboard, Players list, Player Detail, My Team. You've used real data. You've audited the algorithm against your own team. You're in the right position to make this call now if you want, instead of waiting for Step 12.

Two reasons to make the decision now:

Consistency. Right now your app uses two different scales: 38.9% on My Team, but +3 / -4 on individual players. Users will notice. A user looking at their team at 38.9% and clicking through to Palmer at -4 has to mentally map "is -4 good or bad? is it the same scale as the 38.9% I just saw?" That's friction.
Algorithm transparency. You raised this concern earlier — that the integer scale plus visible match-level deltas makes the algorithm easy to reverse-engineer. Switching individuals to percentage closes that loophole partially (and we can address the match cards in the same change).
Two reasons to keep deferring:

DefCon hasn't shipped yet. Once DefCon lands, the leaderboard composition will shift (defensive midfielders moving up). You may want to see that change before re-evaluating the display layer.
You may want to use the app for a captain decision first. The "does the integer feel right" gut-check is more meaningful after you've actually used the metric to make a real choice.
My honest read:

Make the call now if you have a strong instinct. Defer if you don't. Both are valid. The integer-vs-percentage display is a UX layer change that's cheap and reversible at any point.

If you do want to switch individuals to percentage now, the math is the same piecewise function we just shipped:

value ≥ 0 → 50 + (value / 5) × 50
value < 0 → 50 + (value / 4) × 50
So +5 → 100%, +3 → 80%, 0 → 50%, -2 → 25%, -4 → 0%. Same scale as the team, fully consistent.

Tap your answer when ready. While Claude Code is verifying the DefCon API field names, you have a moment to think this through — no rush. If you're not sure, defer is a fine answer; we can swap the display layer at any point with one focused change.

Two things worth thinking about while you wait, that will shape the % display switch when we get to it:

Decimal precision. Team Confidence shows 38.9% (one decimal). Individual values from a small integer scale, mapped to percentage, will produce values like 60.0%, 80.0%, 25.0%. Always exact tenths because the input is integer. So one decimal is correct, but every value will end in .0 — which looks slightly clinical. Worth deciding: drop the decimal entirely on individuals (just "60%", "80%"), or keep one decimal for consistency with team display ("60.0%", "80.0%"). I'd lean drop-the-decimal for individuals because the precision is fake — but it's a small UX call.

Sign handling. Right now ConfidenceNumber renders +3 in green or -4 in red. Under percentage, "60%" doesn't have a sign. You lose the explicit "+/-" glyph that was helping readability. The color still does the work (green for ≥50%, red for <50%, neutral for exactly 50%), but the glyph signal disappears. Worth knowing in advance — when you see the new look, it'll feel different even if it's correct.

These are decisions for when we get there, not now. Keep waiting on Claude's DefCon API output.

You said: That's the full picture.
That's the full picture. Here are the raw findings:
API Contract — Raw Findings

1. History entry keys (Caicedo, MID — GW1)
   Three relevant fields present per match:

tackles — integer (raw count)
clearances_blocks_interceptions — integer (raw count, a pre-existing bundled field)
recoveries — integer (raw count)
defensive_contribution — integer (pre-computed aggregate by FPL)
Representative full history entry
{
"element": 241, "round": 1, "minutes": 90,
"tackles": 5,
"clearances_blocks_interceptions": 6,
"recoveries": 1,
"defensive_contribution": 12,
"total_points": 5,
...
}
What defensive_contribution actually is — and it's position-dependent defensive_contribution is a pre-computed aggregate, not a boolean, but its formula varies by position: PositionFormulaVerified across all GWsDEF (Gabriel)tackles + clearances_blocks_interceptions (recoveries excluded)✓ 100% matchMID (Caicedo)tackles + clearances_blocks_interceptions + recoveries✓ 34/34 matchFWD (Gyökeres)tackles + clearances_blocks_interceptions + recoveries✓ 8/8 match GKP scoring is 0 per the game config — defensive_contribution is presumably computed but irrelevant.
Threshold and points from game_config.scoring No threshold field exists anywhere in bootstrap-static — not in element_types, game_settings, or game_config.rules. The thresholds (10 for DEF, 12 for MID/FWD) are not machine-readable from the API. They must be hard-coded. The points awarded are machine-readable:
"defensive_contribution": {
"DEF": 2,
"FWD": 2,
"GKP": 0,
"MID": 2
}
All outfield positions earn 2 pts per threshold-clearing match. GKP earns 0. 5. Answer to your three questions
A. Boolean/indicator that threshold was hit? — No. defensive_contribution is the raw aggregate count (e.g. 12, 23, 7), not a 0/1 flag. You compare defensive_contribution >= threshold yourself.
B. Raw component counts requiring us to do the threshold math? — Partially yes. You get tackles, clearances_blocks_interceptions, recoveries individually, but defensive_contribution is the pre-summed aggregate (with position-specific formula). You can use defensive_contribution directly with >= threshold and skip re-summing.
C. Both? — Yes, effectively. The pre-summed defensive_contribution field is present alongside the raw components. You only need to apply >= threshold to defensive_contribution — the component math is already done. The one wrinkle: the threshold values (10 DEF / 12 MID+FWD) are not in the API and must be hard-coded.
Recommendation: read defensive_contribution directly from the history entry, compare to a hard-coded position-keyed threshold map, emit a boolean thresholdHit. Don't re-sum the components — the formula differs by position and the API has already done it correctly.

Claude responded: Excellent diagnostic.
Excellent diagnostic. Three things stand out before I draft the implementation prompt:

1. The pre-summed defensive_contribution field is the right input. Just compare it against the position-specific threshold. We don't need to re-sum components, and we don't need to know FPL's exact formula (which has the GK/DEF vs MID/FWD difference around recoveries — interesting trivia but irrelevant to us since we just read the aggregate).

2. Hard-coding the thresholds is unavoidable but acceptable. They're stable league rules — 10 for DEF, 12 for MID/FWD — set by FPL for the whole 2025/26 season. They won't change mid-season. Worst case if FPL changes them next season, we update one constant. Worth a comment in the code: // Threshold values from FPL 2025/26 scoring rules (not exposed via API) so future engineers know why they're magic numbers.

3. GK is correctly excluded. FPL gives goalkeepers 0 points for DefCon. Our algorithm should match — DefCon doesn't apply to GK position, period. If a goalkeeper's defensive_contribution somehow exceeds the threshold (which would be unusual but possible — a sweeper-keeper making lots of recoveries), it doesn't fire our rule.

Here's the DefCon implementation prompt — paste this in:

DefCon API contract verified. Now implementing the DefCon (Defensive Contribution) addition to the Confidence algorithm.

Scoring rule (locked): When a player's defensive_contribution field meets or exceeds their position's threshold, award +1 to confidence regardless of opponent. DefCon does not increment the MOTM counter and does not fire fatigue. DefCon prevents the Blank penalty entirely (a player who hit threshold but did nothing else gets +1 instead of -1 or -2).

Thresholds (hard-coded, not in API):

DEF: 10
MID: 12
FWD: 12
GK: not applicable (DefCon never fires for GK regardless of value)
Follow the same docs-first → tests → code → recompute discipline as the range change.

Step A — Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
§3 Definitions: add a new entry:
DefCon (Defensive Contribution) = defensive_contribution field from FPL match history meets or exceeds the position-specific threshold (DEF: 10, MID: 12, FWD: 12). Not applicable to GK.
§4 Scoring table: add a new row at the bottom:
Event vs Big vs Non-Big
DefCon (DEF/MID/FWD only, threshold met) +1 +1
Add a footnote: "DefCon is a steady-state contribution signal. The reward is flat regardless of opponent because hitting the threshold is an absolute count of defensive actions, not opponent-dependent."
§5 Per-match resolution: update both the MID/FWD path and the GK/DEF path to incorporate DefCon. The new resolution order: MID/FWD:
MOTM performance (1+ goals OR 2+ assists) → MOTM points (DefCon absorbed, does not stack)
Performance (1 assist, 0 goals) → Performance points + DefCon if applicable
DefCon-only (no goals, no assists, but threshold met) → +1
True blank (0 goals, 0 assists, no DefCon) → blank penalty
GK/DEF:
Assist (treated as MOTM) → assist points + Clean Sheet if applicable + DefCon if applicable (DEF only — GK skips DefCon entirely)
Goal (no assist) → MOTM points + Clean Sheet if applicable + DefCon if applicable (DEF only)
Clean sheet (no G/A) → CS points + DefCon if applicable (DEF only)
DefCon-only (no G/A, no CS, threshold met — DEF only) → +1
True blank (no positive events) → blank penalty
§6 Fatigue: add a sentence: "DefCon does not increment the MOTM counter and is not subject to fatigue. Fatigue is reserved for exceptional output (goals or 2+ assists), which DefCon is structurally distinct from."
§8 Worked examples: add new examples covering the DefCon interactions:
EX-16: MID with 0 goals, 0 assists, DefCon threshold met, vs non-big team. Expected: delta=+1, reason="DefCon vs non-big team". (Replaces what would have been a Blank.)
EX-17: MID with 0 goals, 0 assists, DefCon NOT met, vs non-big team. Expected: delta=-2, reason="Blank vs non-big team". (Confirms DefCon-absent still produces blank.)
EX-18: MID with 1 assist (Performance) AND DefCon threshold met, vs big team. Expected: delta=+3, reason="Performance vs big team + DefCon". (Stack: +2 Performance + +1 DefCon.)
EX-19: DEF with 0 G/A, clean sheet, DefCon threshold met, vs non-big team. Expected: delta=+2, reason="Clean sheet vs non-big team + DefCon". (Stack: +1 CS + +1 DefCon.)
EX-20: MID with 2+ assists (MOTM) AND DefCon threshold met, vs non-big team. Expected: delta=+2, reason="MOTM vs non-big team". (DefCon absorbed by MOTM, does not stack.)
EX-21: GK with 0 G/A, no CS, defensive_contribution = 15 (above any threshold). Expected: delta=-1, reason="Blank vs big team" (or non-big). (Confirms DefCon never fires for GK regardless of value.)
EX-22: DEF with defensive_contribution = 9 vs non-big (one below threshold). Expected: blank penalty fires normally.
EX-23: DEF with defensive_contribution = 10 vs non-big (exactly at threshold). Expected: DefCon fires.
Boundary tests on the threshold are critical — >= threshold not > threshold.
PROP-04 (new property test): "DefCon never increments motmCounter for any input." Property: for any valid input where the only positive signal is DefCon, motmCounterAfter equals motmCounterBefore.
§9 Reference implementation pseudocode: update to show the new resolution order with DefCon in place.

aMatch builder defaults defensiveContribution: 0.

src/lib/confidence/calculator.ts:
Add a DEFCON_THRESHOLD: Record<Position, number | null> constant: { GK: null, DEF: 10, MID: 12, FWD: 12 }. Add comment: "Threshold values from FPL 2025/26 scoring rules (not exposed via API)."
Add a helper isDefConThresholdMet(position, defensiveContribution): boolean that returns false for GK (null threshold) and otherwise returns defensiveContribution >= threshold.
Update resolveMidFwd and resolveGkDef to incorporate DefCon per the resolution order in ALGORITHM.md §5.

Read defensive_contribution from the FPL HistoryItem and map it to MatchEvent.defensiveContribution. Pass through as a number, no transformation.

Add a new 'defcon' ReasonKind, or extend the existing categories — your judgment.

Step D — Recompute and verify

A note for you, the human:

When the recompute lands, look at Caicedo and Rodri specifically. They're the canonical "DefCon archetypes" — players who tackle and clear constantly, rarely score or assist, and would have been buried under our old algorithm because clean sheets and Performance events alone don't surface them. Under the new rules, they should have meaningfully higher confidence values. If they don't, something's wrong. If they do, the change is working.

After this lands, the metric will reflect three different kinds of value:

Exceptional output (goals, multiple assists) — MOTM bonus
Defensive outcomes (clean sheets) — CS bonus
Defensive consistency (DefCon) — flat +1 for steady contributors
That's a much fuller picture of player value than what we had before. The metric is genuinely better.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Honest answer: not in the current build, and probably not in v1 at all.
Honest answer: not in the current build, and probably not in v1 at all. But it's a great v2 candidate. Let me explain.

Why it's not in scope right now:

We explicitly ruled this out at the start. Way back when you described "player view with formation, subs, captain, transfers" and I asked which path to take, you picked "stay analytics-focused" and said:

"I want the total confidence of team, just like other fantasy app shows team rating"

That decision shaped everything. The current My Team page is a list-based representation of your squad, not a pitch-based one. That was deliberate — it lets us focus on the analytics layer (per-line breakdown, individual confidence values, captain context) without rebuilding the FPL "Pick Team" UI.

What a pitch view actually requires:

It looks deceptively simple. It's not. A real pitch view needs:

SVG pitch rendering with markings (penalty box, center circle, lines) that scales responsively from 375px to 1280px
Formation logic (3-4-3, 3-5-2, 4-3-3, 4-4-2, 5-3-2, 5-4-1, etc.) — each formation has different positional slots that need to be calculated from squad composition
Player tokens with jersey kits, names, confidence values rendered on the pitch positions
Captain/vice badge overlays on the right tokens
Bench rendering — usually below the pitch as a smaller row, sometimes with auto-sub indicators
Mobile responsive — pitch view at 375px is genuinely hard to design well
Realistically: 2-3 days of focused work, half of which is the SVG/layout math and the other half is making it not look ugly on mobile.

The deeper question worth asking yourself:

What would the pitch view actually do for you that the current list view doesn't?

The list view shows: 11 starters in squad order, each with their team, position, individual confidence, captain/vice badges. Plus the per-line breakdown above (Defence 51.7%, Midfield 20%, Attack 45%) which already tells you which line of your team is strong or weak.

A pitch view would show: the same 11 starters arranged in formation shape, with the same data attached.

The pitch view is prettier and more familiar (because it matches what you see in the FPL app and on TV), but it doesn't add new analytical signal. It's a presentation of the same information, not new information.

That said — pretty matters. Familiar matters. A pitch view is the kind of thing that makes the app feel like an FPL companion rather than a stats tool. There's real product value in matching the user's existing mental model.

My honest take on when this should ship:

Not now. Reasons:

You don't need it to make decisions. The current list view tells you everything actionable.
It's expensive relative to its value. 2-3 days for a presentation-layer change is high cost vs. analytical changes.
You haven't shipped v1 yet. Adding it now delays the moment you can use the app to make real captain/transfer decisions and learn what actually matters.
You should know what other features you also want. Pitch view, P(Goal), P(Assist), maybe more. Better to plan v2 holistically than to start bolting features onto v1.
A v2 plan that includes pitch view:

When you start v2 (probably after using v1 for a few gameweeks), the rough scope would be:

Multi-metric (P(Goal), P(Assist) alongside Confidence)
Pitch view with formation rendering
Possibly: transfer suggester (the metric tells you who to move, the pitch tells you what your team looks like before/after)
Possibly: captain recommender highlighting the highest-confidence starting player
That's a coherent v2 scope. Pitch view isn't a feature to add by itself — it's the visual foundation that lets new features feel native to FPL managers.

One pragmatic offer for v1 polish:

If you really want something pitch-flavored before v1 ships, the cheapest version is a formation header above the starting XI list. Just a small "4-3-3" or "3-5-2" label with a thin diagram showing the shape. Doesn't render players, doesn't replace the list. Pure decorative — but it gives the page some FPL-app DNA. Probably 30 minutes of work in Step 12 polish.

Not the same as a real pitch view, but if the feeling you're missing is "this looks like an FPL tool" rather than the function of seeing players on a pitch, the formation label gets you 30% of the way for 5% of the work.

For now: defer the real pitch view. File it as v2 high priority. Let Claude finish DefCon and we keep moving.
