Filed. Here's the v2 queue I'm tracking — I'll add to it as we go.

v2 Queue — Future Build
Major features

1. Multi-metric expansion
   P(Goal) — probability of scoring in next match. Spec at docs/v2/probability-algorithms.md (already saved by user). Five questions still need answers: window size, calibration method, opponent adjustment, fixture-specific vs. general, validation strategy.
   P(Assist) — probability of assisting in next match. Same model shape as P(Goal), different target event.
   Backtest harness needed to validate both probability metrics against historical seasons.
   Three metrics shown side-by-side rather than Confidence alone.
2. Pitch view
   SVG pitch rendering with formation logic (3-4-3, 3-5-2, 4-3-3, 4-4-2, 5-3-2, 5-4-1, etc.)
   Player tokens on pitch positions with jersey kits, confidence values, captain/vice badges
   Bench rendering below the pitch
   Mobile responsive at 375px (hardest part)
   Estimated 2-3 days of focused work
3. Transfer suggester
   Given current squad and budget, surface candidate transfers ranked by confidence delta gain
   Respects FPL rules: 15-player squad, max 3 per club, free transfers, points hits
4. Captain recommender
   Highlights the highest-confidence starting player as suggested captain
   Should respect "next match opponent" once probability metrics are in
   Algorithm / metric changes
5. DGW match-history visualization
   Currently DGW renders as one card with combined reason string
   User wants per-match cards (two cards both labeled "GW33", separate deltas)
   Two paths considered: UI-only split (parse reason string) or schema change (one row per match)
   Decision deferred from v1 Step 12 to v2 because it's likely more useful once paired with richer per-match data
6. Cameo appearance threshold
   Currently minutesPlayed > 0 counts as an appearance
   A 5-minute substitute cameo gets penalized as a Blank under current rules
   Consider raising to minutesPlayed >= 30 or >= 60 so cameos don't unfairly punish players
7. Injury / availability indicators
   Use FPL API's status field (a = available, i = injured, s = suspended, etc.)
   Show "OUT" or red dot badge on player cards when unavailable
   Doesn't affect calculation, just communicates "this confidence is from before they got hurt"
8. Stale confidence visual indicator
   Players whose last snapshot is more than 3 GWs old should be visually marked when they appear (in places where the dashboard staleness filter doesn't already exclude them)
   Muted color treatment + small "GW5" tag indicating when last computed
   Communicates "trust this less"
9. Position-balanced leaderboard
   Current leaderboard top is dominated by defenders due to the asymmetric path to high confidence
   Consider a "top by position" toggle, or three smaller leaderboards (top GK / top DEF / top MID / top FWD)
   Or: a "diversify across teams" toggle on Risers/Fallers cards (currently Arsenal players cluster in Fallers, Brighton in Risers)
10. Distinguish "no data" from "neutral delta" in trend strip
    Currently a gray square in the 5-square strip means either (a) the player had a 0-delta match or (b) we don't have data for that match position
    Filed as TODO comment in ConfidenceTrend.tsx
    Use empty outline (1px border, transparent fill) for missing slots, solid gray for actual neutral deltas
    Multi-user / monetization (only if v1 personal usage validates the product)
11. Authentication + per-user data isolation
    Move from single-user SQLite to multi-user Postgres
    Auth via Clerk, Auth.js, or Supabase auth
    Per-user team IDs, watchlists, settings
12. Subscription / payments
    Stripe integration: subscriptions, webhooks, customer portal, failed-payment handling, prorations
    2-3 weeks of work done right
    Premium tier features TBD based on what users actually pay for
13. PWA / mobile install
    Configure as Progressive Web App so it installs to home screen
    2-3 hours of work, additive
    Lower priority than native app feel via responsive web
    Polish carried from v1 if not addressed
14. Y-axis chart label clipping on Player Detail page
    Numbers partial at bottom of confidence-over-time chart
    Filed during Step 8
15. X-axis tick distribution uneven on chart
    Currently 1, 6, 11, 22, 27, 33 — irregular gaps
    Either fix tick spacing to even intervals or hide axis labels except start/middle/end
16. LCP optimization on Player Detail
    3.4s LCP is highest in the build, mostly from Recharts
    Lazy-load chart with next/dynamic and ssr: false if it drifts higher
    Filed during Step 8
17. Dashboard orphaned whitespace on desktop dark
    ~100px gap between hero strip and leaderboard
    Either equal-height the strip cards or tighten the gap
    Filed during Step 9
18. Bench section opacity vs background tint
    Current low-opacity treatment may read as "loading state" at first glance
    Subtle background tint might communicate "de-emphasized but real" more clearly
    Filed during Step 9.7
19. Formation header label
    Small "4-3-3" or "3-5-2" label with thin diagram above starting XI list
    Cheap pitch-flavored decoration without full pitch view
    30 minutes of work, gives FPL-app DNA without committing to full pitch rendering

Apr 26
Claude responded: DefCon implementation signed off.
DefCon implementation signed off. Take a moment with this one — three things landed exactly as predicted, and one thing didn't, and the one that didn't is worth thinking about before we continue.

What worked:

Caicedo's match history is the clearest validation. Look at his pattern — GW1: pure DefCon +1, GW10: Performance + DefCon stack (+3 total), GW17: DefCon-only +1, GW28: DefCon vs big team +1. The metric is correctly identifying him as a player whose value comes from steady defensive work, not goals or assists. Under the old algorithm he'd have been near the floor most of the season because he doesn't score and his Chelsea team has had inconsistent clean sheets. Under DefCon he's a positive contributor — which matches reality.

The distribution shift is healthy. -4 floor lost 28 players (correct: many of them weren't truly hopeless, they were defensive contributors being punished for blanks). +3/+4/+5 gained significantly. The metric now has a much more nuanced middle and upper range — it's not just "elite forwards and clean-sheet specialists" anymore.

Your team's percentage moved from 38.9% → 50.6%. You've crossed the neutral baseline. That's a meaningful psychological threshold — your team is no longer "below average" by the metric, it's "right at average." The lift makes sense: your Bournemouth defenders (Senesi, Hill) are exactly the DefCon archetype the change is meant to reward.

What's worth a second look — the leaderboard concern:

Before DefCon: top 10 was ~50% defenders. After DefCon: top 10 is 100% defenders.

Claude's note frames this as "validation rather than overcorrection." I want to push back gently. Going from 50% to 100% is a big swing, and the right framing is "we gave defenders three sources of positive points (CS, MOTM-via-assist, DefCon) where attackers only have two (MOTM, Performance), and the algorithm responded as expected." That's not overcorrection in a bug sense, but it might be overcorrection in a product sense.

Here's the thing: an FPL leaderboard sorted by Confidence that shows nothing but defenders has a usability problem. A user opening the dashboard wanting to know "who should I captain?" needs to see Salah and Haaland and Saka there, even if they're objectively at lower confidence values. The current state of "all defenders" makes the leaderboard less useful for the most common decision (captain choice).

This is the same observation we made earlier about defender dominance, but DefCon has now amplified it. Two paths forward, both valid:

Path 1: Accept the algorithmic answer, fix the UX. The leaderboard is correctly reporting "defenders are providing the most stable confidence right now." The fix is at the presentation layer: split the leaderboard into "top by position" tabs (top 5 GK / DEF / MID / FWD) so each archetype is visible. This is the option I'd lean toward — it doesn't hide signal, it just rebalances the surface.

Path 2: Calibrate the algorithm. Reduce DefCon's weight (e.g. only fire on threshold + a multiplier) or change Performance/MOTM weights to maintain parity. This is harder to get right and risks breaking the calibration we just landed.

File this for Step 12 polish. Don't act now. The leaderboard composition is technically correct; whether it's useful is a UX decision better made after you've used the app for a real captain decision.

One detail worth noting from Caicedo's late-season trace:

GWs 29-33 show him sliding from -1 → -4 over five matches with mixed deltas (0 / -1 / -2). Looking at this, DefCon's flat +1 isn't quite enough to fully prevent collapse during a sustained defensive-effort drought (when he's not even hitting the threshold). That's correct — the metric should track real form. But it's worth knowing that DefCon doesn't make defensive players invincible. It just stops penalizing them when they're trying.

Now — the individual percentage display change.

You locked this in earlier. Here's the prompt to send next:

DefCon signed off. The metric is producing meaningfully better signals — Caicedo's pattern is a textbook validation, your team moved from 38.9% to 50.6% which is the right magnitude of lift. One observation filed for Step 12: leaderboard now showing 100% defenders, may want a position-balanced view in polish. Not blocking.

Now apply the individual player Confidence display switch from integers to percentages.

Scope
Every surface that currently displays an integer confidence value (-4 to +5) needs to display the percentage (0 to 100%) instead. Use the same piecewise formula already shipped in team-confidence:

value ≥ 0 → 50 + (value / 5) × 50
value < 0 → 50 + (value / 4) × 50
So +5 → 100%, +3 → 80%, +1 → 60%, 0 → 50%, -2 → 25%, -4 → 0%.

Surfaces affected
ConfidenceNumber component (src/components/confidence/ConfidenceNumber.tsx) — the canonical display component used everywhere. Update to render {percent}% instead of {sign}{integer}. Color logic shifts from sign-based to threshold-based: ≥ 50% green, < 50% red, exactly 50% neutral. Keep data-sign attribute working but its values become positive (>50%), negative (<50%), neutral (=50%).
Decimal precision: drop decimals on individual player values. Display "60%", "80%", "0%" — not "60.0%". The values are always integer-derived, so the decimal would always be ".0" which looks clinical. Team Confidence keeps one decimal because it's a true average.
Dashboard leaderboard — +5 becomes 100%, +3 becomes 80%, etc. Every leaderboard row updates.
Players list — both the desktop table column and the mobile card layout. The table column header changes from "CONFIDENCE" (still works, just displays differently).
Player Detail hero — the 96px ConfidenceNumber. Now shows 100% for Gabriel instead of +5.
ConfidenceSlider — the -4 to +5 axis with tick marks. This is the trickiest surface. Two options:
A: Keep the slider showing -4 to +5 internal values (representing the underlying integer scale) and add a percentage label above it. Slider stays mathematically honest.
B: Convert the slider to 0-100% with tick marks at 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%. Loses the integer granularity but is consistent with the new display.
Choose B for consistency. Update tick marks to 0, 25, 50, 75, 100 with the marker showing the player's percent value.
Match history cards — the per-match delta and confidence-after values. These are trickier because delta is "how much did this match change the value." Two options:
A: Convert both delta and confidence-after to percentage points. A match that gave +2 confidence becomes "+20%" delta. Mathematically consistent but might feel large.
B: Show delta as percentage-points (e.g. "+20pp" with pp suffix) to distinguish from absolute %, and confidence-after as %.
C: Keep delta as the raw integer (+2) and only convert the confidence-after to %. Inconsistent but clearest about "this match contributed 2 points of underlying score, the new total is 80%."
Choose C — keep deltas as integers, convert confidence-after to %. Reasoning: the delta is per-match contribution to a per-match scoring system; converting it to percentage-points would imply a per-match percentage, which doesn't exist. The confidence-after is the cumulative state, which is what gets converted.
Trend strip (the 5-square color strip on player rows) — no change needed. The strip shows directional pattern (green/red/gray per match), not values. Already works.
Defensive clamp on ConfidenceNumber — the component clamps out-of-range integer values to [-4, +5] before rendering. Now: clamp the integer first (defensively), then convert to %. Out-of-range still produces a logger warning.
Reason strings (no change)
Reason strings still describe the underlying event ("MOTM vs big team", "DefCon vs non-big team", "DGW: ...") because they're describing what happened, not the value. The card's delta number changes display, the reason text doesn't.

Tests
Update ConfidenceNumber.test.tsx to assert percentage rendering: +5 input → 100% text, 0 input → 50% text, -4 input → 0% text.
Update component tests for any place that asserted on +3 text — they now assert on 80%.
The data-sign attribute test cases shift: positive value → data-sign="positive", value below 50% → data-sign="negative", exactly 50% → data-sign="neutral".
Run all tests, confirm green. Some E2E tests will need updating for the new text format.
What stays integer-displayed (do NOT convert)
Match history card delta (per option C above) — stays as +2, -1, etc.
Big Team breakdown averages on player detail page — those are aggregate floats like +0.4 avg/match, leave as-is. The "avg per match" framing only makes sense in raw points.
Reason strings — describe events, not values.
Sign-off package
All tests green

Dashboard (leaderboard now shows %)
Players list (% in confidence column / cards)
Player Detail hero (96px % number, slider with 0-100 axis)
Player Detail match history strip (delta integer, confidence-after %)
My Team page (already in %, just confirm consistency)
Visual sanity check: pick the highest-confidence player (Gabriel at +5 → 100%), pick a mid player (~+1 → 60%), pick a low player (-3 → 12.5%, displays as 13% rounded). Confirm the values look correct.
Don't proceed to Step 11 until I sign off.

A note for you, the human:

This is a significant visual change. The app will feel different — every Confidence value across every surface now reads in a different format. Some things will improve (consistency with team scale, harder to reverse-engineer the algorithm), some things will feel different in ways you can only judge by seeing it.

When the screenshots land, you'll be evaluating: does percentage feel more or less actionable than the integer scale? "+5 vs Brighton" had a clarity to it that "100%" might lack — both communicate "this player is fully confident" but they communicate it differently. Same for the negatives — "-4" felt severe in a way that "0%" might not.

If after seeing it you want to revert to integers, that's a valid decision. We can swap the display layer back in 30 minutes. But ship the change first, evaluate against real screens, and decide from there.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Smart instinct.
Smart instinct. The leaderboard going 100% defenders is a real product issue, and fixing it now is cheaper than fixing it after the percentage display has been wrapped around the broken numbers. Let me think through it carefully because there's more than one way to be wrong here.

What's actually happening (let me show you the math):

Under the old algorithm, a defender's confidence sources per match were:

Clean sheet: +1 (non-big) or +2 (big)
Assist (rare): +2 / +3
Goal (rarer): +2 / +3
Blank: -1 (non-big) or -1 (big)
Under DefCon, the same defender now has a new source firing most weeks:

DefCon: +1 every match they hit threshold
For an Arsenal or Bournemouth defender, hitting DefCon threshold is routine — they're literally on the pitch defending for 90 minutes. Estimating loosely, a regular starter probably hits threshold 60-80% of matches. That's adding +0.6 to +0.8 per match to defenders' confidence floor.

For a midfielder like Caicedo, threshold is harder (12 vs 10) and not all midfielders are defensive. Maybe 30-50% of matches.

For forwards, almost never. Haaland's defensive_contribution is rarely above 5. Threshold is 12.

So DefCon as currently calibrated is essentially "defenders get a steady +1 most matches." Combined with the existing CS bonus (+1 or +2), defenders are now compounding two sources of regular positive confidence. That's why the leaderboard cleared out attackers and filled with defenders.

Five real ways to calibrate, in order of surgical-to-aggressive:

Option 1: Reduce DefCon to +0.5 (or ½) Change the reward from +1 to +0.5. Stops the accumulation from being so steep. Problem: introduces fractional values to an integer scoring system. We'd have to round somewhere, which means some matches register +1 and some register 0 in irritating patterns. Not recommended.

Option 2: DefCon only fires on threshold + something extra Make DefCon harder to trigger. Examples:

Threshold + 50% (DEF needs 15 actions instead of 10)
Threshold + a clean sheet (only counts if team also kept a CS)
Top-N players per match (e.g., only the top 5 DefCon performers across the league each gameweek get the bonus)
The "threshold + 50%" version is the cleanest. It still rewards DefCon but only for exceptional defensive output, not "did your job." Effectively reframes DefCon from "consistency reward" to "standout defensive performance." Closer to MOTM-style than Performance-style.

Option 3: Make DefCon +1 for MID/FWD only, exclude DEF entirely The reasoning: defenders already have CS as their dedicated defensive reward. Stacking DefCon on top of CS for defenders is "double-rewarding the same kind of contribution." For midfielders and forwards, who don't have CS as a path, DefCon is genuinely additive — it gives them a route to confidence they didn't have before.

This would directly address the leaderboard issue (defenders no longer compound CS + DefCon) while preserving DefCon's value for the players who needed it most (defensive midfielders like Caicedo, who you cited earlier).

Option 4: DefCon prevents Blank but doesn't add positive points Change the rule from "+1 if threshold met" to "0 (no penalty) if threshold met, instead of Blank's -1/-2." DefCon becomes a floor signal — "you tried hard, no penalty" — but doesn't actively add to confidence. A player who only hits DefCon stays flat that week instead of climbing.

This is the gentlest possible interpretation. Defenders no longer get a regular +1 lift from DefCon, but they're also not punished for grinding losses. The metric becomes more conservative.

Option 5: Position-weighted DefCon Different DefCon rewards by position:

DEF: +0.5 (small bonus on top of CS)
MID: +1 (full bonus, since they don't have CS)
FWD: +1 (full bonus, encourages tracking back)
Solves the issue surgically but introduces fractional values for DEF (same problem as Option 1).

My recommendation: Option 3 (DefCon for MID/FWD only).

Here's why:

It addresses the actual problem. Defenders compounding CS + DefCon was the cause; removing the compound effect is the fix.
It preserves DefCon's product purpose. The reason we added DefCon was to surface defensive midfielders and hardworking attackers who lacked a path to confidence. Caicedo, Rodri, defensive-minded midfielders. They keep their full +1.
It's clean conceptually. "DefCon is for outfielders who don't have a clean sheet path." Easy to explain in the algorithm doc.
It's surgical. One position-condition change. Doesn't introduce fractional values, doesn't add complexity.
Side effect: defenders' values come down meaningfully across the board. Your team % might drop from 50.6% back to ~42-45%. That's fine — the previous lift was partly artifact, not signal.

Caicedo and Rodri stay where they are. Your Bournemouth defenders (Senesi, Hill) come back to the pack. The leaderboard rebalances toward something like 6 DEF / 3 MID / 1 FWD, which is more useful for the user.

Option 2 (threshold + 50%) is my fallback recommendation if Option 3 feels wrong. It's a "make DefCon harder to trigger" approach that keeps the rule applying to all positions but raises the bar to something genuinely exceptional. Slightly cleaner conceptually because it doesn't single out a position, but it loses the simplicity of "DefCon is for non-DEF outfielders."

Tap your answer when you're ready.

A note on what I'd watch for after the change lands: regardless of which option you pick, the test isn't "is the leaderboard 60/40 instead of 100/0?" — that's a downstream signal. The real test is "does the leaderboard now show players whose confidence values you'd actually use to make decisions?" A good leaderboard surfaces players you might pick. A bad leaderboard surfaces players who happen to score highly on the metric but aren't useful FPL choices. We'll evaluate against that real-world test, not against a position-quota.

You said: Q: How do you want to calibrate DefCon?
Q: How do you want to calibrate DefCon?
A: Option 3 — DefCon for MID/FWD only (recommended)

A note for you, the human:

When the recompute lands, the most important check isn't the math — it's the leaderboard composition. Look at the top 10. Ask yourself: "if I had no other information and had to pick a captain from this list, would I be choosing from a useful pool?"

A useful pool has variety: a couple of in-form forwards (the actual captain candidates), some elite midfielders (Salah, Saka, Bruno-tier), some defenders (set-piece threats like Trent or Saliba). That's what an FPL manager scans before captaining.

A bad pool would be either (a) still 100% defenders or (b) 100% forwards or some other monoculture. If you see something extreme, the calibration is still off and we'll need to revisit.

Claude responded: Stop.
Stop. I want to make absolutely sure we're aligned before we send anything to Claude Code, because what you just described is meaningfully different from the prompt I drafted.

Let me play back what I think you're saying, and you tell me if I have it right.

Your rule:

"DefCon is a fallback. It applies only when the player would otherwise blank. If they did anything else positive (assist, goal, clean sheet, MOTM), DefCon doesn't add anything."

So the resolution would be:

What the player did Result
Goal (or 2+ assists) → MOTM MOTM points only, DefCon ignored
1 assist → Performance Performance points only, DefCon ignored
Clean sheet (DEF) → CS CS points only, DefCon ignored
Goal + CS (DEF stacking) MOTM + CS, DefCon ignored
Assist + CS (DEF stacking) Assist + CS, DefCon ignored
Nothing positive, but DefCon threshold met +1 (DefCon prevents the Blank)
Nothing positive, DefCon not met Blank penalty (-1 / -2)
Is that the rule? If yes, this is actually a really good simplification, and it cleanly solves the leaderboard issue from a different angle than my Option 3.

Here's what changes in your version vs. the prompt I drafted:

My prompt (Option 3): DefCon for MID/FWD only. Defenders excluded entirely. But for MID/FWD, DefCon could stack with Performance ("Performance + DefCon" = +3).

Your rule: DefCon for all outfield positions (DEF, MID, FWD). But it never stacks with anything. It's purely a Blank-prevention mechanic.

Your rule is better than my Option 3 in two important ways:

Conceptually cleaner. "DefCon is a floor signal — if you contributed defensively, you don't get punished for not scoring." That's a one-sentence explanation. My Option 3 required explaining why defenders are special-cased and why MID/FWD get a stack but DEF doesn't.
Calibration is naturally self-limiting. Under your rule, DefCon can only fire when no other event fired. Most defender matches will have either a clean sheet or a blank. DefCon only kicks in on the blank-without-CS matches — those games where the team conceded but the individual defender personally worked hard. Way fewer DefCon firings overall, no compounding with CS.
Caicedo (MID) example: in his match history I saw earlier, GW10 was "Performance + DefCon" giving +3. Under your rule, GW10 becomes just "Performance" giving +2. That's a meaningful reduction. He still gets DefCon credit on pure-blank weeks (GW1, GW17, GW28 etc.) — those stay +1. So the metric becomes more conservative but still distinguishes him from "did literally nothing" players.
What it looks like in practice for your team:

Senesi/Hill/Struijk (DEF, mostly blanks): they hit DefCon often, but on weeks they got CS, they lose the DefCon stack. Confidence drops moderately from current.
Caicedo/Rodri-style MIDs: lose the DefCon stack on Performance weeks but keep the DefCon-only weeks. Net moderate drop.
Forwards: mostly unchanged because forwards rarely hit DefCon threshold anyway.
Pure attackers (Salah, Haaland, Saka): completely unchanged. They never had DefCon firing for them in any meaningful way.
The leaderboard rebalances because defenders no longer compound CS + DefCon, and midfielders no longer compound Performance + DefCon. Both still get DefCon, but only as a substitute for Blank, never as an addition to a positive event.

Two questions before I rewrite the prompt:

Does GK still get excluded entirely? GK gets 0 points for DefCon in real FPL, and your earlier choice was "DefCon never fires for GK regardless of value." I'd keep that — even if a sweeper-keeper somehow racks up tackles, DefCon shouldn't apply. Confirm yes/no.
Edge case — DEF with DefCon + no CS + no goals/assists: under your rule, this defender gets +1 (DefCon prevented Blank). But what if their team conceded 4 goals and they were the worst defender on the pitch — yet they still racked up 10 tackles because they were chasing the ball constantly? That's a tactical artifact (bad defenders accumulate tackles by being out of position). The rule rewards them with +1 instead of -1. Acceptable tradeoff? I'd say yes — we have no way to distinguish "good defensive work" from "desperate defensive work" from raw stats, and erring toward "they tried" is more humane than guessing intent.
Tap your two answers. Once confirmed, I'll scrap the prompt I drafted and write a fresh one for this rule. Don't send the old prompt — it's wrong for what you actually want.

Worth flagging: this is a more elegant rule than what I proposed. I should have suggested it earlier. The "DefCon as a floor only" interpretation is closer to how FPL itself thinks about it (DefCon awards 2 points absolute, not a stacked bonus on top of goals/assists), and it's easier to explain to users. Good call surfacing it before we shipped the wrong calibration.

A: Yes — that's exactly the rule

A: Yes — GK never gets DefCon regardless of value

Claude responded: Locked in.

The rule is now clean: DefCon is a Blank-prevention floor for outfield players. It never stacks. It only fires when no other positive event fires.

Here's the corrected prompt — paste this into Claude Code (NOT the previous one):

DefCon recalibration. Replace the current rule with a cleaner formulation.

New rule: DefCon is a fallback floor for outfield players. When a player meets the position-specific threshold, they earn +1 only if no other positive event fired in that match. DefCon never stacks with goals, assists, or clean sheets. If any positive event fires, DefCon is silent. If the player would have blanked, DefCon converts that blank into +1.

The product reasoning: DefCon's purpose is to prevent unfair Blank penalties for hardworking outfielders. Once a player has any other positive contribution, they don't need additional credit for defensive work — the existing event already proves they contributed.

Position scope: DEF, MID, FWD. GK excluded entirely.

Step A — Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
§3 Definitions: update the DefCon entry. Replace existing language with: "DefCon (Defensive Contribution) — defensive_contribution field from FPL match history meets or exceeds the position-specific threshold (DEF: 10, MID: 12, FWD: 12). Applies only as a Blank substitute: if any other positive event (goal, assist, clean sheet) fired in the same match, DefCon is silent. Not applicable to GK."
§4 Scoring table: the DefCon row stays at +1/+1 flat for DEF/MID/FWD. Update the footnote to: "DefCon only fires when no other positive event fired in the match. It is a Blank substitute, not an additive bonus. The +1 reward is flat regardless of opponent because hitting the threshold is an absolute count of defensive actions."
§5 Per-match resolution: rewrite both paths to put DefCon last, after Blank check: MID/FWD:
MOTM performance (1+ goals OR 2+ assists) → MOTM points, done
Performance (1 assist, 0 goals) → Performance points, done
DefCon threshold met (no goals, no assists) → +1, done
True blank (0 goals, 0 assists, DefCon not met) → blank penalty
GK/DEF:
Assist (treated as MOTM) → assist points + Clean Sheet if applicable, done
Goal (no assist) → MOTM points + Clean Sheet if applicable, done
Clean sheet only (no G/A) → CS points, done
DefCon threshold met (no G/A, no CS, DEF only — never GK) → +1, done
True blank (no positive events) → blank penalty
Critical: in both paths, DefCon is now in a mutually exclusive position with positive events. If MOTM/Performance/CS fired, the resolution returns before reaching the DefCon check. The reason string never contains "+ DefCon" — it's standalone or absent.
§6 Fatigue: unchanged. DefCon still doesn't increment MOTM counter and never fires fatigue.
§8 Worked examples — substantial cleanup needed:
EX-16 (MID DefCon-only, no other events): unchanged. Expected delta=+1, reason="DefCon vs non-big team" (or vs big team). Confirms DefCon prevents Blank.
EX-17 (MID with DefCon below threshold, no other events): unchanged. Blank penalty fires.
EX-18 (MID with 1 assist + DefCon met): CHANGE. Old expected +3 with "Performance + DefCon" stack. New expected delta=+1, reason="Performance vs non-big team" (or +2 vs big). DefCon is silent because Performance fired.
EX-19 (DEF with CS + DefCon met): CHANGE. Old expected stack. New expected just CS bonus, no DefCon. delta=+1, reason="Clean sheet vs non-big team".
EX-19b: NEW. DEF with defensive_contribution = 12, 0 G/A, no CS, vs non-big. Expected delta=+1, reason="DefCon vs non-big team". Confirms DefCon prevents Blank for defenders too.
EX-19c: NEW. DEF with 1 assist + defensive_contribution = 15 vs non-big. Expected delta=+2, reason="Assist vs non-big team (MOTM)". DefCon silent because Assist fired.
EX-20 (MID with MOTM + DefCon met): unchanged. MOTM only. DefCon was already absorbed in the old rule and still is.
EX-21 (GK with high defensive_contribution, no other events): unchanged. Blank penalty fires regardless of DefCon value (GK excluded entirely).
EX-22 (DefCon below threshold): unchanged.
EX-23 (DefCon exactly at threshold, no other events): unchanged.
PROP-04 (DefCon never increments motmCounter): unchanged.
§10 Pseudocode: rewrite both resolveMidFwd and resolveGkDef to reflect the new mutually-exclusive resolution order. DefCon is the second-to-last branch (before Blank). No "+= DefCon" anywhere — it's a standalone branch.
Show me the diff before proceeding.

Step B — Update tests

Add EX-19b, EX-19c.
Audit any test that asserts a stacked reason like "Performance vs ... + DefCon" or "Clean sheet vs ... + DefCon" — these must now expect non-stacked reasons. Update each.
Run, confirm tests fail at the right places. Show me the failing output (these failures are validating that the old stacking behavior no longer happens).
Step C — Update implementation
src/lib/confidence/calculator.ts:
Rewrite resolveMidFwd so DefCon is in a case 3 branch, only reached when MOTM/Performance both didn't fire.
Rewrite resolveGkDef so DefCon is in a case 4 branch, only reached when MOTM-via-assist/MOTM-via-goal/CS all didn't fire. Also enforce GK exclusion (DefCon never returns true for GK).
Update DEFCON_THRESHOLD if needed; keep { GK: null, DEF: 10, MID: 12, FWD: 12 }. The null for GK signals "never applies." DEF stays at 10 because DEF can earn DefCon when no CS/goal/assist fires.
Update isDefConThresholdMet to enforce that DEF/MID/FWD with null threshold returns false. (Just GK in practice.)
Reason strings: never produce a compound "X + DefCon" reason anymore. The reason is either describing what fired (MOTM/Performance/CS/Blank) or just "DefCon vs non-big team" / "DefCon vs big team" if DefCon was the only thing.
Run all tests, confirm green.
Step D — Recompute and verify
Run npx tsx scripts/run-sync.ts.

This rule is genuinely simpler than what we had. That's a sign of good metric design — when the rule feels obvious in retrospect, it's usually right.

One observation worth flagging before Step B starts: the §5 inline example replacements you listed are doing useful documentation work. before I send that I have a question. Is the change we made earlier in terms of build still inflating defenders....where we flatten the blank to -1? do you think we should revert to -2 for non-big team and -1 for big team. Why I am asking is because of defcon fallback. what do you think?

Apr 26
Claude responded: Genuinely good question, and I think you're right to surface it.
Genuinely good question, and I think you're right to surface it. Let me think through it carefully because the interaction between the two rules is more subtle than it first appears.

Recap of the history:

We made the GK/DEF blank symmetric (−1 for both big and non-big) back in Step 5 because the smoke test showed Pickford clamped at −5 across 34 matches with Everton's 32% clean sheet rate. The math at the time: defenders need a 67% CS rate just to break even against the asymmetric blank penalty (−1 big / −2 non-big). That's only achievable for top-6 keepers. Mid-table defenders were guaranteed to floor.

The Option C recalibration said: bring GK/DEF non-big blank from −2 to −1, so break-even drops to a 50% CS rate. Mid-table defenders become "negative but not floored."

What changed since then:

DefCon-as-fallback now adds another upward force on defenders. A defender who blanks but hits DefCon threshold gets +1 instead of the blank penalty. This means:

Old world (pre-DefCon, post-Option-C): Defender blanks vs Burnley → −1
New world (DefCon-as-fallback): Defender blanks vs Burnley + hits DefCon → +1 (a 2-point swing)
Old world (pre-Option-C, original algorithm): Same situation → −2
So your instinct is correct: DefCon-as-fallback is now doing the work that Option C was originally compensating for. The Pickford problem (mid-table keeper guaranteed to floor) is being solved twice — once by softening the blank penalty, and once by giving an alternate path to positive points.

Now — should we revert blanks to asymmetric (−1 big / −2 non-big)?

Let me work through what each player archetype looks like under each combination:

Player archetype 1: Top-6 defender (Saliba, Gabriel, Van Dijk)

High CS rate, high DefCon rate.
Their confidence is dominated by CS stacking, with DefCon-fallback rarely firing.
Either rule produces similar results for them. Not the deciding case.
Player archetype 2: Mid-table defender (Senesi, Hill, your Bournemouth players)

Moderate CS rate (~30-40%), moderate-to-high DefCon rate (~50-70% — they're working hard at a struggling team).
Most matches: blank, but with DefCon firing → +1 instead of penalty.
Under current rule (−1 blank, no DefCon trigger): blanks → −1. With ~50% DefCon coverage on those blanks, half become +1, half stay −1. Net per blank-week: ~0.
Under reverted rule (−2 blank, no DefCon trigger): blanks → −2. With ~50% DefCon coverage, half become +1, half stay −2. Net per blank-week: ~−0.5.
The reverted rule produces a meaningful downward force on mid-table defenders who don't hit DefCon every week.
Player archetype 3: Worst-team keeper (Pickford-style at Everton, or Burnley's keeper)

Low CS rate (~30%), low DefCon-applicable (GK excluded entirely from DefCon).
Under current rule: blanks → −1. Floor at −4 reached reasonably.
Under reverted rule: blanks → −2. Floor at −4 reached much faster. GKs at the worst clubs get floored quickly with no DefCon escape.
This is the case that breaks the reverted rule. GKs have no DefCon fallback because GK is excluded.
The conclusion:

Reverting to asymmetric blanks would penalize the player group that has no DefCon escape: goalkeepers. They'd collapse to the floor as fast or faster than they did pre-Option-C, and DefCon-as-fallback gives them no way out.

However — your underlying intuition is still onto something. The current calibration is over-rewarding mid-table outfield defenders because they get DefCon-as-fallback for free. The fix isn't reverting blanks for everyone, though.

Three options that better address what you're noticing:

Option X — Keep the symmetric blank for GK only, asymmetric for DEF/MID/FWD.

GK blank: −1 / −1 (current symmetric, protects worst-team keepers)
DEF/MID/FWD blank: −1 / −2 (revert to original asymmetric)
DefCon-as-fallback continues to work where applicable, providing escape for outfielders only.
Effect: Mid-table outfield defenders who hit DefCon weekly still climb (correctly rewarded for effort). Mid-table outfield defenders who don't hit DefCon weekly fall faster (correctly penalized for being passengers). Goalkeepers stay protected by symmetric clamp.
This is probably what you actually want.
Option Y — Keep current symmetric blanks for everyone, accept that DefCon-as-fallback is doing some compensation work.

The current calibration. Over-rewards mid-table outfielders slightly but is internally consistent.
Pickford-style goalkeepers protected.
Worth accepting if you find the leaderboard composition feels right after this latest recalibration.
Option Z — Revert blanks fully (−1/−2 for everyone), trust DefCon to handle outfielders, accept GK pain.

Goalkeepers floor faster again. Pickford problem partially returns.
Outfield defenders meaningfully recalibrated.
I don't recommend this — we'd be undoing the GK fix that motivated Option C originally.
My recommendation: do nothing yet, see the recompute first.

Here's why: the DefCon recalibration we just sent (DefCon-as-fallback only) hasn't recomputed yet. We don't actually know what the leaderboard looks like with DefCon-as-fallback alone. Maybe it rebalances meaningfully without any blank-rule change. Maybe defenders drop enough that the leaderboard shows the position mix you want.

If after seeing the new numbers the leaderboard still feels too defender-heavy, then we apply Option X as a second targeted calibration. If the leaderboard feels right, we don't touch the blank rule.

The principle: change one thing, see the effect, decide whether to change another. Stacking calibrations on top of each other before measuring makes it impossible to know which one did what.

Tap your answer. The "wait and see" path is the disciplined one but I want to be honest: there's no wrong answer here. Option X is also defensible if you have strong intuition that it's the right calibration. Forcing you to wait for data when your gut is already speaking can be its own form of overcaution.

Worth knowing: if you pick "wait," and after the recompute we do apply Option X, that's another full docs-tests-code-recompute cycle on top of the one already in flight. Two extra rounds of work. If your gut already says "the asymmetric blank is better with DefCon-as-fallback," picking Option X now ships both changes in one cycle.

Your call.

A few specific things worth calling out before Step C:

For your team specifically: Senesi, Hill, Struijk, O'Reilly, Van Hecke were all at +5 under v1 because of CS+DefCon stacking on most weeks. Apr 26
Claude responded: Stop.
Stop. The diagnostic is sharp, but the proposed fix gets Option X wrong. Let me be direct because this matters.

The honest assessment Claude wrote is excellent. The diagnosis is correct: the leaderboard imbalance is structural, caused by the clean sheet mechanism giving defenders a positive-event path that midfielders and forwards don't have. That's the right insight, and it's a more important framing than I had.

But the recommendation — "make MID/FWD blanks vs big hurt less" — is solving the wrong problem. Let me explain why, then propose what I think actually addresses the structural issue.

Why Claude's Option X recommendation is wrong:

The recommendation says: reduce MID/FWD blank penalty to make them fall slower, compensating for their missing CS path.

But softer blanks for attackers don't give them more positive events — they just slow the descent. A striker who blanks 10 weeks in a row still ends up at the floor; he just takes 8 weeks to get there instead of 5. The leaderboard top is determined by who's at +5, not who's at -2 vs -3 mid-table. Softer blanks for MID/FWD don't change leaderboard composition at all.

Worse: it would actively distort the metric. A striker who's blanked 6 weeks straight should be at the floor — that's the metric working correctly. Softening that punishes the signal, not the imbalance.

What's actually causing the imbalance:

Read Claude's own observation: "Bournemouth had 16 CS by GW33, Crystal Palace similar." Defenders at those teams get 16 standalone positive events purely from team-level outcomes. A forward at the same team has to personally score or assist to get a positive event. The structural asymmetry isn't about how hard players fall — it's about how often they rise.

There are really only three honest fixes for this structural asymmetry, and I want to walk through them carefully:

Real Option A: Reduce CS reward. Currently CS = +1 (non-big) / +2 (big). Reduce to +1 flat for both, OR to +0 (CS gives no Confidence — only blank prevention). This directly attacks the over-reward, but it breaks the metric in a different way: defenders on great teams (Saliba, Van Dijk) genuinely deserve recognition for sustained defensive excellence. Removing CS would make them invisible.

Real Option B: Boost MID/FWD positive-event values. Currently Performance = +1 / +2, MOTM = +2 / +3. Increase to give attackers more headroom. E.g., Performance = +2 / +3, MOTM = +3 / +4. Now a striker who scores once a fortnight reaches +5 in a similar timeframe to a defender's CS pace.

This is the most defensible fix because it addresses the asymmetry from the positive side — giving MID/FWD a faster path up, rather than slowing the negative side for either position. But it changes calibration meaningfully across the metric.

Real Option C: Position-aware leaderboards as a UX layer, not an algorithm change. Accept that the metric correctly identifies "stable defensive contributors" as the highest-confidence players. Fix the leaderboard at the display layer by splitting it into "Top Defenders / Top Midfielders / Top Forwards" tabs. The top forward might only be at +3, but they're the top forward at +3 and that's useful information.

This option is honest about the metric (we don't pretend defenders aren't dominating) but it makes the UI useful for captain decisions (which mostly involve attackers and attacking midfielders).

My honest recommendation: Option C.

I'm changing my position from earlier. I previously suggested calibrating DefCon harder; I now think we shouldn't keep iterating on the algorithm. Three reasons:

The metric is doing what it should. Defenders at top-6 teams are genuinely the most reliable confidence picks. The metric isn't lying — it's reflecting structural reality.
We've calibrated three times in two days. Range change, DefCon-with-stacking, DefCon-as-fallback. Each one was justified, but we're at risk of fitting the algorithm to specific leaderboard outcomes rather than to genuine signal. Another calibration round would be optimization theater.
The user's actual decision flow needs UX support, not algorithm support. When you open the app to pick a captain, you don't want a leaderboard sorted by "highest confidence" — you want to see your top 3 attacking options. A position-tabbed leaderboard delivers that. An algorithm tweak that produces a "balanced" leaderboard delivers worse signal in service of cosmetic balance.
My counter-recommendation: ship the DefCon v2 sign-off as-is. Don't apply Option X. Add "Position-aware leaderboard" as the first item in Step 12 polish. Use the app for a few captain decisions with the tabbed view in mind. If after using it the metric still feels off in a way that requires algorithm changes, we revisit with usage evidence rather than against screenshots.

Tap your answer when you're ready.

A note on what I want to flag before you decide: my reversal here is real and I want to be transparent about it. Earlier in this build I confidently invented the Salah-to-Saudi story. I confidently theorized about a different Leeds Salah. I confidently agreed that DefCon should fix the leaderboard. Each time I had to be course-corrected by you against actual data.

I'm noticing a pattern in myself: when faced with a metric calibration question, I default to "let's calibrate harder" because it feels like progress. But sometimes the right answer is "the metric is fine, the UI needs to do more work." The discipline of preferring UX fixes over algorithm fixes is harder for me to apply consistently than I'd like.

Pick whatever feels right. If you trust your gut more than my newest opinion, I'll back you up. But I want you to have my honest current read, which is: stop changing the algorithm, fix the leaderboard at the display layer.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Good question to pin down before deciding.
Good question to pin down before deciding. Let me show you exactly what's happening, because the rules have shifted twice and it's worth being concrete.

Yes, MID and FWD are still getting blank penalties. Here's the precise current state:

Current blank penalties for MID/FWD (after all recent changes):

Position vs Big team vs Non-big team
MID −1 −2
FWD −1 −2
These are unchanged from the original algorithm. We never touched MID/FWD blanks during any recalibration. Option C (the recalibration we did in Step 5) only changed GK/DEF blanks from asymmetric to symmetric. MID/FWD's blank penalties have been the same since day one.

Current blank penalties for GK/DEF (after Option C in Step 5):

Position vs Big team vs Non-big team
GK −1 −1
DEF −1 −1
Symmetric, both at −1.

DefCon as fallback (just shipped):

If a MID or FWD blanks but hits DefCon threshold (12 actions), they get +1 instead of the blank penalty. Same for DEF (with threshold 10). GK is excluded entirely from DefCon — they always get the blank penalty if they don't have a clean sheet.

So the actual question I think you're asking:

"Is MID/FWD's situation worse than DEF's right now?"

Yes. Specifically:

A MID who blanks vs Burnley without hitting DefCon threshold → −2 (falls fast)
A DEF who blanks vs Burnley without hitting DefCon threshold → −1 (falls slow)
A MID who blanks vs Burnley with DefCon threshold → +1 (rises)
A DEF who blanks vs Burnley with DefCon threshold → +1 (rises)
The asymmetry: MIDs/FWDs without DefCon coverage get hit with −2 on non-big blanks while DEFs only get −1. That's the structural penalty asymmetry on top of the structural reward asymmetry (CS for DEF only).

Claude's recommendation in the sign-off was to flatten MID/FWD blanks to symmetric (−1/−1), matching what we did for DEF. My pushback was that "softer blanks doesn't help leaderboard composition because the issue is positive events, not negative ones."

But re-reading your question, I think you're surfacing something important I dismissed too quickly. Let me reconsider.

If MID/FWD blanks are currently asymmetric (−1/−2) while DEF/GK blanks are symmetric (−1/−1), then yes — there's a hidden penalty asymmetry that we created when we did Option C. We softened DEF/GK blanks to "fix Pickford" but never applied the same softening to MID/FWD. So now:

A defender who blanks vs Burnley falls 1 point.
A midfielder who blanks vs Burnley falls 2 points.
That's structurally inconsistent. We could justify it pre-DefCon ("MID/FWD have higher reward potential via MOTM, so they should have higher penalty"), but with DefCon-as-fallback now, MID/FWD without DefCon coverage are doubly disadvantaged: harder positive path AND harder negative path.

Updated honest recommendation:

You're right to surface this. I was wrong to dismiss Claude's Option X recommendation. The real issue isn't "MID/FWD need softer blanks to compete with defenders on the leaderboard" (that was my framing and it's wrong). The real issue is "we have an inconsistent blank penalty across positions and that's a hidden asymmetry."

The cleanest fix isn't about leaderboard composition — it's about internal consistency. All blanks should follow the same rule. Three options:

Option α — Symmetric blanks for all positions: −1 / −1. Match what we did for GK/DEF. Simplest. Slightly softens the metric for MID/FWD.

Option β — Asymmetric blanks for all positions: −1 / −2. Revert Option C. Goalkeepers go back to flooring fast (Pickford problem returns). Don't do this.

Option γ — Keep asymmetric, but only for positions without DefCon coverage = nobody. Effectively the same as Option α now that DefCon is universal for outfielders. Internal consistency by deduction.

So Option α is the right call for internal consistency — if not for leaderboard balance.

Will it materially change leaderboard composition? Probably not much. As I argued before, slowing the descent doesn't change who's at the top. But it'll make the metric internally honest: same event has the same consequence regardless of position.

Tap your answer.

A note on my reversal: I want to flag that I just changed my recommendation again within two messages, which is the same pattern I've been doing — confidently arguing a position, then conceding when you press. I want to be honest that "Option α for internal consistency" feels right to me now, but I had to be prompted into it by your question. If your question was a leading question that pulled me toward what you already wanted, that's fine — your gut is good. If it wasn't, you should weight my recommendation accordingly. Either way, the consistency argument is the strongest argument here, and it doesn't depend on leaderboard outcomes.

You said: Q: How do you want to handle the MID/FWD blank penalty?
Q: How do you want to handle the MID/FWD blank penalty?
A: I think penalty should be asymmetric doe all. But we should half the penalty>example not -1/-2 but -0.5/-1...what do you think?

Apr 26
Claude responded: Hold on — let me think before answering, because what you're proposing is a meaningful design shift and I want to be honest about both the merits and the costs.
Hold on — let me think before answering, because what you're proposing is a meaningful design shift and I want to be honest about both the merits and the costs.

What you're proposing:

Keep the asymmetric structure (vs big teams hurts less than vs non-big teams), but halve all blank penalties:

Position vs Big vs Non-big
GK −0.5 −1
DEF −0.5 −1
MID −0.5 −1
FWD −0.5 −1
Effectively: blanks become softer overall, asymmetry is preserved, and every position is treated identically.

The genuine merits:

The asymmetric framing has real signal. Blanking against Burnley is more disappointing than blanking against City — managers genuinely think this way. A striker who fails to convert against a weak defense is exposing real form issues; a striker who couldn't break down City is just facing the world's best center-backs. Preserving asymmetry preserves that signal.

Halving softens the descent across the board. Players at the floor become rarer. Recovery feels more achievable. The metric becomes more forgiving overall.

Now for the honest cost — and this is where I want to push back hard:

Fractional values are a much bigger architectural change than they appear. Up to this point, every single Confidence value, every single delta, every single piece of UI has been an integer. The entire mental model is integer-based:

Per-match deltas are integers (+3, −1, +2)
Cumulative confidence is an integer (−4 to +5)
The clamp uses integers
The fatigue counter is integer
All 341 unit tests assume integer math
Property tests rely on integer arithmetic
The percentage display uses the integer scale as input
Introducing −0.5 means: Confidence is no longer an integer. Three players at "−0.5" are technically the same value as a player at "0" minus a player at "−1" plus another at "0.5", which means once you mix fractional deltas the interim values can be 1.5, 2.5, etc. The whole metric becomes continuous-with-integer-pretensions, which is the worst of both worlds.

There are also UI implications:

The match history card currently shows "+2" or "−1". Now it'd show "+0.5" or "−1.5". Visually heavier, harder to scan.
The slider has tick marks at every integer. Fractional values fall between ticks.
The percentage formula gets messier. (−4.5 + 4) / 9 × 100 = 5.5%. Suddenly we have half-percents to display.
Tabular numerics with decimals look different — they're wider, which breaks the table column widths we've spent design effort getting right.
Beyond UI, there's a subtle calibration problem: under the new rule, a defender who blanks vs Burnley once gets −1. Same defender blanks 3 times in a row → −3 (fine, same as today's 3 × −1). But under the proposed −0.5/−1 split, a defender alternating big/non-big blanks would oscillate at fractional intermediate values. Recovery from a bad run requires fractional-delta wins from MOTMs and CSes, which the algorithm doesn't currently produce.

We'd be adding fractional inputs (negative penalties) without adding fractional outputs (positive events stay at +1, +2, +3). That's a one-sided change that distorts the metric's symmetry — players accumulate negative slowly and positive quickly, which is the opposite of what feels natural.

Two cleaner alternatives that achieve the same product goal (softer descent + asymmetry):

Alternative 1: Asymmetric integer blanks, slightly different shape.

Position vs Big vs Non-big
All 0 −1
Blanking against a big team has no penalty (0). Blanking against a non-big team is −1. Stays integer. Preserves asymmetry. Softer overall than current. Internal consistency across positions.

Alternative 2: Keep current asymmetric but apply Option α for consistency.

Position vs Big vs Non-big
All −1 −2
Just normalize all positions to the same asymmetric rule. Pickford goes back to flooring (the original problem we fixed with Option C). DefCon-as-fallback partially compensates for outfielders. GK unprotected.

Alternative 3: Asymmetric integer with a different magnitude.

Position vs Big vs Non-big
All −1 −1
Symmetric blanks across the board, all at −1. Loses the "blanking vs Burnley is worse" signal but stays integer and consistent.

My honest recommendation:

If you want softer-and-asymmetric, go with Alternative 1: 0 / −1. It captures your underlying intuition (blanking against big teams shouldn't hurt; blanking against weaker teams should) while preserving integer values and not requiring schema/test/UI rework.

A defender who plays Liverpool and gets nothing offensively isn't really being "punished" under this rule — they kept their team in a hard match. A defender who plays Burnley and blanks (no CS, no contribution) is genuinely underperforming for the level of opposition. The asymmetry feels justified.

If you really want fractional values, that's a legitimate metric design choice but it's a substantial implementation effort — roughly equivalent to one of the major build steps. Not impossible, just real work, and worth doing only if you have strong conviction it's the right call.

Tap your answer when ready.

A note before you decide: I want to be transparent that my pushback against fractional values is real, not theatrical. We've made three calibration changes already and each one has had a clean cost-benefit because they were all integer-preserving. Going fractional would meaningfully expand the surface area of changes (every test, every component, every percentage formula). It's not "no" — it's "this is a big change that should be considered carefully against alternatives that achieve similar goals."

If after reviewing the alternatives you still feel −0.5/−1 is the right call, tell me why and I'll write the prompt for it. Your conviction matters here.

You said: Q: How do you want to proceed?
Q: How do you want to proceed?
A: the thing is, playing a big team is usually a turning point for confidence shift in real life...how do I model this without causing output issue

Apr 26
Claude responded: You've identified something genuinely important about football, and I think it deserves real consideration before we just pick from the menu I gave you.
You've identified something genuinely important about football, and I think it deserves real consideration before we just pick from the menu I gave you.

Let me make sure I understand what you're saying. In real-world FPL psychology:

A player who's been struggling and then comes up against a big team — managers EXPECT them to fail. So when they blank vs Liverpool, it's not surprising and shouldn't hurt their confidence much. But when they blank against Burnley (a "must-perform" fixture), that's where confidence really cracks.

A player who's in form and faces a big team — that's the test. If they perform vs City, confidence solidifies (the +3 MOTM bonus already captures this). If they blank vs City, it might be expected. But if they blank vs Burnley while in form, doubt creeps in.

In other words: the relationship between opponent strength and the meaningfulness of a result depends on the player's current trajectory, not just on the opponent. A blank vs Burnley means different things to a +5 player vs a -3 player.

This is a real signal. And it can't be modeled with just opponent-based asymmetric penalties because the same fixture contributes different meaning to different players.

This is now the deepest design discussion we've had on the metric. Let me lay out three honest paths:

Path 1: Accept that the metric can't fully model this, and stop trying.

The current algorithm is already a meaningful approximation. It captures:

Opposition difficulty (big vs non-big modifier)
Recent form (rolling confidence value with cumulative deltas)
Fatigue (post-streak regression)
It doesn't capture the interaction between the two, but most metrics don't. Sofascore ratings don't. xGI doesn't. Even FPL's own scoring system doesn't. Your metric being "directionally right" is already better than most production tools, and chasing perfect modeling of human psychology might be overfitting.

This path means: pick one of my Alternative options (1, 2, or 3) for internal consistency, ship v1, use it for a few weeks, and see whether the missing signal actually bothers you in practice. Most of the time, managers using a Confidence metric aren't doing the deep psychological analysis we're modeling — they're just looking at "is this player trending up or down."

Path 2: Add a "trajectory-aware" modifier, but keep the math integer.

Modify the blank penalty based on the player's current confidence value, not just opponent type:

Player's current state vs Big vs Non-big
Confidence ≥ +3 (in form) 0 −1
Confidence between −2 and +2 (neutral) −1 −1
Confidence ≤ −3 (struggling) 0 −1
Translation: if you're hot, blanking vs City barely hurts (you're expected to be reliable, City is the test). If you're cold, blanking vs City barely hurts (we already know you're struggling). Blanking vs weaker teams always hurts because that's where managers expect returns.

This roughly captures your intuition. It's piecewise but stays integer. And it has a real-world story you can explain in one sentence: "Blanking against weak teams hurts the most. Blanking against big teams only hurts when you're at your peak and expected to deliver."

Wait — that last clause is wrong. Re-reading my own table, "in form players blank vs City for 0" means the in-form player isn't punished for the City test either. That's not quite right either.

Let me re-think. What is the actual real-world rule you're describing?

Blank vs Burnley → always disappointing (everyone gets penalized)
Blank vs City → depends on whether you "should have done something"
The "should have done something" lens probably depends on:

Are you at home or away? (FPL API has this)
Is this a player whose archetype is "should produce regardless of opponent" (premium attackers)?
Are you currently in form?
This is starting to look like a 4-dimensional input space (player type × form × home/away × opponent strength) which is firmly into "this is no longer a simple metric" territory.

Path 3: Acknowledge this is a v2 problem.

What you've identified is a contextual scoring problem. The current Confidence metric is event-based: every match event has a fixed value. What you're describing is expectation-relative: the value of an event depends on what was expected of the player.

Expectation-relative metrics are a different beast. They typically use:

Player's price tier (premium vs midrange vs budget) as a proxy for expectation
Opponent difficulty rating
Home/away
Possibly fixture difficulty rating from the FPL API
This is essentially the territory that P(Goal) and P(Assist) were designed to capture in your v2 spec. The probability metrics naturally encode "given this player vs this opponent, what's the expected outcome" — which is exactly the framing you're describing.

If we add a third probability metric — call it P(Confidence-Worthy Match) — that captures "did this player do better or worse than expected against this fixture," we'd model exactly the signal you're talking about. But that's a v2-scope addition.

My honest read:

This is a real insight, but it's not solvable by tweaking the −1/−2 blank penalty. The interaction effect you've identified requires modeling player expectations, which the current Confidence metric doesn't do.

I think the right answer is Path 1 + acknowledge Path 3 is the v2 fix:

For now: pick Alternative 1 (0 / −1) or stay with current state. Both are defensible and neither is wrong. The leaderboard issue is a UX problem solvable in Step 12.
For v2: add the trajectory-aware angle as a fourth metric or as a refinement to Confidence. Specifically: a modifier that adjusts blank penalty based on player price tier and recent form. Premium attackers blanking vs City = no penalty. Premium attackers blanking vs Burnley = full penalty. This is the right shape but it's a substantial design and implementation lift.
Add this conversation to v2 docs. The insight you just had — that opponent-relative penalties depend on player expectation — is genuinely valuable. We should capture it as a v2 design seed even if we don't ship it now.
Tap your answer.

A note on what I'm watching: you're at a point in the build where every algorithm refinement has diminishing returns. The first calibration (Option C) fixed an actual broken thing (Pickford floored). The second (DefCon) added a missing dimension. The third (DefCon-as-fallback) corrected an over-reward. We're now into territory where each tweak captures a real insight but at increasing complexity cost.

There's a version of this conversation where we keep finding real insights and never ship. I want to flag that risk gently. The decisions you've made over the last hour — range change, DefCon, DefCon recalibration — are all genuinely good. Adding a fourth deep change might be right, or it might be where we stop and start using the app.

You decide. I'll back whatever you pick.

You said: Q: How do you want to proceed?
Q: How do you want to proceed?
A: Path 1 — stay with current state, file the trajectory insight for v2

Updating the v2 queue now:

ADDED to v2 queue:

20. Expectation-relative Confidence (the "trajectory insight")
    The current Confidence metric is event-based: every match event has a fixed value regardless of context. Real-world FPL psychology is expectation-relative: the meaning of an event depends on what was expected of the player given their archetype, form, and fixture.

Specifically, the user observed: "Playing a big team is usually a turning point for confidence shift in real life." A player blanking vs Burnley feels worse than blanking vs Liverpool because Burnley is a "must-perform" fixture. But the meaningfulness depends on the player's current trajectory — premium attackers in form face different expectations than struggling backups.

The signal can't be modeled by tweaking blank penalty asymmetry alone (we tried). It requires modeling player expectations, which means inputs the current algorithm doesn't use:

Player price tier (FPL now_cost as proxy for expectation level)
Home/away (FPL was_home field, currently ignored)
Fixture difficulty (FPL team_strength fields)
Recent form trajectory (already computed via current confidence value)
Two implementation paths considered:

a) Refine the existing Confidence metric to be trajectory-aware (piecewise blank penalty based on confidence value × opponent strength × player price tier). Risk: piles complexity onto a metric that's currently simple to explain.

b) Add a new probability-style metric specifically for "expected vs actual performance given context." Closer to xG/xA-style modeling. Best built alongside P(Goal)/P(Assist) since they share infrastructure.

Recommendation: bundle with P(Goal)/P(Assist) work in v2. The expected-vs-actual framing is conceptually adjacent and would share most of the infrastructure (price-tier lookup, fixture difficulty inputs, backtesting harness).

Surfaces affected

Match history cards — important nuance: the per-match delta and confidence-after values diverge. Apply this rule:
Delta stays as raw integer (e.g. +2, -1). The delta is per-match contribution to a per-match scoring system; converting it to a percentage-point would imply a per-match percentage, which doesn't exist semantically.
Confidence-after converts to %. The cumulative state is what gets displayed in the percentage scale.
So a card might read: "MOTM vs big team · +3 · 80%" where +3 is the match contribution and 80% is the resulting state.
Trend strip (5-square color strip on player rows) — no change. Strip shows directional pattern, not values.
Defensive clamp on ConfidenceNumber — clamp the integer first (still to [-4, +5]), then convert to %. Out-of-range still produces a logger warning.

Reason strings still describe the underlying event ("MOTM vs big team", "DefCon vs non-big team", "DGW: ...").

Update component tests asserting on integer text — +3 becomes 80%, etc.

Apr 26
Claude responded: This is a good idea, and it directly addresses the trajectory insight from earlier.
This is a good idea, and it directly addresses the trajectory insight from earlier. FDR (Fixture Difficulty Rating) is FPL's own per-fixture difficulty signal — using it would give us per-match expectation calibration without inventing our own model.

Let me think through it carefully because it's a substantial design shift, then propose a model.

What FDR actually is:

FPL's bootstrap-static exposes team strength via several fields. The most useful are:

team.strength — overall (1-5 scale)
team.strength_overall_home / strength_overall_away (more granular)
team.strength_attack_home / strength_attack_away
team.strength_defence_home / strength_defence_away
Per-fixture, the fixtures endpoint includes team_h_difficulty and team_a_difficulty (the FDR you see on the official FPL site, 1-5 where 1 is easy, 5 is brutal).

For our purposes the cleanest input is per-match opponent difficulty, which we can derive by looking up team.strength_defence_home/away for the opponent on the match date. FPL pre-computes this, so we don't need to build a strength model.

The fundamental question: where does FDR plug in?

There are three possible places:

Option 1 — Replace isOpponentBigTeam (the binary big/non-big flag) with per-match FDR.

Currently the metric uses a hard cutoff: opponent is either "big" or "not big." FDR gives a 1-5 scale that's more nuanced. We could replace the binary modifier with a continuous one:

FDR 5 (toughest opponent): big-team modifier
FDR 4: lean toward big-team modifier
FDR 3: neutral
FDR 2: lean toward non-big modifier
FDR 1 (easiest opponent): non-big modifier
This replaces the user-editable Big Teams list with FPL's official difficulty rating. Cleaner, fixture-specific, and no longer requires the user to maintain a Big Teams set.

Option 2 — Add FDR as an additional modifier on top of existing big/non-big logic.

Keep the Big Teams list (user-controlled, your editorial input) but layer FDR on top to capture fixture-specific nuance. E.g., a non-big team away to Liverpool might still get a moderate "tough fixture" boost even though Liverpool wouldn't be in the user's Big Teams list.

Option 3 — Use FDR only for the "expected vs actual" trajectory insight.

Keep the existing big/non-big logic for base scoring, but use FDR to modify the blank penalty specifically. A player blanking against an FDR-5 fixture gets a softer penalty (it was a hard match, blanking is forgivable). A player blanking against an FDR-1 fixture gets a harder penalty (you should have done something).

My honest recommendation: Option 1.

Here's why:

FDR is what FPL managers actually use. Open the official FPL app, look at any player's upcoming fixtures — you see colored difficulty squares (green for FDR 1-2, yellow for 3, red for 4-5). It's the universally understood lens. Your metric using the same lens means it speaks the same language as the rest of the FPL ecosystem.
It eliminates the user-editable Big Teams list. Right now Settings (Step 11) is going to include an editor for Big Teams. With Option 1, that becomes obsolete — FPL's FDR is the source of truth. Less code, less maintenance, less user-facing friction. The user doesn't have to maintain a list that goes stale every season.
It's per-fixture, not per-team. A team's "bigness" varies by venue and by opponent strength on a given date. Liverpool away to City is a different fixture than Liverpool at home to Brentford. Binary big/non-big can't capture that. FDR can.
The trajectory insight from earlier is partially captured. A player blanking vs FDR 5 (toughest) gets a softer penalty than blanking vs FDR 1. That's the "blanking against City means less than blanking against Burnley" intuition, encoded directly.
How to model it concretely:

Keep the existing scoring table structure. Replace the binary big/non-big columns with five FDR-based columns. Or, more cleanly, define a single multiplier per FDR that scales the base reward/penalty:

FDR Modifier on positive events Modifier on blank penalty
5 (hardest) ×1.5 (rewarded extra for performing) ×0.5 (forgiven for blanking)
4 ×1.25 ×0.75
3 (neutral) ×1.0 ×1.0
2 ×0.75 ×1.25
1 (easiest) ×0.5 (less impressive) ×1.5 (worse for blanking)
The base scoring becomes simpler too: just base values without big/non-big distinction:

Performance (1 assist) base: +1
MOTM base: +2
Clean sheet (GK/DEF) base: +1
DefCon (MID/FWD only): +1 (flat, no FDR multiplier — DefCon is consistency, not output)
Blank base: −1
Apply FDR multiplier to get the actual delta. Round to nearest integer.

Worked examples:

Salah scores vs City (FDR 5) → +2 × 1.5 = +3 (same as current MOTM vs big)
Salah scores vs Burnley (FDR 1) → +2 × 0.5 = +1 (current would be +2 — slightly less reward for the easy match)
Pickford keeps CS vs City (FDR 5) → +1 × 1.5 = +1.5 → +2 (current is +2)
Pickford keeps CS vs Burnley (FDR 1) → +1 × 0.5 = +0.5 → +1 (current is +1)
MID blanks vs City (FDR 5) → −1 × 0.5 = −0.5 → 0 (no penalty, captures trajectory insight)
MID blanks vs Burnley (FDR 1) → −1 × 1.5 = −1.5 → −2 (sharper penalty for must-perform blank)
Notice: the rounded values mostly match the current binary system at the extremes (FDR 5 and FDR 1) but introduce nuance at FDR 2-4 where the binary system was crude. That's the point.

The honest tradeoffs:

Complexity bump. Five FDR levels × multiple event types means more cases to test. Worked examples in ALGORITHM.md grow from ~25 to ~40+.
Rounding artifacts. Multiplying integer base values by non-integer multipliers produces fractional intermediate values that need rounding rules. We'd need to define: "round half toward zero" or "round to nearest, ties round to even" or whatever feels most consistent. This is the kind of decision that affects edge cases and needs documenting.
DGW reasoning becomes more complex. Match 1 vs FDR 5, match 2 vs FDR 1 in the same gameweek — the compound reason needs to encode both fixture difficulties.
Recompute needed across all 524 players × 30 gameweeks. The full sync regenerates everything under new rules. We've done this three times already; we know the muscle.
The user loses control over what counts as "big." Some users might prefer to define their own (e.g., excluding Aston Villa, including Newcastle). FPL's FDR is opinionated and not always agreed-upon.
My recommendation: ship Option 1 if you're going to do this. It's the cleanest model and the most aligned with how FPL itself thinks. Don't half-do it with Option 2 (additive) which just compounds complexity without simplifying anything.

But — I want to flag the same caution I flagged before: we've already changed the algorithm three times in two days. This would be a fourth substantial change. Each change has been justified, but at some point the discipline of "use what we have and ship" becomes more valuable than further refinement.

Two specific questions before deciding:

Tap your two answers.

A note before you decide: my honest read is that this is a better model than what we have, but it's also v2-shaped work, not v1-polish work. The right shape isn't "let me sneak this in before Step 11" — it's "this is one of the three biggest improvements we could make in v2, alongside P(Goal)/P(Assist) and pitch view."

If you ship it now, you'll spend probably half a day on docs/tests/code/recompute, then another half-day on visual verification, and the calibration may land in a way that requires further tuning. If you ship v1 with current algorithm and add FDR in v2, you'd plan it properly alongside P(Goal)/P(Assist) which share infrastructure (both need fixture difficulty inputs).

But if your gut says "the current metric isn't trustworthy enough to ship without FDR," that's a real signal and we should listen. Tap your answers and we'll proceed.

Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. FDR-based recalibration is the next change. Before I draft the prompt, two things to settle first — both genuinely affect the implementation and I don't want to invent answers.

1. The rounding rule.

Multiplying integer base values by FDR multipliers (×1.5, ×0.75, etc.) produces fractional intermediates that need rounding. This is the most important detail and needs explicit definition.

Three options:

a) Banker's rounding (round half to even) — +2.5 becomes +2, +3.5 becomes +4. Most statistically neutral over time but produces some unintuitive single-case results.

b) Round half away from zero — +2.5 becomes +3, −2.5 becomes −3. Natural for users (positive becomes more positive, negative becomes more negative). Standard in sports scoring.

c) Truncate toward zero — +2.5 becomes +2, −2.5 becomes −2. Conservative, biases toward smaller magnitudes. Probably not what you want.

I'd recommend (b) — round half away from zero. Matches user intuition. A defender who barely crosses into +1 territory after a CS vs FDR 4 ("2 × 1.25 = 2.5") gets +3, not +2. Symmetrically, a midfielder who barely crosses into −2 territory after a blank vs FDR 1 ("−1 × 1.5 = −1.5") gets −2.

2. What to do with the user-editable Big Teams list.

Right now Settings (Step 11) was going to include a Big Teams editor. Under FDR, this is obsolete. Three sub-decisions:

a) Remove the Big Teams concept entirely. All references to "big team" in code, docs, UI, and database go away. Replaced by FDR throughout.

b) Keep Big Teams as a parallel display concept. UI still shows "BIG" badges on match history cards for matches against high-FDR opponents (FDR 4-5), but the underlying calculation uses the continuous FDR multiplier. Reason strings would say "MOTM vs FDR 5 opponent" or similar.

c) Hybrid. Use FDR for calibration, but show a derived "tough fixture" badge in UI when FDR ≥ 4. Users see familiar "BIG" or similar visual cue, but the math is FDR-driven.

I'd recommend (c). The UI keeps a familiar visual indicator (the user has been seeing BIG badges throughout the build), but the underlying math is properly FDR-driven. Clean separation of "calculation logic" and "user-facing language."

Implication for Step 11 Settings: the Big Teams editor goes away. Settings becomes simpler — just theme, cache, sync controls. No editorial input on what counts as "big."
