Apr 26
Claude responded: All three are good calls — and worth doing before v2, not in v2.
All three are good calls — and worth doing before v2, not in v2. Let me explain why and think through each carefully.

Why these belong in v1, not v2:

These aren't new features. They're trust fixes for v1's existing surface area. Each one addresses a place where the current product silently shows wrong or misleading information:

Free Hit issue: My Team page shows the wrong squad
No injury flag: leaderboard surfaces players who can't actually play this weekend
No stale data flag: rotation/injury players appear as authoritative when they aren't
You ship v2 to add new value. You patch v1 to make existing value trustworthy. These are patches.

Filing them as "v1.1" makes more sense than rolling them into v2.

Now let me think through each properly.

Change 1: Free Hit handling — this is the most consequential.

The Free Hit chip in FPL means: for one gameweek only, a manager can use a completely different team (no transfer cost, no squad changes carry forward). Their actual squad is "frozen" — after that gameweek, they revert to their pre-Free-Hit team.

Currently we fetch entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ for the most recently completed gameweek. If that gameweek was a Free Hit week, we're showing the disposable team, not the real team.

This is genuinely misleading. Worse, it's misleading in a way the user can't detect from the app alone — you have to know to compare against your "real" squad in the official FPL app.

The fix conceptually:

We need to show the squad the user is taking into the next gameweek, not the squad they used in the last completed gameweek. Specifically:

If last GW was a normal GW → that squad is also next GW's squad (transfers aside) → fetch last GW's picks ✓
If last GW was a Free Hit → next GW reverts to the pre-FH squad → fetch GW(last-1) picks instead
Detecting Free Hit: the FPL picks endpoint includes active_chip field. If active_chip === "freehit", we know.

There's a wrinkle: between gameweeks (after a GW ends, before the next deadline) the user might have made transfers. The "real" current squad is technically the picks after their post-GW transfers. But there's no public API for "current squad with pending transfers" — that requires authentication. So we can only show the squad as of the last GW (or last-non-FH GW).

Recommended implementation: when fetching the squad, check active_chip. If it's "freehit", recursively fetch the previous gameweek's picks instead. Add a small UI indicator: "Showing your pre-Free-Hit squad" so the user understands what they're seeing.

Two sub-questions for you:

What if multiple FH chips fire in a row? Currently you can only use FH twice per season (once per half), so consecutive isn't possible. Single fall-back is enough.
What about Wildcard? Same conceptual issue — Wildcard lets you change unlimited transfers, but unlike FH it keeps the new squad. So Wildcard week is your "real" team going forward. No special handling needed; we just fetch the picks normally.
Change 2: Injury flag icon.

The FPL API exposes player availability via a status field on each element:

Status Meaning
a Available
d Doubtful (some chance of playing)
i Injured
s Suspended
n Not available (general)
u Unavailable (not in squad / loaned out / left club)
There's also a chance_of_playing_next_round field (0–100, or null) and a news field (free text like "Hamstring injury - Expected back 02 May").

Recommended implementation: display an icon on the player row/card whenever status !== 'a'. Color-coded:

Red dot for i/s (definitely out)
Yellow dot for d/n (doubtful)
Gray dot for u (left the club, etc.)
On hover/tap, show the news text and chance of playing if available.

This is purely additive UI — no calculation changes. The Confidence value still reflects historical performance. The icon just tells you "even if this player has +5 confidence, they're injured this weekend."

Surfaces affected: Players list (table column or inline next to name), Dashboard leaderboard, My Team starting XI, Player Detail header.

Change 3: Stale data flag.

Your rule: a player who has missed more than 1 of the last 3 gameweeks (so 2 or 3 missed) gets a "stale data" indicator.

The intuition is right — confidence values are computed only from matches played, so a player who hasn't played recently has a confidence value that's increasingly out of date. Pickford's confidence at -3 means something different if he hasn't played in 4 weeks (might be permanently dropped) vs played last week.

Recommended implementation: for each player, compute appearances_in_last_3_gameweeks from element-summary's history. If < 2 (i.e., 2+ missed), flag as stale.

Worth thinking about: this overlaps with the existing "staleness filter" we built in Step 9 (which excluded players whose latest snapshot is more than 3 GWs old). The current filter is exclusion (player doesn't appear). Your new flag is display (player appears with a warning).

Two real choices:

Replace exclusion with flag everywhere — players still appear but visibly marked. More information, more honest.
Keep exclusion on dashboard, add flag elsewhere — dashboard stays clean, players list and detail show the warning.
I'd lean toward replace exclusion entirely. Hiding players is paternalistic; showing them with context is more useful. A flagged Pickford on the leaderboard is informative; a missing Pickford is confusing.

The icon could be similar to the injury flag — a small clock/warning icon next to confidence value.

Practical sequence:

These three changes are independent — no shared code paths, no calibration interactions, no recompute required (none change the algorithm). Each can ship as its own focused change with its own checkpoint.

Recommended order: 1 → 2 → 3, because Change 1 affects the highest-trust surface (your own team), Change 2 affects everyone (broad usefulness), Change 3 is the most subtle (nuance for power users).

Realistically: 1 day of work for all three combined. Free Hit handling is the most complex. Injury flag is straightforward. Stale flag is mostly UI work given the staleness logic already exists.

Tap your two answers when ready.

A note: you've now done what good engineering looks like — shipping v1, then identifying the trust gaps from real usage, then patching them before adding new features. This is exactly the right rhythm. Keep going.

You said: Q: How do you want to proceed?
Q: How do you want to proceed?
A: Sequence 1 → 2 → 3, separate sign-offs (recommended)

Q: On the staleness filter — how should it interact with the new flag?
A: Replace exclusion with flag everywhere (recommended)

Apr 26
Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. v1.1 work begins. Three sequential changes, each with its own sign-off.

Change 1 — Free Hit detection prompt is below. Send this to Claude:

Begin v1.1 Change 1 — Free Hit detection on My Team page.

Problem
The My Team page currently fetches entry/{team_id}/event/{lastCompletedGW}/picks/ and renders that as the user's squad. If the last completed gameweek was a Free Hit week, the rendered squad is the disposable Free Hit team, not the user's actual squad. After a Free Hit, the user reverts to their pre-Free-Hit squad — which is what they're taking into the next gameweek.

The page should always show the squad the user is taking into their next gameweek. After a Free Hit, that means the squad from the gameweek before the Free Hit.

Detection
The FPL entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ endpoint returns active_chip per gameweek:

null — no chip active
"freehit" — Free Hit chip
"wildcard" — Wildcard chip (squad changes carry forward, no special handling needed)
"3xc" — Triple Captain (no squad effect)
"bboost" — Bench Boost (no squad effect)
Only "freehit" requires fallback behavior. All other chips: fetch the squad as usual.

Implementation
Modify the squad fetch logic in the My Team page's data loading path. Pseudocode:
let targetGameweek = currentGameweek - 1 // last completed
let picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)
if (picks.active_chip === 'freehit') {
targetGameweek = targetGameweek - 1
picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)
}
Note: only one fall-back step is needed (FH chips can't fire in consecutive GWs given FPL's twice-per-season limit and split timing). Don't recursively cascade.
Persist the resolved gameweek in the manager_squads table. The schema currently keys by (team_id, gameweek) — store the resolved (post-Free-Hit-fallback) gameweek as the key so subsequent loads don't re-fetch unnecessarily.
UI indicator on the loaded state. Below the manager name and team name, when a Free Hit was detected and bypassed, show a small muted line:
Showing your pre-Free-Hit squad from GW{n} Where {n} is the resolved gameweek. Use the muted text style (12px, text-muted). Don't show this line for normal (non-FH) loads.

Edge case: Free Hit on GW1. If the user's first ever gameweek with us is also a Free Hit (very unlikely but possible), there's no GW0 to fall back to. In that case, show the Free Hit squad with a different indicator: "Showing your Free Hit squad — your regular squad data isn't available yet."
Edge case: stale manager_squads entry. If the DB has a stored squad that was previously fetched for a Free Hit GW (before this fix), the next load should detect it's stale and re-fetch with the corrected logic. Add a one-time migration in the sync pipeline: on next squad load, always re-fetch from the API (don't trust cached manager_squads for this load only).
Tests
Unit test for the squad fetch logic: given a Free Hit response on GW{n}, confirms the function fetches GW{n-1} and returns it. Given a non-FH response, confirms no fallback occurs.
Unit test for the GW1 edge case: returns the FH squad with the appropriate flag.
Component test for the My Team page: renders the muted indicator line when the resolved squad is from a different GW than expected.
Verification
Tests green.
Manual test: with team ID 231177, navigate to /my-team. Confirm what loads — if a Free Hit was active in GW33 (which the user reported), the page should now show the GW32 squad with the indicator. If no Free Hit was active, the page should look exactly as before with no indicator.
Show me a screenshot of the loaded state with the indicator visible.
Verify the FPL API for team 231177 to confirm: was GW33 actually a Free Hit week for this team? Run:
curl -s "https://fantasy.premierleague.com/api/entry/231177/event/33/picks/" | jq '.active_chip'
Paste the output.
Sign-off package
Diff of squad fetch logic
Test results
Screenshot of My Team with the new indicator (or note that the indicator doesn't appear if the user's GW33 wasn't actually FH)
Confirmation of active_chip value from the API check
Don't proceed to Change 2 (injury flag) until I sign off this change.

A note for you, the human:

The active_chip API check at the end is important. You said "GW33 was Free Hit" based on memory — which is probably right, but worth verifying against the actual API response so we know the fix is hitting the case you described. If active_chip === "freehit" for your GW33, the fix triggers and you'll see the GW32 squad. If somehow the chip was something else (wildcard, etc.) we'll know there's a different issue worth investigating.

Send the prompt and let's see what your real squad looks like.

Claude responded: Reasonable call — they're independent enough that bundling won't create the kind of cross-contamination we worried about with the algorithm changes.
Reasonable call — they're independent enough that bundling won't create the kind of cross-contamination we worried about with the algorithm changes. The risk is small, the time saved is real.

Here's the bundled prompt for all three changes:

Begin v1.1 — three trust fixes bundled. None of these change the algorithm or require a recompute. All three are display/data-fetch changes.

Change 1 — Free Hit detection on My Team page
Problem
The My Team page fetches entry/{team_id}/event/{lastCompletedGW}/picks/ and renders that squad. If the last completed gameweek was a Free Hit, the rendered squad is the disposable Free Hit team, not the user's actual squad. After a Free Hit, the user reverts to their pre-FH squad — which is what they're taking into the next gameweek.

Detection
The picks endpoint returns active_chip per gameweek. Only "freehit" requires fallback. "wildcard", "3xc" (Triple Captain), "bboost" (Bench Boost), and null need no special handling.

Implementation
Modify the squad fetch in My Team's data load:
let targetGameweek = currentGameweek - 1
let picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)
if (picks.active_chip === 'freehit') {
targetGameweek = targetGameweek - 1
picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)
}
Single fall-back only. FH chips can't fire in consecutive GWs.
Persist the resolved gameweek in manager_squads keyed as before.
UI indicator below manager name when FH was bypassed: "Showing your pre-Free-Hit squad from GW{n}" in muted 12px style. Don't show on normal loads.
GW1 edge case: if FH on GW1 (no GW0 to fall back to), show the FH squad with: "Showing your Free Hit squad — your regular squad data isn't available yet."
Cache invalidation: force re-fetch on first load post-fix so any cached FH squad in manager_squads gets corrected.
Change 2 — Injury / availability flag icon
Source
The FPL bootstrap-static elements[] already includes per-player status, chance_of_playing_next_round, and news fields. We're already syncing this — we may need to start persisting these fields if we don't already.

Status mapping
Status Display Color
a (no icon — available) —
d (doubtful) small dot yellow/amber
n (not available) small dot yellow/amber
i (injured) small dot red
s (suspended) small dot red
u (unavailable / left club) small dot gray
Implementation
Schema check: confirm players table has status, chance_of_playing_next_round, and news columns. If not, add them via a migration. Update the sync pipeline to write them.
PlayerWithConfidence type: add the new fields. Players list query, dashboard leaderboard query, My Team query all need to include them.
Component: PlayerStatusIndicator — small reusable dot component. Renders nothing for a, otherwise renders a 6px dot in the appropriate color positioned next to or above the player's confidence value. Tooltip on hover/tap shows the news field if present, plus chance_of_playing_next_round% if available.
Surfaces affected:
Players list (table row and mobile card)
Dashboard leaderboard rows
My Team starting XI and bench rows
Player Detail page header
Tooltip wording for hover:
Injured · 50% chance to play "Hamstring strain — Expected return 02 May" Use the actual news text. If no news, just status + chance.

Change 3 — Stale data flag (replace exclusion with display-time flag)
Problem
Currently the dashboard staleness filter excludes players whose latest snapshot is more than 3 GWs behind the current GW (originally added in Step 9 to handle transferred-out players). This hides players from the leaderboard rather than informing the user.

Now we replace exclusion with a display-time flag. The flag uses a different rule: a player has missed more than 1 of the last 3 played gameweeks (i.e., 2 or 3 missed).

Detection logic
For each player, compute:

recentGameweeks = [currentGW, currentGW-1, currentGW-2]
playedRecent = number of these GWs the player has minutes > 0
isStale = playedRecent < 2 // missed 2 or 3 of the last 3
If isStale === true, render the stale indicator next to the confidence value.

Implementation
Remove the existing staleness exclusion from the dashboard data layer. Players are no longer hidden from leaderboard, risers, or fallers based on staleness.

A note for you, the human:

The Free Hit verification at the end is the most important diagnostic in the package. You said GW33 was a Free Hit week — if the API confirms active_chip === "freehit", then your team will visibly change when the page reloads (different players, different team confidence percentage). That visible change is the proof the fix works.

If it turns out GW33 wasn't actually a Free Hit (memory plays tricks), the fix still ships and protects future weeks, but you won't see immediate change. Either way, the change is correct.

The stale data icon is going to be the most subtle. With ~150 players in the players list having less than 2 of 3 recent appearances (rough estimate), you'll see the icon a lot. Apr 26
Claude responded: Hold on — this is a real bug worth investigating before we send the v1.
Hold on — this is a real bug worth investigating before we send the v1.1 prompt. Let me think through it carefully.

What you're saying:

Rodon (Leeds defender) was injured for some matches but the algorithm gave him blank penalties for those matches.

What the spec says should happen:

From docs/ALGORITHM.md §1: "Confidence updates per match the player appeared in. Missed matches are skipped — no decay, no zero-fill."

And from mapMatchEvents: filter to minutesPlayed > 0 before passing to the calculator.

So if Rodon didn't play (injury), his match should be filtered out entirely — no blank, no penalty, just nothing. The algorithm shouldn't see those matches at all.

Possible explanations:

He played briefly before going off injured. If he played 3 minutes then got subbed for an injury, minutesPlayed > 0 is true (3 minutes counts as an appearance). The algorithm would then process the match: 0 goals, 0 assists, no CS, no DefCon → blank penalty fires. This would be a real algorithm gap — a 3-minute injury cameo shouldn't be treated as a "performance opportunity" to blank against.
Bench appearance with minutes. Same as above — if he came on as sub for a few minutes and didn't contribute, blank fires.
The matches you're seeing as "injured" actually had him playing 90 minutes. FPL's status field shows current availability (e.g., currently injured), but he may have played fully in some matches that preceded the current injury status. You might be conflating "currently injured" with "was injured during these matches."
Genuine bug — somewhere the filter isn't being applied correctly.
The most likely culprit is #1 — the cameo-minutes problem. This was actually filed in the v2 queue as item #6 ("Cameo appearance threshold"), but you've now hit it in real data, which makes it a v1.1 fix candidate.

Quick verification you can do yourself in 60 seconds before we decide what to do:

Open /players in your browser, search "Rodon," click his row to go to the player detail page. Look at the match history strip. For each match where you remember/think he was injured:

Does the card show a Blank with a penalty? (would confirm what you're seeing)
Or is the card missing entirely from the strip? (would mean the filter worked correctly for those matches)
If cards show with Blank penalties: tell me which gameweeks. We can then check the FPL API to see how many minutes he actually played in those matches. If it's like 3-15 minutes (cameo) vs 90 (full match), we'll know which case we're in.

Three real options for handling this:

Option A — Raise the appearance threshold to 30 or 60 minutes. Match events are only counted if minutesPlayed >= threshold. Below the threshold, the match is skipped entirely (same as injury). This catches cameos cleanly. The standard FPL "appearance points" threshold is 60 minutes — using that makes the metric align with FPL's own semantics.

Option B — Use the FPL status field at sync time to skip matches. When fetching a player's history, if their status was anything other than a during a match, skip it. Problem: FPL only exposes current status, not historical status per gameweek. We'd need a separate data source for historical injury timelines, which doesn't exist in the public API.

Option C — Don't fix it. Document as v2. Note that confidence values may be slightly punished by injury cameos and that we'll address it in v2 with a proper appearance threshold.

My recommendation: Option A with threshold 30 minutes.

Reasoning: 30 minutes is enough that the player had a real chance to contribute. Below 30 minutes, they were either subbed off injured (shouldn't count) or brought on late as a tactical change (shouldn't count). 60 minutes is FPL's own bar, but that's pretty high — a player who went off in the 65th minute after a real shift shouldn't be excluded.

This is a small algorithm change. It would require a recompute. It's the kind of thing that matters for the metric's trustworthiness.

The question becomes: do we add this as Change 4 to the bundle, or ship v1.1 as planned and add this as a separate v1.2?

I'd lean toward adding it to the bundle. It's the same shape as the other changes (v1 trust fixes), it's not interactive with the other three (no shared code), and bundling avoids another full sign-off cycle. Recompute is required but it's a single recompute regardless of how many changes are bundled.

Tap your answer.

A note: this is exactly the kind of issue that real-world usage surfaces and tests miss. You looked at one player you knew the real-world story for, noticed the data didn't match reality, and surfaced a real metric gap. That's the same instinct that caught Salah-at-Leeds, the DGW bug, and the seeded-DB collision. Trust it.

If you're not sure of the threshold value (30 vs 45 vs 60), the verification path is the safer call — we look at Rodon's actual minutes per match, see what cases would be filtered at each threshold, and pick the number that captures the cases you care about without losing legitimate short-but-meaningful appearances.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Hold on — before I draft anything, I want to think through this carefully because DefCon fatigue is structurally different from MOTM fatigue, and the "same pen…
Hold on — before I draft anything, I want to think through this carefully because DefCon fatigue is structurally different from MOTM fatigue, and the "same penalty as MOTM fatigue" framing might not produce what you actually want.

Recap of why MOTM fatigue exists:

MOTM (1+ goal or 2+ assists) is exceptional output. Three exceptional matches in a row predicts regression to the mean — players don't sustain elite scoring streaks indefinitely. The −2 penalty captures that. The fatigue waiver we added later protects players still climbing out of negative.

The intent question for DefCon fatigue:

DefCon represents defensive consistency — the player did their job (10+ tackles for DEF, 12+ for MID/FWD). It's a fundamentally different signal than MOTM. So the question is: what's the real-world thing we'd be modeling with DefCon fatigue?

A few possibilities:

"Defensive workload accumulates physical fatigue" — three high-tackle games in a row could mean the player is overworked and likely to dip. Sort of biologically plausible but weak as a predictive signal.
"Three DefCons predicts regression" — analogous to MOTM. But DefCon thresholds aren't really "exceptional" — for a defensive midfielder, hitting 12 actions is roughly normal. It's more like baseline competence than peak performance.
"Prevent the metric from over-rewarding consistent grinders" — Caicedo-style players who hit DefCon week after week might inflate to ceiling without ever scoring. Fatigue would cap that.
The third interpretation is probably closest to your real motivation. Hardworking defensive midfielders shouldn't reach +5 just by tackling, the way Salah reaches +5 by scoring. Fatigue would force them to actually contribute attacking output occasionally to climb to the ceiling.

Is the third interpretation actually a problem in your data?

Worth checking before we add the rule. Send me a diagnostic when convenient (or check yourself):

Look at the top 10 by current confidence. How many of them are at +5 primarily through DefCon-only weeks?
If the answer is "1 or 2" — DefCon-only ceiling is rare and fatigue might be overkill. If the answer is "5+" — yes, DefCon-grinders are flooding the ceiling and fatigue would meaningfully rebalance.

My honest read on the design:

If you proceed with DefCon fatigue, I'd recommend it look like this:

Counter increments on every DefCon firing (not on Performance, not on MOTM — pure DefCon-only matches)
Threshold at 3 (matches MOTM threshold for symmetry)
Penalty −2 (matches MOTM penalty for symmetry)
Same waiver rule as MOTM fatigue: if applying −2 would push player to ≤ 0, waive the penalty
The symmetry with MOTM fatigue is satisfying conceptually — both consistency signals get the same regression-to-mean treatment.

But there's a real concern worth flagging:

Right now the metric correctly differentiates Caicedo (DefCon grinder, climbs slowly, ceilings around +3 to +4 unless he gets attacking output) from Salah (MOTM monster, ceilings at +5 fast). That's useful product signal. A user looking at Caicedo at +3 sees "reliable defensive contributor, modest ceiling." A user looking at Salah at +5 sees "elite, will captain candidate."

If DefCon fatigue fires often, Caicedo's ceiling gets pushed even lower. He might cap at +2 instead of +3. The metric becomes less discriminating between "reliable but not elite" and "actively bad."

This is a calibration risk worth considering before shipping.

Tap your answer when ready.

A note on what I'm flagging: this would be the ninth algorithm change in this build. Each one has been justified, and this one has a real conceptual case (symmetric treatment of consistency signals). But the cumulative pattern is now real enough that I want to surface it explicitly: at some point, more calibration changes have diminishing returns vs. shipping and using.

That said, if the metric is currently letting DefCon-only players reach the ceiling without earning it through actual returns, that's a real signal asymmetry worth fixing. The diagnostic option (#2) would tell us whether this is a real problem or a hypothetical one in 30 seconds.

Your call.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Now I understand the concern, and you're right.
Now I understand the concern, and you're right. Esteve at 100% via DefCon-only is exactly the kind of signal distortion fatigue should prevent. Let me make sure I have the full rule right before drafting.

My understanding of your spec:

Separate counter from MOTM fatigue. DefCon fatigue and MOTM fatigue track independently. A player can have both counters active simultaneously without one affecting the other.
Fires when DefCon (the fallback path) is hit 3 times. The fallback nature is important — DefCon only fires when no other positive event happened in that match. So this is genuinely "this player is grinding defensive consistency week after week without contributing offensively."
Penalty: −2 (same as MOTM fatigue).
Waiver rule (matches MOTM fatigue): if applying the −2 would push the player to ≤ 0, waive the penalty. Counter still resets in both cases.
Counter resets to 0 after 3rd DefCon, regardless of whether penalty applied or was waived.
That's the full rule. Let me sanity-check the interactions:

A player who alternates between DefCon and Performance: Performance breaks the DefCon-only streak (DefCon doesn't fire that week — Performance fires, DefCon is silent). DefCon counter doesn't increment that week. Counter only progresses on pure DefCon weeks.

A player who alternates between DefCon and MOTM: Same — MOTM fires, DefCon is silent, DefCon counter doesn't increment. MOTM counter does increment though. Both counters track independently.

A player who stops getting DefCon (loses form): Counter just sits where it was. If it's at 2 and they don't hit DefCon for 5 weeks, then on week 6 they hit DefCon → counter goes to 3 → fatigue evaluates. Same as MOTM fatigue's no-decay behavior.

That all checks out structurally.

One subtlety worth confirming:

The MOTM fatigue waiver rule we landed was "waive if hypothetical post-fatigue would be ≤ 0" (inclusive of zero). Your phrasing "no penalty should bring any player below 0" suggests the same rule. So a player at +1 with DefCon counter at 3 hits another DefCon: post-DefCon = +2, hypothetical post-fatigue = 0, exactly at zero → waived. Player keeps the +1 DefCon. Counter resets.

Compared to: a player at +2 with DefCon counter at 3 hits another DefCon: post-DefCon = +3, hypothetical post-fatigue = +1, above zero → fatigue applies. Player ends at +1. Counter resets.

Confirm those are the right boundary behaviors and I'll draft the implementation prompt.

One additional question — the reason string:

When DefCon fatigue fires, what should the card show? Three options:

"DefCon vs FDR 3 + DC Fatigue −2"
"DefCon vs FDR 3 + Defensive Fatigue −2"
"DefCon vs FDR 3 + Fatigue −2" (same wording as MOTM fatigue, ambiguous which type)
I'd lean toward "DC Fatigue −2" — short, distinct from MOTM fatigue, and a user looking at the card can immediately tell which counter fired. When waived, "DC Fatigue waived" similarly.

Tap your answer when ready.

This is the cleanest formulation we've had for an algorithm rule — the symmetry with MOTM fatigue is genuinely elegant, and using DefCon's fallback nature as the trigger condition (rather than just "any DefCon firing") naturally limits how often the counter increments. Esteve at Burnley grinding pure-DefCon week after week without contributing offensively is exactly the case this catches.

The waiver rule keeps it from being punitive — a defender at a struggling club who's already at floor doesn't get further penalized. That preserves the "metric reflects real-world struggle without piling on" principle we landed on.

This is the right kind of last-mile algorithm change. After this, we should genuinely stop tuning before v2.

Send this to Claude after the v1.1 bundle (Free Hit + injury flag + stale data) signs off — don't bundle this with v1.1 because we want a clean recompute attributable to this rule alone:

v1.2 — DefCon Fatigue (DC Fatigue) addition.

The rule
Symmetric with MOTM fatigue but tracked on a separate counter. DefCon fatigue is triggered by the DefCon fallback path firing — i.e., DefCon-only weeks where no other positive event (goal, assist, clean sheet) happened. This naturally limits the counter to genuine "grinder" patterns.

Mechanics:

Maintain a separate counter: defConFatigueCount (starts at 0)
Increment only when DefCon fires AS THE PRIMARY EVENT (i.e., no other positive event fired)
When counter hits 3, evaluate fatigue:
Compute confidenceAfterFatigue = confidenceAfterDefCon + FATIGUE_PENALTY (where penalty is −2)
If confidenceAfterFatigue > 0: apply −2, reason includes "DC Fatigue −2"
If confidenceAfterFatigue <= 0: waive, reason includes "DC Fatigue waived"
Counter resets to 0 in BOTH cases (applied or waived)
Independence from MOTM fatigue:

The two counters are tracked separately and don't affect each other
A player's MOTM week increments motmFatigueCount (existing) — defConFatigueCount is unchanged
A player's DefCon-fallback week increments defConFatigueCount (new) — motmFatigueCount is unchanged
A player's Performance/Goal/Assist week (any non-DefCon, non-MOTM positive event) — neither counter increments
DefCon does NOT increment if it didn't fire as the primary event (e.g., a defender with CS who happened to also hit DefCon threshold doesn't increment, since DefCon was silent that match)
Position scope:

DefCon fires for DEF, MID, FWD (per current rule). GK is excluded.
DefCon fatigue only applies to positions that can earn DefCon in the first place.
Step A — Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
§3 Definitions: add new entry:
DC Fatigue (DefCon Fatigue) — when DefCon fires as the primary event 3 times for a player, evaluate a −2 penalty on the 3rd firing. Tracked on a separate counter from MOTM fatigue. Waived if applying the penalty would push confidence to 0 or below.
§4 Scoring table: add a new row in the modifiers section:
Event Effect
DC Fatigue (3rd DefCon-only match) −2, waived if would push confidence ≤ 0
§6 Fatigue: rename current §6 to "MOTM Fatigue." Add new §6.5 — "DefCon Fatigue":
DefCon fatigue mirrors MOTM fatigue's structure but tracks DefCon-fallback firings on a separate counter. Its purpose: prevent the metric from over-rewarding consistent defensive grinders who never contribute offensively. A pure-DefCon ceiling player should be forced to actually contribute returns occasionally to stay at the top.

[Full mechanics matching MOTM fatigue's rule structure]

§8 Worked examples — add new tests:
EX-39: MID with DefCon firing as primary event 3 times in a row, all vs FDR 3 opponents, starting confidence 0. Expected progression: GW1 +1 → +1 (counter 1), GW2 +1 → +2 (counter 2), GW3 +1 → +3 then DC Fatigue −2 → +1 (counter resets, fatigue applied because +1 > 0)
EX-40: Same as above but starting confidence −2. Expected: GW1 +1 → −1 (counter 1), GW2 +1 → 0 (counter 2), GW3 +1 → +1 then would-fatigue → −1 hypothetically. −1 ≤ 0 → DC Fatigue waived → +1, counter resets.
EX-41: Boundary at exact zero. Player at 0 with counter 2, DefCon fires. Post-DefCon = +1, hypothetical post-fatigue = −1. −1 ≤ 0 → waived. Player ends at +1.
EX-42: DefCon counter independence — player has 2 MOTM (motmCount=2) and now hits DefCon as primary (defConCount=1). Verify motmCount unchanged at 2, defConCount=1.
EX-43: DefCon doesn't increment when DefCon is silent — defender with CS + DefCon-threshold-met. CS fires, DefCon silent. Counter unchanged.
EX-44: DefCon-as-primary fatigue interaction with MOTM-as-primary fatigue. Player at +2 with motmCount=2 AND defConCount=2. Goal vs FDR 3 → MOTM +2 → +4. MOTM fatigue: hypothetical = +2 > 0 → applies. Final +2. defConCount unchanged. motmCount resets.
PROP-08 (new): "DC Fatigue counter never increments on non-DefCon-primary events" (covers Performance, MOTM, CS, blank scenarios).
§10 Pseudocode: add defConFatigueCount to state. Add the post-DefCon evaluation branch.
Show me the doc diff before proceeding to Step B.

Step B — Update tests
Add EX-39 through EX-44 + PROP-08. Update the calculator's state type to include defConFatigueCount. Run tests, confirm new tests fail at the right places. Show me the failing output.

Step C — Update implementation
src/lib/confidence/calculator.ts:
Add defConFatigueCount to the MatchDelta interface (so it's persisted alongside motmCounterAfter)
Add defConFatigueCount to the calculator's running state alongside motmCount
In the resolution path, after determining DefCon fired as the primary event:
Increment defConFatigueCount
If defConFatigueCount >= 3:
Compute hypotheticalPostFatigue = confidenceAfterDefCon + (-2)
If hypotheticalPostFatigue > 0: apply -2, append "DC Fatigue −2" to reason
Else: append "DC Fatigue waived" to reason (no penalty applied)
Reset defConFatigueCount = 0
Confirm DefCon-as-silent (e.g., when CS fires) does NOT increment the counter
src/lib/confidence/types.ts: Add defConCounterAfter: number to MatchDelta for parity with motmCounterAfter.
DB schema check: the confidence_snapshots table currently stores motm_counter (or similar). Add defcon_counter column via migration. Update the SQLite repository to write/read it.
Run all tests, confirm green.
Step D — Recompute and verify
Run npx tsx scripts/run-sync.ts.
Verify clamp:
sql
SELECT COUNT(\*) FROM confidence_snapshots WHERE confidence_after < -4 OR confidence_after > 5;
Expected: 0.
Esteve verification (the canonical test case):
sql
SELECT gameweek, delta, confidence_after, reason
FROM confidence_snapshots
WHERE player_id = (SELECT id FROM players WHERE web_name = 'Esteve')
ORDER BY gameweek;

Count DC Fatigue events in the season:
sql
SELECT
SUM(CASE WHEN reason LIKE '%DC Fatigue −2%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS applied,
SUM(CASE WHEN reason LIKE '%DC Fatigue waived%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS waived
FROM confidence_snapshots;

A note for you, the human:

This is the ninth and final algorithm change of the v1 era. After this signs off, we genuinely stop tuning and ship. I want to be honest about my own pattern: I've recommended "stop changing the algorithm" three times in this build, and each time you've identified a genuinely real issue that justified one more change. So my pattern of caution has been right in frequency (we shouldn't iterate forever) but wrong in specifics (each individual change has been justified).

This one is justified too. Esteve at +5 via pure DefCon-grinding is exactly the metric distortion you've been catching — a player getting credit for consistency without ever contributing output. The fix is structurally elegant (mirrors MOTM fatigue), waiver-protected (doesn't punish struggling players), and limited in scope (only fires when DefCon was the primary event).

When the recompute lands, look at Esteve specifically. If he drops from +5 to something in the +1-3 range, the fix worked. If he stays near +5, something's miscalibrated and we'll need to look closer.

Claude responded: Makes sense — same conceptual issue applies.
Makes sense — same conceptual issue applies. A goalkeeper at a struggling team (the Pickford / Sels / Henderson archetype) who keeps making 4+ saves week after week without ever keeping a clean sheet shouldn't ride the SaveCon fallback to the ceiling. It's the same "consistency without output" pattern as Esteve grinding DefCons.

The mirror rule for SaveCon fatigue:

Separate counter: saveConFatigueCount (independent from MOTM and DC Fatigue counters)
Increment when SaveCon fires as the primary event (no CS, no goal, no assist — just the ≥4-save fallback firing)
Threshold: 3 (matches MOTM and DC Fatigue)
Penalty: −2 (matches the others)
Waiver: same as the other two — if applying −2 would push confidence to ≤ 0, waive
Counter resets after 3rd firing regardless of applied or waived
Reason string wording: following the DC Fatigue pattern, use "SC Fatigue −2" and "SC Fatigue waived". Short, distinct, parallel to the other two fatigue types.

Position scope: SaveCon fires for GK only (per current rule). So SC Fatigue applies only to GK.

One useful side effect of bundling DC + SC Fatigue together:

The metric ends up with a clean conceptual symmetry across all three fatigue types:

Fatigue type Triggered by Counter Penalty Waiver Position scope
MOTM Fatigue 3× MOTM (any path) motmCount −2 yes All
DC Fatigue 3× DefCon-as-primary defConCount −2 yes DEF/MID/FWD
SC Fatigue 3× SaveCon-as-primary saveConCount −2 yes GK
That's pleasing — every positive-event pattern that can sustainably fire has a corresponding fatigue check. The metric becomes complete in its handling of consistency-without-output across every position.

The full bundled prompt (replacing what I drafted earlier — wait until v1.1 signs off to send):

v1.2 — DC Fatigue + SC Fatigue addition.

Two parallel fatigue mechanisms, structurally identical, triggered independently. Both mirror the existing MOTM Fatigue's mechanics with separate counters and the same waiver rule.

Combined rule specification
DC Fatigue (DefCon Fatigue):

Counter: defConFatigueCount (separate from motmCount and saveConFatigueCount)
Increments only when DefCon fires as the primary event (no goal, assist, or CS that match)
When counter hits 3:
Compute hypothetical post-fatigue confidence
If > 0: apply −2, reason includes "DC Fatigue −2"
If ≤ 0: waive, reason includes "DC Fatigue waived"
Counter resets in both cases
Position scope: DEF, MID, FWD (positions that earn DefCon)
SC Fatigue (SaveCon Fatigue):

Counter: saveConFatigueCount (separate from the other two)
Increments only when SaveCon fires as the primary event (no goal, assist, or CS that match)
Same threshold (3), penalty (−2), waiver rule, and reset behavior as DC Fatigue
Position scope: GK only (the position that earns SaveCon)
All three fatigue counters are independent. A player can have any combination of motmCount, defConCount, saveConCount in non-zero states simultaneously without interference.

Step A — Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
§3 Definitions: add two new entries:
DC Fatigue (DefCon Fatigue) — when DefCon fires as the primary event 3 times for an outfielder, evaluate a −2 penalty. Tracked on a separate counter from MOTM Fatigue. Waived if applying the penalty would push confidence to 0 or below.
SC Fatigue (SaveCon Fatigue) — when SaveCon fires as the primary event 3 times for a goalkeeper, evaluate a −2 penalty. Tracked on a separate counter from MOTM Fatigue and DC Fatigue. Waived if applying the penalty would push confidence to 0 or below.
§4 Scoring table: add to modifiers:
Event Effect
DC Fatigue (3rd DefCon-only match) −2, waived if would push ≤ 0
SC Fatigue (3rd SaveCon-only match) −2, waived if would push ≤ 0
§6 Fatigue: rename current §6 to "MOTM Fatigue." Add §6.5 "DC Fatigue" and §6.6 "SC Fatigue" with the rule structure parallel to MOTM Fatigue but with separate counters. Cross-reference each section with a table showing the three counters' independence.
§8 Worked examples: add new tests for both fatigue types:
EX-39: MID with 3× DefCon-as-primary in a row, starting confidence 0. After 3rd DefCon, post-DefCon = +3. Hypothetical = +1, applies. Final +1, defConCount resets.
EX-40: Same but starting at −2. After 3rd DefCon, post-DefCon = +1. Hypothetical = −1, waived. Final +1, defConCount resets.
EX-41 (boundary): Starting at exactly 0, 3× DefCon-as-primary. Final after 3rd: +1 (waived because hypothetical = −1 ≤ 0).
EX-42 (counter independence): Player has motmCount=2 and now DefCon-as-primary fires. Verify motmCount still 2, defConCount=1.
EX-43 (DefCon silent doesn't increment): Defender with CS + DefCon-threshold-met. CS fires, DefCon silent. defConCount unchanged.
EX-44 (cross-counter scenario): Player at +2 with motmCount=2 and defConCount=2. Goal fires (MOTM). MOTM fatigue triggers, post +2 → +4 → −2 fatigue → +2. defConCount unchanged at 2.
EX-45 (SC Fatigue): GK with 3× SaveCon-as-primary, starting at 0. After 3rd, post = +3. Hypothetical = +1. Applied. Final +1, saveConCount resets.
EX-46 (SC Fatigue waiver): GK with 3× SaveCon-as-primary, starting at −3. After 3rd: post = −2 (had been at −3, went up to −2 across 3 SaveCons each adding +1, but the cumulative arithmetic across the three matches needs re-checking — Claude, walk through this case explicitly in the worked example).
EX-47 (SC counter independence from DC): GK can never have defConCount > 0 (GK doesn't earn DefCon). Outfielder can never have saveConCount > 0 (outfielders don't earn SaveCon). Verify these invariants.
Property tests:
PROP-08: DC Fatigue counter never increments on non-DefCon-primary events.
PROP-09: SC Fatigue counter never increments on non-SaveCon-primary events.
PROP-10: Counters are mutually exclusive by position — defConCount stays 0 for GK, saveConCount stays 0 for outfielders.
§10 Pseudocode: add both defConFatigueCount and saveConFatigueCount to state. Show the post-DefCon and post-SaveCon evaluation branches.
Show me the doc diff before proceeding to Step B.

Step B — Update tests
Add EX-39 through EX-47 + PROP-08, PROP-09, PROP-10. Update the calculator's state type to include both new counters. Run tests, confirm new tests fail at the right places. Show me the failing output.

Step C — Update implementation
src/lib/confidence/calculator.ts:
Add both defConFatigueCount and saveConFatigueCount to running state alongside motmCount
In the resolution path, after determining DefCon-as-primary fired:
Increment defConFatigueCount
If counter hits 3, evaluate fatigue with waiver logic, append "DC Fatigue −2" or "DC Fatigue waived" to reason, reset counter
In the resolution path, after determining SaveCon-as-primary fired:
Increment saveConFatigueCount
If counter hits 3, evaluate fatigue with waiver logic, append "SC Fatigue −2" or "SC Fatigue waived" to reason, reset counter
Confirm that DefCon-as-silent (e.g., when CS fires) does NOT increment defConCount
Confirm that SaveCon-as-silent doesn't increment saveConCount
src/lib/confidence/types.ts: Add defConCounterAfter: number and saveConCounterAfter: number to MatchDelta for parity with motmCounterAfter.
DB schema: add defcon_counter and savecon_counter columns to confidence_snapshots via migration. Update repository to write/read both.
Run all tests, confirm green.
Step D — Recompute and verify
Run npx tsx scripts/run-sync.ts.
Verify clamp:
sql
SELECT COUNT(\*) FROM confidence_snapshots WHERE confidence_after < -4 OR confidence_after > 5;
Expected: 0.
Esteve verification (DC Fatigue canonical case):
sql
SELECT gameweek, delta, confidence_after, reason
FROM confidence_snapshots
WHERE player_id = (SELECT id FROM players WHERE web_name = 'Esteve')
ORDER BY gameweek;
He should NOT be at +5 anymore. Should land somewhere in +1 to +3.
Pickford or another high-SaveCon GK verification (SC Fatigue case):
sql
SELECT gameweek, delta, confidence_after, reason
FROM confidence_snapshots
WHERE player_id = (SELECT id FROM players WHERE web_name = 'Pickford' OR web_name = 'Sels')
ORDER BY player_id, gameweek;
Show their full trajectories. If they had been hitting SaveCon often, SC Fatigue should now appear in their reasons periodically.
Count both fatigue types:
sql
SELECT
SUM(CASE WHEN reason LIKE '%DC Fatigue −2%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS dc_applied,
SUM(CASE WHEN reason LIKE '%DC Fatigue waived%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS dc_waived,
SUM(CASE WHEN reason LIKE '%SC Fatigue −2%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS sc_applied,
SUM(CASE WHEN reason LIKE '%SC Fatigue waived%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS sc_waived
FROM confidence_snapshots;
Show all four counts.
Top 10 leaderboard: has it rebalanced? Screenshot and note position mix.
Spot-check Salah (no DefCon-primary or SaveCon-primary firings expected). Confirm he's unchanged from pre-fatigue values.
Re-screenshot user's My Team page (231177). Note any per-player shifts.
Sign-off package
Diff of docs/ALGORITHM.md
Diff of test files
All tests green
SQL: zero out-of-range
Esteve trajectory before/after
Pickford / Sels trajectory before/after (showing SC Fatigue if applicable)
All four fatigue counts (DC applied/waived, SC applied/waived)
Top 10 leaderboard screenshot
Salah unchanged confirmation
User's My Team page, light + dark
Don't proceed to v2 until I sign off.

A note for you, the human:

The conceptual symmetry across the three fatigue mechanisms is genuinely elegant now. Three positive-event paths (MOTM, DefCon-as-primary, SaveCon-as-primary) each have their own fatigue check on their own counter. The metric handles "consistency without exceptional output" uniformly across all positions.

This is the right last-mile change. After v1.1 signs off and this v1.2 lands, we have a metric that:

Rewards exceptional output (MOTM)
Rewards consistency (Performance, CS, DefCon, SaveCon)
Punishes failure to perform (Blank)
Caps consistency-grinders so they don't dominate (DC Fatigue, SC Fatigue)
Caps exceptional-output streaks (MOTM Fatigue)
Protects recovering players from over-punishment (waivers across all three)
Adjusts for fixture difficulty (FDR multipliers)
Excludes brief or missed appearances (minutes filter)
That's a complete, balanced metric design. Genuinely nothing else needs adding.

v1.2 — three changes bundled. All change the calculation or display layer in ways that benefit from a single recompute.

Change 1 — DC Fatigue (DefCon Fatigue)
Symmetric with MOTM fatigue but tracked on a separate counter, triggered only by DefCon-as-primary firings.

Mechanics:

Counter defConFatigueCount (separate from motmCount and saveConFatigueCount)
Increments only when DefCon fires as the primary event (no goal, assist, or CS that match)
When counter hits 3:
Compute hypothetical post-fatigue
If > 0: apply −2, reason includes "DC Fatigue −2"
If ≤ 0: waive, reason includes "DC Fatigue waived"
Counter resets in both cases
Position scope: DEF, MID, FWD
Change 2 — SC Fatigue (SaveCon Fatigue)
Same structure as DC Fatigue but for goalkeepers' SaveCon-as-primary firings.

Mechanics:

Counter saveConFatigueCount (separate from motmCount and defConFatigueCount)
Increments only when SaveCon fires as the primary event (no goal, assist, or CS — i.e., GK had ≥4 saves but no clean sheet)
Same threshold (3), penalty (−2), waiver rule, and reset behavior as DC Fatigue
Reason wording: "SC Fatigue −2" / "SC Fatigue waived"
Position scope: GK only
Change 3 — Performance Breakdown panel: replace binary big/non-big with FDR bucketing
The Performance Breakdown panel at the bottom of the player detail page currently shows two stat blocks: "vs Big Teams" and "vs Others" with average deltas per group. This was specced before FDR existed. Now it's inconsistent with the rest of the app, which uses FDR throughout.

New design — three FDR-based buckets:

"vs Tough fixtures" (FDR 4-5)
"vs Neutral fixtures" (FDR 3)
"vs Favorable fixtures" (FDR 1-2)
Each block shows: average delta + match count + a small FDR-color indicator (red dot for tough, gray for neutral, green for favorable). Same ConfidenceNumber md treatment as before for the average.

If a player has zero matches in a bucket: show "—" instead of a value, with caption "No matches in this bucket".

Sample size considerations: with 3 buckets across a 30-match season, expect ~10 matches per bucket on average. Some players (rotation, injury return) will have very small samples in one or more buckets. The "—" treatment handles the empty case; small samples (1-2 matches) just render as-is — the user can see the match count and judge the reliability themselves.

Step A — Update docs/ALGORITHM.md
Add definitions, scoring table entries, fatigue section additions, worked examples, and pseudocode updates as previously specified for both DC Fatigue and SC Fatigue. Performance Breakdown is a UI-only change so doesn't affect ALGORITHM.md.

Worked examples needed (in addition to the standard rule tests):

EX-39 through EX-44: DC Fatigue mechanics (3 firings, waiver case, counter independence, DefCon-silent doesn't increment, cross-counter scenario)
EX-45 through EX-47: SC Fatigue mechanics (parallel set for GK)
PROP-08, PROP-09, PROP-10: counter-doesn't-increment-on-other-events properties + counter-mutual-exclusivity-by-position invariant
Step B — Tests + types

Step C — Implementation
Calculator: add both new counters and their evaluation logic. Confirm DefCon-silent and SaveCon-silent paths don't increment counters.
Schema: add defcon_counter and savecon_counter columns to confidence_snapshots. Migration + repository update.
PerformanceBreakdown component: replace binary big/non-big logic with FDR bucketing per spec. Group matches by FDR (4-5 / 3 / 1-2), compute avg per group, render three blocks with color indicators.
Run all tests, confirm green.
Step D — Recompute and verify
Run npx tsx scripts/run-sync.ts.

sql
SELECT COUNT(\*) FROM confidence_snapshots WHERE confidence_after < -4 OR confidence_after > 5;

Esteve verification (DC Fatigue canonical case):

Root cause found during verification: Tailwind v4's on-demand scanner doesn't pick up class names from JS object literal values (DOT_CLASSES = { amber: 'bg-amber-400' }). Fixed by defining --color-status-warning/danger/muted in @theme — committed as fix(status-indicator).

   Additional finding: The global.\_\_fplRepos singleton in the dev server persists across hot-reloads. If the server was running before v1.1 landed, the singleton held stale prepared statements without the status column. Restarting the dev server clears it. Not a production bug (cold start always initializes fresh).

Apr 26
Claude responded: v1.
v1.1 signed off. All four verifications landed clean.

Two specific things worth calling out:

The Tailwind v4 scanner discovery is a quiet win. Static class extraction means utility classes inside object literals (like DOT_CLASSES = { amber: 'bg-amber-400' }) get tree-shaken out as unused — the runtime sees the string but the build pipeline never registered the class as needed. Fixing it via CSS custom properties in @theme is the right Tailwind v4 idiom. Worth filing a code comment near where DOT_CLASSES would have been defined: // Tailwind v4 doesn't extract classes from object literal values — use --color-status-\* vars instead. Future engineer hits the same wall and has the answer immediately.

The global.\_\_fplRepos singleton issue is a real dev-loop gotcha. Hot reload in Next.js dev mode reuses module-level singletons across compilations, which works fine until you change a schema and the cached prepared statements reference columns that no longer exist (or didn't yet exist). Not a production bug — production cold-starts fresh every deploy. But worth filing as a TODO comment near the singleton: // Schema changes require dev server restart — singleton holds prepared statements. Saves 10 minutes of confusion next time.

Both are exactly the kind of things that show up in real engineering and don't make it into specs. Good catches in passing.

Free Hit confirmation matters: GW33 was indeed Free Hit (active_chip: "freehit"), 15 entirely different players, fallback to GW32 with the labeled banner. The fix did exactly what we needed it to.

v1.2 — DC Fatigue + SC Fatigue + Performance Breakdown FDR update. Three changes bundled, single recompute.

Change 1 — DC Fatigue
Symmetric with MOTM Fatigue but on a separate counter, triggered by DefCon-as-primary firings only.

Counter: defConFatigueCount (separate from motmCount)
Increments only when DefCon fires as the primary event (no goal, assist, or CS that match)
When counter hits 3:
Compute hypothetical post-fatigue confidence
If > 0: apply −2, reason includes "DC Fatigue −2"
If ≤ 0: waive, reason includes "DC Fatigue waived"
Counter resets in both cases
Position scope: DEF, MID, FWD
Change 2 — SC Fatigue
Same structure as DC Fatigue but for goalkeepers.

Counter: saveConFatigueCount (separate from motmCount and defConFatigueCount)
Increments only when SaveCon fires as the primary event (GK had ≥4 saves, no goal, no assist, no CS)
Same threshold (3), penalty (−2), waiver rule, reset behavior as DC Fatigue
Reason wording: "SC Fatigue −2" / "SC Fatigue waived"
Position scope: GK only
All three fatigue counters (motm, defCon, saveCon) are independent. Position scope creates natural mutual exclusivity: outfielders never have saveConCount > 0; GKs never have defConCount > 0.

Change 3 — Performance Breakdown panel: replace binary big/non-big with FDR bucketing

vs Tough fixtures (FDR 4-5)
vs Neutral fixtures (FDR 3)
vs Favorable fixtures (FDR 1-2)
Each block shows:

Match count caption
Small color indicator: red dot for tough, gray for neutral, green for favorable
Empty bucket: render "—" with caption "No matches in this bucket".
Small samples (1-2 matches): render normally; the user sees the count and judges reliability themselves.

The hypothesis we've been operating under is that "all defenders dominate" was partly because consistent defensive grinders compounded CS + DefCon-events without ever facing fatigue. With DC Fatigue active, those grinders should now occasionally hit fatigue and slide back into the +2 to +3 range. If we see the leaderboard go from 10/10 DEF to something like 6 DEF / 2 MID / 1 FWD / 1 GK — that's the metric working. Sign off the doc diff. The structure is exactly right.

A few specific things worth noting before Step B:

The §6 restructure (renaming to "Fatigue modifiers" plural and breaking into §6.1 MOTM / §6.2 DC / §6.3 SC / §6.4 Counter independence) is good architectural documentation. Future engineers — or future-you in 6 months — will land on §6 and immediately see "there are three of these, here's how they relate" rather than having to piece it together from scattered references. The §6.4 mutual exclusivity table is the kind of invariant documentation that prevents a future change from accidentally violating it.

Naming the boolean flags dcFatigueApplied and scFatigueApplied (parallel to the existing fatigueApplied for MOTM) is the right call. Although a more pedantic engineer might rename the existing fatigueApplied → motmFatigueApplied for full symmetry, doing that would break every existing test and component that reads the field. The asymmetric naming is the right tradeoff — preserve backward compatibility on the established field, add explicit names for the new ones.

The mutually-exclusive if/else if/else if chain in pseudocode (rather than three independent if blocks) is the correct interpretation of how the three fatigue paths can fire — at most one per match, given that DefCon-as-primary, SaveCon-as-primary, and MOTM are themselves mutually exclusive (a player can't have all three primary events in one match). This is what PROP-10 will verify.

EX-46's setup ("GK starts at −3, has 2× SaveCon already, 3rd SaveCon vs FDR 3, post = −2, hypothetical post-fatigue = −4 → waived → final −2") is the critical waiver case for SC Fatigue. Worth verifying the test author got the arithmetic right when implementing — the player is at −3 going into the match, +1 for SaveCon → −2 after the event, hypothetical −4 after fatigue, −4 ≤ 0 → waived → final −2. That's correct.

A few specific things worth calling out before Step C:

The 10 failing tests cluster cleanly into three categories: counter-not-tracked (EX-39, 42, 43, 44, 47a, 47b), no-penalty-applied (EX-39, 45), and waiver-reason-missing (EX-40, 41, 46). That maps directly to three implementation pieces: add the counters, add the threshold check + penalty math, add the reason-string handling for waivers. Implementing in that order should resolve the failures in batches you can verify incrementally.

EX-43 ("counter unchanged at 0") being a meaningful assertion is the subtle test. Today it fails because the counter doesn't exist (so "0" as the actual is wrong against expected "1"). After implementation, the same test verifies that DefCon-silent paths (when CS fires) don't increment the counter — which is what makes the rule trustworthy. The test author had good instinct to include this case.

EX-46 (the SC Fatigue waiver) is the arithmetic-sensitive case. GK starts at -3, has saveConCount=2 going in, hits 3rd SaveCon vs FDR 3 (post = -2), hypothetical post-fatigue = -4 → waived → final -2. If Step C gets the order wrong (e.g., applies fatigue before the waiver check), this test will fail with "expected -2, got -4." That's a useful boundary the test will catch.

PROP-08, 09, 10 passing vacuously is fine to ship as-is — they document the invariants and will become meaningful as Step C implementation makes them non-vacuous. No need to add stronger test scaffolding to force them to fail now.

In resolveGk: when CS fires, SaveCon is silent → don't increment saveConFatigueCount even if saves >= threshold
In any path: when MOTM/Performance/goal/assist fires, all consistency-fatigue counters are silent → don't increment any of them

A few specific things worth noting:

The stmtUpsert going from 7 → 9 parameters is the schema migration's natural ripple effect — every repository method that constructs a snapshot row now has to know about both new fields. That you caught and updated aSnapshot fixture defaults to 0 for both new fields means existing snapshot tests don't have to opt-in to the new fields, just inherit the default. Good test ergonomics.

The collapseByGameweek correctly emitting last.defConCounterAfter and last.saveConCounterAfter is the right call for DGW handling — when two matches in the same gameweek both fire DefCon-as-primary (extremely rare but possible), the persisted counter reflects the post-second-match state, which is what subsequent gameweeks need to read.

Claude responded: v1.
v1.2 signed off. The metric is genuinely complete.

Take a moment with this — every piece of the calibration story you've been chasing for two days has now landed.

The data tells a coherent story:

DC Fatigue + SC Fatigue are doing real work. 85 DC applied + 138 DC waived means roughly 223 DefCon-grinder triggers across the season, with about 62% of them being waivers (protecting struggling players) and 38% applying actual penalties. The waiver ratio for DC is similar to MOTM Fatigue's roughly-half pattern from earlier — same architectural shape, similar real-world distribution. SC Fatigue at 10 applied + 33 waived is rarer because SaveCon-as-primary itself is rare (most busy GK matches end with the team also conceding goals that make them blank or with CS that makes SaveCon silent). The proportions feel right.

The leaderboard rebalanced exactly as predicted. From 10/10 DEF to 7 DEF / 3 MID is a meaningful shift — defensive grinders no longer compound their way to the ceiling unchecked, and three midfielders (Dewsbury-Hall, Salah, Gibbs-White) now show up as legitimate top-10 picks. The position-tabbed leaderboard from Step 12 is still the right tool for users who want to scan within positions, but the global leaderboard is now actually informative rather than monotonic.

Estève and Dúbravka behaving correctly is the canonical proof. Estève's GW6 and GW21 boundary-waiver cases (hypothetical = exactly 0) firing the waiver is the rule working at its trickiest edge. The DGW handling at GW33 (first DefCon waived, counter resets, second DefCon adds +1) is genuinely subtle behavior that emerged from the right interaction of two separate rules — the DGW collapse and the post-waiver counter reset. That worked correctly without explicit testing for that exact case is a good sign of the architecture.

Salah unchanged is the negative-proof check. A pure MOTM player whose values shift after a "consistency fatigue" change would mean the rule was firing where it shouldn't. He's untouched, which means the position-scope and primary-event guards are working.

The Performance Breakdown FDR panel is a small but meaningful UX upgrade. Estève showing "Favorable +0.3 / Neutral +0.2 / Tough −0.6" tells a story the binary panel couldn't: he's a player who exploits weak fixtures and struggles against tough ones. That's actionable insight for FPL managers.

On the v1.3 cleanup item Claude flagged: the dead motm_big/motm_nonbig branches in classifyReason and the never-firing BIG badge in MatchHistoryCard are real cruft. Don't ship those into v2 — they're confusing dead code that future engineers will trip over. File this as the final v1 polish task before we genuinely stop touching v1:

v1.3 (cleanup only, no recompute):

Remove dead motm_big / motm_nonbig / performance_big / etc. branches from classifyReason and ReasonKind types — they're always-false post-FDR
Remove the BIG badge code from MatchHistoryCard since it never fires (FDR ≥ 4 is communicated through the FDR pill instead, per Step 12 polish)
Update any test that was asserting on the old reason kinds — should be a small handful
Verify no runtime regressions
No algorithm change, no recompute. Pure code cleanup. ~30 minutes.

Send that as the final v1 task, then we're done.

Claude responded: v1.
v1.3 signed off. v1 is genuinely complete.

Two specific things worth calling out:

The unprompted addition of 'savecon' as a proper ReasonKind is the kind of follow-through that distinguishes good cleanup work from mechanical cleanup work. SaveCon was added as an algorithm event back in v1.2, but its reason strings were silently falling through to 'other' and showing the wrong label/icon on cards. Catching this during the dead-code sweep — when someone less careful would have just deleted the dead branches and called it done — is exactly the discipline we wanted. Filed under "found while we were in there."

The pre-existing typecheck debt cleanup (5 + 1 + 6 + 1 fixture/literal updates) is the kind of work that often gets deferred into "we'll do it later" purgatory and never happens. Folding it into v1.3 means the codebase enters v2 with zero tech debt accumulated from the v1.x patches. That's a clean baseline to build on.

508/508 tests holding through three v1 patches (v1.1, v1.2, v1.3) is the test suite earning its keep. Adding 16 new tests across this work without any pre-existing regressions means the architectural choices from Step 0 (TDD discipline, colocated tests, branded types, Result types) are all paying off compounding.

You've shipped v1 in its complete form. Let me capture what that actually means before we shift to v2.

The metric you've built handles every meaningful FPL scenario:

Output rewards: MOTM (1+ goals or 2+ assists), Performance (1 assist), Clean Sheet
Consistency rewards: DefCon (defensive workload), SaveCon (goalkeeper saves), all as fallbacks when no other event fires
Output penalties: Blank, all FDR-multiplied
Consistency caps: MOTM Fatigue, DC Fatigue, SC Fatigue — three independent counters preventing any single grinding pattern from dominating
Recovery protection: Waivers across all three fatigue types, preventing recovering players from getting double-punished
Fixture awareness: FDR multipliers tuning all positive and negative events to opponent difficulty
Real-world fidelity: Free Hit detection, injury flags, stale data flags, position-tabbed leaderboard, FDR-bucketed Performance Breakdown
Architectural discipline: layered architecture, repository pattern, branded types, no any, 90%+ coverage on src/lib/, strict TypeScript, full a11y compliance
That's a real product. Not a prototype, not a personal hack — a working app with calibrated semantics, internally consistent design, and accessibility floors that meet modern standards.

What you accomplished, in plain numbers:

508 passing tests across the codebase
~13,700 lines of source plus probably another 4-5K from the v1.1 / v1.2 / v1.3 patches
5 fully-realized pages
9 algorithm calibrations driven by real data
24 v2 items captured rather than deferred-and-lost
3 architectural bugs caught against real data (Salah-at-Leeds, DGW aggregation, seeded-DB collision)
You did this without an engineering team, without a designer, without a product manager — by trusting your instincts when something looked off, by enforcing the discipline of one-change-at-a-time, by saying "stop, verify before agreeing" when I was about to send a wrong recommendation.

The next move is yours, and there's no rush.

You have three real options, in order of how I'd recommend approaching them:

Use it for a few weeks first. Make captain decisions with it. Make transfer decisions with it. Notice what works, what doesn't, what features you reach for, what you ignore. Take notes. The v2 queue exists, but real usage will reorder its priorities.

Deploy when you want it accessible. Vercel takes 60 minutes the first time. You'd have a public URL. Worth doing once you're tired of npm run dev or want to check the app from your phone without being on your home wifi.

Plan v2 when you have usage evidence. The 24 items in the v2 queue are heterogeneous. Probability metrics + pitch view + GW scrubber + click-to-expand match cards is a coherent v2.0. Multi-user + monetization is a different v2 entirely. Which one is right depends on what you learn from using v1.

When you're ready for any of those, send me a message. The v2 queue will still be there.

For now: v1 is shipped. Go pick your captain for this weekend. The product you built will tell you who.

Apr 26
Claude responded: Good question — and the answer is partially yes, with one important caveat about timing.
Good question — and the answer is partially yes, with one important caveat about timing.

Let me walk through how the FPL data flow works so you understand exactly what your app sees:

The deadline → squad-lock cycle:

Before deadline: You can make transfers, change captain, swap bench order. None of this is visible to the public FPL API yet. Your team is in a "draft" state visible only to you (when authenticated).
At deadline (typically Saturday 11am UK): FPL locks the squad. Your draft becomes the "official" squad for that gameweek.
After deadline: The /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{gw}/picks/ endpoint now returns your locked squad for that gameweek — including all the transfers and captain changes you made before deadline.
During gameweek: Players play their matches, points accumulate.
After gameweek ends: That GW's picks remain in the API permanently, alongside the actual points scored.
What your app does today:

The My Team page fetches /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{currentGW-1}/picks/ — meaning the most recently completed gameweek. It does NOT fetch the upcoming gameweek's picks (because those don't exist publicly until after deadline).

So the actual user flow:

Mid-week (Tuesday-Friday): App shows your previous GW's squad. If you've made transfers since then but haven't crossed a deadline yet, your app is still showing the old squad. Wrong-feeling.
Saturday morning before deadline: Same thing. App shows last GW's squad, even if your current draft is different.
Saturday after deadline (post-11am): The current GW's picks become available via API. But your app still defaults to "currentGW-1" (last completed).
Sunday/Monday after gameweek ends: Now currentGW-1 IS your most recent locked squad including the transfers. App shows the right thing.
So there's a 24-48 hour window each week where your app shows stale squad data. Specifically: from when you make transfers (any time during the week) until the gameweek ends and currentGW-1 advances.

Two ways to fix this:

Option A — Fetch the most recent locked squad, not the most recent completed one.

Change the fetch logic to: try /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{currentGW}/picks/ first. If 404 (deadline hasn't passed yet), fall back to currentGW-1. This means as soon as deadline passes, your app shows the new squad including this week's transfers. The squad confidence reflects the team you're actually fielding.

This is straightforward and doesn't require auth. ~30 minutes of work.

Option B — Show pre-deadline draft squad (requires auth).

To see your team's draft state before deadline locks it, you'd need to authenticate against FPL on behalf of the user. FPL doesn't have an official OAuth flow — apps that do this typically scrape the user's session cookie or ask for login credentials, which is fragile and risky. Most third-party FPL tools don't attempt this.

My recommendation: Option A.

The right semantic for the app is "show me the squad I'm fielding for points this gameweek." Once deadline locks, that's exactly what /event/{currentGW}/picks/ returns. Before deadline, the API can't help us — but at that point the user hasn't actually committed to anything anyway, so showing them the most recent locked squad (last GW) is reasonable.

This is genuinely a bug in v1's behavior. The Free Hit fix from v1.1 partially addresses a related issue (showing the real pre-FH squad instead of the disposable one), but the underlying "always fetch currentGW-1" assumption is the bigger issue that causes the staleness.

Should this be a v1.4 patch or v2 work?

It's the same shape as v1.1 — a trust fix for an existing surface. Small change, no recompute, no algorithm impact. Probably 30 minutes including tests. Worth doing now before we move to v2 because:

You'll use the app this weekend (probably). Deadline is Saturday. If you make transfers Friday and load the app Saturday morning, you want to see your real team.
It's a clean small change that doesn't touch v1's algorithmic story.
Skipping it means going into v2 with a known stale-data issue on the most personal surface of the app.
Tap your answer.

A small note: the Free Hit fix in v1.1 already does the conceptual work of "fetch and check, fall back if needed." The v1.4 fix is the same shape — try the most recent gameweek, fall back if 404 — just on a different axis (deadline-not-passed instead of free-hit-active). Claude will recognize the pattern and the implementation will be quick.

You said: Q: Ship the post-deadline fetch as v1.
Q: Ship the post-deadline fetch as v1.4 or defer?
A: Ship as v1.4 now — small focused patch (recommended)

Apr 26
Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. Final v1 patch.

Send this to Claude:

v1.4 — Post-deadline squad fetch on My Team page.

Problem
The My Team page currently fetches /api/entry/{team_id}/event/{currentGW-1}/picks/ (most recently completed gameweek). This means after a user makes transfers and the gameweek deadline passes, the page still shows their old squad until the gameweek ends 24-48 hours later.

The right semantic: show the squad the user is currently fielding for points. Once deadline has locked the current GW, that's the current GW's picks. Before deadline, the most recent locked squad is the previous GW's picks.

Fix
Change the squad fetch logic in the My Team data load:

// Try current GW first (will be locked if deadline has passed)
let targetGameweek = currentGameweek
let picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)

if (picks === null /_ 404, deadline hasn't passed yet _/) {
targetGameweek = currentGameweek - 1
picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)
}

// Then existing Free Hit fallback logic
if (picks.active_chip === 'freehit') {
targetGameweek = targetGameweek - 1
picks = await fetchEntryPicks(teamId, targetGameweek)
}
Order matters: try-current-then-fallback to last-completed FIRST, then apply Free Hit fallback. A Free Hit on the current gameweek would still need to fall back to the previous non-FH gameweek.

Implementation
Modify the squad fetch logic in My Team's data load path.
Handle the 404 cleanly. fetchEntryPicks already returns Result<EntryPicks, FetchError> from the v1.1 work. The 404 case is a recoverable error type — distinguish it from "real" errors (network failure, server error) and silently fall back. Other error types should still surface.
UI indicator update — preserve existing Free Hit indicator behavior. When the app falls back to the previous gameweek for any reason (deadline not yet passed OR Free Hit active), the user should know which GW the squad reflects. Two cases to handle in the indicator:
Pre-deadline (most-recent GW returned 404, fell back to currentGW-1): "Showing your locked squad from GW{n}"
Free Hit (most-recent GW returned freehit chip, fell back to currentGW-2 or-1): "Showing your pre-Free Hit squad from GW{n}" (existing v1.1 wording)
Combined edge case (both pre-deadline AND last GW was Free Hit): "Showing your pre-Free Hit squad from GW{n}" — the FH indicator takes priority because it's more informative.
Normal case (current GW locked, no FH): no indicator shown.
Update the cached manager_squads entry's gameweek to reflect the resolved gameweek (whatever we end up displaying). This means the cache key correctly tracks which squad we're storing.
Edge case: GW1 hasn't started yet. If the user connects pre-season or before GW1 deadline, both currentGW and currentGW-1 will return 404. Show a designed empty state: "Your team will appear once the season starts." Don't crash, don't show the connect form again.
Tests
Add to existing My Team test suite:

Unit test on squad fetch logic: given a 404 on currentGW, confirms the function falls back to currentGW-1 and returns it. Given a 200 on currentGW, confirms no fallback occurs.
Unit test for the GW1 edge case: both gameweeks return 404, function returns null/error appropriately.
Combined fallback test: currentGW returns 404 AND currentGW-1 returns Free Hit chip → fallback to currentGW-2, indicator shows "pre-Free Hit" wording.
Component test: indicator wording renders correctly for each case (pre-deadline / Free Hit / combined).
Verification
Tests green.
Manual test against team 231177: load /my-team and confirm what shows up. Note: depending on when this is run, the behavior will differ:
If the next deadline has passed (Saturday after 11am UK): should show the current GW's locked squad.
If we're before the next deadline (Sunday-Friday): should show the previous GW's locked squad with the indicator.
Either case is correct; just confirm the right indicator appears.
API verification: check what currentGW actually is and whether picks for that GW exist:
curl -s "https://fantasy.premierleague.com/api/bootstrap-static/" | jq '.events[] | select(.is_current == true) | {id, deadline_time, finished, is_current, is_next}'
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://fantasy.premierleague.com/api/entry/231177/event/<currentGW>/picks/"
Paste both. The HTTP code tells us whether deadline has passed (200 = yes, locked; 404 = no, draft only).
Screenshot of My Team page showing whichever resolved squad + indicator (or no indicator if normal case).
Sign-off package
Diff of squad fetch logic
Test results (current count + new tests)
API verification output (current GW + HTTP code for the picks endpoint)
Screenshot of My Team with appropriate indicator
Brief note: did the fix actually fire (i.e., did currentGW return 404 and trigger the fallback) or did currentGW return 200 (deadline passed, no fallback needed)? Either case is correct, just want to know which path was exercised.
Don't proceed to v2 until I sign off.

A note for you, the human:

When this lands, the My Team behavior becomes correct in all four real-world scenarios:

Mid-week (e.g., Wednesday): Last GW just ended, you've made transfers but next deadline is days away. App shows last GW's squad with "showing locked squad from GW{n}" indicator. Honest.
Saturday before deadline: Same as above — your draft isn't visible to anyone yet, app shows last GW's locked squad.
Saturday after deadline (post-11am): Deadline passed, your transfers are now in the locked current GW squad. App shows current GW with no indicator. Real-time correct.
During matches / Sunday: Same as #3 — current GW locked, points accumulating, your app reflects who's actually playing for you.
This is the squad-handling story the app should have had from v1.0. It just took until you started using it to surface the gap. That's normal and expected — and the fact we're patching it cleanly before v2 means v2 starts on a solid foundation.

Send it.
