Here's the prompt to paste before you go:

v2.0 expansion. I'll be away ~3 days. Build these items autonomously without sign-off between checkpoints. Same TDD + screenshot discipline as v1.

Work in this exact order. Don't reorder. If you hit a clean phase boundary that needs my judgment, document the question + your recommended answer, then move to the next item.

Item A — #23 GW scrubber on My Team (start here, ~6-8h)
Allow users to navigate to any past gameweek and see what their team looked like + Team Confidence at that point.

Data layer: already supported. manager_squads.(team_id, gameweek) and confidence_snapshots.(player_id, gameweek) give us the join. No schema changes.

UX — horizontal scrolling timeline:

Render gameweeks 1 through current_gameweek as small clickable pills in a horizontal row
Selected GW: filled accent background, bold text
Unselected GW with squad data available: muted background, normal text
GWs the user wasn't connected for (no manager_squads row): visible but at lower opacity, label "—" instead of "GW{n}", non-clickable
Default selected: most recent GW with data (matches v1.4 Free Hit / pre-deadline resolution logic — reuse that function, don't rewrite)
Mobile: horizontal scroll with snap-to-pill, selected always visible
On selecting a different GW:

Reload squad picks for that GW (fetch from API or read from cache, prefer cache)
Update Team Confidence percentage and positional breakdown for that GW
Update Starting XI / Bench rosters
Update timestamp footer to reflect the historical sync
The "Connect team" form section stays unaffected
Edge cases:

GW1 with no data: timeline still shows GW1 as non-clickable
Free Hit gameweek selected: respect existing v1.4 logic — show the FH squad with appropriate indicator (don't fall back further when explicitly selected by user)
Pre-deadline current GW: show indicator the squad is from the resolved fallback GW
Tests: unit test for timeline rendering, click → squad reload, default-to-latest-resolved, axe clean.

Sign-off package: tests green + screenshots of My Team page with 3 different GWs selected (latest, mid-season, GW1), light + dark = 6 screenshots.

Commit with feat(my-team): historical gameweek scrubber.

Item B — #11 Phase 1: Multi-user infrastructure foundations (~10-14h)
This is foundational work that prepares for multi-user support. Do NOT add user-facing auth UI in this phase — that's Phase 3 after we choose the provider.

What's in scope for Phase 1:

User schema design. Add users table to the SQLite schema (we'll migrate to Postgres in Phase 3, but the schema design lives now):
sql
CREATE TABLE users (
id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, -- UUID, will become external auth ID later
created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
updated_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
-- preferences columns to be added as needed by other features
);
User-aware repository extensions. Every existing repository that holds user-specific data needs a user_id foreign key:
manager_squads → add user_id column
Any settings/preferences tables → add user_id
DO NOT modify players, teams, confidence_snapshots, sync_meta — these are global data, not per-user
Update repository methods to require user_id for user-specific reads/writes. Methods on global data stay unchanged.
Migration tooling. Write idempotent migration scripts that:
Create users table
Create a default user (SYSTEM_USER with hardcoded UUID) for the existing single-user data
Backfill existing manager_squads rows with the SYSTEM_USER's user_id
Migration is forward-only; document rollback strategy in a README
Application layer updates. Server Actions and route handlers that operate on user-specific data need to receive user_id. For Phase 1, they all hardcode SYSTEM_USER (no auth yet). The point is to get the wiring right.
Decision document for Phase 2. Write docs/v2/multi-user-decisions.md with three decision questions:
Auth provider: Clerk (paid, easiest), Auth.js (free, more setup), Supabase Auth (free + bundles Postgres). For each: cost, setup complexity, lock-in concerns, FPL-relevant pros/cons. Your recommendation: \_**\_.
Postgres host: Vercel Postgres (tightest integration), Supabase (bundles auth), Neon (cheapest serverless), Turso (closest to current SQLite). For each: cost, setup, FPL-relevant pros/cons. Your recommendation: \_\_**.
User data model: does a user have one FPL team or multiple? Can teams be shared? My recommendation for v2.0: one team per user, no sharing — simplest. Document tradeoffs.
Each decision should have your honest recommendation with reasoning. The user will pick from the options.
What's NOT in this phase:

Auth UI or auth flows
Actual Postgres migration (stays SQLite for now)
Login/logout endpoints
Per-user permissions UI
Tests: repository tests with explicit user_id parameter, migration test confirming SYSTEM_USER backfill works, axe clean (no UI changes).

Sign-off package: tests green + the decision document + diff summary of schema and repository changes.

Commit with feat(multi-user): phase 1 schema and repository foundations.

Item C — #1 Phase 1+2: Probability metrics calculator + backtest (~12-16h)
Translate the spec at fpl_probability_algorithm.md v1.3 (and the patch document I wrote, if it exists in docs/v2/) into working code, plus build the validation framework.

Phase 1 — Calculator:

src/lib/probability/ — new module mirroring src/lib/confidence/ structure. index.ts, types.ts, calculator.ts, calculator.test.ts, **fixtures**/, README.md.
Implement v1.3 spec exactly, including:
Per-90 conversion + shrinkage for low-minute players
Position-adjusted percentile normalization
Defender threat scaling (DEFENDER_THREAT_SCALE = 0.35)
Effective position override (effective_role field NOT included per the patch's Gap C decision)
Match openness factor combining both teams' FDR
Optional form-weighted FDR adjustment (FORM_WEIGHT = 0.0 default)
CRITICAL: apply Gap A from the patch — introduce BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH = 12 and use it correctly in lambda calculation. Don't ship the v1.3 Poisson interpretation as-is.
Apply Gap D from the patch — team_conversion_factor for assist lambda only
Caps: MAX_GOAL_PROB = 0.65, MAX_ASSIST_PROB = 0.55
TDD approach. Write tests from the spec's Test Cases section first (sanity tests for high-percentile striker vs FDR 1, vs FDR 5, both-teams effect, position fairness, low-minutes regression, GK returns 0, caps). Confirm failing, then implement.
Pure function — no I/O, takes player + fixture + league_data, returns {p_goal, p_assist}.
Phase 2 — Backtest harness:

Per the patch's "Validation & calibration framework" section:

scripts/run-backtest.ts — pulls historical FPL data from history_past for last 2 completed seasons (2023/24 and 2024/25). For each (player, gameweek) pair where minutes > 0, compute predicted P(Goal) and P(Assist) using only data available BEFORE that gameweek. Store predictions + actuals in a backtest table.
Calibration analysis function: bin predictions into 10% buckets, compute observed event rate per bucket, generate the calibration data structure described in the patch.
docs/v2/calibration-results.md — auto-generated report with:
Total predictions made (goal + assist separately)
Mean absolute calibration error overall + by position
Brier scores vs baseline
Bucket-by-bucket table
Pass/fail against acceptance criteria (MACE < 5pp overall, no bucket > 10pp off, Brier below baseline)
Recommendation: ship to UI / iterate on calibration / abandon
Optional: SVG calibration plot if Recharts can produce something readable. Skip if it'd take more than 1 hour.
What's NOT in this phase:

UI display of probabilities (waits for user evaluation of calibration)
Player detail page integration
Leaderboard sorted by P(Goal) etc.
Tests: calculator tests from spec + property tests (probabilities always in [0,1], GK always 0, etc.), backtest harness tests with synthetic data.

Sign-off package: tests green + calibration report + brief recommendation note from Claude on whether the model meets acceptance criteria as currently specified.

If anything substantial would need fixing, document it for me rather than fixing it yourself.

Working style for this 3-day session
Order matters strictly. Finish A before starting B, finish B before starting C. This way if you stall mid-session, the simpler items have shipped.
Commit each item separately with conventional commit messages.
Don't wait for sign-off between items, but DO commit before moving to the next item — that way if I come back early, I can review what's done.
Decision documents for B Phase 2 and C calibration evaluation are part of the sign-off, not a deferred follow-up.
If you hit a real blocker (test you can't make pass, schema decision you genuinely need from me), document clearly and skip to the next item. Don't burn the whole session on one issue.
No scope creep. If you finish all four items with time to spare, write a polish report — don't start additional v2 items.
Combined sign-off when all done (or when you've stopped on a blocker): one summary listing what shipped per item, what's open, what's blocked.

1. Save the patch document to your repo. The probability calculator implementation in Item C depends on Claude reading both fpl_probability_algorithm.md v1.3 and my patch. When you return, you'll have substantial v2.0 progress and clear next-step decisions waiting. Sleep well, travel safely.

Worth filing this as a process lesson: autonomous work needs all referenced specs physically present in the codebase before kickoff, not just mentioned in conversation context. I should have asked you to confirm the spec was saved before drafting the prompt. My miss.

Now let me look at what did land:

Item A (GW scrubber) — GwTimeline component, available-GW set merging (handles users joining mid-season), per-GW data refetch, 6 screenshots, 573 tests green. The "available-GW set merging" detail is the kind of subtle thing that matters in real usage — users who connected their team in GW20 won't have data for GW1-19 and the timeline correctly shows that as non-clickable rather than crashing or misrendering. Good defensive design.

Item B (multi-user Phase 1) — Cleanly executed. The user_id parameter being added as the first argument (rather than appended at the end) is the right convention — it makes the user-scoped nature of the call obvious at every call site rather than buried among other parameters. The idempotent migration with DEFAULT 1 for the existing single-user data means production can apply the schema change without breaking. The SYSTEM_USER_ID threading through the API route is correct — keeps the architectural change quiet at the boundary while making it real underneath.

The decision document is what unlocks Phase 2. Let me dig in.

Reviewing Claude's Phase 2 decision recommendations:

Auth: NextAuth (Auth.js) v5 — solid, defensible choice. Free, mature, broad provider support. Reasonable default. Two real considerations:

It has a steeper config learning curve than Clerk, particularly around session callbacks and adapter setup
The v5 (now called Auth.js v5) was beta for a long time; check if it's stable as of your current build date
For a single-developer FPL app where you're the only user for a while, Clerk's "drop in and it works" might genuinely save 4-6 hours of setup time vs Auth.js, at the cost of vendor lock-in and ~$20/month after free tier
I'd weight Clerk slightly higher than Claude did for this specific project — the lock-in concern is overstated when you can swap auth providers fairly cleanly in v3+, and the time savings now matter more than ideological purity about lock-in. But NextAuth/Auth.js is genuinely fine. Either works.

DB: Turso — interesting pick. Turso is Cloudflare-backed SQLite-as-a-service, which makes it the lowest-friction migration from your current SQLite. The recommendation is honest about that: "closest to current SQLite." But there's a real concern worth flagging:

Turso's pricing model and longevity are less established than Postgres alternatives. If Turso shuts down or pivots, you're migrating again. Postgres on Vercel/Supabase/Neon is boring and durable.

Counter: Turso is actively used in production, has reasonable funding, and being SQLite-flavored means migration to alternatives is technically easier than starting on Postgres.

I'd actually push back gently here. Vercel Postgres or Supabase Postgres is the safer call for a single-developer project where you might monetize. Both are dead-stable, both integrate cleanly with Next.js, both have free tiers that cover personal use, both make the eventual scaling story straightforward. The "preserve SQLite-ness" argument is real but loses against "ten years from now, Postgres is still Postgres."

But this is genuinely your call. If you want minimum migration friction now, Turso. If you want maximum durability, Postgres.

User data model: one team per user, no sharing — completely right. The simplest model that serves the immediate use case. Multi-team and team-sharing can be added later if real demand emerges. Don't pre-build for hypothetical complexity.

Where this leaves us:

Item C blocked is recoverable in 5 minutes. Save the v1.3 probability spec + my patch document to docs/v2/ and Claude can pick it up next session. I should have done this for you upfront — that's on me.

The two decisions I want from you:

Tap your two answers when ready.

A note on what's next: once you decide, the workflow is:

Optionally start another autonomous round if you'll be away again.
Whichever auth + DB you pick, I'll back you. Both Claude's choices and my counter-recommendations are defensible — the difference between them is preference, not correctness. The worst call here would be deliberating for a week and shipping nothing.

docs/v2/fpl_probability_algorithm.md — v1.3 spec (canonical)

Read both, then implement per the patch's recommended v2 implementation order at the bottom:

Phase 1 — Calculator
src/lib/probability/ — new module mirroring src/lib/confidence/ structure: index.ts, types.ts, calculator.ts, calculator.test.ts, **fixtures**/, README.md.
Implement v1.3 spec with patches A, C, D applied:
Per-90 conversion + shrinkage for low-minute players (v1.3 Step 1)
Position-adjusted percentile normalization (v1.3 Step 2) including DEFENDER_THREAT_SCALE = 0.35
Effective position override (v1.3 Step 2.3) — but DROP effective_role field per Patch Gap C
Match openness factor combining both teams' FDR (v1.3 Step 3)
Patch Gap A: introduce BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH = 12. Lambda calculation: team_events _ p_per_event _ minutes_factor, NOT xg_team _ p_per_event _ minutes_factor. Use the patch's Option A.1 specifically.
Patch Gap D: add team_conversion_factor to lambda_assist only (NOT lambda_goal). LEAGUE_AVG_CONVERSION = 0.33. Bound the factor to [0.5, 1.5]. Use season-cumulative for v2.0 (rolling-5 is a v2.x refinement).
Caps: MAX_GOAL_PROB = 0.65, MAX_ASSIST_PROB = 0.55
GK: p_goal = 0, p_assist suppressed to 0.05× normal value
TDD approach. Write tests from v1.3 spec's "Test Cases to Implement" section first (sanity tests for high-percentile striker vs FDR 1, vs FDR 5, both-teams effect, position fairness, low-minutes regression, GK returns 0, caps). Plus property tests: probabilities always in [0,1], GK always 0, lambda_assist > lambda_goal for high-Creativity low-Threat players, etc. Confirm failing, then implement.
Pure function — no I/O. Takes player + fixture + league_data, returns {p_goal, p_assist}.
Phase 2 — Backtest harness
Per the patch's "Validation & calibration framework" section:

scripts/run-backtest.ts — pulls historical FPL data from history_past for the last 2 completed seasons. For each (player, gameweek) pair where minutes > 0, compute predicted P(Goal) and P(Assist) using only data available BEFORE that gameweek (no future leakage). Store predictions + actuals in a backtest_predictions table.
Calibration analysis function — bin into 10% buckets, compute observed event rate per bucket, generate the calibration data structure described in the patch.
docs/v2/calibration-results.md — auto-generated report with:
Total predictions made (goal + assist separately)
Mean absolute calibration error overall + by position
Brier scores vs baseline
Bucket-by-bucket table
Pass/fail against the patch's acceptance criteria (MACE < 5pp overall, no bucket > 10pp off, Brier below baseline, position-stratified MACE also passes)

Set piece exposure (Patch Gap E — deferred)

Phase 2A — Postgres migration
Add Supabase client as a dependency. Use @supabase/ssr for the Next.js integration (App Router compatible).
Translate the SQLite schema to Postgres. All tables from src/lib/db/schema.ts need Postgres-flavored equivalents:
SQLite types → Postgres types (TEXT → TEXT, INTEGER → INTEGER, REAL → DOUBLE PRECISION, BOOLEAN handling, etc.)
Use Supabase migrations (supabase/migrations/ dir) — versioned SQL files
The users table from Phase 1 maps to auth.users (managed by Supabase) — drop our local users table, use Supabase's
All user_id references update from our SYSTEM_USER_ID (integer) to Supabase auth auth.users.id (UUID)
Per-user data isolation via Row Level Security (RLS):
Enable RLS on manager_squads and any other user-scoped tables
Policies: users can only SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE rows where user_id = auth.uid()
Global tables (players, teams, confidence_snapshots, sync_meta) — RLS disabled or read-allow-all (these are app data, not user data)
Repository layer rewrite. The Sqlite*Repository classes from v1 need Postgres equivalents (Postgres*Repository). Same interfaces — the abstraction from Step 4 finally pays off. The factory in src/lib/db/index.ts switches from createSqliteRepositories to createPostgresRepositories.
Data migration tooling. Write a one-time script that:
Reads everything from current SQLite data/fpl.db
Writes it to the new Supabase Postgres
For manager_squads rows: prompt the user (or document the manual step) to associate the SYSTEM_USER squad with their actual Supabase user ID
Document rollback strategy
Phase 2B — Supabase Auth integration
Auth UI:
/login route — email + password, magic link option, Google OAuth (Supabase makes all three easy)
/signup route — same, with explicit terms acknowledgment
/auth/callback route handler for OAuth flows
Logout flow — button in Settings that calls Supabase signOut
Middleware:
middleware.ts at project root that protects user-specific routes (My Team, Settings)
Redirects unauthenticated users to /login
Public routes (Dashboard, Players list, Player detail) stay accessible without auth
Server-side auth context:
Helper to get current user's Supabase ID in Server Components and Server Actions
Replace SYSTEM_USER_ID hardcodes with the real auth-derived ID
The repository methods that take user_id first parameter now receive the real ID
My Team data model adjustment:
When a user logs in for the first time and visits My Team, show the Connect form (existing)
Their team_id is stored in manager_squads.user_id matching their Supabase ID
Two different users can both load team 231177 (or any team_id) — each sees the same FPL data but their own preferences/watchlists
Phase 2C — Tests + verification
Repository tests rewritten for Postgres — use a Supabase test project or a Postgres testcontainer
E2E test: sign up → connect FPL team → see My Team → log out → log in → still see same team
RLS policy tests: confirm User A cannot read User B's manager_squads
What's NOT in this phase
Multi-team support (per Phase 1 decision: one team per user, no sharing)
Email customization beyond defaults
Password reset flows (Supabase handles via email but no custom UI)
User profile management (name, avatar) — defer

Claude responded: Two real bugs visible in the screenshots, both worth diagnosing carefully before any fix attempt.
Two real bugs visible in the screenshots, both worth diagnosing carefully before any fix attempt. Let me think through what's actually happening.

Bug 1: Player percentages corrupt after navigating away and back to GW34.

Comparing the screenshots:

Correct state (image 3 — original GW34 load):

Team Confidence: 82.0%
Defence: 90% / Midfield: 66% / Attack: 90%
B.Fernandes 100%, Mbeumo 38%, Bowen 90%, Thiago 90%, Hill 80%, Haaland 100%
Corrupt state (image 1 — after clicking GW33 then back to GW34):

Team Confidence: 70.2%
Defence: 82.5% / Midfield: 58.0% / Attack: 70.0%
B.Fernandes 50%, Mbeumo 50%, Bowen 90%, Thiago 50%, Hill 50%, Haaland 50%
A bunch of players are now showing 50% (the neutral baseline) where they should show their real values. This is state pollution from the GW switch. Specifically, it looks like when the user navigates to GW33 and back, the snapshot lookup is failing for many players — and the failure case is rendering 50% instead of the player's actual GW34 confidence.

The 50% value is significant: it's the percentage representation of confidence value 0. So either:

The lookup is returning null and the fallback is "show 0 = 50%" instead of "show the most recent available value"
The lookup is reading from the wrong gameweek (GW33's data being rendered against GW34's layout)
The state isn't resetting properly between GW navigations
The fact that Bowen stays at 90% while others change is interesting — Bowen's value is the same in both screenshots. That might mean Bowen happens to have a snapshot at exactly the GW the lookup is hitting incorrectly, while others don't.

Bug 2: Only GW28, 32, 33, 34 shown in the timeline.

The user has data for many more gameweeks (the season is at GW34, the user's been connected since at least GW28 based on what's visible). But only 4 GWs are clickable in the scrubber.

Looking at the available-set logic Claude built in Item A — the scrubber shows GWs where manager_squads has a row for that team. If the user only connected mid-season or if some squads weren't fetched/persisted, those GWs appear as non-clickable dashes.

But this doesn't fully explain it. The user has been connected since well before GW28 (the build's been running for weeks; team data would have been persisted across multiple syncs). More likely: the on-demand-fetch logic isn't backfilling historical GWs. The system only stores manager_squads rows for GWs the user has explicitly viewed or the original connect-fetch resolved to. Switching to GW33 in the scrubber should fetch GW33's squad on demand — and apparently does, because clicking it works — but the timeline only shows the GWs already in the DB, not all GWs the user could potentially fetch.

So the fix for Bug 2 is conceptually: the timeline should show all gameweeks from the user's start to current as clickable, fetching squad data on demand when clicked, rather than only showing GWs that already have cached squad data.

Bug 0.A — Player percentages corrupt after GW navigation

The lookup is hitting the wrong gameweek's snapshots

That's two distinct workstreams. Together: ~14-20 hours of focused Claude work. Comfortably fits in 3 days even with verification cycles.

Three honest considerations before we lump:

1. The scrubber bugs MUST be fixed before Item C starts.

Reason: if there's a state-management bug in Item A's GW navigation, it might share a root cause with how the calculator consumes per-GW data. Fixing the scrubber bugs surfaces the real architecture; building the probability calculator on top of broken state handling could compound the problem.

So sequence is: scrubber fixes first, then Item C. Even when lumped in one prompt, the order matters.

2. Item C has a natural stopping point that's autonomous-safe.

Item C breaks into Phase 1 (calculator with TDD) and Phase 2 (backtest harness + calibration report). Phase 1 is pure code translation of a locked spec — fully autonomous-safe. Phase 2 includes the backtest run and an honest recommendation on whether the model passes acceptance criteria — also autonomous-safe because Claude is just reporting findings, not making product decisions.

Phase 3 — UI integration of the probabilities — IS NOT autonomous-safe. It depends on the calibration result ("did the model pass?") plus your judgment on what to display where. Claude should NOT touch UI integration even if Phase 1 + 2 finish early.

So the right scope for this 3-day window is: scrubber bugs + Item C Phase 1 + Item C Phase 2. Stop there. Don't bleed into UI work.

3. What Claude does with leftover time matters.

If everything finishes early (say, day 2.5), Claude needs an explicit "stop work and write a status report" instruction. Otherwise, the unspecified-defaults problem — Claude looks for the next thing to do and starts something we'd want to scope ourselves. This is what happened in the previous autonomous round when Claude correctly stopped at Item C blocked rather than inventing an algorithm.

You said: I've read both documents carefully.
I've read both documents carefully. Before writing any code, I need to flag one genuine technical incoherence and one data availability constraint — per the user's warning about the reconstruction.
Issue 1 — team_event_strength is defined in Step 3 but never consumed (incoherence)
Step 3 computes:

team_event_strength = xg_player_team _ (openness_factor / 1.0)
= xg_player_team _ openness_factor
Step 4 (both original and patched) then uses xg_player_team directly, not team_event_strength:

team_events = BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH _ (xg_player_team / BASELINE_TEAM_GOALS_PER_MATCH)
team_event_strength is a dead computation. This matters quantitatively — the difference between the two options is up to 40% on team_events:
OptionFormulaEffectA — use xg_player_team (as written)12 _ (xg_player_team / 1.4)Only player-team FDR affects lambdas; opponent FDR is ignored in the final calculationB — use team_event_strength12 \* (xg_player_team × openness_factor / 1.4)Both teams' FDRs feed into the lambda; two-FDR-1 match amplifies the player by ~40% vs a neutral match
Option B is more conceptually coherent — the whole point of Step 3 is that match openness (both teams' attacking strength) should influence player probabilities. Option A would make the opponent's FDR computation in Step 3 purely decorative for per-player lambda computation.
My read: Option B is almost certainly what was intended, and xg_player_team in Step 4 is a reconstruction slip — it should be team_event_strength. But I need your confirmation before I code it, because this affects every predicted value.
Issue 2 — Backtest data availability
Gap B specifies a per-(player, gameweek) backtest over "2023/24 and 2024/25 (most recent two completed seasons)." The FPL API's element_summary/{id} returns:

history — current-season per-GW records ✓
history_past — season-aggregate totals only per past season ✗ (no per-GW rows)
Per-GW data for 2023/24 is not retrievable from the current FPL API. The backtest can run against the current season (2024/25) and produce a real calibration curve, but it will cover one season of GWs (GW4–GW38, per the spec's "exclude GW1–3" rule) rather than two.
My read: This doesn't block writing the harness, but the calibration report will say "n=~500–700 predictions" (single season) rather than ~1400. The acceptance criteria still apply; we just have less data. Flagging so you can decide whether to widen the data source or proceed with single-season.
Decision I need from you:

Step 4 lambdas — should they use xg_player_team (as literally written) or team_event_strength (which is what makes Step 3's opponent-FDR computation matter)?
Backtest — proceed with current-season data only, or do you have a way to supply 2023/24 per-GW data?

Apr 26
Claude responded: Both issues are real.
Both issues are real. Claude is right to surface them and right not to proceed without your call. Let me give you my honest read on both, then you decide.

Issue 1 — team_event_strength vs xg_player_team in Step 4

Claude's diagnosis is correct. This is a real incoherence in the spec, and yes, it's likely a reconstruction slip on my part — but I need to be honest that I can't be 100% certain whether the original v1.3 had this same bug or whether I dropped a substitution when reconstructing.

What I am certain about: conceptually, Option B (team_event_strength) is right. Here's why:

The entire purpose of Step 3 is to model that "two attacking teams playing each other" produces more goals for both sides than "two defensive teams playing each other," even when each team's individual FDR multiplier is the same. That's the whole point of the openness_factor calculation. If Step 4 uses xg_player_team directly without multiplying by openness, then opponent FDR is literally purely decorative — it has no effect on any output. That can't be what the algorithm intends.

The Option A interpretation (xg_player_team as written) would mean the spec computes openness for show. That's not a serious algorithm.

Recommendation: use team_event_strength (Option B) in Step 4 lambda computation.

I'd phrase the patch instruction as: "Step 4 should use team_event_strength (computed in Step 3) as the input to the lambda calculation, not xg_player_team directly. The reconstructed spec contains a likely transcription error here. The Gap A patch's team_events formula should read: team_events = BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH \* (team_event_strength / BASELINE_TEAM_GOALS_PER_MATCH)."

This is a meaningful correction worth documenting clearly so it doesn't get re-introduced as drift later.

Issue 2 — Backtest data availability

Claude's finding is also correct, and it's a legitimate constraint I didn't account for in the patch. history_past in the FPL API genuinely is season-aggregate totals only. Per-gameweek granularity for past seasons isn't available through the public API.

Three real options here:

Option (a) — Single-season backtest, current season only.

Use 2025/26 data through the most recent completed gameweek. Excludes GW1-3 per the patch's warm-up rule. That gives roughly N = 524 players × ~31 played gameweeks / ~2.5 (assumed average appearance rate per player) ≈ 6,500 predictions for goal events alone. Plenty for calibration analysis at 10% buckets. The bucket sample sizes will be smaller than 2-season, but still statistically meaningful for the buckets that matter (the dense middle of the distribution where most predictions land).

The only real loss: less seasonal variance. A single season's calibration might be biased by anomalies specific to 2025/26 (a particular team's dominant season, an unusual injury pattern). Two seasons would average that out. With one season, your calibration is a snapshot in time.

For v2.0 ship, this is genuinely fine. We're validating "does the model work at all," not "is the model proven robust across multiple seasons." Robustness across seasons is something you build evidence for over time as more seasons of data accumulate.

Option (b) — External data source for 2023/24 per-GW data.

FPL data archives exist on GitHub — repos like vaastav/Fantasy-Premier-League mirror the API responses for past seasons. You could pull historical season data from there.

Cost: adds a third-party data dependency, even just for the backtest harness. Trust question: is the archive complete and accurate? Probably yes (vaastav's repo is widely used in the FPL analytics community), but it's another moving piece.

I'd avoid this for v2.0 unless single-season calibration shows worrying instability.

Option (c) — Walk-forward validation within the current season.

A different statistical approach: instead of "predict every GW with all prior GWs as training data," use a rolling validation where the model trains on GWs 1-N and predicts GW N+1, then trains on GWs 1-N+1 and predicts GW N+2, etc. This gives you N-3 prediction sets within a single season, each evaluated independently. Better than a static single-season backtest for detecting overfitting.

More sophisticated, more code. Probably overkill for v2.0.

Recommendation: Option (a) — single-season backtest.

Document the limitation clearly in the calibration report: "N predictions from current season only; multi-season validation deferred until either (1) the 2025/26 season completes and 2026/27 starts, providing a second full season, or (2) external data sources are integrated." That's honest about the constraint without blocking shipment.

One thing worth filing for v2.x:

Claude surfaced a real spec issue (the team_event_strength substitution) and a real data constraint (no past-season per-GW data). Both could have shipped silently as bugs if Claude had just powered through. The discipline of "stop and confirm before coding" worked here. Worth filing as a process pattern: algorithm specs should always have at least one calibration check between spec-locked and code-locked, ideally with someone who didn't write the spec. I'm too close to my own patch to spot this kind of substitution slip; Claude's fresh read caught it.

Send this back to Claude, then your sequencing for the 3-day autonomous lump:

Both decisions confirmed:

Issue 1: Use Option B — team_event_strength in Step 4 lambda computation, not xg_player_team. The reconstructed spec contained a transcription error. The Gap A patch's team_events formula should read:

team_events = BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH \* (team_event_strength / BASELINE_TEAM_GOALS_PER_MATCH)
Document this correction inline in the calculator code with a comment referencing this conversation, so future readers don't re-introduce the drift. Update docs/v2/fpl_probability_algorithm.md to fix the xg_player_team → team_event_strength substitution in Step 4. Add a small note to the changelog: "v1.3.1 — corrected Step 4 to use team_event_strength (the openness-adjusted value from Step 3) per implementation review."

Issue 2: Single-season backtest is fine for v2.0. Use 2025/26 data only, exclude GW1-3 per the warm-up rule. Get that answered before we do anything else.

Now — about the calibration finding itself.

Claude's diagnosis is genuinely good. Let me make sure I understand it before reacting:

The model is producing predicted P(Goal) values that are systematically too high — 46% of all predictions hit the 0.65 cap. Median outfield player produces lambda ≈ 3.0, which gives p_goal = 1 - exp(-3.0) ≈ 0.95, then gets capped at 0.65. That's the model saying "everyone scores most weeks," which is obviously wrong.

The proposed fix: lower BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH from 12 to ~1.

This is the right diagnosis but I want to push back gently on the proposed fix.

Setting the constant to 1 makes the math work, but the meaning of the constant becomes incoherent. "Baseline attacking events per match per team = 1" doesn't match physical reality (teams have 10-15 attacking events per match). The constant would be doing arithmetic without semantic meaning.

I think the real issue is somewhere else, and the constant just absorbs the error. Let me think out loud about what else could be wrong:

Looking at the formula: lambda = team_events × p_per_event × minutes_factor

For a median player, p_per_event = influence_pct × threat_pct ≈ 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.25. With team_events = 12 and minutes_factor = 1.0, lambda = 12 × 0.25 × 1.0 = 3.0. That's the problem case Claude identified.

But wait — p_per_event of 0.25 means "given an attacking event, this player has a 25% chance of being the one who scores." That's not what the percentile rank represents. A median Influence player isn't 50% likely to be involved in any attacking event — that would mean roughly half the team's attacking events go through them, which is wildly high.

The bug isn't the constant. It's the interpretation of percentile rank as probability.

A player at the 50th percentile of their position cohort doesn't have a 50% chance of involvement. The percentile rank is an ordering signal, not a probability. To convert it to probability, you'd need to scale it by the realistic share — for a striker, the top-percentile guy might be involved in 30-40% of his team's attacking events, the median maybe 5-10%.

The patch's Gap A discussion mentioned this concern (Option A.1 vs A.2 — the rename from p_goal_per_event to p_goal_per_xg), but the fix landed in the constant rather than the percentile interpretation.

Two possible fixes that preserve semantic coherence:

Fix 1: Scale the percentile by a realistic involvement ratio.

Instead of p_involved = influence_pct, use p_involved = influence_pct × MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO where MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO ≈ 0.15 (top-percentile player involved in ~15% of attacking events, median in ~7.5%). Same idea for p_goal_given_involved.

This makes percentile rank do what it should — order players within position — and a separate constant captures the realistic mapping from "rank" to "probability."

Fix 2: Skip the percentile interpretation entirely and use raw rates.

Per-90 ICT divided by total league per-90 gives a true share. Use that share directly. More principled but requires rebuilding the percentile pipeline.

Fix 3 (Claude's): Set the events constant to 1.

Mathematically equivalent to introducing an unnamed "involvement fudge factor" of 1/12. The math works but the spec becomes harder to reason about.

My honest read:

I'd recommend Fix 1 — introduce an explicit MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO constant. It surfaces the real issue (percentiles aren't probabilities) and keeps the model's narrative coherent.

But here's the harder question: the calibration framework worked. It surfaced a real model bug before any UI shipped. That's exactly what the patch's validation framework was supposed to do. The fact that we're now debugging the model is the system functioning correctly.

Whether you want to recalibrate now or accept the failed verdict and revisit later is a real decision. Three options:

Option A: Fix the model now (Fix 1 above), re-run backtest, ship if it passes. Option B: Accept failed verdict, file model recalibration for v2.x, move to other v2 items first (pitch view, etc.). Option C: Fix with Claude's proposed constant change (Fix 3), accept the spec coherence cost.

I'd lean Option A if you have time before the trip ends, Option B if you're tired of algorithm work and want to move on, Option C only if the others feel too heavy.

Tap your answer once you've also confirmed the Item 0 status.

A note on what just happened: this is genuinely the calibration framework working as designed. We built the patch's validation framework specifically to catch "model exists in code but is systematically wrong." It caught it. Now we have a real signal (46% of predictions hit cap, percentile-as-probability is the likely root cause) to act on.

The calibration report Claude wrote is also worth keeping as a v2 artifact — it documents the failed iteration and the diagnostic reasoning. Future calibration work can reference it.

Confirm Item 0 status, decide on Option A/B/C, and we'll proceed.

Calibration fix using Option A — explicit MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO constant.

Diagnosis recap
The backtest revealed that 46% of predictions hit MAX_GOAL_PROB = 0.65. Median outfield player produces lambda ≈ 3.0 → p_goal ≈ 0.95 → capped. The root cause is interpreting percentile rank as a probability. A player at the 50th percentile of their position cohort doesn't have a 50% chance of being involved in any attacking event — that's wildly high. Percentile rank is an ordering signal, not a probability.

The fix
Introduce a new constant MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO and apply it to scale percentile-based "probabilities" into realistic involvement shares.

In constants.ts:

typescript
// Realistic per-event involvement ratio for a top-percentile player.
// Percentile rank (0..1) is an ordering signal within the position cohort,
// NOT a true probability. A top-percentile player is involved in ~15% of
// their team's attacking events; the median is ~7.5%. This constant scales
// percentile rank into realistic per-event involvement.
//
// Validation: starting value 0.15 derived from the principle that the most
// involved player on a team contributes to ~15% of attacking events.
// Calibrated against backtest in Phase 2; tune from real data.
export const MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15;
In Step 4 of the calculator:

Replace:

typescript
p_involved = influence_pct
p_goal_given_involved = threat_pct
p_assist_given_involved = creativity_pct
With:

typescript
p_involved = influence_pct _ MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_goal_given_involved = threat_pct _ MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_assist_given_involved = creativity_pct \* MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
The semantic interpretation now makes sense: a top-percentile player has 15% involvement; a median-percentile player has 7.5%; a 10th-percentile player has 1.5%. These are realistic per-event share rates for football.

Keep BASELINE_ATTACKING_EVENTS_PER_MATCH = 12 (semantically correct as the actual per-team event count). Do NOT lower it to 1 — the issue isn't the event count, it's the percentile interpretation.

Update v1.3 spec to reflect the new constant
Add MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15 to the Constants section of docs/v2/fpl_probability_algorithm.md.
Update Step 4 in the spec to show the scaled formulas:
p_involved = influence_pct _ MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_goal_given_involved = threat_pct _ MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_assist_given_involved = creativity_pct \* MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
Add a v1.3.2 changelog entry: "Added MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO constant to scale percentile rank into realistic per-event involvement share. Resolves systematic over-prediction discovered in Phase 2 backtest where 46% of predictions hit the goal probability cap."
Tests to update or add
Existing tests will need their expected values recalculated since the lambda magnitudes change. This is expected — the math is fundamentally different now.
Add a new test: "Median-percentile player produces realistic p_goal." A player at the 50th percentile of all three ICT components, vs FDR 3 opponent, with expected_minutes = 90, should produce p_goal in roughly the 0.05-0.15 range. NOT > 0.5.
Add a new test: "Top-percentile striker vs FDR 1 produces high but not capped p_goal." Should be 0.40-0.60, not pinned at 0.65.
Re-run backtest
After the constant fix and tests pass, re-run the backtest and regenerate docs/v2/calibration-results.md.

Expected results (rough predictions):

Cap-hit rate drops from 46% to under 5%
MACE overall should be much closer to acceptance threshold (< 5pp)
Position-stratified MACE may still show issues for FWD position (forwards score more, so the model has less margin for error)
Decision tree
If the recalibrated model passes all acceptance criteria:

Commit as fix(probability): scale percentile rank by MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
Update calibration-results.md to mark verdict ✅ PASS
Stop. Do not start UI integration without my approval.
If it still fails:

Document the new failure mode honestly in calibration-results.md
Recommend specific next step (further constant tuning, structural model change, abandon)
Stop. Don't iterate further without my input.
What stays the same
Item 0 fixes (whatever their current state — confirm separately)
Backtest harness itself (no changes needed)
Phase 1 calculator structure (only the constant + Step 4 formulas change)
All other patch decisions (Gap A team_events formula, Gap C effective_role removal, Gap D team conversion factor)
Don't proceed to UI integration regardless of outcome. That decision stays with the user after seeing the recalibration results.

Two notes for you, the human:

On the calibration cycle: what we're doing here is exactly how probability metrics get built. First implementation almost never calibrates correctly out of the box. The discipline is: build → backtest → identify systematic bias → diagnose root cause → adjust → re-backtest. We're now on iteration 2. It might take 2-3 more before calibration passes acceptance criteria. That's normal. The patch's validation framework is doing what it was designed to do.

On the constant value (0.15): I picked this from the rough heuristic that "the most-involved player on a team is involved in ~15% of attacking events." It's a starting point, not a calibrated value. After this iteration's backtest, we'll see whether 0.15 is approximately right or if we need 0.10 or 0.20. The calibration report will tell us which direction to tune.

Send the Item 0 status confirmation first. Get that answer. Then send the recalibration prompt.

Claude responded: New bug to add to the queue, and a real one — the My Team scrubber click handler is broken in a different way than the navigation-corruption bug I queued earli…
New bug to add to the queue, and a real one — the My Team scrubber click handler is broken in a different way than the navigation-corruption bug I queued earlier. Let me think through what's happening before drafting the lump prompt.

The new bug — clicking a GW pill triggers "team not valid" error.

This is suspicious in a specific way. "Team not valid, enter another team" is the error path that fires when the fetchEntryPicks call returns a 404 or invalid response. If clicking a GW pill is producing this error, it means the click handler is somehow either:

Triggering a re-fetch of the squad with an invalid team_id (clearing or corrupting the team_id during navigation)
Hitting the API with a malformed URL (wrong team_id, wrong gameweek, or some other parameter)
Treating the click as a "disconnect and re-validate" action instead of a "navigate to historical GW" action
Most likely culprit: the GW navigation logic is calling resolveSquadPicks or the API route with a missing or wrong parameter, and the route's error handling falls through to "invalid team" rather than failing more specifically.

This is plausibly the same underlying root cause as the original Bug 1 (player percentages corrupting after navigation) — both are state management bugs in the scrubber's click handler. Or they could be separate bugs that both surface during navigation. Diagnosis will tell us.

Important context: I haven't seen Claude's response on Item 0 status from before. We don't actually know if Bug 1 (50% corruption) and Bug 2 (missing GWs) were ever addressed. The new bug you just flagged might be the same Bug 1, or it might be additional. The diagnostic needs to cover all three.

Suspected cause: the GW click handler is somehow calling fetchEntryPicks with a malformed or missing team_id, OR the navigation is triggering a disconnect-and-revalidate flow instead of a historical-fetch flow. Check MyTeamPageClient.tsx, the GW timeline component, and resolveSquadPicks for how team_id flows through the click path.

Claude responded: Welcome back.
Welcome back. Both items landed cleanly. The discipline held.

A few things worth noting before we decide what's next:

Item 0 — All three scrubber bugs fixed. The diagnosis for 0.A is interesting: the error handler was disconnecting the user instead of restoring previous state when historical GW navigation hit any error. That's a real bug class — error handling that's too aggressive. The fix (restore existingState instead of disconnect) is the right surgical change. This kind of "error path does the wrong thing" bug is the hardest to catch in tests because the happy path looks fine and the error path requires deliberately constructing a failure. Good catch by Claude on the diagnosis.

Item D — Calibration honest verdict. Claude correctly identified this as not-shippable and stopped at the two-attempt boundary. The result is what we hoped the validation framework would produce: an honest "this isn't ready yet" with specific diagnosis rather than a "good enough" silent ship. That's the framework working as designed.

The structural finding is genuinely important: "single global constant can't calibrate all positions." This is the kind of insight that only emerges from real backtesting against real data. A pre-shipped probability metric that calibrated overall but had 15.4pp position-specific error would have been actively misleading for forwards — exactly the players FPL managers care most about for captain decisions.

The two-attempt cap also worked as intended. We could have iterated 5-10 more times tweaking constants, each time achieving small improvements. But the cap forced a stop at the right point: when we have evidence the structural approach is wrong, not when we've found the magic number that obscures the structural issue.

Now — what's next.

You have three real options, and which one is right depends on what you want from the next round:

Option 1: Fix the calibration with a structural change (per the v1.4 options Claude documented).

The calibration report at docs/v2/calibration-results.md apparently documents three structural fix options. I haven't seen them, but the obvious one is "position-specific MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO" — instead of one constant, four constants (one per position) calibrated independently. That would absorb the position-specific calibration errors directly.

This unblocks UI integration. ~1-2 days of focused work plus another backtest cycle.

Option 2: Move on to other v2 items, leave probability metrics as v2.x work.

Pitch view (#2), interactive hero scrubber refinement, transfer suggester groundwork — there's plenty of v2 work that doesn't depend on probability metrics shipping. The probability work sits in the codebase as foundation for whenever you want to revisit calibration.

This ships visible product features faster but defers the analytical depth.

Option 3: Deploy v1 to production first, then decide.

You've built a substantial product. v1 is genuinely shippable. Vercel deployment + Supabase wiring takes ~2 hours. Real users (even if just you on multiple devices) using the app for 1-2 gameweeks would surface what actually matters for v2 — much better signal than theorizing about pitch view vs probability metrics.

My honest recommendation: Read the calibration report's "v1.4 options" section first. That changes the cost calculation. If position-specific constants are a 3-hour fix (just splitting one constant into four and recalibrating each), Option 1 is genuinely cheap and unblocks meaningful UI work. If the structural fix is bigger (rebuilding the percentile interpretation, adding new inputs like per-position attacking-event ratios), the cost might be larger than v2 polish work.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Three real options, increasing in sophistication.
Three real options, increasing in sophistication. Let me think through each honestly so you can decide.

Option 1 — Separate goal/assist involvement constants.

Replaces one constant with two: INVOLVEMENT_RATIO_GOAL = X and INVOLVEMENT_RATIO_ASSIST = Y. Both are still global (same value across all positions), but goal predictions and assist predictions get tuned independently.

This would help if the calibration error pattern was "goals systematically off in one direction, assists systematically off in another direction." Looking at Claude's report — overall goal MACE 2.9pp pass, overall assist MACE 1.3pp pass — overall both pass. So splitting global goal vs assist constants probably doesn't fix the FWD-specific 15.4pp gap, because FWDs would still get the same global goal constant as everyone else.

This option doesn't address the diagnosed structural issue.

Option 2 — Position-specific scaling on top of global constant.

Keep MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15 as the global. Add multipliers per position: FWD = 1.3, MID = 1.0, DEF = 0.7, GK = 0.5 (or whatever the calibration actually requires).

This directly addresses the FWD-specific failure. Forwards can be tuned upward (or downward) independently of midfielders and defenders. Each position's backtest error becomes its own dial.

Cost: modest. Adds 4 new constants. Recalibration cycle of probably 2-3 backtest iterations to dial in the right values per position. ~1 day of focused work including calibration tuning.

Tradeoff: the metric becomes harder to explain ("FWD predictions are scaled 1.3x vs MID") but the math stays interpretable. Each position multiplier has a real-world meaning ("forwards have outsized event involvement compared to other positions for goal-scoring purposes, this constant reflects that").

Option 3 — Isotonic regression calibration layer.

Different approach entirely. Instead of computing a probability and trusting the math, compute a "raw score" then learn an empirical mapping from "raw score percentile" to "actual observed event rate" using historical backtest data.

The backtest already produces (predicted, actual) pairs. Isotonic regression fits a monotonic mapping from predicted to observed. So a model that says "0.40 predicted" but historically those predictions corresponded to 0.25 actual events would output 0.25 going forward.

Pros: mathematically principled, naturally handles position-specific miscalibration (just train per-position mappings), can be retrained on each new season's data without changing any constants.

Cons: introduces a learned model into a previously deterministic system. You can no longer derive a prediction from first principles — it goes through a calibration table. Adds dependency complexity (need an isotonic regression library or implement one). The metric becomes a two-stage thing (raw model + calibration layer) which is harder to debug when wrong. Genuinely harder to ship cleanly.

Cost: 2-3 days minimum. Likely surfaces additional decisions about how to handle low-data buckets, recalibration cadence, version pinning of calibration tables.

My honest recommendation: Option 2.

It's the cheapest fix that directly addresses what the backtest revealed (position-specific calibration errors). It preserves the model's interpretability — every constant has meaning, every prediction can be traced through the math.

Option 3 is more sophisticated and probably the right answer for a v3 metric you've been refining for a year. For v2 of a personal-use FPL tool, it's overengineering. You'd spend 2-3 days adding learned-model complexity to address an issue that 4 position constants would address in 1 day.

Option 1 doesn't address the diagnosed problem.

One thing worth being honest about:

Even with Option 2, the next backtest might still fail on some position (FWD maybe passes, but MID or DEF starts failing because the position multiplier interaction wasn't anticipated). The realistic path is probably 2-3 rounds of calibration before the model passes acceptance criteria across all positions.

That's still bounded — the algorithm is sound, the calibration framework works, position multipliers are a finite set of dials to tune. But it's not a single fix-and-ship change.

If you want to genuinely ship probability metrics for v2.0, Option 2 is the path. Budget 3-5 days of calibration work spread across iterations.

If you want to defer probability metrics and ship other v2 features first, that's also valid — the work-in-progress probability calculator + backtest harness sits in the codebase as foundation for whenever you revisit.

Tap your answer when ready.

A note: this is the right kind of decision to make deliberately rather than instinctively. The probability metrics are the most analytically substantial v2 feature, but they're also the highest-effort path to shipping. Other v2 work (pitch view, transfer suggester groundwork, GW scrubber refinement) is more visually impactful per hour invested.

If you've been using v1 personally and don't actually feel the absence of probability metrics — meaning Confidence alone serves your captain decisions — defer is a defensible answer. The probability work isn't wasted; it sits as foundation. The question is just "what serves the most value next."

A: Option 2 — position-specific multipliers, iterate to acceptance (recommended)

Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. Position-specific multipliers, iterate to acceptance.

Before drafting the prompt, I want to be honest about one thing: this could finish in one autonomous round or it could take three. Calibration tuning is empirical — the first guess at multiplier values might land close to acceptance, or might still fail in unexpected ways (e.g., fixing FWD breaks DEF). I'll structure the prompt with explicit iteration limits and stopping rules so we don't burn an open-ended session on dial-twiddling.

Two questions before I draft:

1. Are you traveling again or are you present this round? This changes how aggressively we let Claude iterate. If you're present, Claude can try one approach, surface results, and you decide whether to iterate or stop. If you're away, we need explicit stop rules baked into the prompt.

2. Starting multipliers — your call or mine?

Looking at the diagnostic data we have:

Goal MACE FWD: 15.4pp off (failing)
Goal MACE other positions: presumably under 5pp (overall passes at 2.9pp)
Assist MACE: passes globally
The forwards are systematically over-predicted (model says they'll score more than they do) or under-predicted (less than they do). I don't know which from the data Claude reported. That direction matters.

If FWD goal predictions are too HIGH on average, FWD multiplier should be < 1.0 (e.g., 0.7).
If FWD goal predictions are too LOW on average, FWD multiplier should be > 1.0 (e.g., 1.3).

Reasonable starting values without knowing the direction:

Position Multiplier on goal involvement Multiplier on assist involvement
GK 0.0 (already enforced via separate logic) 0.05
DEF 0.7 (defenders score less) 1.0
MID 1.0 (baseline) 1.0
FWD 1.5 (forwards score more) 0.8 (forwards assist less than midfielders)
These are guesses informed by football intuition, not by the actual backtest data. Claude should try these as iteration 1, see what happens, then adjust.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Got it.
Got it. Travel mode again, bounded iteration, explicit stop rules. Let me write the prompt with those constraints baked in carefully.

Two design considerations before I draft:

1. How many iterations is the right cap?

Last calibration round had a 2-attempt cap and exhausted it. Those 2 attempts were structurally similar (one global constant tuned in two values). Position-specific multipliers gives Claude more dials, so the search space is bigger, but each iteration is also more targeted because errors map directly to specific multipliers.

I'd cap at 3 iterations for this round. That's enough to find a passing configuration if the structural fix is right, but bounded enough to surface "the position-specific approach also fails" rather than tuning forever.

2. What's the right "stop and report" boundary?

The discipline that worked last round: clear acceptance criteria, two attempts, then stop with honest analysis. Apply the same discipline here. If 3 iterations don't pass, Claude writes a structural recommendation document and stops. Doesn't try a fourth attempt with different multipliers, doesn't try Option 3 (isotonic), doesn't quietly proceed to UI integration.

Here's the prompt:

Calibration v1.4 — position-specific multipliers, bounded iteration. User is traveling for ~3 days. Work autonomously with strict stop rules.

Background
Previous calibration (MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15 global) failed: overall MACE passed but FWD-specific MACE was 15.4pp (acceptance threshold 5pp). Two-attempt cap was exhausted. Verdict was honest "structural fix required."

User chose Option 2 from the v1.4 options: position-specific scaling on top of the global constant. The diagnosis: forwards are systematically miscalibrated relative to other positions because a single global constant can't capture position-specific event involvement patterns.

Implementation
Replace the single MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO constant with position-specific multipliers applied on top of a base constant.

In src/lib/probability/constants.ts:

typescript
// Base involvement ratio — applies to MID as the reference position.
// See MAX_INVOLVEMENT_MULTIPLIERS for position-specific scaling.
export const BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15;

// Position-specific multipliers applied on top of BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO.
// Captures structural differences in how often each position is involved
// in goal-scoring vs assist-creating events. Calibrated empirically via
// backtest. MID = 1.0 by definition (the baseline reference).
export const INVOLVEMENT_MULTIPLIERS: Record<Position, { goal: number; assist: number }> = {
GK: { goal: 0.0, assist: 0.05 }, // GK already enforced separately, included for completeness
DEF: { goal: 0.7, assist: 1.0 }, // Starting guess — defenders score less
MID: { goal: 1.0, assist: 1.0 }, // Reference baseline
FWD: { goal: 1.5, assist: 0.8 }, // Starting guess — forwards score more, assist less
};
Important: these starting values are intuition-informed guesses, NOT calibrated values. Iteration 1 of the backtest will reveal whether they're directionally correct. Adjust per the iteration rules below.

In Step 4 of the calculator (src/lib/probability/calculator.ts):

Replace:

typescript
p_involved = influence_pct _ MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_goal_given_involved = threat_pct _ MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_assist_given_involved = creativity_pct \* MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
With:

typescript
const goalMultiplier = INVOLVEMENT_MULTIPLIERS[player.position].goal;
const assistMultiplier = INVOLVEMENT_MULTIPLIERS[player.position].assist;

p_involved = influence_pct _ BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO
p_goal_given_involved = threat_pct _ BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO _ goalMultiplier
p_assist_given_involved = creativity_pct _ BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO \* assistMultiplier
Note: p_involved keeps just the base constant (involvement is roughly position-agnostic — being involved in attacking events at all is similar across positions). The position-specific scaling lives in the goal/assist outcome probabilities (different positions have different conversion rates given involvement).

If after iteration 1 it becomes clear that involvement itself is also position-specific (e.g., MID players being involved in attacking events more than DEF players regardless of cohort percentile), discuss in the iteration analysis whether to add involvement multipliers too. Don't add them prophylactically.

Update spec doc
docs/v2/fpl_probability_algorithm.md — add v1.3.3 changelog entry:

"Replaced single MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO with BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO + position-specific INVOLVEMENT_MULTIPLIERS (goal and assist independently). Resolves position-specific calibration errors discovered in Phase 2 backtest where FWD MACE was 15.4pp despite overall MACE passing."

Update Step 4 in spec to show the new formulas.

Tests
Update existing calculator tests to expect new lambda magnitudes. Add new tests:

"Position-specific multipliers are applied correctly" — verify that for the same percentile values, FWD goal probability > MID goal probability > DEF goal probability when goal multipliers are 1.5/1.0/0.7.
"MID is the reference baseline" — verify that MID predictions are unchanged when MID multiplier is 1.0 (mathematically the BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO behavior should equal the previous v1.3.2 behavior for MID players).
"Assist multipliers are independent from goal multipliers" — verify that changing goal multipliers doesn't affect assist predictions.
Iteration rules
Maximum 3 iterations. Each iteration: change values, re-run backtest, evaluate calibration report.

Iteration 1: Use starting values above. Run backtest. Generate calibration report. Identify which positions still fail and in which direction (over- or under-predicting).

Between iterations: Adjust multipliers based on the actual calibration error. If FWD goal is still over-predicting (predictions higher than actuals), reduce FWD goal multiplier. If under-predicting, increase. Same logic per position. Use roughly:

new_multiplier = current_multiplier \* (observed_event_rate / predicted_event_rate)
averaged across the position's predictions. Don't change multipliers by more than 50% in any single iteration to avoid overcorrection.

After each iteration: update docs/v2/calibration-results.md with the iteration's results and the change made. Don't overwrite previous iteration results — append. So when user returns they see iteration 1, iteration 2, iteration 3 each with their multipliers and resulting MACE.

Stop rules
Stop and write final status report immediately if ANY of these:

Iteration N passes all acceptance criteria (overall MACE < 5pp, position-stratified MACE < 5pp for all positions, no bucket > 10pp off, Brier below baseline). Commit as fix(probability): position-specific multipliers achieve calibration acceptance. Update calibration-results.md with verdict ✅ PASS. Stop. Do NOT proceed to UI integration — that requires user approval.
3 iterations exhausted without acceptance. Document final state honestly in calibration-results.md. Verdict remains ❌ FAIL. Recommend specific structural change for v1.5 — likely Option 3 (isotonic regression layer) since the multiplier approach has been exhausted. Stop.
Calibration error increases between iterations. If iteration 2 has worse MACE than iteration 1, or iteration 3 worse than iteration 2, that signals the multiplier search is unstable. Stop immediately, document in calibration-results.md, recommend Option 3 for v1.5. Don't try a fourth iteration.
Any iteration produces predictions outside [0, 1] range or other math errors. Stop, document, recommend structural fix.
What stays unchanged
Item 0 fixes (already shipped commit ba1e739 family)
Backtest harness itself
Phase 1 calculator structure (only constants and Step 4 formulas change)
All other patch decisions (Gap A team_events formula, Gap C effective_role removal, Gap D team conversion factor)
DO NOT proceed to UI integration regardless of verdict
Status report format
When stopping (success or failure), write docs/v2/calibration-results.md with:

Final verdict (PASS or FAIL)
Iteration history (multipliers used in each iteration + resulting MACE)
Position-stratified results from final iteration
If PASS: recommendation that user can now decide on UI integration
If FAIL: specific structural recommendation for v1.5 (Option 3 isotonic regression with rationale)
Any anomalies or surprises encountered during iteration
Commit messages:

Each iteration: fix(probability): calibration iteration N (multipliers: GK/DEF/MID/FWD)
Final: chore(probability): calibration complete after N iterations
Hard rules
Maximum 3 iterations. No exceptions.
Don't drift sideways into UI work even if everything passes early.
Don't try Option 3 (isotonic) in this session — that's a separate decision.
Don't change BASE_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15 — only the multipliers move. The base constant is the v1.3.2 calibrated value.
When stopping, post a single combined sign-off summary.
A note for you, the human:

The 3-iteration cap is deliberate. If position multipliers can't achieve acceptance in 3 attempts, the structural approach is wrong and we'd be tuning blindly to chase a failing model. Better to stop and consider Option 3 (isotonic regression) than to ship a barely-passing v1.4 that papers over a structural issue.

The "increasing MACE between iterations" stop rule is also important. If iteration 2 is worse than iteration 1, that's evidence the multiplier search is unstable — possibly because the multipliers interact in non-obvious ways with each other. That's a structural signal, not a tuning signal.

When you're back, you'll have one of three outcomes:

Calibration passes — you decide whether to integrate probabilities into UI, or defer
Calibration fails after 3 iterations — you decide whether to try Option 3 (isotonic) or defer probability metrics to v2.x
Calibration fails with diverging error — strong signal Option 3 is necessary; you decide if it's worth the effort
All three outcomes are useful learning. None are wasted work. The probability calculator + backtest harness + multiplier infrastructure all become foundation for whatever you decide.

Two things to do before you go:

Verify docs/v2/fpl_probability_algorithm.md is in the repo — fourth time I'm mentioning this, sorry. Quick ls docs/v2/ check.
Free port 3000: lsof -ti:3000 | xargs kill -9
Sleep well, travel safe.

You said: Before I send this prompt.
Before I send this prompt. I have a question. Instead of proceeding with this algorithm and taking long, I need your take - all I want is an algorithm that uses the confidence algorithm, influence, creativity, threat, FDR, and any other useful data in fps database to predict probability of goal and assist for a specific game. Do you think we can achieve with the current algorithm path or you can just create a new algorithm that will get to the goal faster

Apr 27
Claude responded: Genuine question and worth thinking about honestly before more work happens.
Genuine question and worth thinking about honestly before more work happens.

Let me be direct about what we have and what you're asking.

What you actually want:

A function that takes (player, fixture) and returns (P(goal), P(assist)) using all available signal — Confidence, ICT, FDR, anything else useful. You want it to work, not necessarily to be theoretically pristine.

What the current algorithm does:

It's a Poisson model that decomposes the probability into chained conditionals (event happens, player involved, player scores). It uses ICT percentiles, FDR multipliers, position-aware scaling. It's mathematically principled. It does not currently use Confidence as an input — that's a separate metric in our codebase that the probability calculator has no knowledge of.

The honest assessment of where we are:

The current algorithm is more sophisticated than what you actually need. We've spent multiple iterations calibrating it (MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO, position multipliers, etc.) and it's still failing position-stratified MACE. The structural complexity is real — chained conditionals with multiplicative interactions are hard to calibrate empirically, especially across positions with different event distributions.

The realistic remaining path with Option 2: probably 2-3 more iterations to get all positions passing acceptance. Could work. Could also reveal that position multipliers can't capture all the structural variance and we need Option 3 (isotonic regression). Total time to a shippable model: 3-7 days realistically.

Now — your question: is there a faster path?

Yes, honestly. And it would use everything you mentioned including Confidence (which the current algorithm ignores).

A simpler approach — empirical regression model:

Instead of "derive probability from first principles," do "fit a model to historical data."

Inputs (per player, per fixture): Confidence, Influence per 90, Creativity per 90, Threat per 90, FDR, opponent FDR, home/away, minutes-played-history, position
Output: did they score? did they assist?
Method: logistic regression (or gradient boosted tree if you want better accuracy) trained on 2025/26 backtest data
Calibration: built in — logistic regression naturally outputs calibrated probabilities; tree models can be calibrated with isotonic afterward
Why this would be faster:

No calibration tuning required. The model fits itself to the data. There are no constants like MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO to dial in. Whatever the data says is what the model produces.
Confidence becomes an input. The current algorithm rebuilds form/quality estimation from scratch using ICT. But you already have a calibrated Confidence metric that captures "is this player in form" in a single number. Letting the model use it as a feature is much simpler than rederiving similar signal from raw ICT.
Position-specific behavior is automatic. The model can learn "FWD position interacts with Threat differently than DEF position" from the data, without manual position multipliers.
Validation is the same backtest framework we already have. No additional infrastructure required.
Realistic time to a working model: 1-2 days. Most of which is data preparation and feature engineering, not algorithm iteration.
Why we didn't go this route originally:

Honestly? We didn't seriously consider it. The probability spec you uploaded was a Poisson model and we worked from there. Switching approaches now requires throwing away most of the current calculator code (though the backtest harness, type definitions, and integration scaffolding all stay).

The honest tradeoffs of switching now:

Pros:

Faster to a passing calibration
Uses Confidence (your other metric) as input — which feels right semantically
Less ongoing tuning work (the model retrains rather than getting hand-tuned)
More accurate on average (regression models tend to outperform hand-derived formulas when data exists)
Cons:

Loses the "explainable from first principles" property. With the current algorithm, you can trace why a player has 35% goal probability through the math. With a regression model, you get "the model says 35% because the weighted sum of features matches that prediction" — interpretable but less narrative.
Throws away ~14 hours of recent calibration work on the Poisson model
Adds a dependency on a regression library or implementation
Requires retraining each season (or each major data shift)
Cons of staying with current approach:

More iteration time before shipping
May still fail position-stratified after iteration 3 (would need Option 3 isotonic anyway)
Doesn't use Confidence — feels like it should but architecturally doesn't
Hand-tuned constants that need re-tuning each season
My honest recommendation: switch to the regression approach.

Three reasons:

You said "I need an algorithm that uses Confidence" — the current algorithm doesn't. That alone is a strong signal we built the wrong thing. The simpler approach naturally incorporates Confidence as a feature.
The current algorithm has been iterating without converging. Two rounds of calibration (MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO, then position multipliers in flight) and we're not yet shippable. Each round adds tuning surface but doesn't solve the underlying issue: we're hand-deriving a probability model that the data should be teaching us.
You asked about speed. The honest answer is the regression approach is faster to ship, lower ongoing maintenance, and naturally incorporates the metrics you wanted to incorporate.
The work we've done on the Poisson model isn't wasted entirely — the backtest harness, type definitions, ICT data plumbing, and integration scaffolding all carry forward. We're swapping the calculator's internals, not the surrounding infrastructure.

One important honest caveat:

I can't promise the regression approach will produce a "good" model. It might learn that the data is too noisy, or that the season's small sample size limits accuracy. But it'll calibrate properly by construction (that's what the validation framework will verify), and it'll be a fast path to "we have something that works" or "we have evidence that this kind of prediction is hard from FPL data alone."

Either outcome is more useful than continuing to iterate on the Poisson calculator.

Tap your answer.

A note on my own reasoning here: I've been complicit in the Poisson approach throughout. I wrote the patch document that locked in the architecture. I should have surfaced "is this the right approach for what the user actually wants?" much earlier, before we spent multiple iterations calibrating it. Your question forced me to actually examine whether what we built matches what you asked for, and the honest answer is "not really." Better to acknowledge that now than after another 3 days of multiplier tuning.

If you go with the regression approach, it'll feel like a reset. It is, partially. But it's the right reset — the surrounding infrastructure carries forward and the work to date informs the feature set we'd use. The calculator internals get replaced, not the whole module.
