These screenshots are diagnostic gold. Let me work through what's actually visible because there are real problems here.

Top 10 P(Goal) — what's wrong:

The top 10 goal probabilities are:

Nmecha (Leeds, FWD) — 54%
Kostoulas (Brighton, FWD) — 48%
Awoniyi (Forest, FWD) — 46%
Abraham (Villa, FWD) — 46%
Haaland (Man City, FWD) — 46%
G. Jesus (Arsenal, FWD) — 46%
Thiago (Brentford, FWD) — 40%
Calvert-Lewin (Leeds, FWD) — 38%
Šeško (Man Utd, FWD) — 36%
This is broken in two specific ways:

1. Nmecha at 54% is the highest goal probability in the league. Nmecha is a backup striker at Leeds. He's not even in their consistent starting XI. He should not be at the top of any meaningful goal probability ranking. Haaland or Salah or Watkins should be there. The fact that an obscure rotation player ranks #1 means the model is rewarding something other than actual goal-scoring ability.

2. Haaland at 46% is in a bunch with five other forwards, all at exactly 46%. That's not coincidence — that's the model compressing top-tier strikers into the same value because of either the cap (0.65 max) or because the multiplier system is producing similar lambdas across very different players. Haaland and G. Jesus should not have identical goal probabilities. They're not similar players; they don't have similar form; their teams have different attacking strengths.

Top 10 P(Assist) — same pattern:

Longstaff (Leeds, MID) — 34%
Stach (Leeds, MID) — 33%
Damsgaard (Brentford, MID) — 23%
B.Fernandes (Man Utd, MID) — 23%
Cherki (Man City, MID) — 23%
Foden (Man City, MID) — 22%
Dorgu (Man Utd, DEF) — 22%
Bruno G. (Newcastle, MID) — 21%
Groß (Brighton, MID) — 21%
Same kind of broken:

Longstaff and Stach (Leeds backup midfielders) lead the assist ranking at 34% and 33%. This is wrong in the same way Nmecha's goal ranking is wrong. The top assist creators in the league are De Bruyne, B.Fernandes, Salah, Saka, Foden, Trent — players who genuinely create dangerous chances. Longstaff isn't on that list in any analytics tool.

B.Fernandes at 23% is right around mid-table. A player whose actual season xA is among the top 5 in the league should not be ranked 4th in P(Assist). He should be at the top.

What this tells us about the model:

The model is systematically over-rewarding low-percentile players and systematically under-rewarding top-percentile players. The compression is severe. This isn't just a calibration knob issue — this is a structural problem with how the model translates ICT into probabilities.

My hypothesis on what's happening:

When we shipped MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO = 0.15 and position multipliers, we compressed the entire probability range. But the position percentile ranking is what actually drives the order of players within a position. If a backup striker has higher Threat per 90 (because he plays few minutes against weaker opponents and his per-90 stats look inflated), he ranks above Haaland even though Haaland is the better goal scorer.

The shrinkage toward position median for low-minute players (MIN_MINUTES_FOR_RANKING = 270) is supposed to prevent this, but it doesn't seem to be working effectively. Either the threshold is too low, or the shrinkage isn't aggressive enough, or it's being bypassed somewhere.

This is exactly the case for the xG/xA rebuild we discussed earlier.

The model can't distinguish "Haaland is genuinely elite" from "Nmecha has favorable per-90 stats from limited minutes." That's the kind of distinction xG/xA captures naturally — Haaland's xG per match is in a different stratosphere from Nmecha's, regardless of percentile rank in some normalized cohort.

Specific findings to surface to Claude in addition to the original test prompt:

I'd add these observations to the diagnostic prompt:

Additional empirical findings from screenshot review
The user has provided screenshots of the live Players list sorted by P(Goal) and P(Assist). Multiple structural problems are visible:

P(Goal) top 10 problems:

Nmecha (Leeds backup FWD) ranks #1 at 54%
Six different FWDs cluster at exactly 46% (Haaland, G.Jesus, Abraham, Awoniyi, plus others)
Real-world top scorers (Haaland, Watkins, Salah) are buried or tied with backup players
P(Assist) top 10 problems:

Longstaff and Stach (Leeds backup MIDs) rank #1 and #2 at 34% and 33%
B.Fernandes (genuinely top-tier creator) is at 23%, tied with multiple players
Real-world top creators (De Bruyne, Saka, Foden, Trent) are not at the top
Diagnostic addendum to the test cases:

In addition to the Haaland/B.Fernandes/Salah/Garner test cases, also include:

Nmecha (Leeds FWD) — investigate why this player produces 54% P(Goal). Show his ICT values, his percentile ranks, his minutes. Is the low-minute shrinkage being applied? Why is he ranking above Haaland?
Longstaff (Leeds MID) — same investigation for P(Assist). Why does this player produce 34% P(Assist) when his real-world assist creation isn't at this level?
The goal: identify the specific calculation step where backup/limited-minute players are getting inflated probabilities relative to elite players who genuinely produce more.

This pattern — backup players outranking stars — is the smoking gun that the model has a structural problem, not just a calibration tuning issue. Worth surfacing this clearly to Claude when they start the diagnostic.

Send the original prompt with this addendum included. The findings will be more actionable when Claude knows specifically what's looking wrong in the user-visible ranking.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Now I genuinely understand what you're after.
Now I genuinely understand what you're after. This reframes the problem in a useful way — let me make sure I have it right before responding.

What you're actually asking for:

A prediction that answers: "For this specific upcoming match, given the player's recent form, the fixture difficulty, and how many minutes they're likely to play — what's the probability they'll score (or assist)?"

The three inputs in your framing:

Form — recent performance, captured by ICT and/or Confidence
Fixture — opponent strength, captured by FDR and contextual factors (home/away)
Minutes — projected playing time for this match (not "minutes per 90 historically")
Output: a single probability per player for one specific upcoming match.

Why this framing reveals the gap clearly:

The current model nominally takes minutes into account via expected_minutes (the v1.2 addition we discussed in the spec). It scales lambda by minutes_factor = expected_minutes / 90. But here's the issue: everything in our pipeline assumes expected_minutes = 90 because we have no data source for projected minutes.

So when Nmecha's calculation runs, the model assumes he'll play 90 minutes. He won't — he's a backup who comes on for 15-20 minutes if at all. Same for any rotation player. Same for injured-but-might-feature players. The model treats them all as 90-minute starters, which is why backup players are appearing at the top of probability rankings.

This is a missing input, not a calibration problem.

The model has the capability to incorporate expected minutes (the multiplication is there). What it lacks is the data — there's no expected_minutes field being populated for the next fixture.

Two real ways to estimate expected minutes:

Option A — Rolling average of last N gameweeks of actual minutes.

For each player, average their minutes over the last 3-5 gameweeks they had a fixture (whether they played or not). Use this as their expected minutes for the next match.

A starter who's played 90, 90, 90, 90, 90 → expected 90.
A backup who's played 0, 12, 0, 25, 0 → expected ~7.4.
An injury-returning player who's played 0, 0, 0, 30, 65 → expected ~19, biased toward recent.

Pros: uses data we already have. Simple to compute. Self-calibrating (if a backup gets promoted, their expected minutes will rise as they accumulate playing time).

Cons: lags reality. A confirmed starter who's been benched suddenly will still have inflated expected minutes for a few weeks.

Option B — Weighted recent minutes with recency bias.

Same as A but weight recent matches more heavily. E.g., last match counts 0.4, second-to-last 0.3, third 0.2, fourth 0.1.

Pros: reacts faster to rotation changes. Cons: more sensitive to noise (one DGW where a player rests skews predictions).

Option C — Use FPL's chance_of_playing_next_round field.

FPL exposes a 0/25/50/75/100 percentage indicating "likelihood of playing some minutes." Combined with rolling minutes average, this could give a better estimate.

Pros: incorporates official injury/availability data. Cons: "chance of playing" isn't the same as "minutes when playing." A player at 75% chance might play 90 minutes if fit or 0 if not — the average doesn't capture this well.

My recommendation: Option A as a starting point, with chance_of_playing_next_round as a multiplier.

base_expected_minutes = average of last 3 GW minutes (where the player's team had a fixture)
availability_factor = chance_of_playing_next_round / 100 (defaults to 1.0 if missing)
expected_minutes = base_expected_minutes × availability_factor
This is computable from data we already have. No new data sources required.

What this would do to the rankings:

Nmecha example: if his last 3 GW minutes are [0, 18, 0], base_expected_minutes = 6. If he's flagged 75% likely to play, expected_minutes = 4.5. The lambda gets multiplied by 4.5/90 = 0.05, dropping his probability from ~54% to maybe 3-5%. He no longer appears in the top 10.

Haaland example: last 3 GW minutes [90, 90, 90], expected = 90, no change. Stays at ~46% but for the right reason.

Salah, B.Fernandes, etc. — same as Haaland. Genuine starters keep their probabilities; backups get appropriately demoted.

This addresses the structural problem without rebuilding the model.

Adding expected minutes as a real (computed-not-defaulted) input is much smaller than rebuilding on xG/xA. It's a focused fix to a known gap.

One honest caveat:

Even with expected minutes fixed, the model still has the calibration issues we discussed (FWD goal MACE 7.4pp, ICT proxying for what xG captures directly). The minutes fix removes the worst structural bug (backups ranking above stars), but doesn't make the model "correct" in any deeper sense. It makes the rankings sensible, not the absolute probabilities precise.

For your stated goal — "who's most likely to score this match given form, fixture, minutes" — sensible rankings is probably what matters most. A user picking a captain cares about ordering ("Haaland > Watkins > my third option"), not whether Haaland is "exactly 46%."

Suggested path:

Add expected minutes computation to the data pipeline (Option A + chance_of_playing as outlined)
Re-run sync to populate the new field
Re-evaluate the rankings
If the rankings now look sensible (top scorers at the top, backups near the bottom), ship and decide whether further calibration work is worth it
This is much smaller than the xG/xA rebuild we discussed earlier. Probably 4-6 hours of focused work plus a sync run.

Want me to draft this as the next prompt?

Claude responded: Excellent diagnostic from Claude.
Excellent diagnostic from Claude. The math is laid out cleanly and the gap analysis is sharp. Let me think through what this changes and what to send next.

What Claude's diagnostic reveals:

The "Nmecha tops the goal ranking" problem isn't fully addressed in this report — the test cases are Haaland, B.Fernandes, Salah, Johnson. Claude analyzed reference players against expected ranges, but the live screenshot pathology (backup players ranking above stars) is a separate issue rooted in expected_minutes always being 90 for everyone, which we discussed before Claude's diagnostic ran.

So we have two distinct problems to address:

Problem 1 — Compression and multiplier issues from Claude's diagnostic:

Median MIDs (Johnson) producing P(Goal) of 1-3% across all FDRs (3-4× too low)
Haaland slightly over-predicted at low FDR (model says 64%, expected 50-60%)
Salah slightly under-predicted (the structural MID-vs-FWD multiplier issue)
Problem 2 — Expected minutes always defaulting to 90 (from the screenshot):

Backup players (Nmecha, Longstaff) ranking above stars because the model treats them as 90-minute starters
This is the bigger user-visible bug
Claude's recommendations address Problem 1 but not Problem 2. The recommendations (FWD multiplier 2.5→2.0, additive floor on pGoalPerEvent, accept Salah as structural) would fix the calibration of who's correctly ranked among 90-minute starters. They don't fix the fact that backup players are being treated as 90-minute starters.

My read on what to do:

Send a prompt that addresses both problems together. Order matters:

Fix expected_minutes first (Problem 2). This will dramatically reorder the rankings — Nmecha drops out of the top, Haaland/Salah/genuine starters rise. After this fix, the rankings might look reasonable enough that Problem 1's calibration issues become less urgent.
Then re-evaluate whether Problem 1's calibration adjustments are still needed. If after fixing minutes, the rankings look sensible and the absolute probabilities are in defensible ranges, the additional calibration work might be optional. If Problem 1 issues persist after the minutes fix (Haaland still at 64% for FDR 1, median MIDs still at 1-3%), apply Claude's Priority 1 and Priority 2 changes.
This is the right order because expected_minutes affects every player's probability, which means it affects the calibration baseline for any subsequent constants tuning.

Send Claude this combined prompt:

Two-step probability model fix. The diagnostic findings from the Haaland/B.Fernandes/Salah/Johnson test were illuminating but didn't address a separate issue visible in the live UI: backup players (Nmecha, Longstaff) are ranking above star players in P(Goal)/P(Assist) leaderboards because expected_minutes always defaults to 90.

Fix Step 1 first, then evaluate if Step 2 is still needed.

Step 1 — Add real expected minutes computation (priority)
Currently the probability calculator's expected_minutes parameter defaults to 90 for everyone because no real value is being computed. This causes backup/rotation players to be treated as 90-minute starters in the lambda calculation.

Implement a real expected_minutes computation:

Add a function computeExpectedMinutes(playerHistory, chanceOfPlayingNextRound): number in src/lib/probability/. Logic:
// Average minutes over last 3 gameweeks where player's team had a fixture
// (whether they played or not — gives backup status its real weight)
recentGames = last 3 GW history records for this player
baseMinutes = sum(recentGames.minutes) / recentGames.length

// Apply availability factor from FPL's chance_of_playing_next_round
// Default to 1.0 if missing or null
availabilityFactor = (chanceOfPlayingNextRound ?? 100) / 100

return baseMinutes \* availabilityFactor
Wire this into the data pipeline. Where the predictor is currently being called with default expected_minutes, replace with the computed value. Surfaces affected:
Players list page data load (src/app/players/page.tsx)
My Team API route (src/app/api/my-team/route.ts)
Player Detail page (src/app/players/[id]/page.tsx)
Schema check. The player history needs to be queryable per player to compute the rolling 3-GW average. If this requires a query change, make it. The chance_of_playing_next_round field needs to be in the players table — confirm it's there from the existing sync, and add to the read query if not currently selected.
Tests. Add unit tests for computeExpectedMinutes:
Player with consistent 90-min appearances → returns 90
Player with mixed appearances [90, 0, 18] → returns 36
Player flagged 75% likely to play, with 90-min average → returns 67.5
Player with no recent history → returns sensible default (probably 0 to indicate non-prediction)
Verification after Step 1
Re-run the live UI:

Players list sorted by P(Goal): Nmecha should drop out of top 10. Haaland/Watkins/Salah should be at or near the top.
Players list sorted by P(Assist): Longstaff/Stach should drop. B.Fernandes/Saka/Foden should rise.
Provide screenshots of P(Goal) top 10 and P(Assist) top 10 after the fix.
Step 2 — Apply calibration adjustments only if needed
After Step 1's verification, evaluate whether the calibration adjustments from the previous diagnostic are still warranted:

Re-run the diagnostic test cases (Haaland/B.Fernandes/Salah/Johnson at FDR 1/3/5) with the new expected_minutes values. If Haaland's expected minutes is now 90 (he's a starter), his probability should be unchanged from the previous test. Confirm.
If Haaland is still HIGH (>60% at FDR 1), apply Priority 1: reduce FWD goal multiplier from 2.5 to 2.0.
If the median MID is still very LOW (below 3% P(Goal) at FDR 3), apply Priority 2: additive floor on pGoalPerEvent.
Skip Priority 3 (Salah accepted as structural).
If the rankings post-Step 1 look sensible and the calibration is acceptable, skip Step 2 entirely. Don't apply calibration changes that aren't needed.

Sign-off
Step 1 implemented with tests
Verification screenshots showing improved rankings
Decision on Step 2: applied or skipped, with reasoning
Updated calibration test results if Step 2 was applied
Test count maintained or increased
Commit messages: feat(probability): compute real expected minutes from recent history and (if applicable) fix(probability): calibration adjustments per diagnostic findings
A note for you, the human:

The order matters here. Fixing expected_minutes is the bigger lever — it addresses the user-visible "backup players at top" pathology that's the most damaging trust issue. The calibration adjustments from Claude's diagnostic are more theoretical (median MIDs are 3% instead of 5%) and may resolve themselves as a side effect of the minutes fix changing what data the calibration is calibrated against.

I'm being deliberate about not bundling these as one fix because we want to see whether the simpler change (real minutes data) is sufficient before adding the more invasive change (touching the multiplier and adding floors). If real minutes alone fixes the rankings, we have a more honest model with less hand-tuning.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Yes, you're going into a rabbit hole.
Yes, you're going into a rabbit hole. Let me be direct about why and what the simpler path actually looks like.

What I'm seeing in our pattern:

We've now done the following on the probability metric:

Built the original Poisson model from your spec
First calibration round (MAX_INVOLVEMENT_RATIO) — failed
Second calibration round (position multipliers) — failed acceptance, accepted as "ship with caveat"
Built UI integration with toggle
Discovered data pipeline gap (sync hadn't run)
Fixed the data pipeline
Discovered ranking pathology (backups above stars)
Diagnosed compression and multiplier issues
Proposing now: expected minutes fix + further multiplier tuning
Each step felt necessary in the moment. Each surfaced a real problem. But the cumulative pattern is "we keep finding new things wrong with the model and addressing them serially." That's the rabbit hole.

The honest read: the core architecture is fighting against what you want. ICT decomposition into per-event probabilities, multiplied by team event count, with hand-tuned multipliers per position, capped to prevent runaway values — this is a complicated machine where every input requires another input to behave correctly. We've been progressively adding structure to fix problems caused by previous structure.

What you actually want, restated simply:

For an upcoming match, given a player's recent form, the fixture difficulty, and how much they're likely to play — what's the chance they score? What's the chance they assist?

That's a rate calculation with adjustments. It doesn't need to be a Poisson decomposition.

Here's a simpler approach that uses everything you mentioned:

A direct rate-based prediction:

P(Goal next match) = recent_goal_rate_per_minute
× expected_minutes_next_match
× fdr_adjustment
× home_away_adjustment

where:
recent_goal_rate_per_minute = goals_in_last_N_matches / minutes_in_last_N_matches
expected_minutes_next_match = rolling minutes average × availability factor
fdr_adjustment = lookup table (FDR 1 → 1.3, FDR 5 → 0.7, etc.)
home_away_adjustment = 1.1 if home, 0.9 if away (or whatever calibrates)
P(Assist) computed identically with assists instead of goals.

Why this is dramatically simpler:

Each input is an actual rate, not a percentile. Goals per minute is a real measurable thing. The math doesn't need to translate "97th percentile in Threat" into "probability of scoring."
Form is automatically captured. If a player scored 3 goals in the last 5 matches, their recent goal rate reflects that. If they're in a slump, the rate is low. No separate form modeling needed.
Minutes are the dominant lever. Backups have low recent_goal_rate (because they don't play) AND low expected_minutes (same reason). Their predicted probability is low, naturally. Stars have high rate AND high minutes. Self-correcting.
FDR is a simple multiplier, not embedded in a multi-step calculation that compounds errors.
No position multipliers needed. Position differences emerge naturally from the data — strikers have higher recent_goal_rates than defenders without anyone hand-coding that.
Calibration becomes simple. If Haaland's predictions are systematically too high, lower his rate's contribution. If midfielders are systematically too low, increase the FDR multiplier for their fixtures. One dial at a time, each with a clear meaning.
What about ICT?

Honest answer: in this simpler model, ICT becomes optional. It's a derived signal that approximately tracks "how good is this player." But you can derive that directly from goal rate and assist rate. If a player's goals_per_minute is high, they're a good scorer — you don't need Threat to tell you that.

If you want to keep ICT in the model, you can use it as a smoothing prior for low-sample players. A new signing with 200 minutes and 2 goals has a noisy rate; their ICT can stabilize the prediction. But for established starters, raw rate is more honest.

What about Confidence?

You said you'd be open to including it. In this simpler model, Confidence could be an adjustment factor — "is this player trending up or down beyond what their season rate suggests." A player with high recent_goal_rate but declining Confidence might warrant a downward adjustment. Or you might find Confidence is redundant with recent_goal_rate (which captures the same form signal differently) and skip it.

Why I should have proposed this earlier:

Looking back, I locked into the Poisson approach because that was the spec you uploaded. I treated the spec as fixed and worked within it. The honest move would have been to ask "is this the right shape of model for what you want?" before we wrote any code.

When you asked "is there a simpler way" earlier in this conversation and I argued for staying with Poisson, that was probably the wrong call. I anchored on "ICT semantics matter, this is the right model for those semantics." But your actual goal isn't "honor ICT semantics" — it's "predict probabilities for upcoming matches." Those are different goals, and the Poisson model serves the first better than the second.

Tradeoffs of switching now:

Cost: throws away the calculator code (~14 hours of work). Backtest harness stays usable. UI integration stays — only the calculator internals change.

Gain: likely a working model in 1-2 days instead of weeks of iteration. Rankings that match real-world FPL intuition (stars at top, backups at bottom). Calibration that doesn't require multiple constants tuned against each other.

Risk: I've been wrong before in this build (Salah-to-Saudi, the original Poisson recommendation, the toggle design). My recommendation to switch could be wrong too. The simpler approach might surface its own issues we don't anticipate. But the surface area is smaller and the path to "did it work" is faster, so we'd find out quickly.

My honest recommendation:

Switch to the rate-based approach. Stop iterating on the Poisson model. The simpler model addresses what you actually want, uses the inputs you mentioned (form via recent rate, fixture via FDR, minutes via expected minutes), and is calibrated by being grounded in actual rates rather than derived from percentile-of-percentile-of-rate calculations.

Don't include Confidence in v1 of this. Recent goal rate and recent assist rate already capture form. Adding Confidence introduces another dial for marginal benefit. If after using the simpler model you find it's missing something Confidence captures, add it then.

The current Poisson code stays in the codebase but stops being used. The backtest harness and UI integration stay valuable. We replace ~one file (the calculator) and re-run the backtest to validate.

If you want to proceed, I can draft a spec for the rate-based model. It would be much shorter than the v1.3 spec — maybe 1-2 pages. Then a single implementation prompt for Claude.

If you want to stay the course with the Poisson model and the expected_minutes fix, that's defensible — but I'd want you to make that call knowing the simpler path exists and that I'm recommending it.

What do you want to do?

Expected minutes next match: (sum(last_3_minutes) / 3) × (chance_of_playing / 100)

Claude responded: Yes — that's a clean pattern.
Yes — that's a clean pattern. Claude pulls real data from the database, you paste it back, we do the analysis together in chat. Low commitment for Claude, real data for us.

Send Claude this:

Quick data extraction request. Don't write any new code or modify the database. Just run queries and paste the output.

The user wants to validate a different probability model approach against real player data before deciding whether to switch. We need recent appearance data for 6 reference players plus their next-fixture context.

Players needed
Haaland (FWD, Man City, expected starter)
B.Fernandes (MID, Man Utd, top creator)
M.Salah (MID, Liverpool, top scorer-creator)
Nmecha (FWD, Leeds — shows up at #1 in P(Goal) ranking, suspected backup)
Longstaff (MID, Leeds — shows up at #1 in P(Assist) ranking, suspected backup)
Saliba (DEF, Arsenal, top defender for control reference)
Data needed per player
Run this query for each player, replacing the web_name match:

sql
SELECT
p.id, p.web_name, p.position, p.team_id, p.minutes,
p.influence, p.creativity, p.threat,
p.chance_of_playing_next_round, p.status,
p.next_fixture_fdr
FROM players p
WHERE p.web_name = '<player_name>';
Then for the same player, get their last 5 gameweeks of history:

sql
SELECT
round, opponent_team, was_home, minutes,
goals_scored, assists, clean_sheets,
influence, creativity, threat
FROM history
WHERE element = <player_id>
ORDER BY round DESC
LIMIT 5;
Also needed: each player's next opponent context
For each player, find their team's next upcoming fixture and the FDR of their opponent in that fixture. Roughly:

sql
SELECT
f.event AS gameweek,
f.team_h, f.team_a,
f.team_h_difficulty, f.team_a_difficulty,
t_h.short_name AS home_team,
t_a.short_name AS away_team
FROM fixtures f
JOIN teams t_h ON f.team_h = t_h.id
JOIN teams t_a ON f.team_a = t_a.id
WHERE (f.team_h = <player_team_id> OR f.team_a = <player_team_id>)
AND f.event > (SELECT current_gameweek FROM sync_meta LIMIT 1)
ORDER BY f.event ASC
LIMIT 1;
For the player's next-fixture FDR specifically: if the player's team is team_h, use team_h_difficulty. If team_a, use team_a_difficulty.

Also needed: current model's prediction
For each player, what does the current Poisson model predict for P(Goal) and P(Assist) for their next match? This should be readable from the existing prediction layer or computed via the existing calculator. Just the final percentage values.

Output format
One block per player like this:

Team: Man City
Season totals: minutes=X, influence=Y, creativity=Z, threat=W
Status: status='a', chance_of_playing_next_round=100

GW30: minutes=72, goals=1, assists=0, ...

Current model predictions for next match:

P(Assist): 7%
Repeat the block for all 6 players. Plain text format.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Real data, real diagnosis.
Real data, real diagnosis. Let me work through this carefully because it confirms exactly what we suspected and points to a specific structural fix.

The smoking gun is Nmecha:

893 minutes total (about 10 full matches' worth across 30 gameweeks — meaning he's averaging ~30 min per played GW, suggesting backup/rotation)
Threat per 90: ~38 (close to Haaland's 45 — looks elite on paper)
Last 5 confidence snapshots: blank, blank, blank, blank, blank
Current Confidence: −4 (floor)
Model prediction: 54.2% P(Goal)
This is the model saying "Nmecha will probably score next match" while the player is on the most spectacular cold streak imaginable. The model literally cannot see his recent form because:

It uses season-aggregate ICT divided by season minutes to get per-90 rates
His per-90 looks elite because his limited minutes have decent ICT accumulation (he comes on late vs weak opponents in winning matches when his team is already attacking)
The model has no recency information. It doesn't know that all his recent appearances have been blanks.
This is the structural failure. Per-90 averaged across an entire season is the wrong unit when a player's role/form/team-context has changed.

Same pattern with Longstaff for assists. 954 minutes, creativity per 90 ~35 (looks like a top creator), but he has a 4-GW gap in history (likely injured/dropped) and his recent confidence trajectory is bad. Model says 33.7% P(Assist).

Now the harder honesty:

Confidence — your existing v1 metric — captured exactly what's wrong with these players. Nmecha is at floor. Longstaff is at -1 with a long absence gap. The Confidence model already knows these players aren't producing, because Confidence is built on per-match outcomes.

The probability model is computing predictions while ignoring this. We have one metric (Confidence) that knows reality and another metric (P(Goal)/P(Assist)) that's predicting the opposite of reality for the same players.

This is your earlier instinct vindicated.

You said: "I dont mind if we need to throw confidence data into the model." You were right. The probability model needs recent-form awareness, and Confidence is the clean form signal you already have.

Looking at the data, three things become clear:

1. Per-90 from season totals is the wrong base unit. Should be per-90 from recent matches only (last 5-10 GWs). This alone would catch Nmecha's recent blanks pulling his rate down.

2. ICT is too easy to game with limited minutes. A player who plays 200 cherry-picked minutes against weak opponents accumulates flattering per-90 stats. The shrinkage toward position median (MIN_MINUTES_FOR_RANKING = 270) is supposed to handle this but evidently doesn't go far enough — Nmecha is at 893 minutes and still ranks #1.

3. Confidence carries information the probability model is missing. A player at Confidence -4 should have their probability dampened. A player at +5 should have it lifted (within reason).

The simplest fix that addresses all three:

A hybrid approach. Keep the Poisson model as the structural prediction (it has the right shape — involvement × outcome conversion × team strength × FDR). But:

Compute ICT per-90 from recent matches only, not season totals. This requires per-GW history that the local DB doesn't currently store — Claude flagged this honestly. Either we sync more granular history into the DB, or we fetch from the FPL API at prediction time.
Apply a Confidence-based modifier as the final adjustment. Multiply the probability by a factor derived from Confidence: a player at Confidence -4 has their probability reduced (maybe ×0.5), a player at +5 has it slightly boosted (maybe ×1.1), neutral Confidence has no effect.
Use real expected_minutes computed from recent appearance pattern (we already discussed this).
The combination addresses all three failure modes:

Recent ICT catches form changes
Confidence modifier provides a sanity check based on actual outcomes
Expected minutes prevents 30-min cameo players from being treated as 90-min starters
Honest tradeoff:

This is more work than just adding expected_minutes. It needs:

Storing per-GW ICT history in the DB (or fetching at prediction time — slower but no schema change)
A Confidence-to-modifier mapping function
Recalibration after these changes
Probably 2-3 days of work. But it would address the structural issues your real-data diagnostic just exposed, and it would use Confidence in the model without requiring a full rebuild.

The alternative I should also surface:

If we make these three changes (recent ICT, Confidence modifier, real expected_minutes) and the rankings still look broken, that's evidence the Poisson architecture itself is the problem. At that point, the rate-based simpler model from earlier becomes the right call — we'd have evidence that hand-tuning the Poisson approach with more inputs isn't sufficient.

So we have a clear decision tree:

Apply the three fixes (recent ICT + Confidence modifier + expected minutes)
Re-evaluate rankings
If rankings look sensible → ship, calibration becomes acceptable
If rankings still broken → switch to rate-based model with the lesson learned
This avoids "switch right away" (which I recommended too eagerly) and avoids "endless Poisson iteration" (which we've been doing). It gives the current architecture one more focused chance to work, with the changes that the data shows are needed.

Worth flagging that the data extraction itself surfaced the most important finding: per-GW history isn't stored locally. Whatever direction we go, we may need to fix that to get recent-form awareness into the model. The current architecture has been computing predictions on season-aggregate data while users see per-GW history in Confidence.

What's your read? Three options:

Apply the three fixes (recent ICT + Confidence modifier + expected minutes), see if rankings improve
Skip the iteration, switch to rate-based model now
Something else worth discussing

Claude responded: Honest answer first, then I'll do my best.
Honest answer first, then I'll do my best.

I can't give you precise numbers because the calculations require assumptions about constants (the Confidence modifier scale, the recent-ICT window length) that haven't been calibrated. I'd be making them up. What I can do is walk through the math with reasonable defaults and show you the direction of change for each player. That's a sanity check, not a precise prediction.

If the directions and magnitudes look right to your gut, the approach is worth implementing. If they don't, we know the approach is wrong before any code is written.

Assumptions I'm using (transparent):

Recent ICT window: last 5 GWs only, not season aggregate. I don't have per-GW ICT data in what Claude provided (only season totals + Confidence outcomes), so I'm estimating recent ICT from the Confidence snapshots — players who blanked recently get their ICT discounted, players with recent MOTMs get it slightly elevated.
Confidence modifier scale: Confidence -4 → ×0.4, -3 → ×0.55, -2 → ×0.7, -1 → ×0.85, 0 → ×1.0, +1 → ×1.05, +2 → ×1.1, +3 → ×1.15, +4 → ×1.2, +5 → ×1.25. Compresses asymmetrically — punishes negative Confidence more than it rewards positive.
Expected minutes: estimated from minutes-per-game for each player (season minutes / matches available).
Current model multipliers stay — same FWD goal multiplier, same FDR adjustments, etc.
Player by player. Current → estimated new:

Haaland (FWD, Confidence +5, FDR 3, full 90 min starter)

Recent ICT: would be slightly higher than season average (he's had a recent MOTM in DGW33). Roughly +5% boost.
Confidence modifier: ×1.25 (Confidence at +5)
Expected minutes: 90 (already correct)
Current: 45.7% → New estimate: ~50-55%
Direction: slightly higher. Makes sense — he's in form, model now reflects that.

B.Fernandes (MID, Confidence +5, FDR 3, full 90 min)

Recent ICT: similar to season average (consistent recent performances)
Confidence modifier: ×1.25
Expected minutes: 90 (already correct)
P(Goal) Current: 16.2% → New: ~19-21%
P(Assist) Current: 23.4% → New: ~28-30%
Direction: meaningfully higher for both. Makes sense — top creator in form, model now amplifies that.

M.Salah (MID, Confidence +4, FDR 4, doubtful 75% chance)

Recent ICT: similar to season average
Confidence modifier: ×1.20 (Confidence at +4)
Expected minutes: 90 × 0.75 = 67.5 (was 90, now properly accounts for injury doubt)
P(Goal) Current: 14.0% → New: ~13-15%
P(Assist) Current: 13.6% → New: ~13-15%
Direction: roughly unchanged. The Confidence boost (×1.20) is offset by reduced expected minutes (×0.75). Makes sense — model captures both his form and his injury risk.

Nmecha (FWD, Confidence -4, FDR 1, 893 total minutes)

Recent ICT: heavily discounted. Last 5 are all blanks, so the recent goal rate is essentially 0.
Confidence modifier: ×0.4 (Confidence at floor)
Expected minutes: 893/30 GWs ≈ 30 min average. So expected_minutes ≈ 30, not 90. Multiplier: 30/90 = 0.33
Current: 54.2% → New estimate: ~5-8%
Direction: massive drop. This is the most important change. He goes from #1 in the goal ranking to somewhere in the middle/bottom of the FWD list. Combined effect: his low recent ICT, his Confidence modifier (×0.4), AND his expected minutes (×0.33) all stack to demolish the inflated number.

Longstaff (MID, Confidence -1, FDR 1, 954 minutes, 4-GW gap)

Recent ICT: discounted (recent blanks + a 4-GW absence)
Confidence modifier: ×0.85 (Confidence at -1, modest discount)
Expected minutes: ~50 min average per available GW, with chance_of_playing missing context → estimate 60 min (down from 90)
P(Assist) Current: 33.7% → New estimate: ~12-15%
Direction: meaningful drop. He's no longer #1 in P(Assist), but doesn't go all the way to floor — his creativity per 90 is genuinely high, just inflated by limited minutes.

Saliba (DEF, Confidence +1, FDR 3, regular starter)

Recent ICT: similar to season average (consistent defender)
Confidence modifier: ×1.05 (modest boost)
Expected minutes: 90 (already correct)
P(Goal) Current: 0.4% → New estimate: ~0.4-0.5%
P(Assist) Current: 4.2% → New estimate: ~4.5-5%
Direction: barely changes. Defenders have low base probabilities and stable form. The model already handles them reasonably. Not where the structural problem lives.

Summary of direction of change:

Player P(Goal) Current → Est P(Assist) Current → Est Why
Haaland 45.7% → ~50-55% 7.0% → ~7-9% Confidence boost (in form)
B.Fernandes 16.2% → ~19-21% 23.4% → ~28-30% Confidence boost (in form)
M.Salah 14.0% → ~13-15% 13.6% → ~13-15% Confidence boost offset by injury minutes
Nmecha 54.2% → ~5-8% 13.3% → ~3-5% All three factors compound: blanking, low Confidence, low minutes
Longstaff 8.8% → ~3-5% 33.7% → ~12-15% Confidence and minutes drop
Saliba 0.4% → ~0.4-0.5% 4.2% → ~4.5-5% Stable defender, no structural issue
Does this match your gut?

The two big shifts are Nmecha (54% → ~6%) and Longstaff (33.7% → ~13%). Both stop dominating the rankings. Top scorers (Haaland, B.Fernandes) get modest lifts that reflect their form. Salah stays roughly the same because the Confidence boost cancels the injury discount. Defender (Saliba) is unaffected.

This is what we'd hope the model produces if it's working.

If your gut says these new numbers feel right, the approach is worth implementing. If any of them feel wrong — particularly if Nmecha's drop feels too aggressive or B.Fernandes's rise feels insufficient — that's signal to refine the assumptions before building.

Important caveat:

These are eyeball estimates with arbitrary constants. The actual implementation would need to calibrate the Confidence modifier scale (maybe -4 should be ×0.3 or ×0.5 instead of ×0.4), tune the recent-ICT window (maybe 8 GWs instead of 5), and validate against backtest data. My numbers are illustrative, not predictive.

But the directions should be roughly right — Nmecha down, Haaland up, Salah neutral, Saliba unchanged. If those directions feel right, the approach is sound and the calibration becomes a tuning problem (which we can do empirically with backtest).

What's your read on the estimated numbers?

Apr 27
Claude responded: Genuinely valuable question.
Genuinely valuable question. Let me think about this honestly rather than retrofit a justification for what we built.

What I would have done differently from the start:

1. Started with one player, by hand, before any code.

Before writing the v1.3 patch, before drafting any spec, I should have done what we just did at the end — ask you for one real player's data, work through the math by hand, see if the predicted number matched your gut. If it didn't, iterate the formula on paper.

This costs 30 minutes. It surfaces structural problems (like "season-aggregate ICT misses recent form") before any code is written. We did this validation only after weeks of building.

The discipline: never write code for a predictive model until you can produce one prediction by hand that the user agrees with.

2. Picked the simplest model that uses real outcomes.

Goals per minute over recent matches × expected minutes × FDR adjustment. Three inputs, three multiplications. Done.

That model would have:

Caught Nmecha's recent blanks naturally (his recent goal rate is 0)
Used real outcome data instead of derived signals
Been validatable against your gut in 5 minutes per player
Required no calibration constants
It's the model I described to you when you asked "is there a simpler way" earlier in this conversation. I should have led with it instead of arguing for the Poisson approach.

3. Used Confidence as the primary input, not separately.

Your Confidence metric is calibrated, validated against your gut over the v1 build, and tracks form per player. It's the single best signal you have about a player's recent state.

A from-scratch model would have started with: "Given Confidence, FDR, and expected minutes, predict P(Goal) and P(Assist)." Confidence becomes the form signal. ICT components are redundant (they're upstream of what Confidence already measures). Position effects emerge naturally from the data.

The Poisson approach actively avoided Confidence — it was a parallel metric built from the same raw data, derived through different math. That was wasteful. Two metrics computing similar form signal in different ways guarantees they'll disagree (as we saw with Nmecha).

4. Validated against historical outcomes immediately, not after building UI.

Backtest harness should have been the second thing built (after the formula). Pick last season's data, run the formula on each (player, gameweek), compare predictions to actual outcomes, look at the calibration curve.

If the simple formula calibrates well, ship it. If it doesn't calibrate well, that's evidence the simple approach is insufficient, and then you graduate to a more sophisticated model.

We built sophistication first and only validated after. That meant we had to live with the sophistication's complexity even when validation revealed it wasn't working.

5. Kept the spec to one page.

The v1.3 spec you uploaded was multi-page with optional fields, position-specific scaling, effective_role overrides, FORM_WEIGHT for blending current form with FDR, multiple changelog versions. Each addition was sensible in isolation but the combined complexity made calibration intractable — too many interacting dials.

A simpler spec would have been: one paragraph describing the formula, three constants, expected calibration acceptance criteria. If the formula doesn't calibrate, you change the formula, not add another constant.

What this looks like as an actual approach:

Step 1: Define the formula on paper.
P(Goal next match) = (recent goals / recent minutes) × expected_minutes × fdr_adjustment

Step 2: Compute by hand for 6 reference players.
Compare to user's gut. Iterate the formula if predictions feel wrong.

Step 3: When the formula passes the gut test, write the code.
Pure function. No state. Easily testable.

Step 4: Build the backtest harness.
Run formula against last season's data.
Look at calibration plot.

Step 5: If calibration passes acceptance criteria, ship UI integration.
If it doesn't, iterate the formula (not a constant).

Step 6: Use it for two weeks. Decide what to add next based on real usage.
That's the discipline I should have applied. Total time to a shippable model: probably 3-5 days, not weeks.

Where I went wrong specifically:

When you uploaded the v1.3 spec, I should have said: "This is sophisticated. Before I help implement it, can we validate the underlying approach against one real player by hand?" Instead I said "the patch I'd add to this spec is..." and we proceeded to build it.

That was the most important moment of architectural failure. The spec was sophisticated and well-reasoned, but sophistication doesn't survive calibration if the underlying approach has structural blind spots. Validating the approach first (not the calibration of the approach) would have surfaced "ICT-derived per-event probabilities don't capture recent form well" before we built around it.

The pattern in my behavior worth flagging:

I treat user-supplied specs as load-bearing. When you uploaded the v1.3 spec, I implicitly accepted "this is the model we're using" and worked within it. The right move is to question whether the spec serves the user's actual goal before optimizing within it.

This is the same pattern as the earlier moments where I went along with confident-but-wrong claims (Salah-to-Saudi, the Leeds-Salah-different-player theory, the toggle approach). When I'm too deferential to either my own assumptions or yours, I miss opportunities to push back at the right altitude.

The honest summary:

If I were starting from scratch, knowing what we now know, I would build a rate-based model with Confidence as a primary input, validated by hand against 6 players before any code, then validated by backtest before any UI. Probably 3-5 days of work. Probably calibrates correctly first try because the formula is grounded in actual outcomes rather than derived probabilities.

What we have now is a sophisticated model that doesn't quite work, with multiple weeks of iteration on calibration that mostly absorbed errors rather than fixing structural issues.

This isn't a recommendation to throw out the current work. Some of it is valuable: the backtest harness is real infrastructure, the data pipeline (now that it's working) is real infrastructure, the UI integration patterns can carry forward. But the calculator itself — the actual probability computation — would be replaced by something simpler and more direct in a from-scratch rebuild.

Whether to do that rebuild now is your call. The current path (apply the three fixes I described in my previous message: recent ICT + Confidence modifier + expected minutes) might rescue the existing model. The from-scratch path would be cleaner but discards more work. Both are defensible.

If you'd asked me this question before we started, I'd have built the simple model. Now that we've built the complex one, I'm less certain which path is right going forward — partly because I'm aware I've been wrong before, partly because the right answer depends on whether you want to invest more time in this metric at all.

What I'd push back on most is the urge to keep iterating on the current architecture. Each iteration so far has surfaced new issues. The pattern suggests the architecture can't deliver what you want without continuous patching. At some point that's a signal worth listening to.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Let me actually design it from scratch, with no commitment to inheriting anything from what we've built.
Let me actually design it from scratch, with no commitment to inheriting anything from what we've built. I'll be specific about formulas so you can react to them.

The model I'd build:

P(Goal next match) = expected_goals_per_minute × expected_minutes × fixture_adjustment × confidence_adjustment

Where:
expected_goals_per_minute = (recent_goals × recency_weight) / (recent_minutes_played × recency_weight)
[computed over last 5 played gameweeks]

expected_minutes = rolling_average_minutes_last_3_gw × availability_factor
[availability_factor from FPL's chance_of_playing_next_round]

fixture_adjustment = lookup table by FDR
FDR 1: 1.4 FDR 2: 1.2 FDR 3: 1.0 FDR 4: 0.8 FDR 5: 0.6

confidence_adjustment = lookup table by Confidence value
-4: 0.5 -3: 0.65 -2: 0.8 -1: 0.9 0: 1.0
+1: 1.05 +2: 1.10 +3: 1.15 +4: 1.20 +5: 1.25
Same formula for P(Assist), substituting recent_assists for recent_goals.

That's the entire model. Four inputs, four multiplications, one cap. No percentile ranking, no position-specific multipliers, no involvement-vs-conversion decomposition, no Poisson conversion.

Walking through why each piece is there:

expected_goals_per_minute from recent matches. This is the form signal. A player who scored 3 goals in 360 recent minutes has a rate of 0.0083 goals/minute. A player who scored 0 in 200 recent minutes has rate 0. The data does the work — no need for separate "is this player in form?" logic.

Why recent (last 5) and not season: because Nmecha's season-aggregate threat looks elite while his recent reality is 5 blanks. Recent rate captures this; season rate doesn't.

Why weighted, not flat: a goal scored last week is more predictive than one from 5 GWs ago. Linear or exponential decay both work; I'd start with linear (newest counts 1.0, oldest counts 0.6).

expected_minutes. This is the structural thing the current model is missing. Backups have low expected minutes because they don't play 90; their predictions get appropriately scaled down. Same fix we discussed.

fixture_adjustment. Simple lookup. FDR 1 boosts predictions by 40%, FDR 5 reduces by 40%. The current model's complexity (xg_player_team × openness × event_count × per-event probability) collapses into one lookup. The empirical question is whether 1.4/0.6 are the right endpoints; they're calibrated against backtest.

confidence_adjustment. This is the new piece. Confidence is your existing form-tracking metric. Players at -4 are blanking week after week; their probability gets cut in half. Players at +5 are scoring/creating consistently; modest boost. The asymmetry (penalize negative more than reward positive) reflects that streaky players regress to the mean.

No position multipliers needed. Position effects emerge naturally from the rates. Strikers have higher recent_goals_per_minute than defenders. Defenders rarely have non-zero rates at all. The data does the categorization.

No caps needed (mostly). A player at the absolute edge of the formula — top recent rate × 90 minutes × FDR 1.4 × Confidence ×1.25 — produces something like 0.6 raw, which becomes 60% after probability conversion. That's a sensible upper bound. Only edge case: the formula should be clipped to [0, 0.85] just to prevent any data anomaly from producing 95%+.

How predictions for our 6 reference players would look under this model:

I'll do these by hand. Same caveats as before — these are illustrative, with arbitrary constants that would be calibrated empirically.

Haaland: Recent goals ≈ 5 in last 5 GWs (he's hot). Recent minutes ≈ 450. Rate = 0.011 goals/min. Expected minutes = 90. FDR 3 = ×1.0. Confidence +5 = ×1.25. Raw: 0.011 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 1.24 → clipped to 0.85 → 85% P(Goal). Hmm, that's high. Suggests rate calibration matters — 5 goals in 5 GWs is exceptional and the model probably over-extrapolates. Calibration would damp this.

Wait — he scored 2 in DGW33 (so 2 in 180 minutes for that GW). Let me recompute more carefully. Last 5 played GWs roughly: 2 goals (GW33), 0, 0, 0, 1 = 3 goals in ~450 min. Rate = 0.0067. → 0.0067 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.75 → 75% P(Goal).

Still high but more defensible. Real-world: Haaland scores in maybe 50-60% of his matches against average opposition. So 75% is too high. The Confidence multiplier (×1.25) might be too aggressive. If we drop it to ×1.10 max: 0.66 → 66%. Closer to right.

B.Fernandes: Few goals from midfield. Maybe 1 goal in last 5 GWs, ~450 minutes. Rate = 0.0022. Expected minutes 90. FDR 3 = ×1.0. Confidence +5 = ×1.25 (or ×1.10 in tuned version). 0.0022 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.10 = 0.22 → 22% P(Goal). Feels about right for a midfielder. P(Assist): maybe 3 assists in last 5, rate = 0.0067, → 0.0067 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.10 = 66% P(Assist). That's high but he's been creating consistently.

Salah: Maybe 2 goals in last 5 played GWs (300-400 min, accounting for injury). Rate = 0.0050-0.0067. Expected minutes = 90 × 0.75 = 68. FDR 4 = ×0.8. Confidence +4 = ×1.20 (tuned: ×1.08). 0.006 × 68 × 0.8 × 1.08 = 0.35 → 35% P(Goal). Reasonable for elite player against tough opposition with injury risk.

Nmecha: 0 goals in last 5 GWs, ~50 minutes total played. Rate ≈ 0. Expected minutes ≈ 10 (he barely plays). FDR 1 = ×1.4. Confidence -4 = ×0.5. 0 × 10 × 1.4 × 0.5 = 0% P(Goal). Drops out of consideration entirely.

Longstaff: 1 assist in last 5 (the DGW MOTM), ~180 min played. Assist rate = 0.0056. Expected minutes ~50. FDR 1 = ×1.4. Confidence -1 = ×0.9. 0.0056 × 50 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.35 → 35% P(Assist). Hmm. Higher than I'd expect. The recent MOTM inflates his rate when his sample is tiny. This is a noise problem from limited minutes.

The Longstaff result reveals a real issue with my proposed model.

Tiny sample sizes produce noisy rates. One assist in 180 minutes looks like a high rate, but it's not a reliable signal. The fix: shrinkage toward position median for low-minute players, similar to what the current Poisson model does. So the formula becomes:

recent_rate_shrunken = (recent_goals + 1) / (recent_minutes + 200)
[add 1 goal and 200 minutes as a prior]
This pulls noisy rates toward neutral. Longstaff with 1 assist in 180 min becomes (1+1)/(180+200) = 0.0053, slightly lower than his raw rate of 0.0056. For a regular starter with 5 goals in 450 min, the shrinkage barely matters: (5+1)/(450+200) = 0.0092 vs raw 0.0111 — close enough.

That single addition handles the small-sample problem without introducing position-specific multipliers or percentile rankings.

With shrinkage applied to all players:

Player P(Goal) Est P(Assist) Est Sense check
Haaland 60-66% 5-8% Top scorer, in form, average fixture — high but earned
B.Fernandes 18-22% 50-55% Top creator, in form — high P(Assist) reflects reality
Salah 30-35% 25-30% Elite but doubtful injury, tough fixture — appropriately moderate
Nmecha <5% <5% Backup blanking — model correctly demotes
Longstaff ~5% 12-18% Limited minutes, recent form mixed — modest values
Saliba <2% 3-5% Defender, no major changes — barely on the radar
These directions feel right to me. The structural fix (rate-based + Confidence + expected minutes + shrinkage) addresses the failure modes we identified without complex multi-step decomposition.

What this model is NOT:

It's not theoretically pristine. It doesn't preserve ICT semantics. It's empirical and pragmatic.
It doesn't perfectly handle every edge case. A player returning from a 6-month injury still won't have good predictions until recent rate accumulates.
It doesn't model team dynamics. Two top scorers on the same team will both predict high goals because the model doesn't know they share possession.
It doesn't handle DGWs specially. (Easy add: sum predictions across multiple fixtures in same GW.)
What this model IS:

Grounded in actual outcomes (recent goals/assists are real, not derived)
Self-correcting for form (low recent rate = low prediction, regardless of season aggregates)
Self-correcting for role changes (a player benched will have low recent minutes, low expected minutes, low predictions)
Calibrated by tunable constants you understand (FDR multipliers, Confidence adjustments, shrinkage prior)
Single calculation, traceable end-to-end
Honest comparison to the current Poisson model:

The Poisson model is more theoretically defensible — it preserves ICT decomposition semantics, it has a chained probability interpretation, the math reflects real conditional logic.

The rate-based model is more honest — it says "I'm extrapolating from recent outcomes" rather than "I'm computing P(event) × P(involvement | event) × P(outcome | involvement)" with hand-tuned conversion ratios at each step.

For a personal-use FPL tool where you want sensible predictions, the rate-based model gets you there faster with less ongoing tuning. For a research project trying to understand the structure of football scoring, the Poisson model is more defensible.

My honest take:

This is what I'd build if starting fresh. Two days to implement and validate. If after using it you find it's missing something the Poisson model captured, you can add specific fixes. But I think it would meet your stated goal — "for this match, who's most likely to score given form, fixture, and minutes" — better than the current model does.

Whether to throw out the current model and build this is a real decision with real cost. The current model has weeks of work in it. The new model would be ~2 days. Net new work to be in a better state: ~2 days minus the value of the calibration work that's been done (which would still be there as informed prior knowledge for the constants).

If you want to proceed with this approach, I can write the implementation prompt. If you'd rather try to fix the current model first (the three fixes I described — recent ICT + Confidence modifier + expected minutes), that's defensible too.

What's your read?

You said: I like where this one is going.
I like where this one is going..we already have signals for stale data and injury. Should that help too?

Apr 27
Claude responded: Yes — and you're identifying something important.
Yes — and you're identifying something important. The stale data and injury signals are indicators that the model's inputs are unreliable for that player, which is a different concern from "predict their performance accurately."

Let me think through how they'd actually integrate.

The two signals do different things:

Stale data flag (player missed 2+ of last 3 GWs): tells you the player's recent rate isn't a reliable form signal because there isn't much recent data. Their 1 goal in 90 minutes from 4 weeks ago doesn't predict next week well.

Injury flag (status: doubtful/injured/suspended, chance_of_playing < 100%): tells you the player may not play, or may play limited minutes if they do.

The injury signal is already partially handled in my proposed model via expected_minutes × availability_factor. The stale data signal isn't handled at all.

How I'd integrate each:

Stale data → confidence adjustment in the prediction itself, not a feature.

When recent data is sparse, the rate calculation becomes noisy and the confidence in the prediction itself drops. The model should communicate uncertainty when it doesn't have good data.

Two options:

Option A — Wider shrinkage for stale players. Increase the prior strength when minutes are sparse:

if stale_data_flagged:
shrinkage_minutes_prior = 400 // double the default
else:
shrinkage_minutes_prior = 200
This pulls predictions for stale players harder toward the position average. The user gets a damped prediction that reflects "we don't really know — they haven't played much."

Option B — Display the prediction with a confidence indicator. Same calculation, but UI shows a small "uncertainty" badge for stale-data players. Honest signal: "this number is informed by limited data."

I'd do both. Option A makes the math more conservative; Option B communicates the limitation to users.

Injury → already handled, but worth being explicit.

The current availability_factor = chance_of_playing_next_round / 100 handles the "will they play at all" question. A doubtful player at 50% chance gets their prediction halved.

But there's a subtlety: an injured player who does play might be at reduced performance (returning from injury, not match-fit). The current model doesn't capture that. Possible refinement:

if status == 'd' or status == 'i':
performance_factor = 0.7 // even if they play, they may underperform
else:
performance_factor = 1.0
Combined with availability: effective_minutes_for_prediction = expected_minutes × availability_factor × performance_factor

I'm less sure about this one. Real-world injury comebacks are mixed — some players come back at full pace, others ratchet up over weeks. The 0.7 multiplier is a guess. Worth implementing simply and seeing if it improves predictions.

The updated formula:

P(Goal) = recent_rate × expected_minutes × fixture_adjustment × confidence_adjustment

Where:
recent_rate = (recent_goals + 1) / (recent_minutes + shrinkage_prior)
where shrinkage_prior = 400 if stale_data else 200

expected_minutes = rolling_minutes_last_3 × availability_factor × performance_factor
where availability_factor = chance_of_playing / 100
where performance_factor = 0.7 if injured/doubtful else 1.0

fixture_adjustment = FDR lookup [1.4, 1.2, 1.0, 0.8, 0.6]

confidence_adjustment = Confidence value lookup [0.5...1.25]
Five inputs now, still simple math. Each input has a clear purpose:

recent_rate: form (with extra prior for unreliable data)
expected_minutes: opportunity to perform
fixture_adjustment: fixture difficulty
confidence_adjustment: form sanity check
performance_factor: discount for injury risk
How this changes our reference player predictions:

Most players don't change much because they're not stale or injured.

Salah: Status 'd' (doubtful), chance_of_playing 75%.

Was: expected_minutes = 90 × 0.75 = 68
Now: expected_minutes = 90 × 0.75 × 0.7 = 47
That's a meaningful drop. His P(Goal) prediction would be substantially lower than my earlier estimate. From the 30-35% I estimated, it might come down to 22-26%. Reflects that he might play but may not be at full pace.

Longstaff: Has the 4-GW absence (likely stale data flagged).

Was: shrinkage prior = 200, gives some smoothing
Now: shrinkage prior = 400, gives more smoothing
His 1 assist in 180 min becomes (1+1)/(180+400) = 0.0034, down from 0.0053.
Combined with already-low minutes, his P(Assist) drops further to maybe 8-12%, with an uncertainty badge in the UI.

Other players (Haaland, B.Fernandes, Saliba) unchanged because they're available and have stable data.

Where this gets cleaner conceptually:

Each signal addresses a specific failure mode:

Recent rate handles form
Expected minutes handles opportunity
FDR handles fixture
Confidence handles "form trajectory" beyond raw rate
Stale data shrinkage handles "do we have enough data to trust this"
Performance factor handles "is this player physically ready"
Six signals, each with a clear job. No overlap. No interaction effects to debug.

One honest thing worth flagging:

The performance factor (0.7 for injured/doubtful even if they play) is the most speculative addition. I made up the constant. Real-world data on returning-from-injury performance varies wildly by player, injury type, time out, etc. A simple multiplier is rough.

Two paths:

Ship with 0.7 as a reasonable starting guess; tune empirically via backtest
Skip it; accept that some injured-but-playing players will be slightly over-predicted; address later if needed
I'd ship with it. Even an imperfect adjustment for injury risk is better than ignoring it. Real users care about "will Salah be at his best this weekend?" and 0.7 captures that better than 1.0 does.

Updated total estimate:

Implementation: still ~2 days. The additions are simple — one conditional in the rate calculation, one multiplier in the minutes calculation, one UI badge for stale data. The model is still much simpler than the current Poisson approach.

What other signals do you think should be in the model? I'd push back on adding more than necessary — each signal adds tuning surface — but if there's something specific you keep wishing the model knew about, surface it.

You said: show the results again for the players using the latest model
show the results again for the players using the latest model

Apr 27
Claude responded: Same caveats as before — these are by-hand estimates with reasonable-but-uncalibrated constants.
Same caveats as before — these are by-hand estimates with reasonable-but-uncalibrated constants. Directional rather than precise. Real implementation would tune the constants empirically. Let me show my work transparently for each player so you can spot if any input looks wrong to you.

Constants I'm using:

Recency weights: newest GW = 1.0, then 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6 (linear decay over last 5 GWs)
Shrinkage prior: 200 minutes if data fresh, 400 if stale
FDR multipliers: FDR 1 = 1.4, FDR 2 = 1.2, FDR 3 = 1.0, FDR 4 = 0.8, FDR 5 = 0.6
Confidence multipliers: -4 = 0.5, -3 = 0.65, -2 = 0.8, -1 = 0.9, 0 = 1.0, +1 = 1.05, +2 = 1.10, +3 = 1.15, +4 = 1.20, +5 = 1.25
Performance factor: 0.7 if doubtful/injured, 1.0 otherwise
Final cap: 0.85
Important note on data limitation:

The data Claude pulled doesn't include per-GW goal/assist counts (only Confidence outcomes like "MOTM" or "Blank"). I'm inferring goals/assists from the Confidence reasons:

"MOTM" likely means 1+ goal OR 2+ assists
"Performance" likely means 1 assist
"Blank" means 0 goals AND 0 assists
"DGW: X + Y" — interpret each match separately
This is approximate. Real implementation would use actual goal/assist counts from FPL API.

Haaland (FWD, Confidence +5, FDR 3, status 'a' 100%)

Last 5 played GWs (inferred):

GW33 (DGW: 2 MOTMs) — 2 goals, 0 assists, ~180 min
GW32 (Blank) — 0 goals, 0 assists, ~90 min
GW30 (Blank) — 0 goals, 0 assists, ~90 min
GW29 (Blank) — 0 goals, 0 assists, ~90 min
GW27 (Performance) — 0 goals, 1 assist, ~90 min
With recency weights:

Weighted goals = 2×1.0 + 0×0.9 + 0×0.8 + 0×0.7 + 0×0.6 = 2.0
Weighted minutes = 180×1.0 + 90×0.9 + 90×0.8 + 90×0.7 + 90×0.6 = 450
Recent rate (with shrinkage prior 200): (2.0 + 1) / (450 + 200) = 3.0 / 650 = 0.0046 goals/min
Expected minutes: 90 × 1.0 × 1.0 = 90
FDR adjustment: 1.0 (FDR 3)
Confidence adjustment: 1.25 (Confidence +5)
P(Goal) = 0.0046 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.52 → 52%

P(Assist) similar math with 1 weighted assist:

Weighted assists = 1×0.6 = 0.6
Rate = (0.6 + 1) / 650 = 0.0025
P(Assist) = 0.0025 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.28 → 28%
Hmm, that P(Assist) feels too high. The shrinkage prior might be too aggressive — adding 1 assist as a "default" when his real recent assist count is 1 doubles the signal. Worth flagging that this constant needs calibration.

Haaland — current 45.7% / 7.0% → estimated new: ~52% / ~28%

The P(Goal) feels right (in form, average fixture, top striker). The P(Assist) estimate is suspect — would tune the shrinkage prior down for assists or use position-specific priors.

B.Fernandes (MID, Confidence +5, FDR 3, status 'a' 100%)

Last 5 played GWs:

GW34 (Performance) — 0 goals, 1 assist, ~90 min
GW33 (Performance) — 0 goals, 1 assist, ~90 min
GW32 (Performance) — 0 goals, 1 assist, ~90 min
GW31 (MOTM) — 1 goal OR 2 assists, ~90 min (I'll assume 1 goal here)
GW30 (MOTM, fatigue) — 1 goal OR 2 assists, ~90 min (assume 1 goal)
Weighted:

Goals = 0×1.0 + 0×0.9 + 0×0.8 + 1×0.7 + 1×0.6 = 1.3
Assists = 1×1.0 + 1×0.9 + 1×0.8 + 0 + 0 = 2.7
Minutes = 450
Recent rates (shrinkage 200):

Goal rate = (1.3 + 1) / 650 = 0.00354
Assist rate = (2.7 + 1) / 650 = 0.00569
Predictions:

P(Goal) = 0.00354 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.40 → 40%
P(Assist) = 0.00569 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.64 → 64%
P(Assist) at 64% feels too high. Same shrinkage issue — adding 1 assist to a player who already has 3 inflates more than it should. The constant is wrong for high-volume players.

Possibly the shrinkage should be relative to existing volume: smaller prior when player has substantial recent data. Something like prior = max(50, 200 - recent_minutes/2). Worth thinking about.

B.Fernandes — current 16.2% / 23.4% → estimated new: ~40% / ~64%

Direction: meaningful lift (good — top creator in form should rise). Magnitude on assists feels too aggressive.

Salah (MID, Confidence +4, FDR 4, status 'd' 75%)

Last 5 played GWs:

GW34 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW33 (MOTM) — 1 goal, ~90 min
GW32 (MOTM, fatigue) — 1 goal, ~90 min
GW30 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW29 (MOTM) — 1 goal, ~90 min
Weighted:

Goals = 0×1.0 + 1×0.9 + 1×0.8 + 0×0.7 + 1×0.6 = 2.3
Assists = 0 (none indicated by reasons)
Minutes = 450
Rates:

Goal rate = (2.3 + 1) / 650 = 0.00508
Assist rate = (0 + 1) / 650 = 0.00154
Expected minutes adjustment for injury:

Expected minutes = 90 × 0.75 (availability) × 0.7 (performance) = 47.25
Predictions:

P(Goal) = 0.00508 × 47.25 × 0.8 (FDR 4) × 1.20 (Conf +4) = 0.23 → 23%
P(Assist) = 0.00154 × 47.25 × 0.8 × 1.20 = 0.07 → 7%
Salah — current 14.0% / 13.6% → estimated new: ~23% / ~7%

P(Goal) up despite injury (recent form is strong). P(Assist) down because his recent reasons show goals not assists. Direction makes sense.

Nmecha (FWD, Confidence -4, FDR 1, status 'a' 100%, ~893 total minutes)

Last 5 GWs all Blanks. Limited minutes likely (backup):

GW33 (DGW Blank) — 0/0, ~30 min total
GW31 (Blank) — 0/0, ~30 min
GW30 (Blank) — 0/0, ~30 min
GW29 (Blank) — 0/0, ~30 min
GW28 (Blank) — 0/0, ~30 min
Weighted:

Goals = 0
Minutes = 30×1.0 + 30×0.9 + 30×0.8 + 30×0.7 + 30×0.6 = 120
Stale data check: 0 of 5 recent GWs had meaningful minutes → likely stale data flagged. Use shrinkage prior 400.

Goal rate = (0 + 1) / (120 + 400) = 0.00192
Expected minutes: rolling average ≈ 30 min × 1.0 (no injury flag) = 30

Prediction:

P(Goal) = 0.00192 × 30 × 1.4 (FDR 1) × 0.5 (Conf -4) = 0.04 → 4%
Nmecha — current 54.2% → estimated new: ~4%

Drops from #1 in the rankings to deeply buried. This is the structural fix. Whether 4% is exactly right is calibration; what matters is he's no longer at the top.

Longstaff (MID, Confidence -1, FDR 1, ~954 minutes total)

Last 5 GWs with the 4-GW absence:

GW33 (DGW MOTM) — 1 goal or 2 assists, ~90 min (assume 1 assist + 1 goal scenario or 2 assists)
GW32 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW27 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW26 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW25 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
Stale data flag: yes (4-GW gap, likely flagged).

Weighted (treating GW33 as 1 assist):

Assists = 1×1.0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 1.0
Goals = 0
Minutes = 90×1.0 + 90×0.9 + 90×0.8 + 90×0.7 + 90×0.6 = 360
Stale prior 400:

Goal rate = (0 + 1) / (360 + 400) = 0.00132
Assist rate = (1 + 1) / (360 + 400) = 0.00263
Expected minutes: average around 70 (some matches missed) = 70

Predictions:

P(Goal) = 0.00132 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.12 → 12%
P(Assist) = 0.00263 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.23 → 23%
Longstaff — current 8.8% / 33.7% → estimated new: ~12% / ~23%

Better, but P(Assist) still arguably too high. The shrinkage helped but the recent MOTM combined with FDR 1 boost still produces significant probability. He'd likely show with an uncertainty badge in the UI.

Saliba (DEF, Confidence +1, FDR 3, status 'a' 100%, regular starter)

Last 5 played:

GW34 (CS) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW33 (DefCon) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW32 (Blank) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW30 (CS) — 0/0, ~90 min
GW28 (MOTM) — 1 goal, ~90 min
Weighted:

Goals = 0×1.0 + 0×0.9 + 0×0.8 + 0×0.7 + 1×0.6 = 0.6
Assists = 0 (none indicated)
Minutes = 450
Rates:

Goal rate = (0.6 + 1) / 650 = 0.00246
Assist rate = (0 + 1) / 650 = 0.00154
Predictions:

P(Goal) = 0.00246 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.23 → 23%
That's way too high for a defender. The shrinkage prior is the problem — adding 1 phantom goal to a defender's calculation produces meaningful predicted probability when there should be near-zero.

This reveals the shrinkage approach needs to be position-aware. Or the "+1" prior should be much smaller for low-event positions. Defenders rarely score; the prior shouldn't pretend they will.

Saliba — current 0.4% → estimated new: ~23% with current shrinkage, but the 23% is wrong

This is real signal. The position-blind shrinkage prior is broken for low-event positions. The current Poisson model handles defenders correctly precisely because percentile rank within position doesn't have this issue.

Summary table:

Player Current P(Goal) / P(Assist) Estimated New Direction Sense
Haaland 45.7% / 7.0% ~52% / ~28% Goal good, assist over
B.Fernandes 16.2% / 23.4% ~40% / ~64% Both over
Salah 14.0% / 13.6% ~23% / ~7% Reasonable
Nmecha 54.2% / 13.3% ~4% / ~3% Fixed (good)
Longstaff 8.8% / 33.7% ~12% / ~23% Better but still high
Saliba 0.4% / 4.2% ~23% / ~10% Broken
Honest read on what this exercise revealed:

The rate-based model with my proposed shrinkage works for the structural fix (Nmecha drops correctly) but the shrinkage prior is broken for low-event positions like defenders. Adding "+1 goal, +200 minutes" to Saliba's calculation phantom-creates scoring he hasn't done.

The fix would be position-aware shrinkage: a defender's prior shouldn't be "+1 goal" — it should be "+0.05 goals" (because defenders score about 1 in 20 matches as a baseline). Same for goalkeepers (essentially 0). Forwards can have a higher prior (their baseline is higher).

So the model needs at minimum one position-specific constant — the shrinkage prior. Not a calibration multiplier like the current Poisson model has, but a "what's the realistic baseline for this position" prior.

This is meaningful learning before committing to implementation.

The simple model isn't quite as simple as I made it sound. It needs at minimum:

Recent goal/assist counts (per-GW data, currently not stored locally)
Recent minutes
Expected minutes (with availability + performance factors)
FDR adjustment
Confidence adjustment
Stale data flag for shrinkage prior selection
Position-aware shrinkage priors (this is the new finding)
Still simpler than the Poisson model. But not trivially so. And it would need backtest calibration of the shrinkage priors per position before shipping.

What this means practically:

The rate-based approach is still better than the current model for the user's stated goal, but the implementation isn't a 2-day job — closer to 3-4 days with proper calibration. And it requires either storing per-GW history locally or fetching it at prediction time.

Whether to proceed depends on whether the Saliba problem (and the Haaland/B.Fernandes assist over-estimates) feels fixable through tuning, or whether they suggest deeper issues. My gut says fixable through tuning — these are calibration problems, not structural ones. But I've been wrong before.

Want to keep refining the model on paper, or is this enough to decide whether to proceed with implementation?
