is the threat and creativity in the equation...this should show how Salah distribution should be...I believe that is a good and accurate ratio indication for goal and assist

Apr 27
Claude responded: You're right and I missed it.
You're right and I missed it. Let me think through what you're saying because it changes the model meaningfully.

What you're pointing out:

ICT's Threat and Creativity capture how a player produces — the relative split between goal-producing actions and assist-producing actions for that player.

Looking at the data:

Salah: Threat 741, Creativity 711 — roughly equal. He's a balanced scorer-creator.
Haaland: Threat 1350, Creativity 282 — overwhelmingly Threat-dominant. He's a pure scorer.
B.Fernandes: Threat 523, Creativity 1655 — overwhelmingly Creativity-dominant. He's a pure creator.
Saliba: Threat 76, Creativity 172 — both low, but Creativity is higher (set pieces, defensive contributions to attacks).
The Threat/Creativity split tells us for any given attacking event this player is involved in, what's the likely outcome — goal vs assist.

That's exactly the chained-conditional logic from the original Poisson model. But the Poisson model's failure wasn't in this part — it was in trying to derive involvement probability from Influence, which doesn't work well when comparing players with vastly different minutes/contexts.

Where this fits in the rate-based model:

The rate-based model derives goal rate and assist rate independently from raw counts. That ignores the Threat/Creativity ratio. Salah's recent rate might happen to be 5 goals and 1 assist over 5 GWs, but his ICT distribution says he should be balanced. The recent rate is small-sample noisy; the ICT ratio is the more stable structural signal.

A better model that uses both:

Use the rate-based formula as the combined output magnitude, then split the output between goals and assists according to the player's Threat/Creativity ratio.

Step 1: Compute combined output rate
combined_rate = (recent_goals + recent_assists + 1) / (recent_minutes + 200)
[single shrinkage instead of two separate ones — fixes the Saliba problem]

Step 2: Determine player's goal/assist split from Threat/Creativity
threat_share = threat / (threat + creativity)
creativity_share = creativity / (threat + creativity)

Step 3: Split combined into goal and assist rates
goal_rate = combined_rate × threat_share
assist_rate = combined_rate × creativity_share

Step 4: Apply expected minutes, FDR, Confidence adjustments
P(Goal) = goal_rate × expected_minutes × fdr_adj × confidence_adj
P(Assist) = assist_rate × expected_minutes × fdr_adj × confidence_adj
Why this is better:

The recent rate captures "how productive has this player been overall." The ICT ratio captures "what kind of contribution does this player produce." Together they give you both the magnitude (recent form) and the type (player archetype).

A pure scorer like Haaland gets 80% of his combined rate as goal probability, 20% as assist. A pure creator like B.Fernandes gets the opposite split. Salah gets ~50/50.

This also fixes the Saliba problem cleanly — defenders rarely produce attacking events, so their combined recent rate is low, and the split happens to put more on the assist side (matching reality where defenders occasionally cross or set up corners).

Re-running the predictions with this model:

I'll use the same recent rates as before but now combined and split.

Haaland:

Threat/Creativity split: 1350/(1350+282) = 0.83 goal, 0.17 assist
Recent (Goals + Assists): 2 goals + 1 assist = 3 total events in 450 min
Combined rate: (3 + 1) / (450 + 200) = 0.0062
Goal rate: 0.0062 × 0.83 = 0.0051
Assist rate: 0.0062 × 0.17 = 0.0011
P(Goal) = 0.0051 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.57 → 57%
P(Assist) = 0.0011 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.12 → 12%
The 28% P(Assist) over-estimate from before is gone. Now correctly low for a pure striker.

B.Fernandes:

Split: 523/(523+1655) = 0.24 goal, 0.76 assist
Recent: 2 goals + 3 assists = 5 events in 450 min
Combined rate: (5 + 1) / (450 + 200) = 0.0092
Goal rate: 0.0092 × 0.24 = 0.0022
Assist rate: 0.0092 × 0.76 = 0.0070
P(Goal) = 0.0022 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.25 → 25%
P(Assist) = 0.0070 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.79 → cap at 0.85 → 79%
P(Assist) at 79% is high but B.Fernandes is genuinely a top creator in form. Whether that's correct or over depends on calibration. Real-world: top creators get assists in maybe 35-50% of matches against average opposition. So 79% is too high — the rate calibration needs tuning, not the structural approach.

Salah:

Split: 741/(741+711) = 0.51 goal, 0.49 assist (almost exactly 50/50)
Recent: 3 goals + 0 assists = 3 events in 450 min
Combined rate: (3 + 1) / (450 + 200) = 0.0062
Goal rate: 0.0062 × 0.51 = 0.0032
Assist rate: 0.0062 × 0.49 = 0.0030
Adjusted minutes: 47 (0.75 × 0.7 for injury)
P(Goal) = 0.0032 × 47 × 0.8 × 1.20 = 0.14 → 14%
P(Assist) = 0.0030 × 47 × 0.8 × 1.20 = 0.14 → 14%
Notice the Threat/Creativity ratio gives Salah a balanced split even though his recent matches were goals-only. The model correctly says "his recent random sample was goals, but his player profile is 50/50 — predict balanced."

This is exactly what you intuited. The ICT ratio captures structural player identity that recent small-sample data misses.

Nmecha:

Threat/Creativity split: 379/(379+106) = 0.78 goal, 0.22 assist
Recent: 0 events in 120 min, stale prior 400
Combined rate: (0 + 1) / (120 + 400) = 0.0019
Goal rate: 0.0015
Assist rate: 0.0004
Expected minutes: 30
P(Goal) = 0.0015 × 30 × 1.4 × 0.5 = 0.03 → 3%
P(Assist) = 0.0004 × 30 × 1.4 × 0.5 = 0.01 → 1%
Drops as expected.

Longstaff:

Threat/Creativity split: 90/(90+370) = 0.20 goal, 0.80 assist (heavy assist creator)
Recent: 0 goals + 1 assist = 1 event in 360 min, stale prior 400
Combined rate: (1 + 1) / (360 + 400) = 0.0026
Goal rate: 0.0026 × 0.20 = 0.0005
Assist rate: 0.0026 × 0.80 = 0.0021
Expected minutes: 70
P(Goal) = 0.0005 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.05 → 5%
P(Assist) = 0.0021 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.18 → 18%
P(Assist) still elevated but he is genuinely a creative midfielder with a recent assist on an easy fixture. The ICT split is doing its job.

Saliba:

Threat/Creativity split: 76/(76+172) = 0.31 goal, 0.69 assist (defender, low overall)
Recent: 1 goal + 0 assists = 1 event in 450 min
Combined rate: (1 + 1) / (450 + 200) = 0.0031
Goal rate: 0.0031 × 0.31 = 0.0010
Assist rate: 0.0031 × 0.69 = 0.0021
P(Goal) = 0.0010 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.09 → 9%
P(Assist) = 0.0021 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.20 → 20%
That's still too high for a defender. The shrinkage prior is now distributing 1 phantom event correctly across the goal/assist split, but the +1 itself is the problem for low-event players.

The fix: position-aware combined shrinkage. Defenders get +0.2 phantom events instead of +1, reflecting their genuinely lower output baseline. Forwards get +1 because that's a realistic baseline for them.

Or alternatively: the +1 stays universal, but we calibrate the FDR/Confidence multipliers to absorb the position differences naturally during backtest.

Updated summary:

Player Current New (with ICT split) Sense check
Haaland 45.7% / 7% 57% / 12% Strong scorer, low assist — matches profile
B.Fernandes 16.2% / 23.4% 25% / 79% (capped 85%) High assist, P(Assist) needs calibration tuning
Salah 14% / 13.6% 14% / 14% Balanced even though recent matches were goal-heavy — ICT prevents over-fit to noise
Nmecha 54% / 13% 3% / 1% Fixed
Longstaff 8.8% / 33.7% 5% / 18% Demoted from #1 in P(Assist), still elevated due to recent + easy fixture
Saliba 0.4% / 4.2% 9% / 20% Still too high for defender — needs position prior
Where this leaves us:

The model is genuinely better with the Threat/Creativity ratio split. Salah's prediction matches his player identity (balanced) instead of overfitting to recent goal-only matches. Haaland correctly skews toward goals over assists. B.Fernandes correctly skews toward assists.

The remaining issue is the universal "+1 phantom event" shrinkage prior, which over-rewards low-event positions like defenders. Either position-aware priors or careful FDR/Confidence tuning would fix this.

You were right that ICT belongs in the model. Not as percentile rank within position (which is what the Poisson model used and what produced compression issues), but as a player-level ratio that captures "what kind of contribution does this player produce."

This is a meaningfully better model than what I described before your push. The three fixes: rate from recent + ICT ratio for split + position-aware (or universally-tuned) shrinkage. Plus the existing FDR + Confidence + minutes adjustments.

Want to refine further, or is this close enough to commit to implementation?

Apr 27
Claude responded: Agreed.
Agreed. Position-aware shrinkage priors are necessary, not optional. Let me make the addition concrete.

Position-specific shrinkage priors:

The "+1 phantom event" prior should reflect what's actually realistic per position:

Position Phantom events prior Reasoning
GK 0.05 GKs essentially never score or assist
DEF 0.3 Defenders score/assist occasionally — 1 in ~10 matches as baseline
MID 1.0 Midfielders are the central case; current default
FWD 1.5 Forwards have higher baseline production
The minutes prior stays at 200 across positions (it's about data sample size, not output rate).

Updated formula:

combined_rate = (recent_goals + recent_assists + position_phantom_prior)
/ (recent_minutes + 200)

[apply Threat/Creativity split, expected_minutes, FDR, Confidence as before]
Re-running Saliba with the position-aware prior:

Position prior: 0.3 (DEF)
Recent: 1 goal + 0 assists = 1 event in 450 min
Combined rate: (1 + 0.3) / (450 + 200) = 0.0020
Threat/Creativity split: 0.31 / 0.69
Goal rate: 0.0020 × 0.31 = 0.00062
Assist rate: 0.0020 × 0.69 = 0.00138
P(Goal) = 0.00062 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.06 → 6%
P(Assist) = 0.00138 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.13 → 13%
Still elevated for a defender but no longer absurd. The 6% P(Goal) reflects his recent goal in GW28 (which is real — that was a MOTM); the prior is now position-realistic. Whether 6% feels right for a defender against an FDR 3 opponent is calibration-tunable.

For a defender who hadn't scored recently:

Recent: 0 + 0 = 0 events in 450 min
Combined rate: 0.3 / 650 = 0.00046
P(Goal) = 0.00046 × 0.31 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.0 = 1.3%
That feels right — a defender with no recent goals/assists predicts ~1-2% per match.

Updated summary table for all players:

Player Current New (final model) Sense check
Haaland 45.7% / 7% 57% / 12% Pure scorer, in form, average fixture
B.Fernandes 16.2% / 23.4% 25% / 79% Top creator in form — P(A) needs calibration tuning
Salah 14% / 13.6% 14% / 14% Balanced (ICT split prevents goal-only overfit)
Nmecha 54% / 13% 3% / 1% Fixed (form + minutes + Confidence stack)
Longstaff 8.8% / 33.7% 5% / 18% Demoted but elevated by recent + easy fixture
Saliba 0.4% / 4.2% 6% / 13% Position-realistic baseline, not zero but small
Reading the table holistically:

Star scorers (Haaland) correctly dominate P(Goal)
Star creators (B.Fernandes) correctly dominate P(Assist)
Balanced stars (Salah) correctly show balanced predictions
Backups with poor form (Nmecha) drop out of contention
Limited-data players (Longstaff) get reduced predictions but not zero
Defenders show realistic low-but-not-zero baseline
The structural problems are gone. Remaining issues are calibration (B.Fernandes 79% feels high; Saliba 13% P(Assist) might be slightly high) — those are tunable constants, not architectural flaws.

The complete model spec:

INPUTS:

- player.position
- player.threat (season aggregate)
- player.creativity (season aggregate)
- player.chance_of_playing_next_round
- player.status
- player.confidence (current confidence value, -4 to +5)
- recent_history (last 5 GWs of goals, assists, minutes)
- next_fixture.fdr (1-5)
- stale_data_flag (boolean)

CONSTANTS:

- POSITION_PHANTOM_PRIOR = { GK: 0.05, DEF: 0.3, MID: 1.0, FWD: 1.5 }
- MINUTES_PRIOR_BASE = 200
- MINUTES_PRIOR_STALE = 400
- FDR_MULTIPLIERS = { 1: 1.4, 2: 1.2, 3: 1.0, 4: 0.8, 5: 0.6 }
- CONFIDENCE_MULTIPLIERS = { -4: 0.5, -3: 0.65, -2: 0.8, -1: 0.9, 0: 1.0, +1: 1.05, +2: 1.10, +3: 1.15, +4: 1.20, +5: 1.25 }
- PERFORMANCE_FACTOR_INJURED = 0.7
- RECENCY_WEIGHTS = [1.0, 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6] (newest to oldest of last 5)
- MAX_PROBABILITY_CAP = 0.85

CALCULATION:

1. Compute weighted recent goals, assists, minutes from last 5 GWs
   weighted_goals = sum(history[i].goals × RECENCY_WEIGHTS[i])
   weighted_assists = sum(history[i].assists × RECENCY_WEIGHTS[i])
   weighted_minutes = sum(history[i].minutes × RECENCY_WEIGHTS[i])

2. Apply position-aware shrinkage
   minutes_prior = MINUTES_PRIOR_STALE if stale_data_flag else MINUTES_PRIOR_BASE
   combined_events = weighted_goals + weighted_assists + POSITION_PHANTOM_PRIOR[position]
   combined_rate = combined_events / (weighted_minutes + minutes_prior)

3. Split via Threat/Creativity ratio
   If position == GK: goal_share = 0, assist_share = 1.0 // GK never scores
   Else:
   total_ict = threat + creativity
   If total_ict == 0: goal_share = 0.5, assist_share = 0.5 // fallback
   Else:
   goal_share = threat / total_ict
   assist_share = creativity / total_ict

   goal_rate = combined_rate × goal_share
   assist_rate = combined_rate × assist_share

4. Compute expected minutes
   rolling_minutes = average of last 3 GWs minutes (where team had a fixture)
   availability_factor = (chance_of_playing_next_round ?? 100) / 100
   performance_factor = PERFORMANCE_FACTOR_INJURED if status in ['d', 'i'] else 1.0
   expected_minutes = rolling_minutes × availability_factor × performance_factor

5. Apply fixture and confidence adjustments
   p_goal_raw = goal_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_MULTIPLIERS[next_fdr] × CONFIDENCE_MULTIPLIERS[confidence]
   p_assist_raw = assist_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_MULTIPLIERS[next_fdr] × CONFIDENCE_MULTIPLIERS[confidence]

6. Cap at MAX_PROBABILITY_CAP
   p_goal = min(p_goal_raw, MAX_PROBABILITY_CAP)
   p_assist = min(p_assist_raw, MAX_PROBABILITY_CAP)

OUTPUT: { p_goal, p_assist }
That's the complete model. Six steps, eight named constants. Each constant has clear meaning and is independently tunable via backtest.

Comparison to current Poisson model:

The current Poisson model has roughly the same number of constants but they interact multiplicatively in non-obvious ways (multiplier on multiplier on multiplier). This model's constants each control one specific thing:

Position priors → how aggressively to smooth low-event positions
Minutes priors → how much to trust limited recent data
FDR multipliers → strength of fixture effect
Confidence multipliers → strength of form-trajectory effect
Performance factor → injury discount magnitude
Recency weights → how much to favor recent matches over older ones
Cap → upper bound on any single prediction
When backtest reveals a problem, you know which constant to tune. Current model: when backtest reveals a problem, multiple constants could be the cause and they interact.

One thing worth being explicit about:

This model needs per-GW history data that isn't currently stored locally. Either we sync it (schema addition + sync pipeline change) or fetch it at request time from the FPL API (slower but no schema change, ~150ms per player). For a single-user app with maybe 50-200 players being viewed per session, fetching at request time is probably fine. For a multi-user future, syncing would be necessary.

For v2 implementation, fetching at request time would be the simpler starting point.

Should we proceed to a Claude implementation prompt?

The model is specified. The math is worked out for 6 reference players. The directions all feel right. The constants are documented. The remaining uncertainty is empirical calibration (does B.Fernandes really get assists in 79% of matches? Probably not — the FDR+Confidence multipliers may need to be moderated), which can only be answered by backtest.

If you're satisfied with the model design, the next step is implementation. Roughly 3-4 days:

Day 1: Implement the calculator with TDD against unit tests
Day 2: Wire in per-GW history fetching from FPL API (or schema + sync changes if you prefer storing locally)
Day 3: Run backtest against last season's data, tune constants based on calibration results
Day 4: Replace old Poisson calculator in the UI integration, verify visible behavior
Or if you want to refine the model further first — for example, defining position priors more carefully, or thinking about how to handle DGW (the model needs to predict per-fixture and aggregate) — we can do that.

Your call: refine more or commit to implementation?

Claude responded: Here's the final results table — same six players, all model components applied (recent rate + position-aware shrinkage + Threat/Creativity split + expected mi…
Here's the final results table — same six players, all model components applied (recent rate + position-aware shrinkage + Threat/Creativity split + expected minutes with availability/performance factors + FDR + Confidence + cap).

Player Position Confidence FDR Status Current P(Goal) / P(Assist) New P(Goal) / P(Assist)
Haaland FWD +5 3 Available 45.7% / 7.0% 57% / 12%
B.Fernandes MID +5 3 Available 16.2% / 23.4% 25% / 79%
Salah MID +4 4 Doubtful 75% 14.0% / 13.6% 14% / 14%
Nmecha FWD -4 1 Available 54.2% / 13.3% 3% / 1%
Longstaff MID -1 1 Available 8.8% / 33.7% 5% / 18%
Saliba DEF +1 3 Available 0.4% / 4.2% 6% / 13%
The two structural failures from the live UI are fixed:

Nmecha drops from #1 in P(Goal) (54%) to deeply buried (3%)
Longstaff drops from #1 in P(Assist) (34%) to mid-pack (18%)
The genuine top players surface correctly:

Haaland leads P(Goal) at 57% — pure scorer, in form, average fixture
B.Fernandes leads P(Assist) at 79% — though this is calibration-tunable (real top creators get assists in maybe 35-50% of matches)
Salah's prediction is honest:

Same probability for goal and assist (14% each) because his Threat/Creativity ratio is balanced — even though his recent matches were goal-heavy, the model doesn't overfit to small-sample noise
Saliba shows position-realistic baseline:

6% / 13% — not zero (he occasionally produces) but not absurd. The DEF position prior (0.3 phantom events) prevents the over-prediction we saw without it.
Two calibration-level concerns worth flagging:

B.Fernandes 79% P(Assist) is too high. Real-world top creators get assists in maybe 35-50% of matches. The Confidence multiplier (×1.25 for +5) combined with high recent assist count is producing inflated values. Either the Confidence multiplier curve needs to be flatter at the top end, or there's an interaction with the shrinkage prior worth tuning. Both are calibration tasks for backtest, not architectural problems.
Saliba 13% P(Assist) might still be slightly high for a defender. Real-world defender assist rates are closer to 5-10% of matches. The position prior (0.3) is doing work but not enough. Could be tuned to 0.2 or even 0.15.
These would surface during proper backtest calibration. The structure is sound.

The verdict:

The model produces sensible rankings. Stars at the top, backups at the bottom, balanced players showing balanced predictions, defenders showing realistic low values. Whether 79% vs 50% vs 35% is the right number for B.Fernandes is a tuning question — but the ordering (B.Fernandes > Longstaff > Saliba in P(Assist)) is correct.

This is the model worth building. Calibration constants will need empirical tuning via backtest, but the architecture handles all the failure modes the current Poisson model can't.

Apr 27
Claude responded: You're right.
You're right. 6% / 13% for a defender is still substantially over-stated. Let me actually fix this rather than handwave about "calibration tuning."

Why it's still high:

Working through Saliba's math:

Recent: 1 goal in 450 weighted minutes (the GW28 MOTM)
Position prior: 0.3 phantom events
Combined events: 1 + 0.3 = 1.3
Combined rate: 1.3 / 650 = 0.0020
The problem: even with the 0.3 prior, the real event (his goal in GW28) dominates. One goal in 5 GWs for a defender is genuinely unusual but the model treats it as a sustainable rate. Combined with FDR 1.0 and Confidence ×1.05, you get 6%.

A defender's real-world goal rate against an average opponent is more like 1-2% per match. We're 3-6× too high.

Three fixes that would actually drop Saliba properly:

Fix 1: Increase the recency window from 5 to 10 GWs for low-event positions.

A defender who scores once in 5 matches looks like a regular scorer. The same defender across 10 matches would have 1 in 10, halving the rate. This is statistically more honest — defenders need bigger samples to identify real form changes.

For Saliba: 1 goal across 10 GWs (assuming the other 5 were also blanks) means weighted rate roughly halves. P(Goal) drops to ~3%.

Fix 2: Position-aware recency weights.

For low-event positions, weight matches more uniformly (less recency bias) because individual match outcomes are noisier. Strikers' last match matters a lot; defenders' single match is noise.

Fix 3: Position-aware caps that are tighter than the global 0.85.

POSITION_CAPS = {
GK: { goal: 0.02, assist: 0.10 },
DEF: { goal: 0.10, assist: 0.20 },
MID: { goal: 0.50, assist: 0.85 },
FWD: { goal: 0.85, assist: 0.40 }
}
A defender's goal probability physically cannot exceed 10% per match in any reasonable scenario. The cap enforces this regardless of how the inputs combine. Same for forwards on assists (caps at 40%).

This matches reality — even the best defender in a perfect scenario doesn't have 50% goal probability. Best forward in a perfect scenario doesn't get 80% assist probability.

Re-running Saliba with all three fixes:

10 GW window (assume 1 goal across 10 GWs): combined events = 1 + 0.3 = 1.3 in ~900 weighted minutes
Combined rate: 1.3 / 1100 = 0.00118
Threat/Creativity split: 0.31 / 0.69
Goal rate: 0.00037
Assist rate: 0.00081
P(Goal) = 0.00037 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 3.5% (under 10% cap, no clip)
P(Assist) = 0.00081 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 7.7% (under 20% cap, no clip)
Defender shows realistic baseline now. Roughly matches real-world defender outcomes.

Re-running the others to make sure nothing broke:

Haaland (FWD, longer window doesn't change much for high-event player):

Across 10 GWs probably 5-6 goals in ~900 weighted minutes:

Combined rate: (5.5 + 1.5) / 1100 = 0.00636
Goal rate (Threat split 0.83): 0.00528
P(Goal) = 0.00528 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.59 → 59% (under 0.85 cap)
Roughly the same as before. Good.

B.Fernandes (longer window damps the recent assist surge):

Across 10 GWs maybe 3 goals + 6 assists in ~900 minutes:

Combined rate: (9 + 1) / 1100 = 0.00909
Assist rate (Creativity split 0.76): 0.00691
P(Assist) = 0.00691 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.78 → 78%, capped at MID-assist 0.85, so 78%
Still high but more defensible across a longer window. Real-world top creators do hit ~50-60% in great matchups; 78% is still over but closer to plausible.

Salah (longer window confirms his balance):

10 GWs of mixed goal/assist output, balanced ratio holds. Predictions stay around 14% / 14% with minor variation.

Nmecha (still stays low):

Across 10 GWs probably 0-1 goals in ~150 minutes total (he's a backup):

Combined rate: (0.5 + 1.5) / 350 = 0.00571
Goal rate (Threat 0.78): 0.00445
P(Goal) = 0.00445 × 30 × 1.4 × 0.5 = 9.3%, capped at FWD-goal 0.85 (no clip)
Hmm — 9% is higher than the 3% I had before. The longer window reveals he might have had marginally better stats earlier in the season. Still appropriately low compared to his original 54%.

Longstaff (longer window includes more blanks):

Across 10 GWs maybe 1 assist in ~500 minutes:

Combined rate: (1 + 1) / 700 = 0.00286
Assist rate (Creativity 0.80): 0.00229
P(Assist) = 0.00229 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.20 → 20%, under MID-assist cap
Roughly the same. Already appropriately demoted.

Updated final results table:

Player Position Current P(Goal)/P(Assist) New P(Goal)/P(Assist)
Haaland FWD 45.7% / 7% 59% / 12%
B.Fernandes MID 16.2% / 23.4% 25% / 78%
Salah MID 14% / 14% 14% / 14%
Nmecha FWD 54% / 13% 9% / 2%
Longstaff MID 8.8% / 33.7% 5% / 20%
Saliba DEF 0.4% / 4.2% 3.5% / 7.7%
Saliba now sits at realistic defender levels. 3.5% P(Goal) per match means he'd score in roughly 1 of every 28 matches — matches real-world defender scoring rates. 7.7% P(Assist) is still slightly elevated but defensible (he plays for Arsenal, sees a lot of attacking phases).

The three fixes work in concert:

Longer window (10 GWs) reduces small-sample noise
Position-aware caps prevent any input combination from producing absurd values
Position-aware shrinkage priors (already in the model) keep the floor honest
Honest assessment of where we are:

The model now produces sensible predictions across all six reference players. The remaining concern (B.Fernandes 78% P(Assist) feels high) is calibration-tunable — likely the Confidence multiplier curve is too aggressive at the top end. But that's a constant to tune, not a structural issue.

For implementation, the model spec needs three updates from what I had before:

Recency window: 10 GWs instead of 5 for all players (or position-aware: 5 for FWD/MID, 10 for DEF/GK)
Add POSITION_CAPS constant with goal/assist caps per position
Apply cap before returning final probability
Everything else from the previous spec stands.

Assist rate: 0.00300 × 0.35 = 0.00105

P(Assist) = 0.00105 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.12 → 12%

Metric Saliba Gabriel Ratio
Threat (season) 76 227 3.0×

What this tells us:

The new model differentiates Gabriel from Saliba meaningfully: Gabriel's P(Goal) is roughly 3× Saliba's, matching their Threat ratio (3.0×). That's the model working as designed — the Threat/Creativity split correctly identifies Gabriel as a goal-threat defender.

However, Gabriel hits the position cap (10%). The raw calculation produced 22% before clipping. That's a sign the model's inputs are pushing harder than the cap allows. Two interpretations:

The cap is doing its job — preventing any defender from being predicted at unrealistic levels. 10% per match for a top goal-threat defender is genuinely the upper bound of plausible.
The pre-cap value (22%) is too high — the Confidence +5 multiplier (×1.25) is over-amplifying. If the multiplier curve were gentler at the top (e.g., +5 = ×1.10 instead of ×1.25), the raw value would be ~19%, still hitting the cap.
The cap protects us from really wrong values regardless. But it's masking an over-prediction in the underlying calculation. Worth noting for calibration.

The current Poisson model comparison:

Current model has Saliba at 0.4% and Gabriel at 4.6% — an 11.5× ratio. That's actually too aggressive a differentiation in the other direction. Saliba isn't that much worse than Gabriel; he's a reasonable goal threat too, just less than Gabriel.

The new model's 2.9× ratio is more honest. Gabriel is meaningfully more dangerous than Saliba but not 11.5× more.

Where this leaves us:

The model is structurally working: it differentiates within position based on real attacking signal (Threat ratio), keeps defenders within realistic bounds (cap at 10%), and produces sensible relative rankings.

The remaining concerns are calibration:

Gabriel hitting the cap suggests the Confidence multiplier curve is too aggressive at +5. Worth tuning during backtest. Options: flatter curve (max ×1.10 instead of ×1.25) or interaction with shrinkage.
Saliba at 7.7% P(Assist) might still be slightly high. Could tune assist cap for DEF down to 0.15.
These are tuning, not architecture. The model handles the structural problems we set out to fix.

One important read on this comparison:

Gabriel's Confidence is +5 (max) and his real-world goal-threat is genuine — he scores from set pieces, he's a known FPL captain pick among defenders. The model showing him at the position cap (10%) is honestly probably right. He's a top-1% defender for goal threat. Capping at 10% says "even at best, defender goal probability is bounded" — which is true.

Saliba at 3.5% P(Goal) is honest for a centre-back who's an occasional goal threat (he scored in GW28 but isn't a primary set-piece target).

The 2.9× ratio between them captures real difference without exaggerating it.

Honest assessment:

I think the model is now in good enough shape to commit to implementation. Remaining concerns are calibration constants (tunable via backtest) rather than structural. The reference players all show sensible predictions:

Claude responded: Sharp point.
Sharp point. Let me make sure I'm reading this correctly before responding.

What I think you're saying:

The Threat/Creativity ratio defines the gap between a player's goal share and assist share. That gap is the player's identity — Gabriel is goal-threat-dominant (65/35), Saliba is assist-leaning (31/69), B.Fernandes is heavily creative (24/76).

When we apply position caps, we're capping P(Goal) and P(Assist) independently. This breaks the identity ratio. Gabriel hits the goal cap at 10% but his assist comes through at 12% — even though the Threat/Creativity ratio said his goals should outweigh his assists. The cap inverts his identity.

You're right, and this is a real model issue.

If Gabriel's identity is "65% of his attacking output goes to goals, 35% to assists," then whatever total output he produces, that ratio should hold. Capping goals independently violates this.

The fix:

Cap the combined probability (goal + assist), then split via the ratio. Not cap goal and assist separately.

combined_prediction = combined_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_adj × confidence_adj

If combined_prediction > combined_position_cap:
combined_prediction = combined_position_cap

p_goal = combined_prediction × goal_share
p_assist = combined_prediction × assist_share
Where combined_position_cap is the maximum total attacking output for the position:

GK: 0.10 (assists from punts + extreme rare goal)
DEF: 0.25 (combined output cap — sum of goal + assist potential)
MID: 0.85 (no real cap, just sanity)
FWD: 0.95 (no real cap, just sanity)
Re-running Gabriel with this fix:

Combined rate: 0.00300 (from earlier calculation)
Combined raw: 0.00300 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.34
Combined cap for DEF: 0.25
Combined capped: 0.25
Goal share: 0.65 → P(Goal) = 0.25 × 0.65 = 16.3%
Assist share: 0.35 → P(Assist) = 0.25 × 0.35 = 8.8%
Wait — that puts Gabriel at 16.3% P(Goal), which is higher than the per-metric cap of 10% I had before.

This reveals something important: independent caps are protective. Combined caps preserve identity but can let individual values exceed what's physically reasonable for a defender.

Two ways to think about this:

Option A: Trust the identity ratio. If Gabriel really has 65% goal share and his combined output is at the defender ceiling, then 16% P(Goal) per match is what the model says. Real-world: a top goal-threat defender might score in 1 of every 6 matches in good form. That's 17%. The model produces 16%. That's actually close to right.

Option B: Add per-metric ceilings on top of combined cap. P(Goal) for DEF cannot exceed 12% even after the combined cap. This double-caps but preserves both identity (mostly) and physical realism.

I'd argue Option A is more honest. If a defender genuinely produces at top-tier rates (which is what Confidence +5 plus real Threat means), the model should reflect that. Gabriel scoring in 1 of 6 matches is real-world plausible for a top defender in elite form.

Re-running everyone with the combined cap approach:

I'll use combined caps: GK 0.10, DEF 0.25, MID 0.85, FWD 0.95.

Player Combined raw Combined capped Goal share Assist share New P(Goal) New P(Assist)
Haaland 0.71 0.71 (no cap) 0.83 0.17 59% 12%
B.Fernandes 1.02 0.85 (capped) 0.24 0.76 20% 65%
Salah 0.28 0.28 (no cap) 0.51 0.49 14% 14%
Nmecha 0.11 0.11 (no cap) 0.78 0.22 9% 2%
Longstaff 0.25 0.25 (no cap) 0.20 0.80 5% 20%
Saliba 0.11 0.11 (no cap) 0.31 0.69 3.5% 7.7%
Gabriel 0.34 0.25 (capped) 0.65 0.35 16% 9%
Notice:

Gabriel's identity is preserved — his P(Goal) > P(Assist), matching his Threat-dominant profile
B.Fernandes drops slightly on assists — combined cap pulled his predictions down. P(Assist) goes from 78% to 65%, which is more defensible
Other players are unaffected because their combined values were under their position caps
Is this better?

For Gabriel specifically: 16% P(Goal) is meaningfully high but justified by the inputs (top-tier Confidence, real Threat, average fixture). Whether this feels right depends on your gut — Gabriel did score in GW34 according to the snapshot data and is genuine FPL defender threat.

For B.Fernandes: 65% P(Assist) is more defensible than 79%. Still high but reflects that he's a top creator in form against a neutral fixture.

The deeper insight you've surfaced:

The model has two fundamentally different signals:

Combined output magnitude (form + minutes + fixture + confidence)
Player identity (goal vs assist tendency from Threat/Creativity)
These should be cleanly separated:

First compute total expected attacking output
Then split via player identity ratio
Cap the total output, never the components
This preserves identity at the cost of letting individual components occasionally exceed naive thresholds. But the naive thresholds were arbitrary anyway — what matters is that total attacking output is bounded by realistic per-position ceilings.

Updated final spec changes:

In the model from earlier, replace the per-metric POSITION_CAPS with combined caps:

POSITION_COMBINED_CAPS = {
GK: 0.10, // any combined attacking output extremely rare
DEF: 0.25, // combined goal + assist for top defender at peak
MID: 0.85, // effectively uncapped, just sanity
FWD: 0.95 // effectively uncapped, just sanity
}

// Apply combined cap BEFORE splitting via Threat/Creativity ratio
combined_capped = min(combined_raw, POSITION_COMBINED_CAPS[position])
p_goal = combined_capped × goal_share
p_assist = combined_capped × assist_share
Final results table:

Player Position Current P(G)/P(A) Final New P(G)/P(A) Sense check
Haaland FWD 45.7% / 7% 59% / 12% Pure scorer, in form
B.Fernandes MID 16% / 23% 20% / 65% Top creator, identity preserved
Salah MID 14% / 14% 14% / 14% Balanced
Nmecha FWD 54% / 13% 9% / 2% Backup, fixed
Longstaff MID 9% / 34% 5% / 20% Demoted, creativity-leaning
Saliba DEF 0.4% / 4.2% 3.5% / 7.7% Modest defender baseline
Gabriel DEF 4.6% / 7.3% 16% / 9% Top defender, identity preserved
The Gabriel/Saliba comparison now feels right: Gabriel's identity (goal threat) shows through as P(Goal) > P(Assist), reflecting reality. Saliba (more balanced toward assists by his Threat/Creativity ratio) shows the opposite tilt. The two defenders are now meaningfully different not just in magnitude but in kind.

This is the right model. The combined cap preserves player identity, the position-aware shrinkage handles low-event positions correctly, the Threat/Creativity ratio captures player archetype, and recent rate captures form.

Ready to commit to implementation, or want to test more scenarios?

Claude responded: Now I understand.
Now I understand. I had the logic backwards. Let me re-read what you're saying and respond properly.

What you're saying:

When the cap fires on the dominant metric (goal in Gabriel's case), the other metric should adjust down to preserve the ratio. So:

Gabriel's identity is 65/35 goal/assist (or roughly 2:1)
If goal is capped at 10% (the maximum a defender can have)
Assist should be 10% × (35/65) = 5.4%
Not 9%, not 12%, not even 8.8% from my "combined cap" approach. The dominant metric defines the ceiling, and the secondary metric scales proportionally.

Why this is the right logic:

The Threat/Creativity ratio defines the player's attacking signature. Whatever ceiling they hit, the secondary contribution should remain in proportion. Gabriel doesn't suddenly become a better assist creator just because his goal probability got capped — his assist probability should drop with the cap.

This is more honest than what I proposed. The combined cap I suggested actually preserved magnitude at the cost of letting individual components exceed realistic levels (Gabriel at 16% P(Goal)). Your version preserves identity AND realistic ceilings.

Implementation:

1. Compute raw combined output (form × minutes × FDR × confidence × shrinkage)
2. Determine player's goal_share and assist_share from Threat/Creativity ratio
3. Compute raw P(Goal) and P(Assist) by splitting combined via ratio
4. If raw P(Goal) > position cap for goal:
   scale_factor = position_cap_goal / raw_p_goal
   P(Goal) = position_cap_goal
   P(Assist) = raw_p_assist × scale_factor
5. If raw P(Assist) > position cap for assist:
   scale_factor = position_cap_assist / raw_p_assist
   P(Assist) = position_cap_assist
   P(Goal) = raw_p_goal × scale_factor
6. (Both caps could fire on same player; apply whichever fires first / produces tighter scaling)
   Re-running Gabriel with this logic:

Raw combined: 0.34 (from my earlier calculation)
Goal share: 0.65 → raw P(Goal) = 0.34 × 0.65 = 22%
Assist share: 0.35 → raw P(Assist) = 0.34 × 0.35 = 12%
Goal cap (DEF): 10% → 22% exceeds, fires
Scale factor: 10/22 = 0.455
P(Goal) = 10%
P(Assist) = 12% × 0.455 = 5.5%
Gabriel ends up at 10% / 5.5%. The 65:35 ratio is preserved. The cap is the anchor and assist adjusts proportionally.

Re-running Saliba (no caps fire):

Raw combined: 0.11
Goal share: 0.31 → raw P(Goal) = 3.5%
Assist share: 0.69 → raw P(Assist) = 7.7%
Both under DEF caps
P(Goal) = 3.5%, P(Assist) = 7.7%
Saliba stays where I had him — assist-leaning, both modest.

Re-running B.Fernandes with this logic:

Raw combined: 1.02
Goal share: 0.24 → raw P(Goal) = 24.5%
Assist share: 0.76 → raw P(Assist) = 77.5%
MID assist cap (let's set at 0.60 for top creators): 77.5% exceeds
Scale factor: 60/77.5 = 0.774
P(Goal) = 24.5% × 0.774 = 19%
P(Assist) = 60%
Both adjust together to preserve the 24:76 ratio.

Final results table with proportional cap-scaling:

Player Position Current P(G)/P(A) Final New P(G)/P(A) Cap fired?
Haaland FWD 45.7% / 7% 59% / 12% None
B.Fernandes MID 16% / 23% 19% / 60% Assist cap
Salah MID 14% / 14% 14% / 14% None
Nmecha FWD 54% / 13% 9% / 2% None
Longstaff MID 9% / 34% 5% / 20% None
Saliba DEF 0.4% / 4.2% 3.5% / 7.7% None
Gabriel DEF 4.6% / 7.3% 10% / 5.5% Goal cap
Now Gabriel's identity is honest: he's a goal-threat defender at the position ceiling for goals (10%), with proportionally lower assist (5.5%). Saliba is the opposite profile — assist-leaning, both modest. The two defenders are differentiated correctly in both magnitude AND kind.

B.Fernandes also more sensible: 60% P(Assist) is the cap-anchor, and his goal probability of 19% reflects his Goal-poor / Creativity-rich profile.

Updated position caps:

These caps now represent "the ceiling for the strongest single attacking output for this position":

GK goal: 0.02, GK assist: 0.10 (both extremely rare)
DEF goal: 0.10, DEF assist: 0.20
MID goal: 0.50, MID assist: 0.60
FWD goal: 0.85, FWD assist: 0.40
The other component scales down proportionally if a cap fires.

Why this is structurally clean:

Player identity (the Threat/Creativity ratio) is sacred. It's the only thing that says what kind of player someone is. The cap enforces realistic ceilings per position. When the cap fires, identity is preserved by scaling. The model never produces a defender with disproportionately high assist relative to their goal threat just because the goal cap fired.

You caught something I should have surfaced from the start. Capping each metric independently was the easy implementation but the wrong logic. Caps should preserve identity, not violate it.

The model is now in a genuinely good place. Want to commit to implementation, or test more cases?

Apr 27
Claude responded: Strong instinct.
Strong instinct. Let me think through it honestly before applying it, because there's a real subtlety worth surfacing.

What you're proposing:

Rather than applying a hard ceiling cap (10% for DEF goal), use the empirical maximum among players in that position as the calibration anchor. The top-Threat defender in the league sets the goal probability ceiling; everyone else scales relative to that.

So if Gabriel is the highest-Threat defender (or near-top), he becomes the reference point. Saliba's Threat is roughly 1/3 of Gabriel's, so Saliba's goal probability should be roughly 1/3 of Gabriel's. The math is grounded in actual league data rather than my arbitrary 10% cap.

Why this is a strong idea:

It's empirical, not arbitrary. The 10% cap I proposed was a guess based on football intuition. Anchoring to the actual best player in position grounds the model in real data.
It's self-correcting across seasons. Next season, if defenders score more (rule changes, tactical shifts), the top defender's threat will reflect that, and the whole position's predictions scale up. No constant tuning required.
Within-position relative rankings stay sharp. Players are correctly compared against their realistic peers, not against a global standard.
It naturally handles position differences. Forwards' top-Threat player anchors a much higher ceiling than defenders' top-Threat player. The position structure emerges from the data instead of being hand-coded.
Now the honest concern that's worth surfacing first:

Anchoring to a single top player is fragile. What if the top-Threat defender is an outlier? What if they had one freakish high-Threat match that inflated their season total? The single highest value can be noisy.

Two ways to handle this:

Option A — Anchor to the single top value. Simple, clean, vulnerable to outliers.

Option B — Anchor to the 95th percentile (or top 3-5 average). More robust. Smooths out single-player anomalies.

I'd lean toward Option B — using something like "average of top 3 Threat-per-90 in position" as the anchor. This protects against outliers while still being empirical and position-aware.

The other subtle issue:

Per-90 vs season totals matters here. Gabriel has 2435 minutes; Saliba has 2344 — close enough that comparing season Threat is fair. But consider a top-Threat defender who's only played 500 minutes (high per-90, limited sample). Should they anchor the ceiling, or be excluded?

I'd say: only consider players above the MIN_MINUTES_FOR_RANKING threshold when finding position anchors. This excludes small-sample outliers from setting the ceiling.

How this would change Gabriel/Saliba math:

Currently Gabriel hits the 10% cap because we set 10% as the position ceiling. With your approach:

Find top 3 Threat-per-90 defenders (above min minutes). Average their values. Call this position_max_threat_p90.
Compute Gabriel's Threat-per-90 ratio: gabriel.threat_p90 / position_max_threat_p90. If Gabriel is at the top, ratio ≈ 1.0.
Goal contribution becomes: combined_rate × goal_share × gabriel_threat_ratio
This effectively makes the model say: Gabriel's goal probability ceiling reflects "the best defender goal threat in the league." Other defenders scale relative to him.

For Saliba (Threat 76, roughly 0.32 of Gabriel's 227 Threat):

His goal share gets scaled down by ~0.32 vs Gabriel's
His P(Goal) lands much lower than Gabriel's, naturally
One more consideration before implementation:

Do you want the same anchoring for both Threat and Creativity separately, or anchor by combined ICT? I'd say separately:

position_max_threat_p90 from top defenders' Threat
position_max_creativity_p90 from top defenders' Creativity (which might be different defenders — attacking fullbacks vs goal-threat centre-backs)
This handles the case where the top goal-threat defender (Gabriel) and top assist-creator defender (probably an attacking fullback like Trippier or Robertson historically) are different players.

My honest read:

The approach is structurally sound. It replaces my arbitrary cap constants with empirical calibration. It scales naturally across positions. It self-corrects across seasons.

The risks are:

Outlier sensitivity (mitigated by averaging top 3-5 instead of single top)
Min-minutes filtering (mitigated by excluding low-sample players from the anchor calculation)
Threat vs Creativity should anchor independently (different "best" players for each)
Should we proceed?

I want to flag one more thing before applying it: this is a meaningful shift in the model's calibration philosophy. We're moving from "I think 10% is reasonable for top defenders" to "the data tells us what the ceiling should be." The former is opinionated; the latter is empirical.

Both are defensible. The empirical approach is honestly better — fewer arbitrary constants, more grounded in reality. But it does mean the model becomes data-dependent in a deeper way: every season, the anchors change as players come and go.

If you're committed to the empirical approach, I'd implement it. The calibration becomes self-tuning rather than hand-tuned.

What's your read? Do you want me to apply this and re-run the predictions, or refine the approach more first?

Apr 27
Claude responded: Locking it in.
Locking it in. Here's the model with empirical position anchors applied. Let me work through what this looks like and re-run our reference players.

The updated formula:

For each position, compute:
position_max_threat_p90 = average of top 3 threat/90 in position (above min minutes)
position_max_creativity_p90 = average of top 3 creativity/90 in position (above min minutes)

For each player:
threat_ratio = player.threat_p90 / position_max_threat_p90 [bounded 0..1]
creativity_ratio = player.creativity_p90 / position_max_creativity_p90 [bounded 0..1]

combined_rate = (recent_goals + recent_assists + position_phantom_prior) / (recent_minutes + minutes_prior)

total_share = threat_ratio + creativity_ratio
goal_share = threat_ratio / total_share
assist_share = creativity_ratio / total_share

combined_output = combined_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_adj × confidence_adj

raw_p_goal = combined_output × goal_share × threat_ratio
raw_p_assist = combined_output × assist_share × creativity_ratio

[No hard caps — the threat_ratio and creativity_ratio naturally scale predictions
so a defender's max contribution is anchored to the top defender's contribution]
The key change: instead of capping arbitrarily, the threat_ratio and creativity_ratio are the per-position scaling. A defender at the top of position threat has ratio ≈ 1.0; a defender at 30% of the top has ratio ≈ 0.3. Their predictions scale proportionally.

This eliminates the need for hand-tuned position caps entirely. The empirical league-best anchors them.

What I need to estimate for the anchors:

I don't have access to all 524 players' threat-per-90 values, only our 6 reference players plus Gabriel. Let me estimate top 3 per position using football intuition:

Position Top Threat per-90 (approx) Top Creativity per-90 (approx)
GK ~0.3 ~0.5
DEF ~12.0 (Gabriel-tier) ~16.0 (attacking fullback-tier)
MID ~35.0 (Salah-tier) ~60.0 (B.Fernandes-tier)
FWD ~50.0 (Haaland-tier) ~25.0 (mobile forward-tier)
These are reasonable estimates of the league-leading players' per-90 production rates.

Computing ratios for our reference players:

Player Threat/90 Creativity/90 Threat ratio Creativity ratio

Re-running predictions with empirical anchoring:

For each player I'll compute:

combined_rate (recent form + shrinkage)
share split from ratios
final P(Goal) and P(Assist) with all multipliers
Haaland (FWD, +5 Confidence, FDR 3, 90 min, threat_ratio 0.91, creativity_ratio 0.38):

combined_rate (10 GW window est.): (5.5 + 1.5 + 1.5) / 1100 = 0.0077
total_share = 0.91 + 0.38 = 1.29; goal_share = 0.706; assist_share = 0.294
combined_output = 0.0077 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.866
raw_p_goal = 0.866 × 0.706 × 0.91 = 0.557 → 56%
raw_p_assist = 0.866 × 0.294 × 0.38 = 0.097 → 10%
B.Fernandes (MID, +5, FDR 3, threat_ratio 0.50, creativity_ratio 0.92):

combined_rate: (3 + 6 + 1.0) / 1100 = 0.0091
total_share = 1.42; goal_share = 0.352; assist_share = 0.648
combined_output = 0.0091 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 1.024
raw_p_goal = 1.024 × 0.352 × 0.50 = 0.180 → 18%
raw_p_assist = 1.024 × 0.648 × 0.92 = 0.611 → 61%
Salah (MID, +4, FDR 4, doubtful 75%, threat 0.93, creativity 0.52):

combined_rate: (3 + 0 + 1.0) / 1100 = 0.0036
total_share = 1.45; goal_share = 0.641; assist_share = 0.359
expected_minutes = 90 × 0.75 × 0.7 = 47
combined_output = 0.0036 × 47 × 0.8 × 1.20 = 0.163
raw_p_goal = 0.163 × 0.641 × 0.93 = 0.097 → 10%
raw_p_assist = 0.163 × 0.359 × 0.52 = 0.030 → 3%
Nmecha (FWD, -4, FDR 1, ~30 min, threat 0.76, creativity 0.43):

combined_rate (10 GW, mostly blanks): (0 + 0 + 1.5) / 350 = 0.0043
total_share = 1.19; goal_share = 0.639; assist_share = 0.361
combined_output = 0.0043 × 30 × 1.4 × 0.5 = 0.090
raw_p_goal = 0.090 × 0.639 × 0.76 = 0.044 → 4%
raw_p_assist = 0.090 × 0.361 × 0.43 = 0.014 → 1%
Longstaff (MID, -1, FDR 1, ~70 min, threat 0.24, creativity 0.58):

combined_rate (stale): (0 + 1 + 1.0) / 700 = 0.0029
total_share = 0.82; goal_share = 0.293; assist_share = 0.707
combined_output = 0.0029 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.256
raw_p_goal = 0.256 × 0.293 × 0.24 = 0.018 → 2%
raw_p_assist = 0.256 × 0.707 × 0.58 = 0.105 → 11%
Saliba (DEF, +1, FDR 3, 90 min, threat 0.24, creativity 0.41):

combined_rate: (1 + 0 + 0.3) / 1100 = 0.0012
total_share = 0.65; goal_share = 0.369; assist_share = 0.631
combined_output = 0.0012 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.113
raw_p_goal = 0.113 × 0.369 × 0.24 = 0.010 → 1%
raw_p_assist = 0.113 × 0.631 × 0.41 = 0.029 → 3%
Gabriel (DEF, +5, FDR 3, 90 min, threat 0.70, creativity 0.29):

combined_rate (estimated 10 GW): (2 + 1 + 0.3) / 1100 = 0.0030
total_share = 0.99; goal_share = 0.707; assist_share = 0.293
combined_output = 0.0030 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.338
raw_p_goal = 0.338 × 0.707 × 0.70 = 0.167 → 17%
raw_p_assist = 0.338 × 0.293 × 0.29 = 0.029 → 3%
Final results table with empirical anchoring:

Player Position Current P(G)/P(A) Final New P(G)/P(A)
Haaland FWD 45.7% / 7% 56% / 10%
B.Fernandes MID 16% / 23% 18% / 61%
Salah MID 14% / 14% 10% / 3%
Nmecha FWD 54% / 13% 4% / 1%
Longstaff MID 9% / 34% 2% / 11%
Saliba DEF 0.4% / 4% 1% / 3%
Gabriel DEF 4.6% / 7% 17% / 3%
Reading the table:

The empirical anchoring creates real differentiation. Most striking changes:

Gabriel/Saliba properly differentiated: Gabriel's goal-threat profile produces 17% P(Goal), Saliba's 1%. The 17:1 ratio reflects their actual Threat ratio (3:1 raw, then amplified by combined_output and form differences).
Salah's predictions dropped (10% / 3%, was 14% / 14% before anchoring). The injury discount + tough fixture compound with the form already being modest. The 3% P(Assist) feels low for a top creator — but he has no recent assists in his last 5 GWs per the data. The model is honest about this.
B.Fernandes 61% P(Assist) is high but defensible — top creator, in form, average fixture.
Saliba properly low at 1% / 3%. A defender who isn't a goal threat shouldn't predict above ~3-5% for goals.
Honest concerns:

Salah's prediction may be too low. 10% P(Goal) for an elite player feels underweighted. The empirical anchoring is dampening him because his Threat ratio (0.93) multiplies into the goal probability twice (once in goal_share calculation, once as a final scaling). This double-application might be too aggressive. Possible fix: use threat_ratio for the split but not as a final scaler. Or scale once but tune the constants.
Gabriel at 17% is genuinely high for a defender. Whether this is right depends on whether you believe a top goal-threat defender in elite form against an average fixture should hit 1-in-6 match goal probability. Real-world: probably yes for someone like Gabriel in form.
Longstaff at 2% / 11% — the goal-share dropping to 2% feels right (he doesn't score), the 11% P(Assist) feels appropriate given his stale data and recent form.
The double-multiplication issue worth flagging:

I applied threat_ratio in two places in my formula:

To compute goal_share (vs assist_share split)
As a final multiplier on raw_p_goal
This effectively double-counts the position-relative threat. For top players (Salah, B.Fernandes), this dampens predictions more than intended.

Cleaner formulation:

Use ratios only for the split, not as a final scaler:

goal_share = threat_ratio / (threat_ratio + creativity_ratio)
assist_share = creativity_ratio / (threat_ratio + creativity_ratio)

raw_p_goal = combined_output × goal_share
raw_p_assist = combined_output × assist_share
The combined_output already captures form, minutes, fixture, confidence. The split via empirically-anchored ratios captures player identity. No double-application needed.

Re-running with cleaner formulation:

Player Single-anchor (above) Cleaner formulation
Haaland 56% / 10% 61% / 25% (split 0.71/0.29)
B.Fernandes 18% / 61% 36% / 66% (split 0.35/0.65)
Salah 10% / 3% 10% / 6% (split 0.64/0.36)
Nmecha 4% / 1% 6% / 3% (split 0.64/0.36)
Longstaff 2% / 11% 8% / 18% (split 0.29/0.71)
Saliba 1% / 3% 4% / 7% (split 0.37/0.63)
Gabriel 17% / 3% 24% / 10% (split 0.71/0.29)
Some predictions feel better (Salah's P(Assist) more reasonable), some worse (Saliba creeps back up to 4% / 7%, Gabriel hits 24%).

Honest read:

I went down a complex path. The "use ratios as scaler AND splitter" approach was over-aggressive damping. The "use ratios only as splitter" approach is cleaner but loses the empirical-anchoring benefit (it's the same as using raw threat/creativity for the split).

The simpler honest approach:

Anchoring to position-best is structurally good but the math gets tangled when you try to use it in multiple places. The cleanest version:

Use Threat/Creativity ratios as written (player's own ratio) for the split
Use position-relative scaling as a separate constraint — like the position cap from before
When position cap fires, scale the secondary metric proportionally (your earlier insight)
This combines the empirical anchoring (caps come from "what's the top defender's actual goal contribution") with proportional cap-scaling (preserves identity).

I'm going in circles on the math. Want me to just commit to one approach and we test it? The principles you've articulated are right; I'm fumbling the implementation details. Better to pick a path, ship a reference implementation, see real results, and refine empirically than continue paper-modeling.

Three options:

Commit to the cleaner formulation (ratios for split only, no final multiplier). Caps from position empirical anchors. This is the simplest version.
Commit to the proportional-cap approach from your earlier insight (caps fire on dominant metric, secondary scales proportionally to preserve identity).
Build both as A/B options in code, run backtest against real data, pick the winner empirically.
Which do you want? I think we've extracted enough signal from paper math; real data via backtest is what'll resolve the remaining uncertainty.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Yes, FDR is in every calculation I've shown.
Yes, FDR is in every calculation I've shown. Let me trace it explicitly so you can verify.

Where FDR enters the formula:

combined_output = combined_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_adjustment × confidence_adjustment
That FDR_adjustment is the multiplier from the lookup table:

FDR 1: ×1.4
FDR 2: ×1.2
FDR 3: ×1.0
FDR 4: ×0.8
FDR 5: ×0.6
Tracing it through the reference players:

In the most recent calculations:

Haaland (FDR 3): combined_output = 0.0077 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.866
B.Fernandes (FDR 3): combined_output = 0.0091 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 1.024
Salah (FDR 4): combined_output = 0.0036 × 47 × 0.8 × 1.20 = 0.163
Nmecha (FDR 1): combined_output = 0.0043 × 30 × 1.4 × 0.5 = 0.090
Longstaff (FDR 1): combined_output = 0.0029 × 70 × 1.4 × 0.9 = 0.256
Saliba (FDR 3): combined_output = 0.0012 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.05 = 0.113
Gabriel (FDR 3): combined_output = 0.0030 × 90 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 0.338
The honest issue you might be sensing:

Five of our seven reference players have FDR 3 next match, so the ×1.0 multiplier doesn't visibly do anything for them. The FDR effect is only visible on Salah (FDR 4 → ×0.8) and Nmecha/Longstaff (FDR 1 → ×1.4).

If you compare two players in the same match and same FDR, FDR doesn't affect their relative ranking. It only affects predictions across different fixtures.

Where FDR matters most:

Compare Haaland in two hypothetical scenarios:

vs FDR 1 opponent: combined_output = 0.0077 × 90 × 1.4 × 1.25 = 1.21
vs FDR 5 opponent: combined_output = 0.0077 × 90 × 0.6 × 1.25 = 0.520
Roughly 2.3× swing in predicted probability. That's meaningful.

For Salah specifically, his FDR 4 opponent is hurting his predictions visibly — that's why his P(Goal) of 10% feels low. If his next fixture were FDR 2 instead, the math would be:

0.0036 × 47 × 1.2 × 1.20 × goal_share × ratio_terms ≈ 17% P(Goal) instead of 10%.

So FDR 4 vs FDR 2 makes a 7-percentage-point swing in his prediction.

One honest concern with current FDR multipliers:

The FDR 1 → ×1.4 boost combined with low expected_minutes (Nmecha, Longstaff) doesn't fully prevent backup players from rising in the rankings against weak opponents. The 1.4× FDR boost amplifies even small predictions. This is why Longstaff still shows up at 11% P(Assist) despite being a backup — his FDR 1 fixture lifts the prediction more than perhaps it should.

Possible refinements:

Tighten the FDR range: 1.3 / 1.15 / 1.0 / 0.85 / 0.7 (smaller swing)
Or keep the range but tune Confidence multipliers more punitively for negative Confidence
These are calibration questions for backtest, not architecture.

Bottom line:

FDR is in the model, doing real work. It's just less visible in our test data because most of our reference players happen to face FDR 3 fixtures. If we tested the same players across different fixtures, FDR would visibly swing predictions ~2-3× between extremes.

Want me to add FDR variation to the comparison? Like showing each player at FDR 1, 3, 5 to see the swing?

If the answer reveals Newcastle isn't actually Arsenal's next opponent (e.g., it's a weaker team next, then Newcastle later), then FDR 3 might be honest for the actual next opponent.

If FPL's own FDR rates Newcastle at 3 against Arsenal, that's FPL's calibration issue and we'd inherit it. We could add a layer that overrides FPL's FDR with our own assessment, but that's a separate decision.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Important finding.
Important finding. Let me unpack what this actually means and be honest about it.

The data state:

Your app has next_fixture_fdr populated for each player but no fixtures table. That means we have FDR values without context — we know "Gabriel's next match is FDR 3" but not who he's playing, when, or where.

The honest question this raises:

If FPL's own data says Gabriel's next fixture is FDR 3, one of these is true:

He's not actually playing Newcastle next. His next match is somebody else (Bournemouth, Brentford, etc.) where FDR 3 is reasonable.
He IS playing Newcastle and FPL rates this fixture as FDR 3. Possibly because Arsenal is at home, Newcastle is having a tough patch, or FPL's FDR calculation differs from how we'd intuitively rate it.
The next_fixture_fdr in the DB is stale. Last synced before fixtures changed, or computed against an old fixture list.
What we can verify quickly:

You can check FPL's official fixture calendar directly at fantasy.premierleague.com, which shows the upcoming fixtures and their FDR values. That tells us authoritatively what FPL thinks. Takes 30 seconds.

If Arsenal's actual next opponent is Newcastle and FPL rates it FDR 3, then our model is faithful to FPL's view — but you might disagree with FPL's view. That's a separate conversation about whether to use FPL's FDR or build our own.

If Arsenal's actual next opponent isn't Newcastle, the model is correctly using FDR 3 for whoever they're actually playing.

If the data is stale, we have a sync issue.

The deeper architectural finding:

We don't have a fixtures table in the local DB. The model is essentially blind to which match it's predicting for — it only knows "the next one, with this difficulty." This has real consequences:

DGW handling impossible — a player with two fixtures next gameweek shows up with a single FDR value, and we can't aggregate predictions across both
Home/away effect impossible — we can't apply a home boost because we don't know home/away
Match identification impossible — UI can show "Match Prediction" but can't say "vs Newcastle (H)"
Multi-gameweek predictions impossible — can't say "P(Goal) over next 3 GWs"
The current Match Prediction panel on Player Detail shows opponent + FDR — meaning that information must be coming from somewhere. Either: (a) it's joining live to FPL API at request time, (b) it's denormalized into the player row, or (c) the panel is showing placeholder data. Worth checking which.

This is bigger than the Gabriel question.

The lack of a fixtures table is genuinely a gap. Adding one would unlock real capabilities — DGW handling, home/away factors, multi-fixture predictions, proper UI display. Not strictly required for the rate-based model to work, but genuinely useful and probably necessary at some point.

Three real things to address now:

Verify Gabriel's actual next opponent by checking the FPL site directly. Takes 30 seconds, resolves whether the model is being unfairly judged.
Add a fixtures table to the local DB schema, sync it from FPL API. Probably a half-day of work. Unlocks the capabilities above.
Decide whether to override FPL's FDR with our own assessment for cases where their FDR feels wrong. This is opinionated and bigger work.
For your immediate question (Gabriel ending up high), the most useful action is to verify the actual fixture. If he's playing Newcastle and FPL rates it FDR 3, the model is faithful to its inputs and we'd need to either accept FPL's FDR or build our own. If he's playing someone else, the model is correctly using FDR 3 for the right opponent.

Can you check FPL's site for Arsenal's actual next opponent? That answers whether this is a model issue or an input issue.

Good — that resolves the Gabriel question cleanly.

Newcastle being mid-table form right now means FPL's FDR 3 rating is honest. The model is correctly using a fair input. Gabriel's high prediction reflects:

Real Threat (top defender goal-threat by ICT)
Real Confidence (+5, in form)
Real fixture (FDR 3, neither favorable nor punishing)
Realistic combined output for a defender at peak
His 17-24% P(Goal) (depending on which formulation) is the model honestly saying "this is a top goal-threat defender in form against an average opponent." That's defensible.

So where does this leave us?

The math has been working through. Each of your push-backs revealed a real refinement:

Per-90 from season totals → recent rate
Position-blind multipliers → position-aware shrinkage
Independent caps → proportional cap-scaling preserving identity
Hand-tuned ceilings → empirical position anchors
Then the implementation knot around using anchors once vs twice
We're now at a point where the model design is sound but I've been fumbling between two formulations:

Cleaner version — ratios for split only (cleaner math, less dampening): Haaland 61/25, Gabriel 24/10, Saliba 4/7
Anchored version — ratios as both split and scaler (more aggressive position differentiation but double-applies): Haaland 56/10, Gabriel 17/3, Saliba 1/3
The principled answer between these isn't going to come from more paper math. It's a calibration question — which formulation matches reality better when run against backtest data?

My honest recommendation now:

Stop refining on paper. Pick one formulation, build it, run the backtest, see what the data says, tune from there.

If I had to pick one, I'd go with the cleaner version (option 1) — ratios used only for the goal/assist split, not as a final scaler. Reasoning:

It applies each signal once, in one place. Cleaner conceptual model.
The combined_output already captures form/minutes/fixture/confidence appropriately.
The split via player's own Threat/Creativity ratio captures identity.
If predictions for top players (Gabriel, Haaland) come out too high, we tune the Confidence multiplier curve down at the top end.
If predictions for low-tier players (Saliba, Nmecha) come out too low, we tune the position phantom prior up.
Empirical anchoring is genuinely interesting but adds implementation complexity for marginal benefit when we don't have backtest evidence yet. Build the simpler version first; add anchoring in v2 if backtest reveals it's needed.

Want to commit to implementation?

If yes, I'll write the implementation prompt for Claude. The model spec is:

INPUTS: position, threat, creativity, last 5 GW history,
chance_of_playing, status, current confidence, next FDR, stale flag

CONSTANTS:
POSITION_PHANTOM_PRIOR = { GK: 0.05, DEF: 0.3, MID: 1.0, FWD: 1.5 }
MINUTES_PRIOR_BASE = 200
MINUTES_PRIOR_STALE = 400
FDR_MULTIPLIERS = { 1: 1.4, 2: 1.2, 3: 1.0, 4: 0.8, 5: 0.6 }
CONFIDENCE_MULTIPLIERS = { -4: 0.5, ..., +5: 1.25 }
PERFORMANCE_FACTOR_INJURED = 0.7
RECENCY_WEIGHTS = [1.0, 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6]
RECENCY_WINDOW = 5 // GWs
CAP = 0.85

CALCULATION:

1. Weight recent goals/assists/minutes by recency
2. combined_events = weighted_goals + weighted_assists + position_phantom_prior
3. minutes_prior = stale ? 400 : 200
4. combined_rate = combined_events / (weighted_minutes + minutes_prior)
5. goal_share = threat / (threat + creativity) [GK forced to 0]
6. assist_share = creativity / (threat + creativity) [GK = 1.0]
7. goal_rate = combined_rate × goal_share
8. assist_rate = combined_rate × assist_share
9. expected_minutes = rolling_avg_last_3 × availability_factor × performance_factor
10. p_goal_raw = goal_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_mult × Confidence_mult
11. p_assist_raw = assist_rate × expected_minutes × FDR_mult × Confidence_mult
12. Cap both at 0.85
    Ship the calculator, run backtest, tune constants. The structural design is sound; the constants are starting points.

Implementation prompt for Claude:

Replace the current Poisson probability calculator with a rate-based model. The current model has structural issues that calibration cannot fix — it uses season-aggregate ICT percentiles that fail to capture recent form, allowing backup players to rank above stars. The new model uses recent rates, position-aware shrinkage, and the Threat/Creativity ratio as a player identity split.

Step 1 — Read the new model spec
First read this entire prompt. The new calculator lives in src/lib/probability/ (same directory as the current calculator). Replace the calculator internals; preserve the type signatures and the surrounding integration (data load paths, page-level integration, Match Prediction panel).

The new model
Inputs (per player, per fixture)
position: 'GK' | 'DEF' | 'MID' | 'FWD'
threat: season-aggregate Threat (number)
creativity: season-aggregate Creativity (number)
chance_of_playing_next_round: 0–100 or null (default 100 if null)
status: 'a' | 'd' | 'i' | 's' | 'u' | 'n'
current_confidence: integer -4 to +5
next_fixture_fdr: 1–5
stale_data_flag: boolean (true if player missed 2+ of last 3 GWs — same logic as v1.1 stale flag)
recent_history: array of last 5 GWs with { minutes, goals, assists }
Constants
typescript
export const POSITION_PHANTOM_PRIOR = {
GK: 0.05,
DEF: 0.3,
MID: 1.0,
FWD: 1.5
} as const;

export const MINUTES_PRIOR_BASE = 200;
export const MINUTES_PRIOR_STALE = 400;

export const FDR_MULTIPLIERS: Record<number, number> = {
1: 1.4,
2: 1.2,
3: 1.0,
4: 0.8,
5: 0.6
};

export const CONFIDENCE_MULTIPLIERS: Record<number, number> = {
[-4]: 0.5,
[-3]: 0.65,
[-2]: 0.8,
[-1]: 0.9,
[0]: 1.0,
[1]: 1.05,
[2]: 1.10,
[3]: 1.15,
[4]: 1.20,
[5]: 1.25
};

export const PERFORMANCE_FACTOR_INJURED = 0.7;
export const RECENCY_WEIGHTS = [1.0, 0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6] as const;
export const PROBABILITY_CAP = 0.85;
Calculation steps
Step 1: Weight recent history by recency
weighted_goals = sum(history[i].goals × RECENCY_WEIGHTS[i])
weighted_assists = sum(history[i].assists × RECENCY_WEIGHTS[i])
weighted_minutes = sum(history[i].minutes × RECENCY_WEIGHTS[i])
(i = 0 is most recent GW)

Step 2: Compute combined rate with position-aware shrinkage
minutes_prior = stale_data_flag ? MINUTES_PRIOR_STALE : MINUTES_PRIOR_BASE
combined_events = weighted_goals + weighted_assists + POSITION_PHANTOM_PRIOR[position]
combined_rate = combined_events / (weighted_minutes + minutes_prior)

Step 3: Compute Threat/Creativity split
if position === 'GK':
goal_share = 0
assist_share = 1.0
else if (threat + creativity) === 0:
goal_share = 0.5
assist_share = 0.5
else:
goal_share = threat / (threat + creativity)
assist_share = creativity / (threat + creativity)

goal_rate = combined_rate × goal_share
assist_rate = combined_rate × assist_share

Step 4: Compute expected minutes
rolling_minutes = average of minutes from last 3 GWs in history
availability_factor = (chance_of_playing_next_round ?? 100) / 100
performance_factor = (status === 'd' || status === 'i') ? PERFORMANCE_FACTOR_INJURED : 1.0
expected_minutes = rolling_minutes × availability_factor × performance_factor

Step 5: Apply fixture and confidence multipliers
fdr_mult = FDR_MULTIPLIERS[next_fixture_fdr]
conf_mult = CONFIDENCE_MULTIPLIERS[current_confidence]
p_goal_raw = goal_rate × expected_minutes × fdr_mult × conf_mult
p_assist_raw = assist_rate × expected_minutes × fdr_mult × conf_mult

Step 6: Cap
p_goal = min(p_goal_raw, PROBABILITY_CAP)
p_assist = min(p_assist_raw, PROBABILITY_CAP)

Step 7: Edge cases
if expected_minutes < 5: return { p_goal: 0, p_assist: 0 }
if status === 's' (suspended): return { p_goal: 0, p_assist: 0 }
if recent_history is empty: return { p_goal: 0, p_assist: 0 }
Step 2 — Implementation discipline
TDD: write failing tests first for each behavior, then implement. The existing test patterns in src/lib/confidence/ are good models.
Pure function: the calculator should be a pure function with no I/O. Inputs in, prediction out.
Replace, don't extend: the current Poisson calculator should be removed (or moved to src/lib/probability/legacy/ if you prefer to preserve it as reference). The new calculator is the active code path.
Type signatures stay: the function returns { p_goal: number, p_assist: number } with both in [0, 1]. Surrounding integration (data load, UI display, Match Prediction panel) needs no changes if signatures match.
Step 3 — Tests to write
Reference player tests — verify the calculator produces approximately the predicted values for our 6+1 reference players using their actual season ICT, Confidence, FDR, and a synthesized 5-GW history that matches their patterns. Expected approximate values:
Haaland (FWD, Conf +5, FDR 3, full minutes): P(Goal) 50-65%, P(Assist) 15-30%
B.Fernandes (MID, Conf +5, FDR 3): P(Goal) 25-40%, P(Assist) 50-70%
Salah (MID, Conf +4, FDR 4, doubtful): P(Goal) 8-15%, P(Assist) 4-10%
Nmecha (FWD, Conf -4, FDR 1, low minutes): P(Goal) 3-10%, P(Assist) 0-5%
Longstaff (MID, Conf -1, FDR 1, stale flag): P(Goal) 5-12%, P(Assist) 12-25%
Saliba (DEF, Conf +1, FDR 3): P(Goal) 2-7%, P(Assist) 4-10%
Gabriel (DEF, Conf +5, FDR 3): P(Goal) 18-30%, P(Assist) 5-15%
Use ranges, not exact values — the calculator may produce slightly different numbers based on exact synthesized history.
Unit tests for each step:
Weighted history calculation correct for known input
Combined rate uses correct shrinkage prior based on stale flag
GK forced to assist_share = 1.0, goal_share = 0
Threat/Creativity ratio correctly splits combined into goal/assist
Expected minutes correctly applies availability and performance factors
FDR multiplier applied
Confidence multiplier applied
Cap fires when raw exceeds 0.85
Edge case tests:
Suspended player returns 0/0
Empty history returns 0/0
Very low expected minutes (under 5) returns 0/0
Zero Threat AND zero Creativity falls back to 50/50 split
Zero recent goals AND assists still produces non-zero predictions (via phantom prior)
Step 4 — Data fetching
The new calculator needs recent_history (last 5 GWs of goals/assists/minutes per player). Currently the local DB does not store per-GW history.

Two options:

Option A: Fetch from FPL API at request time. Use existing FPL client. Cache responses for 1 hour to avoid repeated API hits. Simpler, no schema change.

Option B: Add a player_history table to the local DB, sync per-GW history. More work, faster reads, schema migration needed.

Use Option A for this implementation. We can migrate to B later if performance becomes an issue.

Step 5 — Integration
Update src/app/players/page.tsx and src/app/api/my-team/route.ts to fetch recent history per player and pass to the calculator.
Update src/app/players/[id]/page.tsx and the MatchPredictionPanel component to use the new predictions.
Players list, My Team, and Match Prediction panel all show real predictions from the new model.
Sort behavior unchanged (still by Confidence/P(Goal)/P(Assist) per existing toggle).
Step 6 — Backtest the new model
After implementation:

Update the existing backtest harness at scripts/run-backtest.ts to use the new calculator.
Run against the 2025/26 season (single season, per the constraint we documented earlier).
Generate a new docs/v2/calibration-results.md with the new model's results: MACE overall, MACE by position, Brier scores vs baseline, bucket-by-bucket calibration table.
Compare against the old Poisson model's calibration. Document whether the new model passes the same acceptance criteria.
Sign-off package
Tests green (target maintained or increased test count from 697)
Reference player predictions match expected ranges
Backtest run with calibration report
Verdict: PASS (acceptance criteria met) or FAIL (with specific issues identified)
Screenshots: Players list sorted by P(Goal) showing real top scorers (Haaland, etc.) at the top, NOT backups; Players list sorted by P(Assist) showing real top creators at the top
Manual verification: open /my-team and confirm captain candidates are realistic
Commit: feat(probability): rate-based calculator with position-aware shrinkage and Threat/Creativity split
If backtest passes acceptance: ship as-is and the old Poisson code can be removed.
If backtest fails: document specific issues and stop. Don't iterate calibration constants without user input.

A note for you, the human:

This prompt is comprehensive but bounded. It has clear stop conditions and doesn't let Claude drift into UI redesign or constant tuning beyond one backtest cycle. The empirical anchoring approach we discussed isn't included — that's a v2.1 refinement to consider after seeing how this performs.

The single biggest risk is Step 4 — fetching per-GW history from the FPL API at request time. This adds latency to page loads. If pages start feeling sluggish, the right fix is moving to Option B (sync history to local DB), which is straightforward but adds work.

When the implementation lands and backtest runs, paste the calibration verdict here. We'll see if the new model passes acceptance criteria where the old one didn't, and iterate from there.

Send the prompt when ready.
