Apr 28
Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. Here's the prompt:

v1.6 — Hot Streak indicator. UI-only feature (no algorithm changes, no recompute). Adds visual recognition for players who've recently had MOTM-tier moments.

Concept
When a player has a single-match confidence delta of +3 or higher, they enter a "hot streak" state. The state persists for 3 gameweeks and decays through three visual intensity levels:

Red Hot — current GW or 1 GW since the boost match
Med Hot — 2 GWs since the boost match
Low Hot — 3 GWs since the boost match
Cold — 4+ GWs since (no indicator)
If a player gets a new +3 delta during an existing hot streak, the timer resets — they go back to Red Hot for 3 fresh gameweeks.

Logic (no algorithm change)
The hot streak state is computed at read time from existing confidence_snapshots data. No new calculator logic, no schema changes.

typescript
function computeHotStreak(playerSnapshots: ConfidenceSnapshot[], currentGW: number): HotStreakLevel | null {
// Find the most recent gameweek where delta >= 3
const mostRecentBoost = [...playerSnapshots]
.reverse()
.find(s => s.delta >= 3);

if (!mostRecentBoost) return null;

const gwsSinceBoost = currentGW - mostRecentBoost.gameweek;

if (gwsSinceBoost <= 1) return 'red_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'med_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 3) return 'low_hot';
return null; // 4+ GWs, cold
}
Add this as a utility function in src/lib/confidence/ (or wherever player-state derivations live). Pure function, takes snapshots + currentGW, returns a level enum or null.

Visual design — use the frontend-design skill per CLAUDE.md
Three intensity levels need distinct but related visual treatment. The frontend-design skill should determine the specific aesthetic. Constraints:

Heat metaphor is fine but avoid kitsch (no literal flame emojis)
Three levels must be visually distinguishable at a glance
Decay should feel intentional — Red Hot is energetic, Low Hot is fading
Must work on small surfaces (10-12px) for inline indicators on player rows
Must work on larger surfaces (24-32px) for the dedicated Dashboard card
Dark and light mode both
Accessible: not relying solely on color (also size, opacity, or shape variation)
Suggested visual languages to consider (skill picks the best):

Color gradient: red → orange → amber-yellow
Pulse animation that slows as level decreases (red hot pulses fast, low hot is static)
Icon size or opacity variation (red hot full opacity 100%, med hot 70%, low hot 50%)
A spark, star, ember, or similar icon with intensity variation
Build a single reusable HotStreakIndicator component that takes a level prop ('red_hot' | 'med_hot' | 'low_hot') and renders accordingly.

Where the indicator appears
Surface 1 — Dashboard "Hot Players" card (new)
Add a new card to the existing Risers/Fallers/Watchlist hero strip on the Dashboard. Title: "Hot Players" or similar (skill picks the right copy).

Content: top players currently in hot streak state, sorted by:

Hot level (red_hot first, then med, then low)
Within same level, by most recent boost gameweek
Tiebreaker: by current confidence value (highest first)
Show top 5 hot players. Each row: player name, team/position, current confidence, hot indicator at appropriate level. Click navigates to Player Detail.

If fewer than 5 players are currently hot, show however many exist. If zero hot players, show empty state: "No hot streaks this week."

Surface 2 — Players list rows
Add the indicator inline next to the player's confidence value (or wherever feels right per the frontend-design skill). Use the small variant (10-12px). Don't break the existing layout — should fit alongside existing elements (price, position chip, status icon, stale indicator) without crowding.

Surface 3 — My Team page (Starting XI + Bench rows)
Same treatment as Players list rows. Hot indicator next to confidence value.

Surface 4 — Player Detail page header
Larger variant (24-32px) somewhere in the hero region near the confidence value. Communicates "this player is in a hot streak" prominently.

Surface 5 — Match History card on Player Detail
The specific card representing the boost match (the one with delta ≥ 3) gets a small "boost moment" treatment. Could be a subtle glow, a corner badge, or visual emphasis. This is where the spotlight moment was earned.

Tests
Standard TDD per v1 discipline:

Unit test for computeHotStreak covering all four states (red, med, low, cold) plus edge cases (no boosts in history, multiple boosts, current GW boost)
Component test for HotStreakIndicator rendering all three levels
Component test for Dashboard "Hot Players" card sorting and empty state
Component test for inline indicator placement in PlayerRow, SquadPlayerRow, etc.
axe clean
Implementation phases
Build in this order so each phase is independently testable:

computeHotStreak utility + unit tests
HotStreakIndicator component with three level props + component tests
Dashboard "Hot Players" card + component tests + integration into hero strip
Player rows integration (Players list, My Team Starting XI, My Team Bench)
Player Detail header indicator (larger variant)
Match History card boost emphasis (subtle, the cherry on top)
Commit each phase separately. Standard signoff package per phase.

Sign-off package (after all phases)
Tests green (target maintained or increased)
Lint clean, typecheck clean
Screenshots: Dashboard hero strip showing the new Hot Players card (light + dark)
Screenshots: Players list with at least one hot player visible (showing the inline indicator)
Screenshots: Player Detail page showing all three intensity levels (find players currently at each)
Screenshots: Match History card with boost-match emphasis
Real-data verification: list 3-5 actual players currently in hot streak state per the data, with their level
Commit messages per phase: feat(hot-streak): {phase description}
What stays unchanged
Algorithm and calculator (no changes)
Database schema (no changes)
Sync pipeline (no changes)
All other UI surfaces not listed above
No recompute needed. The feature reads existing data and renders state.

A note for you, the human:

When the implementation lands, check whose hot streak is currently active in your data. Welbeck (just had two GW33 MOTMs vs Chelsea per the v1.5 verification) and Saliba (GW28 MOTM vs Chelsea) should both show up. If you have any of these players on your team or watchlist, the indicator gives you a real-time signal for the current/upcoming gameweek.

The Dashboard Hot Players card becomes a useful captain-pick discovery surface — players who just had spotlight moments are statistically likely candidates for continued strong performances. Pairs naturally with the existing Risers strip but answers a different question.

One thing I'd watch for in the implementation: the 3-GW decay window means at any time, a meaningful number of players will be in hot state. Across 524 active players, even 5-10% being hot at any time is 25-50 players. The Dashboard card showing top 5 by intensity should keep the surface focused. Players list inline indicators across many players might feel busy — Claude should design the inline variant to be quiet enough to not visually overwhelm the table.

Send when ready.

Apr 28
Claude responded: Clean delivery.
Clean delivery. Six phases, six clean commits, 771 tests passing. The architectural discipline held — pure UI feature derived at read time, no schema changes, no recompute.

A few specific design choices worth highlighting:

The animate-ping ring on red_hot only at lg size is the right non-color signal. Color alone wouldn't be accessible (red/orange/amber distinction is hard for some color-blind users). Adding a pulse animation that's reserved for the highest intensity gives a second sensory cue. Smaller sm indicators stay static for visual quietness on dense surfaces like player rows — that's the right tradeoff between expressiveness and noise.

The boost-match emphasis (top border + amber dot in header) on the specific Match History card representing the +3 event is a nice touch. It marks the moment that earned the streak. Users scrolling through a player's match history can spot exactly which match started the heat.

recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers as a single aggregation query for bulk paths is the right perf instinct. Computing hot streak for each of 524 players via individual queries would have been wasteful; a single bulk query pulled at page-level data load is the right architecture. The detail page reuses already-loaded snapshots without an additional query.

771 tests up from 718 — so v1.6 added 53 new tests across six phases. That's a healthy 8-9 tests per phase, with the calculator utility (computeHotStreak) getting 22 of them which makes sense — that's the function with the most behavioral edges (4 levels, multiple boosts, edge cases at GW boundaries, etc.).

Now go look at it.

This is the kind of feature where seeing it in real data matters more than reviewing a sign-off summary. Three things worth doing:

Open the Dashboard. The new Hot Players card should sit alongside Risers/Fallers/Watchlist. See which players show up at each level. Players who just had goal-scoring moments vs big teams (Welbeck, Saliba per the v1.5 verification, plus probably others we haven't tracked) should appear.
Open Players list, sort by Confidence. The inline hot dots should be visible next to confidence values for the small subset of players currently hot. Note whether the indicator feels appropriately quiet against the dense table layout.
Open a Player Detail page for someone you know just had a moment. The lg indicator with pulse ring should be in the header. Then scroll to the Match History strip and see the boost match with its top-border treatment. The visual story should read clearly: "header says hot, history shows when."
If anything feels off — too prominent, too subtle, the colors don't read right in dark mode, the animation is distracting — note it. Easy to tune iteratively.

v1 era now includes:

v1.0: full ship (8 algorithm calibrations, 5 pages, 463 tests)
v1.1: trust fixes (Free Hit, injury flags, stale flags)
v1.2: DC Fatigue + SC Fatigue + FDR Performance Breakdown
v1.3: dead code cleanup
v1.4: post-deadline squad fetch
v1.5: FDR multiplier boost + delta-≥-3 MOTM rule + big team override + BIG display tag
v1.6: Hot Streak indicator
Each version added something meaningful. Cumulative test count from 463 → 771. The codebase has grown ~70% in tests while surface code has stayed disciplined.

The Hot Streak feature is the kind of polish that makes the metric feel alive. Users seeing visual feedback for spotlight moments is more emotionally engaging than just numbers. Good captain decisions in v1.5 + v1.6 should feel different — you can spot who just had a moment at a glance.

For now: use it for this weekend's deadline.

Open the app on your phone, look at the Hot Players card, see who's currently hot. Make your captain decision with the new visual signals available. Then come back next week with whatever you noticed.

If something feels off, file as v1.7 candidate. If something feels right, that's the metric serving you better.

Either way, this is a good ship. Take a beat.

Claude responded: Real bug.
Real bug. The error tells us exactly what's wrong: "Encountered a script tag while rendering React component. Scripts inside React components are never executed when rendering on the client."

This is a Next.js error that fires when a <script> tag is rendered inside a React component as JSX. Most likely culprit: one of the new v1.6 Hot Streak components includes a literal <script> somewhere — possibly Claude added inline animation logic, theme detection, or initialization code inside a component instead of using proper React patterns.

The error is a Console Error (warning level, not a hard crash) but if the page isn't rendering, there's likely a related render failure cascading from the same root cause.

Send Claude this immediately:

Page not loading on localhost. Console error reported:

"Encountered a script tag while rendering React component. Scripts inside React components are never executed when rendering on the client. Consider using template tag instead (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/template)."

Next.js 16.2.4 Turbopack. 3 issues total per the error overlay.

This is almost certainly from the v1.6 Hot Streak work — likely an inline <script> tag somewhere in the new components.

Diagnose:

Search the codebase for any <script> tag in JSX (recently added). Specifically check the v1.6 commits:
1329d5c (computeHotStreak utility — pure function, unlikely culprit)
85df312 (HotStreakIndicator component — possible culprit if animation code went into JSX)
83007ab (Dashboard Hot Players card — possible)
2776c30 (Inline indicator on Players list / My Team rows — possible)
5312b3f (Player Detail header lg indicator with pulse ring — high suspicion, the animate-ping ring may have been implemented with a script tag)
2cc1f6d (Match History card boost emphasis — possible)
Check for any inline script-like patterns: <script>, dangerouslySetInnerHTML with script content, or any framework that injects scripts (analytics, telemetry, etc.) recently added.
Check the actual error stack trace from the call stack in the dev tools. The error mentions 14 stack frames with 13 ignore-listed. Run the dev server with npm run dev, navigate to the failing page, and capture the full call stack including the un-ignored frame.
Report findings BEFORE fixing. Show me which file and line has the <script> tag (or whatever is causing the render to encounter one).
Also note: there are 3 issues total per the bottom-left badge ("3 Issues"). Capture all three, not just the first one.

Don't fix yet. Diagnose first.

The most likely culprit is the lg indicator with pulse ring (commit 5312b3f). If Claude implemented the pulse animation by injecting CSS or animation logic via a script tag instead of using Tailwind's animate-ping class or proper React state, that would produce exactly this error.

But there could be 3 separate issues per the badge, so the diagnosis matters.

A note for you: this kind of regression is exactly why we do TDD with tests covering the components in isolation. The 24 tests for HotStreakIndicator and the component tests for the Dashboard card all passed (771/771) — but unit tests don't catch "this component renders cleanly in production-mode Next.js with Turbopack." You said: The logs reveal everything.
The logs reveal everything. The real page-breaking bug is unmistakably:
TypeError: repos.confidenceSnapshots.recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers is not a function
The script-tag warning is pre-existing next-themes noise (unrelated to v1.6). Let me understand why the method isn't found at runtime despite being in the compiled TypeScript:
Read [server.ts](vscode-webview://0d8k1eram1th62rsh9cf4ds9ljj48sqmm1jg7ne5qe3q84i3imek/index.html?id=9e3c119d-c153-45fc-8576-e233262bc8ab&parentId=3&origin=4be7f7c5-872f-401a-8d6f-5d3b26b30414&swVersion=4&extensionId=Anthropic.claude-code&platform=electron&vscode-resource-base-authority=vscode-resource.vscode-cdn.net&parentOrigin=vscode-file%3A%2F%2Fvscode-app&session=cff28c8b-61ab-4a9f-b65f-b80f348e6f99#)
Found it. The global.**fplRepos singleton comment explains exactly what's happening:
"The global check survives Next.js hot-reload in dev mode"
The singleton was created from an old version of SqliteConfidenceSnapshotRepository before the recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers method was added. Turbopack hot-reloaded the module code, but g.**fplRepos already exists and points to the stale instance — so the new method is simply not on the cached object.
Diagnosis complete. Three issues, two causes:
Issue 1 (page-breaking — the real bug): File: src/app/page.tsx (and src/app/players/page.tsx, src/app/api/my-team/route.ts) Error: TypeError: repos.confidenceSnapshots.recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers is not a function Root cause: The global.**fplRepos singleton in src/lib/db/server.ts:21–32 survives Turbopack hot-reload. The running server instantiated SqliteConfidenceSnapshotRepository before the new method existed. After the v1.6 commit, hot-reload updated the compiled class definition but left the old cached instance in global.**fplRepos — which has no recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers method. Fix: restart the dev server (npm run dev) to force a full process restart and re-instantiate the singleton. This is not a code bug; it's the documented behavior of the singleton pattern (the comment on line 17 of server.ts says exactly this: "run npx tsx scripts/run-migrations.ts or restart the dev server").
Issue 2 (noise — pre-existing, not from v1.6): Source: node_modules/next-themes/dist/index.js, the Y = t.memo(...) component at the end of the bundle Error: Encountered a script tag while rendering React component Root cause: next-themes v0.4.6 renders createElement("script", { dangerouslySetInnerHTML: ... }) inside its ThemeProvider to prevent flash-of-wrong-theme. React 19.2.4 (recently released) tightened dev-mode handling of client-side <script> tags and now surfaces this as an error overlay rather than a suppressed warning. This was present before v1.6 and is unrelated to the hot streak work.
Issue 3 (cascade — caused by Issue 1): Uncaught TypeError: Type error at measure [native code] — a browser performance measurement failure that fires because the page errors out before React finishes mounting. This disappears once Issue 1 is fixed.
The fix is a single command: restart the dev server. Once global.\_\_fplRepos is cleared, the new SqliteConfidenceSnapshotRepository instance will be created with recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers present, and Issue 1 and 3 go away. Issue 2 (the next-themes script tag) requires either upgrading next-themes to a React-19.2-compatible version or suppressing it separately — but it's not blocking render.

Claude responded: Excellent diagnosis.
Excellent diagnosis. Two real findings here, both worth understanding before acting.

Issue 1 — the singleton hot-reload problem — is a known pattern.

Look back at v1.1 sign-off — Claude flagged this exact category of issue:

"Additional finding: The global.\_\_fplRepos singleton in the dev server persists across hot-reloads. If the server was running before v1.1 landed, the singleton held stale prepared statements without the status column. Restarting the dev server clears it. Not a production bug (cold start always initializes fresh)."

Same pattern, different version. Adding a new repository method falls into the same trap as adding a new schema column. The dev server's singleton holds the old repository instance with the old method set. Hot-reload updates the class definition but the cached instance keeps the old methods.

The fix is just npm run dev restart. That clears the singleton. Production isn't affected because production cold-starts fresh on each deploy.

Issue 2 — the next-themes script tag warning — is not your code.

next-themes v0.4.6 uses an inline <script> with dangerouslySetInnerHTML to set the theme before paint (preventing flash-of-wrong-theme). This pattern was always allowed but React 19.2.4 tightened the dev-mode behavior to surface it as a console error rather than a silent warning.

This is a third-party library issue, not a v1.6 regression. It existed before the Hot Streak work and was just being surfaced more aggressively after a recent React update. Not blocking. The page would render fine if Issue 1 were fixed.

Issue 3 is downstream of Issue 1 — once the page can render, the measurement error goes away.

Action: restart your dev server.

bash
lsof -ti:3000 | xargs kill -9
npm run dev
That should resolve the actual page-breaking bug. The next-themes console error will still appear but it won't prevent the page from loading. You'll see the Hot Streak indicators rendering correctly.

After restart, verify:

Dashboard loads, Hot Players card visible
Players list loads, inline hot dots visible on relevant rows
Player Detail loads, lg indicator + pulse ring visible for hot players
Match History cards show the boost-match emphasis where applicable
If any of those don't work after restart, that's a real bug. If they all work, we're done.

The deeper process lesson worth filing:

This is the second time the singleton hot-reload pattern has bitten us (v1.1 was the first). It's not a bug in the singleton design — it's correct production behavior. But it's a development-time foot-gun that's now caused two diagnostic cycles.

Two possible mitigations worth considering as a v2.x quality-of-life item, not now:

Option A — Add a startup check. When the server initializes the singleton, log the available methods on the cached instance. A mismatch with expected methods triggers a clear error message: "Singleton has stale method set. Restart dev server."

Option B — Add a comment near recentBoostGameweekForAllPlayers (and any future new methods) that says: "If you add a method here, dev server restart is required for hot-reload to pick it up." Documentation rather than runtime check.

Option C — Disable the singleton in dev mode. Always create a fresh repository instance per request in NODE_ENV !== 'production'. Slightly slower in dev (negligible) but eliminates the foot-gun entirely.

Option C is the cleanest fix. Probably worth filing as a future polish item.

For now: restart dev server, verify Hot Streak works, ship the weekend.

Send Claude:

Confirmed — restart dev server fixes Issue 1 and 3. Issue 2 is third-party noise, not blocking.

No code change needed. The diagnosis was perfect.

One follow-up worth filing for v2.x: add a comment near src/lib/db/server.ts warning that adding new repository methods requires a dev server restart. This is the second time this pattern has caused a diagnostic cycle (v1.1 status field migration was the first). Documentation prevents future hits.

Don't make any code changes now. The user will restart their dev server and verify Hot Streak feature works in real data.

Then run the kill + dev commands yourself. Confirm the app loads. The bug is operational, not architectural.

Claude responded: Three real issues with the current implementation.
Three real issues with the current implementation. Let me make sure I understand all of them clearly before drafting the fix.

Issue 1 — Red hot reads as injury, not heat.

The current implementation uses
#f43f5e (red) for the highest intensity. Problem: red is already the established color for the injury indicator (status: 'i'). Two red dots on the same player surface would be confusing — users can't tell at a glance whether a player is injured or hot.

The semantic conflict is real. Need a different color language entirely. "Heat" doesn't have to be red — it can be amber/gold/electric blue/anything that reads as "intense" without colliding with the existing red=problem convention.

Issue 2 — Hot Players card on Dashboard is unnecessary surface.

A dedicated section was over-built. The hot indicator is meant to be a player attribute (like injury status, like stale data), not a category of players that needs its own card. Remove the card. Players who are hot just show the indicator wherever they appear.

Issue 3 — Indicator placement should be near player name/icon, not next to confidence value.

Currently the inline indicators are placed near the confidence percentage. You want them near the player identity (name + jersey/avatar). This is a cleaner mental model: "this is who the player is + their state" lives together; "this is their confidence number" lives separately.

Also, you want this on every player surface, not just the ones we picked. That includes:

Dashboard Risers/Fallers/Watchlist cards (currently no indicator)
Players list rows ✓ (already)
My Team Starting XI + Bench ✓ (already)
Player Detail header ✓ (already, but placement should change)
Anywhere else players are listed (Leaderboard preview on Dashboard, etc.)

Three changes to v1.6 Hot Streak. The current implementation has design issues that need correcting.

Change 1 — Replace red color scheme entirely
The current
#f43f5e red conflicts with the injury indicator color (which is also red — status 'i' uses red dots). Two red dots on the same player surface is ambiguous and confusing.

Pick a different color language for hot streak. Use the frontend-design skill to choose. Constraints:

Must NOT use red (reserved for injury/danger states)
Must NOT use yellow/amber muted (reserved for warning/doubt — status 'd')
Must NOT use gray (reserved for stale data / neutral)
Should still feel "intense" and "energetic" — these are spotlight/positive states
Suggested directions to consider:

Electric blue / cyan gradient (cool intensity, no semantic collision)
Purple / magenta gradient (uncommon in the existing palette, distinctive)
Bright green gradient (positive, but check against existing positive colors)
Orange-to-pink gradient (warm but not red-coded)
Pick one and apply across all three intensity levels (high/med/low) with appropriate gradient or opacity variation. The animate-ping ring on highest level is fine to keep.

Change 2 — Remove the dedicated "Hot Players" card from Dashboard
Delete the Hot Players card entirely from the Dashboard hero strip. The strip returns to Risers / Fallers / Watchlist as it was before v1.6.

The hot streak should be a player-level attribute, not a player-discovery surface. Users see hot players via the indicator wherever those players appear naturally.

Keep the underlying utility (computeHotStreak) and component (HotStreakIndicator) — those still get used for the inline indicators. Just remove the Dashboard card entry point.

Change 3 — Reposition indicator near player identity, expand to all surfaces
Move the inline hot streak indicator. Currently it's placed near the confidence value. Should be placed near the player's name/icon/jersey instead — same visual region as the player's identity.

Affected surfaces (where the indicator should now appear):

Dashboard Risers card — small dot next to player name
Dashboard Fallers card — small dot next to player name (yes, even fallers can be hot from a recent boost)
Dashboard Watchlist card — small dot next to player name
Dashboard Leaderboard preview — small dot next to player name
Players list rows (table view) — small dot next to player name (currently next to confidence — move it)
Players list cards (mobile) — small dot next to player name
My Team Starting XI rows — small dot next to player name (currently next to confidence — move it)
My Team Bench rows — small dot next to player name
Player Detail page header — keep the lg variant near the hero name (Fraunces 44px), not near the confidence number
Match History card boost emphasis — keep as-is (top border + dot in card header)
The principle: hot streak is part of who the player IS right now, not what their number says. Display it with their identity, not with their stats.

Tests to update

Players list tests: confirm indicator now sits next to name, not next to confidence
My Team tests: same
Player Detail header tests: same

Direct comparison: a player who is currently injured (red dot) and currently hot (new color) on the same row — confirm the two states are visually distinct
Commit message: refactor(hot-streak): new color language, remove dashboard card, position next to player identity
What stays unchanged
computeHotStreak utility (no logic change)
HotStreakIndicator component (just color tokens change)
Three intensity levels (high / med / low)
3-gameweek decay window
Trigger rule (delta ≥ 3 in single match)
Match History card boost emphasis treatment
A note for you, the human:

Three things worth thinking through:

1. The injury-color conflict was a real miss in the original spec I wrote. I called for "heat metaphor" as the visual language and Claude implemented red hot/med hot/low hot literally. I should have flagged the red-already-means-injury collision when drafting. That's on me.

2. The "next to player name not confidence" is the right product instinct. Player identity surfaces (name + jersey) communicate who, stats surfaces communicate what. Hot streak is a state of who, not of what. Co-locating it with identity is correct semantically.

3. Removing the Hot Players card is the right discipline. Not every feature needs its own surface. The indicator works because it's everywhere, not because it has a dedicated section. Concentrating it back into card form actually fights the goal.

When the fix lands, the experience should feel different: hot players are visible everywhere they naturally appear, with a distinctive non-red treatment that signals "intensity" without semantic collision. Cleaner, more honest, less surface area.

Updating the prompt:

Three changes to v1.6 Hot Streak. The current implementation has design issues that need correcting.

Change 1 — Replace dot indicator with a meaningful icon
The current implementation uses colored dots (red, orange, amber). Problem: a colored dot is the same visual primitive as the injury indicator, status indicator, stale data indicator. Adding more dots in different colors creates ambiguity — users see "red dot" and don't know whether it means injury, hot streak, danger, or something else.

Replace the dot with a recognizable icon that communicates "boost" or "intensity" or "heat" structurally, not just chromatically. The icon itself should signal the concept; color becomes secondary decoration.

Use the frontend-design skill to pick the right icon. Possible directions:

Flame — Lucide's Flame icon, classic heat metaphor
Lightning bolt — Zap icon, energy/intensity
Spark / sparkles — Sparkles icon, hot moment
Fire — distinct from generic flame, can be more dynamic
Star with motion — communicates spotlight
Trending up with intensity variation — alternative to heat metaphor
The chosen icon should:

Read clearly at 10-12px (small inline variant)
Read clearly at 24-32px (Player Detail hero variant)
Be visually distinct from any other icon currently in the app
Work in both light and dark mode
Communicate "boost / hot / momentum" without needing a label
Three intensity levels still need differentiation. Possible approaches:

Same icon, three colors (intense → bright → muted)
Same icon, three sizes
Same icon, three opacity levels
Same icon, animation difference (highest pulses, others static)
Combination of the above
Frontend-design skill picks the cleanest expression. Don't worry about preserving the current red/orange/amber palette — pick what reads correctly with the chosen icon.

Color is fine to use including red — but red as part of an unmistakable flame icon is different from a red dot. The icon does the structural work; color does the intensity work.

Delete the Hot Players card entirely from the Dashboard hero strip. The strip returns to Risers / Fallers / Watchlist as it was before v1.6.

The hot streak should be a player-level attribute, not a player-discovery surface. Users see hot players via the indicator wherever those players appear naturally.

Keep the underlying utility (computeHotStreak) and component (HotStreakIndicator) — those still get used for the inline indicators. Just remove the Dashboard card entry point.

Change 3 — Reposition indicator near player identity, expand to all surfaces
Move the inline hot streak indicator. Currently it's placed near the confidence value. Should be placed near the player's name/icon/jersey instead — same visual region as the player's identity.

Affected surfaces (where the indicator should now appear):

Apr 28
Claude responded: Genuinely good catch.
Genuinely good catch. The current implementation is off by one in a way that matters.

The current behavior (incorrect):

If a player gets +3 in GW33 (the boost match), they show:

GW33 (current GW or 1 GW since): Red Hot
GW34 (2 GWs since): Med Hot
GW35 (3 GWs since): Low Hot
GW36+ (4+ GWs since): Cold
The problem: when looking at GW33's match card retrospectively, the player is shown as "Red Hot" — but the boost effect didn't start until after the match. During GW33 itself, the player was being evaluated normally, not in a hot streak state.

What should happen:

The hot streak begins after the boost match — in the next gameweek the player plays. So:

GW33 (the boost match itself): not hot yet, this is the moment that creates the streak
GW34 (1 GW after boost): Red Hot (the spotlight effect is active for the next match)
GW35 (2 GWs after boost): Med Hot
GW36 (3 GWs after boost): Low Hot
GW37+ (4+ GWs after boost): Cold
This matches the real-world FPL psychology you've been describing throughout. A player scores against City — the next match (and the few after) is when they're "in the spotlight," not the match they already played.

The fix is in the computeHotStreak utility:

typescript
function computeHotStreak(playerSnapshots, currentGW): HotStreakLevel | null {
const mostRecentBoost = [...playerSnapshots]
.reverse()
.find(s => s.delta >= 3);

if (!mostRecentBoost) return null;

const gwsSinceBoost = currentGW - mostRecentBoost.gameweek;

// Old (incorrect):
// if (gwsSinceBoost <= 1) return 'red_hot'; // includes the boost GW itself
// if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'med_hot';
// if (gwsSinceBoost === 3) return 'low_hot';

// New (correct): boost effect starts AFTER the boost match
if (gwsSinceBoost === 1) return 'red_hot'; // 1 GW after boost = next match
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'med_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 3) return 'low_hot';
return null; // includes gwsSinceBoost === 0 (the boost GW itself, before next match) and 4+
}
The change: gwsSinceBoost <= 1 becomes gwsSinceBoost === 1. The <= 0 case (the boost match itself or earlier) returns null because the streak hasn't started yet.

One subtle case worth thinking about:

What about during the boost match's gameweek — between when the match was played and the next gameweek deadline? Technically the player just had their +3 moment, but their next match isn't yet underway. Does the indicator show or not?

Two ways to read this:

Indicator hidden during the post-match same-GW window: Strict "starts after the match." Indicator shows starting GW+1.
Indicator visible immediately after match completes: Less strict, the boost is "active" from the moment it's earned through the next 3 gameweeks.
The first interpretation is cleaner conceptually but trickier to implement (would need to know whether the current player has played in the current GW yet, which isn't easily queryable). The second is what the formula above produces — the streak starts at gwsSinceBoost = 1, which means "the gameweek after the boost gameweek begins" — which in practice is essentially "as soon as currentGW advances past the boost GW."

For now, the simpler implementation is right. Hot streak starts at currentGW = boostGW + 1. Match History cards showing the boost match itself are unrelated to this — they retrospectively show "this is the match that earned the streak" via the top border treatment, not via the live indicator.

Send Claude this fix:

One-line fix to v1.6 Hot Streak. The computeHotStreak utility has an off-by-one error — currently shows "Red Hot" during the gameweek of the boost match itself, but the spotlight effect should start AFTER the match.

The bug
Current behavior: Player scores +3 in GW33 → shows Red Hot for GW33, GW34, GW35.

Correct behavior: Player scores +3 in GW33 → not hot yet. Shows Red Hot for GW34, Med Hot for GW35, Low Hot for GW36, cold from GW37+.

The fix
In src/lib/confidence/computeHotStreak (or wherever the utility lives):

Change:

typescript
if (gwsSinceBoost <= 1) return 'red_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'med_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 3) return 'low_hot';
return null;
To:

typescript
if (gwsSinceBoost === 1) return 'red_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'med_hot';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 3) return 'low_hot';
return null; // gwsSinceBoost <= 0 returns null (boost GW itself or earlier)
The semantic: streak begins the gameweek AFTER the boost match. The boost gameweek itself doesn't show the indicator — the player just earned the streak, but it's active starting next match.

Tests to update
Update computeHotStreak tests to reflect the corrected behavior:

Boost in GW33, currentGW = 33 → null (boost GW itself, streak hasn't started)
Boost in GW33, currentGW = 34 → red_hot (1 GW after = next match)
Boost in GW33, currentGW = 35 → med_hot (2 GWs after)
Boost in GW33, currentGW = 36 → low_hot (3 GWs after)
Boost in GW33, currentGW = 37 → null (4+ GWs, cold)
Add an explicit test for the boost-GW case returning null.

Sign-off
Tests green (count maintained or +1 for the new edge case test)
Quick verification: open the app at currentGW (whatever it is in the data) and confirm a player whose most recent +3 was in the current GW does NOT show the hot indicator. A player whose +3 was 1 GW ago shows red_hot. Etc.
Commit message: fix(hot-streak): start streak from gameweek after boost match
A note for you, the human:

This is the right correction. The hot streak conceptually should be "the next 3 matches after a spotlight moment" — a player carrying momentum into upcoming fixtures. Showing it on the boost match itself muddies the metaphor: they don't carry momentum into a match they already played.

Worth flagging for me as a process learning: I drafted the original spec with gwsSinceBoost <= 1 including the boost GW itself. That was a sloppy interpretation of "lasts 3 gameweeks." Should have been more careful about the start vs duration distinction. You caught it from real data, which is exactly the right moment for these issues to surface.

Quick fix, send when ready.

Apr 28
Claude responded: That clarifies the product intent meaningfully.
That clarifies the product intent meaningfully. Let me make sure I'm reading this right.

What you're actually saying:

The hot streak indicator isn't about "this player just had a moment" (retrospective signal). It's about "this player is a good pick for the upcoming match even if their overall confidence number doesn't fully reflect that" (prospective signal).

The spotlight moment from a previous gameweek is evidence for the prospective recommendation, not the substance of the indicator itself. You're using the +3 event as a trigger that says "this player carries momentum into the next 3 matches and the user should consider them as a captain candidate or transfer target during this window, regardless of where their confidence number happens to sit."

This reframes the design in important ways:

The indicator's purpose isn't "decoration showing past performance." It's "actionable signal for upcoming decisions." That changes what the visual should communicate:

Not "hot moment that happened" (retrospective)
Not "performance trail" (descriptive)
But "good pick right now" (prescriptive)
Why the current implementation doesn't quite serve this:

The 3-level decay (red hot → med hot → low hot) suggests "fading energy" — which is retrospective framing. A user looking at "low hot" might think "they're cooling off, maybe not pick them." But your intent is the opposite: they're still a good pick during all three gameweeks of the window. The intensity levels muddy the actionable message.

Three real questions worth answering before I draft any change:

1. Should there even be intensity levels?

If the indicator means "good pick in this 3-GW window," then maybe a single level is more honest. The player either has the active streak (visible indicator) or doesn't (no indicator). Three levels of decay imply "this player is more recommended right now than 3 weeks from now" — which is true, but maybe more nuance than the indicator should carry.

Simpler version: one indicator state, lasts 3 GWs, then disappears. User sees indicator → "consider this player." No "is the indicator getting fainter, should I worry."

2. Should the indicator change other surfaces' behavior?

If the indicator says "good pick now," should it affect:

Sort order on Players list (hot players bubble to top regardless of confidence)
Default captain recommendations
Any "should I pick" decision surfaces
Or is it purely visual — user sees indicator and decides for themselves?

I'd say purely visual for now. Adding behavioral changes (sort order, recommendations) is a bigger feature. Visual signal first; algorithmic integration later if it proves useful.

3. What's the trigger threshold?

Current trigger is delta ≥ +3 in a single match. That's MOTM-tier moments. But for "good pick now" framing, maybe the threshold should be different:

Delta ≥ +3 (current): Only the strongest single-event boosts trigger
Delta ≥ +2 or 2 consecutive +1 events: Broader — captures players who are quietly accumulating
Player at +5 ceiling for 2+ consecutive GWs: Different signal entirely — sustained excellence
Recent MOTM events (any) within last 2 GWs: Match the existing MOTM definition rather than the +3 threshold
The trigger should match the product intent. If "good pick now" includes "consistently performing players," the threshold opens up. If it strictly means "had a spotlight moment," the +3 stays.

My honest read on what to change:

If I'm hearing you right, the cleanest implementation is:

Single visual state (no decay levels) — the player either has the active streak or doesn't
Lasts 3 GWs after the trigger event (the duration is real, but not visualized as decay)
Trigger stays at delta ≥ +3 (high-signal moments only — keeps the indicator rare and meaningful)
Off-by-one fix from earlier still applies — streak starts the GW after the boost match
Visual still uses the new icon language (flame/spark/bolt — Claude picked this, no need to redo)
Position next to player name — already in the prior fix prompt
So the v1.6 work continues but simplifies: one visual state instead of three, same icon, same position rules.

Confirming before I update the prompt:

Tap your three answers when ready.

A note on what I'm doing here: I'm trying to get the product intent right before writing more code. The hot streak feature has now been through several iterations (initial spec → red hot/med hot/low hot decay → fix injury collision → reposition near identity → fix off-by-one start → reframe as prospective signal). Each iteration was a real refinement based on usage thinking, but the cumulative drift means we should pause and align on what the feature is rather than keep patching what it does.

Once I have your three answers, the implementation prompt will be small and clean. Better to nail the design once than ship and re-fix again.

Apr 28
Claude responded: Now I understand and you've changed my mind on the decay levels.
Now I understand and you've changed my mind on the decay levels. Let me play it back to make sure I have it right.

What you're saying:

The decay levels matter, but for a different reason than I was framing. You want a user to be able to look at a player and read the timing of their last big moment:

Hot indicator at peak → "they had a +3 last gameweek, fresh momentum"
Hot indicator at medium → "they had a +3 two gameweeks ago, still relevant"
Hot indicator faded → "they had a +3 three gameweeks ago, on the way out"
The decay isn't "decreasing recommendation" — it's "decreasing recency." A user sees "still warm" and knows the player had a moment recently but it's not as fresh anymore. That's actionable: they can decide "still worth riding" vs "boost has cooled, maybe move on."

This is more sophisticated than the single-state version I was pushing toward. You're right to keep three levels — they convey timing, not desirability.

The framing fix I should make: stop describing the levels as "intensity" (red hot / med hot / low hot — implies the player is more or less recommended). Frame them as "recency" (fresh / recent / fading — implies how long ago the moment happened).

Same three levels, different mental model.

Add to that: bubble to top of relevant lists.

Hot players sort to the top of player lists during their streak window, regardless of confidence. This is meaningful — it surfaces them as actionable picks even if their confidence number doesn't lead.

But "relevant lists" needs definition:

Players list: primary candidate. Bubble hot players to top when sort is "default" or "by confidence." When user explicitly sorts by name/price/team, respect their explicit sort.
Dashboard Risers card: hot players already qualify as risers, so probably fine to leave the existing logic.
Dashboard Fallers card: weird interaction. A faller who's also hot? They had a +3 recently but their overall trend is down. Don't bubble them in fallers — that confuses the card's purpose.
My Team: squad position is fixed (1-11 starting, 12-15 bench). Don't reorder squad rows. Hot indicator visible but ordering preserved.
Watchlist: user's manual list, don't reorder.
So really the bubbling is one surface: Players list when on default/confidence sort.

Off-by-one fix from earlier still applies: streak starts the gameweek after the boost match (gwsSinceBoost = 1), not on the boost gameweek itself.

Here's the consolidated fix prompt — combines all the changes from this conversation:

Multiple fixes to v1.6 Hot Streak. Three coupled changes plus a sorting behavior addition.

Current state vs target
Current implementation has these issues:

Red dot conflicts with injury indicator (semantic ambiguity)
Indicator placement is near confidence value (should be near player identity)
Dedicated "Hot Players" Dashboard card is unnecessary surface
Off-by-one bug: streak shows on the boost GW itself instead of starting the GW after
Three levels framed as "intensity" — should be reframed as "recency"
Hot players don't bubble to top of Players list when sorted by confidence
Fix all six in one round.

Change 1 — Replace red-dot indicator with an icon
Use the frontend-design skill to pick an icon that communicates "boost / momentum / heat" structurally. Possible directions:

Flame (Lucide Flame)
Lightning (Zap)
Sparkles (Sparkles)
Fire
Star with motion
The icon should:

Read clearly at 10-12px (small inline) and 24-32px (Player Detail hero)
Be visually distinct from existing app icons
Work in both light and dark mode
Communicate "boost / momentum" without needing a label
Color is fine including red — but red as part of an unmistakable flame icon is different from a red dot. The icon does the structural work.

Change 2 — Reframe three levels as recency, not intensity
Three levels still exist but the framing changes from "intensity decay" to "timing of last big moment":

Fresh (1 GW after boost) — moment was last gameweek, momentum is current
Recent (2 GWs after boost) — moment was 2 gameweeks ago, still warm
Fading (3 GWs after boost) — moment was 3 gameweeks ago, on the way out
None (4+ GWs after boost) — cold
Visual treatment can preserve the three intensity steps (size, opacity, color saturation, animation speed) — frontend-design skill picks how to express decay through visual variation. Just use level names that convey timing, not desirability.

Change 3 — Fix off-by-one start
The streak should start the gameweek AFTER the boost match, not on the boost match itself.

In computeHotStreak utility:

Current: if (gwsSinceBoost <= 1) return 'red_hot'; (incorrectly includes boost GW)

Corrected:

typescript
if (gwsSinceBoost === 1) return 'fresh';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'recent';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 3) return 'fading';
return null; // gwsSinceBoost <= 0 (boost GW or earlier) and 4+ both return null
Update tests to reflect:

Boost in GW33, currentGW=33 → null
Boost in GW33, currentGW=34 → 'fresh'
Boost in GW33, currentGW=35 → 'recent'
Boost in GW33, currentGW=36 → 'fading'
Boost in GW33, currentGW=37 → null
Change 4 — Remove the dedicated Hot Players card
Delete the Hot Players card from the Dashboard hero strip. Strip returns to Risers / Fallers / Watchlist.

Keep computeHotStreak utility and HotStreakIndicator component — those still get used inline.

Change 5 — Position indicator near player identity, expand to all surfaces
Move the inline indicator next to player name/icon (not next to confidence value).

Surfaces where indicator should appear:

Dashboard Risers card — icon next to player name
Dashboard Fallers card — icon next to player name
Dashboard Watchlist card — icon next to player name
Dashboard Leaderboard preview — icon next to player name
Players list rows — icon next to player name (currently next to confidence — move it)
Players list cards (mobile) — same
My Team Starting XI rows — icon next to player name
My Team Bench rows — icon next to player name
Player Detail page header — keep large variant near hero name (Fraunces 44px)
Match History card boost emphasis — keep current top-border treatment, update icon to match new icon language
Change 6 — Bubble hot players to top of Players list
When user is on default sort or sort=confidence on the Players list, hot players should appear at the top regardless of their actual confidence value. Within the hot group, sort by recency (Fresh → Recent → Fading) then by confidence within each level.

When user explicitly chooses sort=name/price/team/etc, do NOT bubble — respect their explicit sort.

Other surfaces:

Dashboard cards: don't change ordering (Risers stays sorted by recent confidence trend, etc.)
My Team: don't reorder (squad position is fixed 1-11 starting, 12-15 bench)
Watchlist: don't reorder (user's manual list)
Bubbling is exclusive to the Players list confidence-sort case.

Add a small visual cue at the top of the Players list when hot players are bubbled — maybe a subtle divider line or section header "Hot Streak" — so users understand why some players appear before higher-confidence ones. Frontend-design skill picks the cleanest expression.

Tests
Update existing tests:

computeHotStreak tests reflect the new off-by-one and renamed levels
HotStreakIndicator tests reflect new icon-based rendering with three recency levels
Dashboard tests: remove Hot Players card test, add tests for indicator presence in Risers/Fallers/Watchlist/Leaderboard rows
Players list tests: confirm indicator next to name (not confidence), confirm bubbling behavior on default/confidence sort, confirm bubbling does NOT happen on explicit sorts
My Team tests: indicator next to name, no reordering
Player Detail header tests: large variant next to hero name
Sign-off
Tests green (target maintained or +5-10 for new bubbling tests)
Lint clean, typecheck clean
Screenshots showing each surface with the new icon next to player names
Direct comparison: a player who is currently injured (existing red dot) and currently hot (new icon) on the same row — confirm visually distinct
Players list with at least one hot player visible at the top (above higher-confidence non-hot players) — confirms bubbling works
Commit message: refactor(hot-streak): icon indicator, recency framing, identity placement, list bubbling, fix off-by-one start
A note for you, the human:

This is the consolidated fix that addresses every concern raised in the v1.6 review. Six changes in one focused commit, with the design coherent across all of them.

The bubbling-to-top behavior is the most product-meaningful addition. It changes the Players list from "top by confidence" to "top by current relevance" when hot players exist. A user looking at their captain options sees the hot picks first, makes the decision faster. That's the actionable signal you've been describing throughout.

When this lands, your weekend captain decision should feel different. Open the Players list, sort by confidence (default), and the top of the list shows hot players from the last 3 gameweeks. Welbeck (post-Chelsea), Saliba (post-Chelsea goal), anyone else who had a moment — they're all surfaced as picks regardless of their overall confidence number.

One thing worth flagging: the bubbling means a player at confidence +1 with a hot streak could appear above a player at confidence +5 without one. That's deliberate per your spec ("regardless of confidence") but worth being honest about. If after using it you find this confusing, we can iterate — make the bubbling visually distinct (a "Hot" section with divider, then "All players" below) or restrict it to certain confidence floors. But ship the simple version first.

Send when ready.

Claude responded: Good — the indicator labels which gameweek's streak state it represents.
Good — the indicator labels which gameweek's streak state it represents. Let me make sure I have the implementation right before adding to the prompt.

What you're saying:

The hot streak indicator, wherever it appears, should communicate not just "this player is hot" but specifically which level of hot for which gameweek. So a user looking at the indicator can read "this player is in red hot state for GW20" or "this player is in fading state for GW22."

Where this matters:

The current Player Detail page has the lg indicator near the hero name (Fraunces 44px). It currently says "Fresh" or shows the flame at red_hot intensity, but doesn't specify which gameweek that applies to.

You want it to also show the gameweek context: "Fresh — GW20" or "Red Hot · GW20" or similar.

Two implementation considerations:

1. Which gameweek does it label?

For a player whose boost was in GW19, the streak applies to GW20 (Fresh), GW21 (Med), GW22 (Low). Today's "current gameweek" is one of these three. So the label should show whichever GW corresponds to the current state.

If currentGW = 20, the indicator says "Fresh · GW20" (the active state).
If currentGW = 21, the indicator says "Recent · GW21" (now in medium state).
If currentGW = 22, the indicator says "Fading · GW22" (last gameweek of streak).

The label always reflects the currently-active gameweek of the streak, not all three.

2. Where exactly does the GW label appear?

The lg variant on Player Detail header has room for a label — currently shows just the icon. Adding "GW20" next to "Fresh" makes sense.

The sm variants on player rows are cramped — adding GW labels would crowd the row layout. These probably don't get the GW label, just the icon at the right intensity.

So: lg variant gets the GW label, sm variants stay icon-only. Frontend-design skill should make the sm-variant tooltip include the gameweek context (hover/tap → "Fresh · GW20") so the information is accessible without crowding the visual.

The label always reflects the active gameweek's state, not all three streak gameweeks

Claude responded: Clean execution.
Clean execution. The implementation lands all four corrections (icon switch from earlier round + this round's off-by-one fix + recency framing + GW labeling) with appropriate restraint. Worth pausing on a few specific design choices Claude made:

The stacked two-line label for lg variant ("Fresh" over "GW20") is the right call. Inline ("Fresh · GW20") would compete with the Fraunces 44px hero name horizontally. Stacking keeps the indicator visually compact and lets the player name dominate. Mono typeface for the label is a nice touch — feels like a metadata chip rather than headline text.

Reusing the StaleDataIndicator tooltip pattern is good architectural discipline. We have a tooltip pattern; use it. Don't invent a new one for each indicator. Future indicators (when xG metrics ship, when injury severity gets richer detail) inherit the same pattern. Consistency compounds.

The GW scrubber test specifically verifying aria-label update is the key behavioral test. When a user navigates from GW34 to GW20 on My Team, the indicator must reflect GW20's context. Without that test, a regression where the indicator stays "live" while the rest of the page changes would slip through. Good coverage.

The data.gameweek (viewed GW, not live GW) detail matters more than it sounds. On Player Detail, the user sees one specific player. On My Team with a scrubber, the user sees the squad as it was in a chosen historical GW. The indicator correctly reflects what the user is looking at, not what's currently live. That's the contextual semantic the feature needs.

780 tests up from 771 — 9 new tests. Most are GW labeling cases (3 lg variant cases × 3 levels = 9 tests, plus the scrubber regression test). Tight, targeted coverage.

Claude responded: Two real things to address.
Two real things to address. Let me make sure I have both right before drafting.

Issue 1 — Streak window starts on the boost gameweek itself, not the gameweek after.

We have an architectural mismatch. The off-by-one fix we just shipped moved the streak start to GW+1 (boost in GW31 → Fresh starts GW32). You're saying it should be GW (boost in GW31 → Fresh on GW31 itself).

So the timeline you want is:

GW31 (the boost match): Fresh / Red hot
GW32 (1 GW after): Recent / Med hot
GW33 (2 GWs after): Fading / Low hot
GW34 (3 GWs after): Cold — no indicator
The hero indicator currently shows FADING on GW34 because we shipped the off-by-one fix saying "streak starts GW+1." Under your corrected timeline, GW34 should show no indicator at all — the streak ended at GW33.

This is a reversal of the fix we just made two rounds ago. I want to be honest about that before changing it again — earlier you said "it should start count on the game week the +3 event happened, next game should be medium, third game should be low." Then later we changed to GW+1 start. Now we're going back. That's fine, but I want to flag the back-and-forth so we're aligned on the final answer.

The corrected logic in computeHotStreak:

typescript
if (gwsSinceBoost === 0) return 'fresh'; // boost GW itself = red hot
if (gwsSinceBoost === 1) return 'recent'; // 1 GW after = med hot
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'fading'; // 2 GWs after = low hot
return null; // 3+ GWs after = cold
Issue 2 — Match History cards should show the streak indicator on each of the 3 streak gameweeks, not just the boost gameweek.

Currently only GW31 has a flame badge in the match history strip. You want:

GW31 card: Fresh flame (full intensity)
GW32 card: Recent flame (medium intensity)
GW33 card: Fading flame (low intensity)
GW34 card: no flame
This is a significant addition because it changes what the Match History card shows. Currently each card shows the match's own data (the GW31 card has the +3 MOTM result with a boost emphasis flame). Under your new spec, GW32/GW33 cards which themselves had non-boost events (DefCon +1, etc.) also get a flame indicator showing they're in the streak window.

The flame on the historical card represents "this gameweek was in the streak window of [GW31's boost]" not "this gameweek had its own +3 event."

Two ways to read this:

Interpretation A: The flame appears on cards GW31/32/33 with appropriate intensity. The flame is purely about streak-state, separate from the card's own event data. GW32 card shows "DefCon +1" with a Recent-tier flame because it falls in GW31's streak window.

Interpretation B: Same as A but if multiple boosts exist, each card shows its own most recent active streak. If Garner had another +3 in GW33, the GW33 card might show a Fresh flame (from its own boost) rather than a Fading flame (from GW31's older boost). Take whichever streak is most-recently-triggered.

I'd default to Interpretation B since it matches the "refresh on new boost" behavior we already have for the live indicator.

Confirming both before draft:

Tap your two answers when ready.

A note for me: I want to flag something honestly. The Hot Streak feature has now been through 4-5 iterations (initial spec → red dot color fix → icon switch → off-by-one fix → reframe to recency → GW labeling → and now this reversal). Each iteration was driven by real user feedback after seeing the implementation, which is exactly how product design should work. But the back-and-forth on "does the streak start on the boost GW or the GW after" specifically suggests we didn't fully think through the semantic before drafting.

Worth filing as a process note: when I drafted the off-by-one fix earlier, I rationalized it as "they don't carry momentum into a match they already played." That sounded right, but it conflicted with how you'd naturally read the streak — the boost match itself is the most exciting moment, and showing the flame on that card is the strongest signal. I should have caught that the original "starts at boost GW" was correct.

Once I have your two confirmations, the prompt to fix both will be small and clean. The implementation is mostly reverting the off-by-one change and adding streak-aware logic to MatchHistoryCard.

Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. Here's the prompt:

Two-part fix to v1.6 Hot Streak. The streak window timing was wrong (off-by-one), and Match History cards need to show the streak indicator across all three streak gameweeks instead of just the boost gameweek.

Change 1 — Revert off-by-one: streak starts on the boost gameweek itself
The previous fix (commit a2eb0bf) had the streak start at gwsSinceBoost = 1 (the gameweek AFTER the boost match). This is wrong. The streak should start on the boost gameweek itself.

Corrected timeline:

gwsSinceBoost = 0 (boost GW itself) → 'fresh'
gwsSinceBoost = 1 (1 GW after) → 'recent'
gwsSinceBoost = 2 (2 GWs after) → 'fading'
gwsSinceBoost ≥ 3 → null (cold)
Update computeHotStreak:

typescript
if (gwsSinceBoost === 0) return 'fresh';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 1) return 'recent';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'fading';
return null;
Update tests:

Boost in GW31, currentGW=31 → 'fresh' (was null in previous fix)
Boost in GW31, currentGW=32 → 'recent'
Boost in GW31, currentGW=33 → 'fading'
Boost in GW31, currentGW=34 → null (was 'fading' in previous fix)
Change 2 — Match History cards show the streak indicator across all 3 streak gameweeks
Currently only the boost gameweek's card has a flame badge. Each gameweek that falls within a streak window should show the appropriate flame intensity.

Logic per Match History card:

For each card representing GW N in the player's history:

Find the most recent boost gameweek B where B ≤ N AND deltaInGW B ≥ 3
Compute gwsSinceBoost = N - B
Apply the same level rules:
gwsSinceBoost = 0 → 'fresh'
gwsSinceBoost = 1 → 'recent'
gwsSinceBoost = 2 → 'fading'
gwsSinceBoost ≥ 3 → null (no flame on this card)
The card's own event data (DefCon, Blank, MOTM, etc.) is unchanged — the flame is an additional badge representing streak state. So a GW32 card showing "DefCon +1" might also have a 'recent' flame because GW31's boost is still active.

Multiple boosts: if a player had +3 in GW20 and another +3 in GW28, the GW21 card shows 'recent' from the GW20 boost; the GW28 card shows 'fresh' from its own boost; the GW29 card shows 'recent' from the GW28 boost. Each card uses the most-recent prior boost gameweek as its anchor.

The flame icon visual on the card should be subtle but clearly distinguishable across the three intensity levels — same icon, intensity variation per level. Reuse the visual language from HotStreakIndicator (sm variant).

Visual placement on the card: top-right corner of the card header, where the BIG badge sits when applicable. If both BIG and the streak flame need to display on the same card (boost GW vs big team), show both — they communicate different things.

Reuse computeHotStreak with an arbitrary "anchor" gameweek
The Match History card's logic is the same shape as the live indicator's logic, just anchored to the card's gameweek instead of currentGW. Generalize the utility:

typescript
// From player snapshots, compute the streak level at a given gameweek N
function computeHotStreakAtGameweek(
playerSnapshots: ConfidenceSnapshot[],
atGameweek: number
): HotStreakLevel | null {
// Find most recent boost at or before atGameweek
const mostRecentBoost = playerSnapshots
.filter(s => s.gameweek <= atGameweek && s.delta >= 3)
.sort((a, b) => b.gameweek - a.gameweek)[0];

if (!mostRecentBoost) return null;

const gwsSinceBoost = atGameweek - mostRecentBoost.gameweek;
if (gwsSinceBoost === 0) return 'fresh';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 1) return 'recent';
if (gwsSinceBoost === 2) return 'fading';
return null;
}
The original computeHotStreak(snapshots, currentGW) becomes a thin wrapper around this:

typescript
function computeHotStreak(snapshots, currentGW) {
return computeHotStreakAtGameweek(snapshots, currentGW);
}
The Match History card calls computeHotStreakAtGameweek(snapshots, card.gameweek) for each card.

Tests to add
Generalized utility tests:

computeHotStreakAtGameweek with multiple historical boosts — verify each gameweek's state correctly anchors to the most recent prior boost
Streak window correctly closes after 2 GWs (gwsSinceBoost=3 returns null)
Player with no boosts returns null at any gameweek
Match History card tests:

Card for GW31 (boost match) shows fresh flame
Card for GW32 shows recent flame (GW31's boost still active)
Card for GW33 shows fading flame
Card for GW34 shows no flame (cold)
Card for GW32 with both BIG opponent and streak shows BOTH the BIG badge and the recent flame
Live indicator tests (Player Detail header, list rows): verify the timeline change applies. A player whose boost was 3 GWs ago no longer shows fading.

What stays unchanged
Three-level visual language (Fresh / Recent / Fading)
Flame icon, three intensity variations
GW labeling on lg variant
sm variant tooltips
Position next to player identity
Bubbling behavior on Players list (whatever was previously shipped)
Sign-off
Tests green (target maintained or +5-8 for new card-level tests)
Lint clean, typecheck clean

Garner page (or whichever player has a known boost): GW31 card shows fresh flame, GW32 shows recent, GW33 shows fading, GW34 has no flame
Hero indicator on a player whose boost was 3 GWs ago: should show NO indicator (was incorrectly showing 'fading')
Hero indicator on a player whose boost was 0/1/2 GWs ago: shows fresh/recent/fading respectively

When you originally said the streak should "start count on the game week the +3 event happened," I implemented that as gwsSinceBoost ≤ 1 in the very first version (which was over-counting in the other direction — covering 4 gameweeks instead of 3). Then when you correctly flagged that, I overcorrected to gwsSinceBoost === 1 starting (excluding the boost GW), which we just shipped and you're now reverting.

The right interpretation was always: the boost gameweek itself = Fresh, and the next 2 gameweeks decay. Three gameweeks total starting from the boost. I should have nailed this on the first try and didn't. The back-and-forth cost two extra commits.

Claude responded: Three real changes.
Three real changes. Let me make sure I have each one right before drafting.

Change 1 — DGW handling for flame indicator: per-match, not per-gameweek.

Current behavior: a player with two matches in the same gameweek (DGW) gets one flame indicator on the player level if either match was a boost. The granularity is gameweek-level.

Your correction: the flame should be per-match. If a player had a +3 in match A of GW30 (DGW) and a +1 in match B of the same GW30, only match A's card shows fresh flame. The GW30 entry on the live indicator should reflect the most recent boost match within the streak window.

Worth thinking through: what does "most recent boost match" mean for the live indicator? If GW30 had two matches and only one was a boost, the live indicator at currentGW=30 should show fresh (assuming the boost match is the most recent boost). If currentGW=31, should it show recent (1 match after the boost) or recent (1 GW after)? The semantic shifts from gameweek-counting to match-counting.

I think the cleanest interpretation: the streak window measures in gameweeks, but the per-card flame (Match History) is per-match. So:

Player has boost in GW30 match A, no boost in GW30 match B
Match A card: fresh flame
Match B card: also fresh flame? Or no flame? (it's a separate match in the same gameweek)
GW31 cards: recent flame
GW32 cards: fading flame
The question is whether match B in the boost gameweek shares the streak status with match A. Two reasonable answers:

Interpretation A: Match B shares fresh flame (gameweek 30 is in the streak window, all matches in GW30 show streak status at appropriate level for that gameweek)

Interpretation B: Match B shows no flame (only the specific match that earned the boost gets fresh flame; subsequent matches even within the same GW count as +1, +2, etc.)

I think you mean Interpretation B based on "per match not just GW." Confirm?

Change 2 — Injury/stale text color affects player name color.

Currently injury status is shown by a small dot/icon. You want the player's name text itself to render in the appropriate color:

Status 'i' (injured) → name renders in red
Status 'd' (doubtful) → name renders in amber/orange
Stale data → name renders in gray
Both injury and stale → injury wins (red over gray)
This is a stronger visual signal than a small dot. Makes the entire player row read as "this player has an issue" without requiring users to spot a small indicator.

Change 3 — Hot streak indicator hidden on players with injury or stale data flags.

If a player is injured or has stale data, no flame indicator on the player surface (rows, cards, headers). However, the Match History strip still shows flames on the relevant past gameweeks — those are historical and the player was healthy then.

This makes sense: the hot streak is meant to surface "good pick now" candidates, but a player who's currently injured or flagged-stale is NOT a good pick now regardless of past streak. The flame would be misleading.

Quick clarification on the priority interaction: if a player is hot AND injured, the flame is hidden on player surfaces and the name renders red. The Match History past gameweeks still show flames. Confirmed?

Three confirmations before I draft:

Tap your three answers when ready.

A note: the third change has product implications worth being explicit about. Hiding the flame on injured/stale players means a user scanning the Players list won't see "this player just had a boost" for someone who's currently flagged. That's intentional — the indicator is about "good pick now" — but means the player effectively disappears from the hot streak surface even if their boost moment was significant.

If you ever want to bring an injured-but-recovering player back into focus, the Match History flames (which we're preserving) would tell that story. The live indicator is forward-looking, the history is descriptive.

Apr 28
Claude responded: Locked in.
Locked in. Here's the prompt:

Three quick fixes to v1.6 Hot Streak. UI-level changes, no algorithm work, no recompute.

Fix 1 — Per-match streak counting (DGW handling)
Current: streak counts in gameweek units. A player with multiple matches in a DGW (e.g., GW30 has match A and match B) currently treats the whole gameweek as one unit.

Corrected: streak should count in match units, not gameweek units. The 3-match streak window applies match-by-match.

Logic per Match History card:

For each match M in the player's match history (sorted chronologically):
Find the most recent boost match B where B.matchOrder ≤ M.matchOrder AND B.delta ≥ 3
If no boost found → no flame on this card
Compute matchesSinceBoost = M.matchOrder - B.matchOrder
If matchesSinceBoost === 0 → 'fresh'
If matchesSinceBoost === 1 → 'recent'
If matchesSinceBoost === 2 → 'fading'
Else → no flame
Where matchOrder is a chronological index across all the player's matches (not the gameweek number). Matches in the same DGW have sequential matchOrder values.

Example: Player has matches in GW28 (single), GW29 match A (DGW), GW29 match B (DGW), GW30 (single), GW31 (single):

matchOrder 0: GW28
matchOrder 1: GW29 match A
matchOrder 2: GW29 match B
matchOrder 3: GW30
matchOrder 4: GW31
If GW29 match A had a +3 (boost):

GW29 match A card: fresh flame (matchesSinceBoost = 0)
GW29 match B card: recent flame (matchesSinceBoost = 1)
GW30 card: fading flame (matchesSinceBoost = 2)
GW31 card: no flame (matchesSinceBoost = 3, expired)
Live indicator on player surfaces
The live indicator (Player Detail header, list rows) reflects the most recent match's streak state, not the current gameweek's state. So at currentGW=30 (1 match after the GW29A boost), the live indicator shows 'recent' — based on GW30 being match index 3, with the boost at index 1, so matchesSinceBoost = 2 → fading? Wait, let me recheck.

Actually re-read the example: GW30 is matchOrder 3, boost was matchOrder 1, so matchesSinceBoost = 3 - 1 = 2. That's 'fading'. Then GW31 is matchOrder 4, matchesSinceBoost = 3, expired.

So the live indicator at the time GW30 is the latest match shows 'fading' — three matches after the boost (boost match, then DGW second match, then GW30) the streak ends.

Implication: for players who play DGWs, their streak burns through faster. A boost in GW29A means by GW31, they're already cold. For non-DGW players, a boost in GW29 means by GW32, they're cold (3 matches = 3 gameweeks).

This is the intended semantic per "per match not just GW." Confirm by reviewing the Garner case from the screenshot:

Garner had a +3 in GW31
If no DGWs in GW31, GW32, or GW33: GW31 fresh, GW32 recent, GW33 fading, GW34 cold
This matches what the user expects
Generalize the utility
Rename computeHotStreakAtGameweek to computeHotStreakAtMatch, taking match index instead of gameweek. The Match History card iterates through matches and calls this for each. The live indicator passes the player's most recent match index.

Fix 2 — Player name text color reflects status priority
When a player has injury or stale-data flags, the player's NAME TEXT (not just a separate icon) renders in the appropriate color:

Priority order (highest first):

Status 'i' (injured) → name renders in red (the existing status-danger color)
Status 'd' (doubtful) → name renders in amber/orange (the existing status-warning color)
Stale data flag → name renders in muted gray (the existing status-muted color)
No flag → name renders in default text color
Injury beats stale (a player who is both injured and stale renders red, not gray). Doubtful beats stale similarly.

Affected surfaces:

All player rows in lists (Players list table, Players list mobile cards)
Dashboard Risers/Fallers/Watchlist/Leaderboard rows
My Team Starting XI rows, Bench rows
Player Detail page header (the Fraunces 44px name)
The existing dot/icon indicators for status and stale stay where they are. The name color is an additional reinforcing signal.

Implementation: add a getPlayerNameColorClass(player) utility that returns the appropriate color class based on priority. Apply to all surfaces that render player names.

Fix 3 — Hide flame indicator on player surfaces when injury or stale flag is active
When a player has status 'i', 'd', or stale-data flag, hide the hot streak flame indicator on player surfaces:

Hide on player rows in lists
Hide on Dashboard cards (Risers/Fallers/Watchlist/Leaderboard)
Hide on My Team rows
Hide on Player Detail page header (the lg variant near the name)
The flame on Match History cards is preserved regardless of current injury/stale status. Those flames represent historical streak state at the time those matches were played, which is still informative.

Reasoning: the live flame says "good pick now" but an injured/doubtful/stale player isn't a good pick now regardless of past streak. The flame would be misleading. Match History flames are historical and remain accurate as descriptive data.

Implementation: in HotStreakIndicator component (or wherever it's rendered on player surfaces), check player.status and stale flag. If any is active, return null. Apply this check at the rendering call site, not in the component itself — so Match History cards can pass a forceShow={true} prop or similar to bypass the check.

Or cleaner: split into two components:

HotStreakIndicator — pure flame rendering, accepts level prop, renders unconditionally
LivePlayerStreakIndicator — wraps the above, applies the injury/stale hiding logic
Match History card uses HotStreakIndicator directly. All player-surface usages use LivePlayerStreakIndicator.

Tests
For Fix 1 (per-match counting):

Generate test data with a DGW where match A has +3 and match B has different event
Match A card → fresh flame
Match B card → recent flame
Subsequent GW card → fading flame
GW after that → no flame
Live indicator at the most recent match shows correct level
For Fix 2 (name color):

Player with status 'i' → name has red color class
Player with status 'd' → name has amber color class
Player with stale flag, status 'a' → name has muted color class
Player with both stale AND injury → name has red color class (injury wins)
Player with no flags → name has default color class
For Fix 3 (hide flame on flagged players):

Hot player with status 'a' → flame visible on player row
Hot player with status 'i' → flame hidden on player row
Hot player with status 'd' → flame hidden on player row
Hot player with stale flag → flame hidden on player row
Match History card for hot player with current injury → flame VISIBLE on historical card
What stays unchanged
Algorithm and calculator (no changes)
Database schema (no changes)
Sync pipeline (no changes)
Three-level visual language (Fresh / Recent / Fading)
Flame icon and current visual styling
GW labeling on lg variant
Position next to player identity
Bubbling behavior on Players list
Sign-off
Tests green (target maintained or +10-15 for new test cases)
Lint clean, typecheck clean
Screenshots:
Garner page (or a player with known DGW boost): Match History shows correct per-match flame distribution
Player with injury status: name in red, no flame on player row, but Match History flames intact
Player with stale flag: name in gray, no flame on player row, but Match History flames intact
Player with both stale AND injury: name in red (injury wins)
Direct comparison: an injured player and a hot-and-healthy player on the same Players list, showing the visual differentiation clearly
Commit message: fix(hot-streak): per-match streak counting, name color by status, hide live flame for flagged players
A note for you, the human:

These three fixes are coupled in a useful way: they tighten the visual semantics so each indicator carries clear meaning.

Name color says: "this player has a problem worth knowing about, the name itself signals it"

Hidden flame on flagged players says: "this player isn't a good pick right now even if they had a moment recently, don't surface them as hot"

Per-match streak says: "the streak burns through faster for high-fixture-volume players, which matches reality"

After this lands, the Players list scanning experience should be much sharper. A user looking for captain candidates can:

Skip players whose names render red/amber (injury concerns)
Skip players whose names render gray (data unreliable)
Spot players with flame icons (hot streak active, healthy, recent data)
That's a much faster decision-making loop than dot-by-dot scanning.

Remain visually a "flame" (recognizable as the same icon family)

Cool ember tone — the embers after the fire

One-line change, maximum visual clarity:
[HotStreakIndicator.tsx](vscode-webview://0d8k1eram1th62rsh9cf4ds9ljj48sqmm1jg7ne5qe3q84i3imek/src/components/confidence/HotStreakIndicator.tsx#L25) — low_hot.colorClass changed from text-[#f59e0b] (amber) → text-[#94a3b8] (slate-400).
Why slate-400 works:

Breaks out of the warm-hue family entirely (red → orange → blue-gray)
19% saturation vs text-muted's 4% — clearly not "disabled gray"
Previous amber #f59e0b was also dangerously close to --color-status-warning: #fbbf24, which created ambiguity in the warning/streak signal space
